program_outcomes: Matematik, fen ve mühendislik bilgilerini uygulama becerisi
Deney tasarlama, deney yapma, deney sonuçlarını analiz etme ve yorumlama becerisi

Ekonomik, çevresel, sosyal, politik ahlaki, sağlık ve güvenlik, üretilebilirlik ve sürdürülebilirlik gibi gerçekçi gereksinimleri karşılayacak biçimde bir sistemi, parçayı ya da süreci tasarlama becerisi
Mühendislik problemlerini saptama, tanımlama, ve çözme becerisi
Mühendislik çözümlerinin, evrensel ve toplumsal boyutlarda etkilerini anlamak için gerekli genişlikte eğitim
Yaşam boyu öğrenmenin gerekliliği bilinci ve bunu gerçekleştirebilme becerisi
Mühendislik uygulamaları için gerekli olan teknikleri ve modern araçları kullanma becerisi

courses.name: Algorithms and Programming I
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to basic computer literacy and fundamental programming concepts using Java. It aims to develop an object-oriented engineering approach for analyzing and designing solutions to large problems.
courses.description: Basic computer literacy: terminology, system components, and operation. Fundamentals of computer programming with Java: sequence, decision, repetition, methods, parameters, arrays, syntax, style, compilation, debugging and maintenance. Object-oriented programming with Java: objects, classes, inheritance, polymorphism, abstract classes, and interfaces. The emphasis is on an object-oriented engineering approach with proper analysis and design to solve large problems using computers.
learning_outcomes: Make informed judgments in engineering situations that consider the impact of engineering solutions in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts
Develop and conduct an experiment
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction, Algorithms & Computers
Hafta 2: Java basics. Fundamental data types.
Hafta 3: Input/output, assignment.
Hafta 4: Decisions
Hafta 5: Decisions and repetition.
Hafta 6: Repetition
Hafta 7: Repetition
Hafta 8: Methods
Hafta 9: ArrayLists
Hafta 10: Arrays
Hafta 11: Partially-filled arrays.
Hafta 12: OOP: implementing and using classes.
Hafta 13: OOP: implementing and using classes.
Hafta 14: Basic Searching & Sorting
resources: Big Java: Late Objects, Big Java Late Objects, Enhanced eText, 2nd Edition, 2016, Wiley

courses.name: Orientation
courses.purpose: This course introduces first-year students to the university's academic and social environment through orientation activities. It helps students become familiar with campus life, services, and opportunities while supporting their transition to university.
courses.description: Introduction to the university's academic and social environment through a series of activities. The course includes talks by university administrators and guest speakers, workshops, concerts, and tours of departments. Sports centers, computing facilities, and the library are also part of the orientation program. Students complete the required set of activities and the minimum requirements in the orientation book.
learning_outcomes: Describe the academic requirements and expectations
Describe the campus environment, services and opportunities provided
Develop strategies for supporting educational and personal goals
Establish interaction with peers, faculty and staff
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Attending to the activities offered in the selected/assigned program option held over a three-day period

courses.name: English and Composition I
courses.purpose: The central basis of ENG 101 is to introduce students to an academic approach to thinking, reading, speaking and writing in an integrated, meaningful manner such that they are able to apply the skills learnt to their departmental studies. In addition, the ENG 101 course aims to further develop the students' linguistic accuracy and range in English.
courses.description: This course introduces students to academic thinking, reading, speaking, and writing in an integrated way, with emphasis on applying these skills to departmental studies. It also focuses on improving students' linguistic accuracy and range in English.
learning_outcomes: Can critically analyse and evaluate academic texts to understand and record both their meaning and potential value
Can critically analyse and evaluate spoken input to understand and record both their meaning and potential value
Can develop well-reasoned written arguments that are supported by valid and reliable evidence
Can develop well-reasoned spoken arguments that are supported by valid and reliable evidence
Can write focused, coherent, thesis driven academic essays
Can incorporate citation skilfully and accurately, and distinguish between own and others' voice
Can effectively communicate with others orally in the form of formal presentations and discussions/debates
Can use the language accurately and appropriately
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course + Diagnostic reading and writing task + Student learning outcomes activity
Hafta 2: Analysis and discussion of readings for academic essay 1 + Input and practice on academic summary and critical response
Hafta 3: Analysis and discussion of readings for academic essay 1 + Input and practice on academic summary and response + Input on writing introductions
Hafta 4: Input on outlining an academic essay + Input on organizing academic essay + Input and practice on writing body paragraphs + Analysis and discussion of readings for academic essay 1
Hafta 5: Input on writing conclusion paragraphs + Brainstorming and outlining for academic essay 1 + In-class draft writing + Input on student led discussions
Hafta 6: Tutorials on academic essay 1 + Input on in-text and end-text citations and essay format + Academic essay 1 final draft + Student led discussions
Hafta 7: Tutorials on academic essay 1 + Analysis and discussion of readings for academic essay 2 + Feedback on academic essay 1 + Input on writing a clear and well developed argument
Hafta 8: Analysis and discussion of readings for academic essay 2 + Input on academic register + Input on clarity, concision and readability + Input on providing effective text based evidence
Hafta 9: Analysis and discussion of readings for academic essay 2 + Input on self-progress reflection task
Hafta 10: Brainstorming and outlining for academic essay 2 + In-class writing of academic essay 2 + Self-progress reflection
Hafta 11: Feedback on academic essay 2 + Input on logical fallacies + Input on common language mistakes and editing + Analysis and discussion of readings for oral component
Hafta 12: Analysis and discussion of readings + Feedback on academic essay 2 + Input on delivering effective presentations
Hafta 13: Brainstorming and outlining for oral component
Hafta 14: Student presentations + Revisiting student learning outcomes + Recap on the course topics, critical reading and academic essay writing

courses.name: Introduction to Modern Biology
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the fundamental concepts and major topics of modern biology, providing a broad foundation for understanding living systems.
courses.description: This course introduces students to important topics of biology such as molecules of life, organization of the cell, chromosomes and cell division, genetics, molecular genetics, recombinant DNA technology, genetic diseases, evolution, animal development, biotechnology and recent scientific advancements.
learning_outcomes: Describe common properties shared by all forms of life and explain why evolution is the core theme of biology
Describe the life supporting properties of water
Describe the macromolecules building up cells and understand the biological organization of a cell
Understand the perpetuation of life: cell division and reproduction
Describe the patterns of inheritance
Explain how DNA serves as the basis of heredity, how it replicates, and how it controls the cell
Describe the patterns of gene expression in response to the organism’s needs.
Understand the basis of methods and applications of gene cloning, DNA profiling, genetically modified organisms and genomics
Understand how populations evolve
Figure out the primate diversity
Learn how varied defenses work to protect the body
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: INTRODUCTION TO THE COURSE
Hafta 2: Chapter 1. Biology: Exploring Life
Hafta 3: Chapter 2. The Chemical Basis of Life
Hafta 4: Chapter 3. The Molecules of Cells
Hafta 5: Chapter 4. A Tour of the Cell
Hafta 6: Chapter 8. The Cellular Basis of Reproduction and Inheritance
Hafta 7: Chapter 9. Patterns of Inheritance
Hafta 8: Chapter 10. Molecular Biology of the Gene & Movie (GATTACA, available on DVD)
Hafta 9: Chapter 10. Molecular Biology of the Gene
Hafta 10: Chapter 11. How Genes are Controlled
Hafta 11: Chapter 12. DNA Technology and Genomics
Hafta 12: Chapter 13. How populations Evolve & Supplementary Documentary (What Darwin Never Knew, available online)
Hafta 13: Chapter 19. The Evolution of Vertebrate Diversity & Supplementary Documentary (Ape To Man, available online)
Hafta 14: Chapter 19. The Evolution of Vertebrate Diversity
Hafta 15: Chapter 24. The Immune System
resources: Campbell Biology: Concepts and Connections, Campbell, Reece, Taylor, Simon, Dickey, 9th or 10th Global Edition, Pearson

courses.description: Review of object-oriented programming concepts with Java. Object-Oriented Design. Graphical User Interfaces. Recursion. Algorithm analysis: time and space considerations. Sorting and searching. Streams. Exceptions. Basic data structures: lists, stacks, and queues. Java Collections Framework. Students undertake a large software project involving teamwork, independent learning, writing and presenting requirements, user interface design, and project documentation.

Apply engineering design process to produce solutions that meet specified needs

courses.purpose: The central basis of ENG 102 is to consolidate students' academic approach to thinking, reading, speaking and writing and language usage, as initiated in ENG 101. courses.description: The course focuses on academic reading, writing, speaking, and language use. It includes text discussion and analysis, revision of academic writing skills, academic essay writing, oral presentation skills, research skills, outlining, research paper development, and academic writing language workshops.

courses.name: Discrete and Combinatorial Mathematics
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the fundamental ideas and techniques of discrete mathematics that are widely used in mathematics, computer science, and related fields. It builds the logical and combinatorial foundation needed for rigorous problem solving and further study.
courses.description: Fundamental principles of counting, including rules of sums and product, permutations and combinations. Fundamentals of logic and integers, including mathematical induction, recursive definitions, prime numbers, greatest common divisor, Cartesian products and relations, pigeonhole principle, partial orders, equivalence relations and partitions. The principle of inclusion and exclusion. Sums and recurrence relations: first and second order linear recurrence relations, finite and infinite calculus, infinite sums. Integer functions including floor and ceiling applications and recurrences, and the modulo operation. Generating functions including the method of generating functions for solving recurrences and exponential generating functions. Introduction to graph theory including graph isomorphism, Euler tours, Hamiltonian paths and cycles, planar graphs, and graph coloring.
learning_outcomes: Apply knowledge of counting principles, logic, sets, probability, properties of integers, relations, functions, mathematical induction, pigeonhole principle, generating functions, solving recurrence relations, and graphs
Create and validate computer programs using logic, properties of integers, mathematical induction, pigeonhole principle, generating functions, and graphs
Optimize computer programs using counting principles, probability, relations, functions, solutions of recurrence relations, and graphs
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Cardinality of sets (2.5.1, ..., 2.5.3)
Hafta 2: Modular arithmetic, primes and greatest common divisor (4.1.1,...,4.1.5, 4.2.4, 4.3.1,...,4.3.4)
Hafta 3: Modular arithmetic, primes and greatest common divisor (4.3.6,...,4.3.8)
Hafta 4: Congruences and their applications (4.4.1,...,4.4.7)
Hafta 5: Congruences and applications (4.6.7, 5.1.1,...,5.1.8)
Hafta 9: Advanced Counting Techniques (8.1.1, 8.1.2, 8.2.1, 8.2.2, 8.2.3)
Hafta 10: Advanced counting techniques: (8.3.1, 8.3.2)
Hafta 11: Advanced counting techniques: (8.4.1,8.4.2,8.4.3,8.4.4,8.4.5)
resources: Discrete Mathematics and its Applictaions, Kenneth H. Rosen, 2012, McGraw-Hill

courses.name: Digital Design
courses.description: Number systems, binary numbers, logic levels, transistors, gates, Boolean expressions. Combinational logic: Boolean algebra, simplification of Boolean expressions. Logic minimization with Karnaugh maps, don't-care conditions. Introduction to System Verilog. Combinational building blocks, multiplexers, decoders, propagation delays, glitches. Verilog modeling. Sequential logic: SR latch, D-latch, D flip-flop, synchronous sequential circuits. Finite State Machine design, Moore and Mealy models, state encodings, timing of sequential circuits. Verilog modeling of sequential circuits. Signed numbers, adders, ALU, comparators. Registers, register files. Counters, timers. High level state machines, RTL design, RAM, ROM. FPGA, programmable processors.
learning_outcomes: Apply engineering design process to produce solutions that meet specified needs
Take into consideration public health, safety, and welfare, as well as global, cultural, social, environmental, and economic factors in engineering design process
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction, digital values, number systems: decimal, binary and hexadecimal
Hafta 2: Logic gates and physical characteristics, CMOS transistors, power consumption; Boolean algebra; Boolean equations, canonical forms
Hafta 3: Combinational logic, hardware reduction, X and Z logic values, introduction to Verilog
Hafta 4: Karnaugh maps, MUXes and decoders; Combinational timing & non-ideal behavior, Verilog modelling
Hafta 5: Latches & flip-flops, basic register, synchronous logic design
Hafta 6: Finite state machines, encoding, Mealy vs. Moore
Hafta 7: FSM examples, Verilog modelling
Hafta 8: Timing, parallelism, Verilog modelling; MIDTERM EXAM
Hafta 9: Arithmetic functions, adders, subtractors, comparators, shifters, ALU, Verilog models
Hafta 10: Counters, shift registers, Timers, Verilog models
Hafta 11: Counters, shift registers, Timers, Verilog models
Hafta 12: High Level State Machines
Hafta 13: High Level State Machines
Hafta 14: Programmable Processors
resources: Required - Textbook: Digital Desgn and Computer Architecture, David M. Harris, Sarah L. Harris, 2013 Second Edition, Morgan Kaufmann
Recommended - Textbook: Digital Design, with RTL Design ,VHDL and Verilog, Farank Vahid, 2011 2nd Edition, John Wiley

courses.name: Collegiate Activities Program I
courses.purpose: This course provides a ground for students to engage in diversity, creativity, and commitment outside coursework through participation in activities mainly provided by student clubs. It also encourages students to take part in designing, shaping, monitoring, and grading the course through their activity participation.
courses.description: Participation in various activities provided mainly by student clubs. Student activity in designing and shaping the course as well as monitoring and grading performance. Grading is based on points accumulated by participation in activities. The course is mandatory for four-year students and is taken in the third semester. It is a non-credit, pass/fail course and is a prerequisite for GE 251.
learning_outcomes: Develop strategies for broadening perspectives for reaching educational and personal goals
Establish interaction with other students and student clubs
Develop respect to the wider community and the world
Develop interest in intellectual and artistic/creative life of humanity
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Attending to a diverse set of activities offered throughout the semester

courses.name: Cultures Civilizations and Ideas I
courses.purpose: This half of the year-long course introduces students to the study of culture and civilization through close reading of primary texts in the ancient traditions of the Near East and the Mediterranean. It also introduces students to modern critical readings and discussion of the value and weight of this tradition.
courses.description: The course focuses on close reading of primary texts from the ancient Near Eastern and Mediterranean traditions, together with modern critical readings. It covers major works and ideas related to literary craft, philosophical thought, and the interpretation of cultural and civilizational traditions.
learning_outcomes: Prepare presentations using relevant evidence and examples from original sources.
Critically analyze primary texts via close reading in order to assess the contributions of major figures in the western tradition.
Produce short essay responses that demonstrate understanding of historical developments, key ideas and debates that have shaped the ancient world.
Produce a complex and in-depth response (written/oral) on schedule by planning, setting goals and evaluating progress to reach a desired outcome.
Appraise how language, form and genre may shape what it is possible to think and know at particular historical moments.
Apply readings from one or more texts to elucidate others from very different contexts across a broad chronological frame.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction. What is "culture" and "civilization" and why should we study them? What is an Epic?
Hafta 2: Epic of Gilgamesh
Hafta 3: Finish Epic of Gilgamesh, Introduction to the Iliad
Hafta 4: Iliad Books 1, 3
Hafta 5: Iliad, books 9, 16 (and some in between!)
Hafta 6: Iliad, 18, 20, 21
Hafta 7: Finish Iliad
Hafta 8: Introduction to Greek Theater Begin King Oedipus
Hafta 9: King Oedipus
Hafta 10: Introduction to Greek philosophy Republic
Hafta 11: The Republic, books 1, 2, 3
Hafta 12: The Republic, 4, 5, 6
Hafta 13: The Republic, 7, 10
Hafta 14: Revision
resources: The Epic of Gilgamesh, Penguin
Republic, Plato, Hackett
Iliad, Homer, Fagle, Penguin
Three Theban plays, Fagle, Penguin

courses.name: Data Structures
courses.description: Introduction to C++. Data abstraction and dynamic memory management. Implementation and analysis of abstract data types. Lists. Trees. Tables. Priority Queues. Heaps. Balanced trees. Hashing. Graphs.

courses.name: Linear Algebra and Differential Equations
courses.description: Systems of linear equations, elimination methods, matrices and matrix operations. Invertible matrices, determinants. Vector spaces, basis and dimension. Inner product spaces, orthogonality, orthogonal basis, rank and nullity. Eigenvalues and eigenvectors, systems of differential equations, matrix-valued functions. First and second order linear differential equations. n-th order linear differential equations, method of undetermined coefficient, variation of parameters.
learning_outcomes: Express basic skills of ordinary differential equations and how such equations are used in engineering and science
Solve systems of linear equations
Compute and use determinants to solve system of linear equations
Analyze vectors in Euclidean spaces geometrically and algebraically
Discuss the concepts of span, linear independence, basis, dimension, and aplly these concepts to various vector spaces
Determine and use orthogonality to find a basis for the nullspace of a matrix
Compute eigenvectors and eigenvalues of a matrix and apply them to some diagonalization processes
Use MATLAB for visualization and calculation of differential equations and linear algebra concepts
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Linear Systems, Matrices and Gaussian Elimination, Reduced-Row Echelon Matrices: 3.1, 3.2, 3.3
Hafta 2: Matrix Operations, Inverses of Matrices: 3.4, 3.5

courses.description: Standards and units; vectors and coordinate systems; kinematics; dynamics; work, energy and power; conservation of energy; dynamics of system of particles; collisions; rotational kinematics and dynamics; oscillations.

Analyze problems in basic mechanics by applying universal physical laws using algebra and calculus based mathematics.
Design and implement lab projects on basic mechanics.
Communicate the results of a performed experiment effectively by means of a written report and/or a poster.

courses.description: MIPS instruction set and registers. Branching and procedure calls, addressing modes. Single cycle processor datapath. Single cycle control. Multicycle processor datapath. Multicycle control. Exceptions, interrupts. Pipelining. Pipelining hazards and stalls. Superscalar processor, out of order execution, Register renaming. IA-32 architecture. Memory system performance and hierarchy, caches. Memory system optimization, virtual memory. Memory mapped I/O.
learning_outcomes: Develop and conduct an experiment
Analyze and interpret data
Draw conclusions from experimental data using engineering judgment

courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the principles and stages of object-oriented software development. It also aims to familiarize students with object-oriented software modeling using UML and with CASE tools used in object-oriented development.

Overview of object-oriented software modeling with Unified Modeling Language and exposure to CASE tools for object-oriented development. Experience with such tools and environments through programming assignments and/or a term project.

courses.name: Cultures Civilizations and Ideas II
courses.purpose: The second half of the year-long course "Cultures, Civilizations and Ideas" continues the study of culture through close examination of texts from the Late Middle Ages to modern times. It aims to develop students' ability to interpret major ideas, historical developments, and cultural transformations through primary sources.
courses.description: The course examines texts from the Late Middle Ages, the Renaissance, the Enlightenment, and up to modern times. It focuses on themes such as Modernity and Knowledge, Individualism, Cross-Cultural Contact, and Social Order and Disorder. Close reading and discussion of primary texts are central to the course.
learning_outcomes: Prepare presentations using relevant evidence and examples from original sources.
Critically analyze primary texts via close reading in order to assess the contributions of major figures in the western tradition.
Produce short essay responses that demonstrate understanding of historical developments, key ideas and debates that have shaped the modern world.
Produce a complex and in-depth response (written/oral) on schedule by planning, setting goals and evaluating progress to reach a desired outcome.
Appraise how language, form and genre may shape what it is possible to think and know at particular historical moments.
Apply readings from one or more texts to elucidate others from very different contexts across a broad chronological frame.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction
Hafta 2: Francesco Petrarch, "Letter to Posterity" and "Ascent of Mount Ventoux"; Christine de Pizan, "Treasure of the City of Ladies"
Hafta 3: Pico della Mirandola, "Oration on the Dignity of Man"
Hafta 4: Niccolo Machiavelli, "Letter to Vettori"; Niccolo Machiavelli, "The Prince", ch. 1-8
Hafta 5: Niccolo Machiavelli, The Prince, chs. 9-19
Hafta 6: Niccolo Machiavelli, The Prince, chs. 20-25
Hafta 7: Michel de Montaigne, "On Cannibals"
Hafta 8: Rene Descartes, "Discourse on Method", Parts 1-2
Hafta 9: Rene Descartes, "Discourse on Method", Parts 3-4
Hafta 10: Rene Descartes, Discourse on the Method, Parts 5-6
Hafta 11: Immanuel Kant, "What is Enlightenment"
Hafta 12: Mary Shelly, "Frankenstein"
Hafta 13: Mary Shelly, "Frankenstein"
Hafta 14: Mary Shelly, "Frankenstein"

Frankenstein, Or, The Modern Prometheus, Shelly, Maty, 2003, London, Penguin Books

courses.name: Probability and Statistics for Engineers
courses.description: Basic concepts of probability (sample spaces and events, permutations, combinations, conditional probability and independence). Discrete and continuous random variables, their probability distributions, expected value and distribution parameters. Discrete probability distributions: Binomial, geometric, negative binomial, Poisson distributions. Continuous probability distributions: uniform, normal, exponential, gamma and chi-squared distributions. Jointly distributed and independent Random Variables. Covariance and correlation. The sampling distribution of sample mean. Central Limit Theorem and its applications. Estimation. Confidence Intervals, Hypothesis Testing.
learning_outcomes: Compute probabilities by modeling sample spaces and applying rules of permutations and combinations, independency and conditional probability
Construct the probability distribution of a discrete random variable, based on a real-world situation
Identify the random variable(s) of interest in a given scenario
Find expected values and variances of both discrete and continuous random variables
Demonstrate knowledge of joint probability distributions
Find estimators for unknown parameters of distribution using mathematical methods; such as the Method of Moments and the Method of Maximum Likelihood
Demonstrate ability to do interval estimation for parameters of distribution/population
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Interpreting Probabilities (2.1), Axioms, Interpretations, and Properties of Probability (2.2)
Hafta 2: Counting Methods (2.3). Conditional Probability (2.4), Independence (2.5).
Hafta 3: Random Variables (3.1), Probability Distributions for Discrete Random Variables (3.2), Expected Value and Standard Deviation (3.3), Moments and Moment Generating Functions (3.4)
Hafta 4: The Binomial Distribution (3.5). Poisson Distribution (3.6).
Hafta 5: Other Discrete Distributions (3.7).
Hafta 6: Continuous Random Variables. Probability Density Functions and Cumulative Distribution Functions (4.1). Expected Values and Moment Generating Functions (4.2).
Hafta 7: The Normal (Gaussian) Distribution (4.3), Exponential and Gamma Distributions (4.4). Midterm.
Hafta 8: Other Continuous Distributions (4.5), Transformations of a Random Variable (4.7).
Hafta 9: Jointly Distributed Random Variables (5.1), Expected Values (5.2)
Hafta 10: Covariance and Correlation (5.2), Linear Combinations (5.3)
Hafta 11: Statistics and Their Distributions (6.1). Distribution of Sample Totals, Means, and Proportions; the Central Limit Theorem (6.2).
Hafta 12: Chi-squared and t distributions (6.3). Distributions Based on Normal Random Samples (6.4).
Hafta 13: Point Estimation (7.1). Methods of Moments and Maximum Likelihood (7.2)
Hafta 14: Confidence Intervals (8.1)
resources: Modern Mathematical Statistics with Applications, Jay L. Devore, Kenneth Berk, and Matthew A. Carlton, 3rd edition, Springer

courses.purpose: This course provides students with practical experience in a company setting, allowing them to apply the knowledge and skills learned at school to real engineering problems. It also helps students develop professional responsibility, teamwork, and awareness of the broader impact of engineering solutions.

courses.name: Collegiate Activities Program II
courses.purpose: This course is the second part of the GE 250/251 sequence and is designed to support student participation in collegiate activities. It aims to broaden students' perspectives and encourage engagement in educational, personal, intellectual, and artistic life.
courses.description: Second part of the GE 250/251 sequence. Total points accumulated during GE 250 and GE 251 converted to a letter grade. Participation in student clubs activities.

Establish interaction with other students and student clubs
Develop respect to the wider community and the world
Develop interest in intellectual and artistic/creative life of humanity

courses.name: Mathematical and Computational Foundations of Artificial Intelligence
courses.purpose: This course provides the mathematical foundations needed to analyze and develop intelligent systems. It aims to build theoretical understanding together with computational skills relevant to artificial intelligence applications in computer science.
courses.description: Foundations for analyzing and developing intelligent systems with essential concepts in linear algebra, probability, calculus, optimization, and statistical inference. The course covers estimation and decision theory, regression and regularization, optimization, dimensionality reduction, matrix/tensor decomposition and efficient implementations, Monte Carlo sampling, graphical models and efficient algorithms, variational inference, and performance evaluation.
learning_outcomes: Identify and formulate a complex engineering problem
Solve complex engineering problems by applying principles of engineering, science, and mathematics

courses.name: Algorithms I
courses.description: Asymptotic notation. Divide and conquer approach. Solving recurrences asymptotically. Worst case vs expected run time analysis. Medians and order statistics. Lower bound on comparison-based sorting algorithms vs sorting in linear time. Dynamic programming: 4-step formulation procedure with optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems properties. Greedy algorithms: greedy choice and overlapping subproblems properties. Greedy vs DP algorithms and problem reduction. Amortized analysis.
learning_outcomes: Identify and formulate a complex engineering problem
Solve complex engineering problems by applying principles of engineering, science, and mathematics
Apply engineering design process to produce solutions that meet specified needs
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: analysing algorithms, designing algorithms.
Hafta 2: Asymptotic notation.
Hafta 3: Divide and conquer: Strassen
Hafta 4: Randomized quicksort: analysis.
Hafta 5: Medians and order statistics.
Hafta 6: Heaps: heapsort, priority queues.
Hafta 7: Sorting in linear time.
Hafta 8: Dynamic programming: matrix-chain multiplication, longest common subsequence.
Hafta 9: Dynamic programming: subset sum, number partitioning, optimal binary search tree
Hafta 10: Greedy algorithms: activity selection problem, Hufmann codes.
Hafta 11: Greedy/ DP algorithms: fractional knapsack, 0/1 knpsack
Hafta 12: Amortized Analysis: aggregate, accounting and potential methods.
Hafta 13: Dynamic Tables.
Hafta 14: Review
resources: Introduction to Algorithms, T. H. Cormen, C. E. Leiserson, R. L. Rivest, and C. Stein, 2009/3rd Ed, Mit Press and McGraw-Hill

courses.name: Science Technology and Society
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the major ideas, debates, and historical developments in the field of science, technology, and society (STS). It also seeks to help students think critically about the social, political, ethical, and environmental dimensions of technology.
courses.description: Introduction to science technology and society. History of technology. Technology and politics. Engineering, ethics and responsibility. Social studies and philosophical perspectives. Environmental issues. Health, medicine and technology. Technological controversies. Technology and democracy. Intellectual property and emerging developments. Technology, innovation and responsibility. Special topics.

Use case studies to examine technological trajectories and outcomes
Assess the role that different stakeholders play in shaping technology
Describe how Science, Technology and Society (STS) approaches and multiple disciplinary efforts can lead to alternative technical outcomes
Recognize how historical insights may be used in engineering practice and design skills
Produce a term project

courses.name: History of Turkey
courses.purpose: This course focuses on aspects of Turkey's history with an emphasis on research. It is designed as an interactive course that helps students investigate events, short historical periods, and historic representations.
courses.description: The course introduces students to Turkey's history through a research-oriented and interactive approach. It emphasizes investigating events, short chronological periods, and historic representations, while also developing students' ability to work with historical sources, formulate research questions, and conduct historical analysis.
learning_outcomes: Distinguish between primary and secondary sources.
Identify and establish a meaningful historical question.
Construct an outline that demonstrates awareness of organization and chronology, making use of primary and secondary sources.
Demonstrate knowledge of historical context.
Construct an effective argument in support of a thesis.
Identify and evaluate evidence.
Define basic concepts of historical methodology.
Subject primary sources to internal and external critique.
Cite and reference sources appropriately.
Prioritize key aspects of the research and explain these aspects within a prescribed time.
Function as part of a team.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course, course structure and objectives, teamwork information, and overview of the syllabus. Guidance on narrowing or broadening research scope and formulating research topics with detailed examples from previous projects.
Hafta 2: Research methods, sources of history, and oral history studies. Formation of teams.
Hafta 3: Rules to follow in academic article writing and Chicago style citation. Teams begin structuring their research topics and questions and receive feedback from instructors.

Hafta 4: Weekly individual meetings with the instructor begin. Each team presents its project topic proposal orally and submits the proposal in Microsoft Word format via Moodle by the end of the week.

Hafta 5: The Turkey History Evaluation Committee communicates feedback on project topic proposals. Teams finalize their research topics based on instructor feedback. Methodology quiz is held in the second class of the week.
Hafta 6: Research and meeting with the instructor.
Hafta 7: Research and meeting with the instructor.
Hafta 8: Submission of the preliminary report in Microsoft Word format via Moodle at the end of the week.
Hafta 9: Research and meeting with the instructor.
Hafta 10: Reminders on academic article writing before the submission of the article draft.
Hafta 11: Submission of the first draft of the research article in Microsoft Word format via Moodle.
Hafta 12: Research and meeting with the instructor.
Hafta 13: Research and meeting with the instructor.

Hafta 14: All students attend class and each team gives a 15-20 minute presentation of their project. The 3000-word research article is uploaded to Moodle as a PDF, and oral history projects also submit audio recordings and written transcripts. Peer evaluation forms must be completed and submitted via Moodle.

courses.name: Technical Report Writing and Presentation
courses.purpose: This course aims to help engineering students present various types of information effectively in both written and oral forms. It prepares students to write and organize technical reports and to present academic and technical papers effectively.
courses.description: The course covers technical report writing and presentation skills for engineering students. It includes choosing a project topic, preparing report proposal components, using IEEE referencing style, constructing tables and graphics, formatting reports, developing presentation skills, writing abstracts, organizing final reports, preparing tables of contents and lists of figures/tables, using effective headings and sub-headings, and practicing interview strategies and technical language.
learning_outcomes: Can conduct focused research about an engineering related topic
Can apply basic conventions of technical writing according to IEEE style to document and refer to outside sources and visual information (charts, figures, graphs, tables, diagrams etc.).
Can produce a technical project proposal by participating in the writing process of planning, drafting, revising and editing
Can produce a technical report by participating in the writing process of planning, drafting, revising and editing
Can produce technical proposals and reports considering rhetorical concerns (audience, purpose, tone, organisation, development, coherence)
Can prepare and deliver technical report presentations incorporating research and using visuals.
Can produce accurate, fluent, relevant and focused impromtu speech.
Can use technical language and vocabulary in written and oral production.

courses.name: Principles of Engineering Management
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the fundamental concepts and analytical tools used in engineering management and engineering economy. It aims to develop the ability to model, analyze, and solve managerial and economic problems encountered in engineering practice.
courses.description: Introduction to management analysis such as management layers, network analysis, project management via CPM/PERT networks, optimization concepts, linear programming, integer programming, and decision analysis; and economic concepts such as cash flow, interest rates, rate of return, demand supply relations, product pricing, taxes, inflation, and related subjects.
learning_outcomes: Construct linear and integer models of common processes in engineering and management
Solve linear programs using the simplex method and perform sensitivity analysis
Solve economical problems involving interest rates, cash flows, rate of returns
Solve a representative real world application
Use linear programming software such as Excel and Xpress
Do effective teamwork
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Operations Research & Brief History of OR
Hafta 2: Linear Programming
Hafta 3: Graphical Solution to 2-variable LP Problems
Hafta 4: Simplex Method to Solve LP Problems
Hafta 5: Simplex Method to Solve LP Problems
Hafta 6: Simplex Method to Solve LP Problems
Hafta 7: Integer Programming
Hafta 8: Integer Programming
Hafta 9: Network Models and Solution Techniques
Hafta 10: Network Models and Solution Techniques
Hafta 11: Project Management via CPM/PERT
Hafta 12: Dynamic Programming
Hafta 13: Dynamic Programming
Hafta 14: Engineering Economy
resources: Operations Research: Applications and Algorithms, Wayne L. Winston, 2004/4th edition, Thomson Brooks/Cole
Contemporary Engineering Economics, C.S. Park, 2010/5th edition, Prentice Hall

courses.name: How Houses Build People
courses.purpose: This course aims to use the basic unit of the house to understand how spaces frame and guide the practices, movement and behavior of people.
courses.description: People have built houses across the globe for millennia. These houses served functions beyond being merely shelters, and they have both been affected, and in turn shaped the societies they are a part of. The cases discussed will be global (ranging from eastern Asia to the Americas) and diachronic (from the prehistory to the contemporary period) in their scope.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course & concepts
Hafta 2: Concepts and Contexts: Lecture 1
Hafta 3: Concepts and Contexts: Lecture 2
Hafta 4: Concepts and Contexts: Lecture 3
Hafta 5: Concepts and Contexts: Lecture 4
Hafta 6: Midterm
Hafta 7: Social and Physical Construction: Lecture 5
Hafta 8: Social and Physical Construction: Lecture 6
Hafta 9: Social and Physical Construction: Lecture 7
Hafta 10: Screening & Discussion
Hafta 11: Contemporary Issues: Lecture 8
Hafta 12: Contemporary Issues: Lecture 9
Hafta 13: Discussion: Synthesis, Review, Exit ticket
Hafta 14: Final Reviews
resources: 6,000 Years of Housing, Schoenauer, Norbert, 2003, Norton (New York)

courses.name: Introduction to Theatre History
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a historical understanding of theatre as one of the oldest art forms. It also helps students appreciate the aesthetics and collaborative nature of theatre while developing their analytical skills through the reading of dramatic works from different periods.
courses.description: This is a survey course focusing on Western theatre history from its origins to the present. It examines how theatre was manifested in different historical periods, including major developments, genres, styles, and representative playwrights and works.
learning_outcomes: Explain different periods, genres, and styles of drama from the origin of performance to the Renaissance
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the Community. Review syllabus. Drama and the Theatre, The Origins of Theatre.
Hafta 2: Ancient Greek Theatre. Greek Tragedy.
Hafta 3: Ancient Greek Theatre. Greek Comedy.
Hafta 4: Roman Theatre, Roman Stage.
Hafta 5: European Medieval Theatre.
Hafta 6: Theatre in Early Modern Europe. The European Renaissance: Humanism and the Classical Past. Theatre Experience in Renaissance Italy.
Hafta 7: Midterm.
Hafta 8: English Theatre and Drama, 1576 - 1642. Theatre in Elizabethan Era. Shakespeare.
Hafta 9: French Theatre, 1630 - 1700. Neoclassicism. Moliere.
Hafta 10: The Enlightenment. German Theatre and Drama. Romanticism and Melodrama, 1800 – 1880.
Hafta 11: Modern Theatre (1880 -1945) – Realism and Naturalism. Henrik Ibsen, Anton Chekhov, August Strindberg
Hafta 12: Avant-Garde and Modernism in Theatre - Epic Theatre - Bertolt Brecht .
Hafta 13: American Psychological Realism. Arthur Miller, Tennessee Williams
Hafta 14: Absurd Theatre. Samuel Beckett, Eugène Ionesco, Edward Albee. Theatre in the 1960s and 70s.
resources: History of Theatre, Oscar G. Brockett, Franklin J. Hildy, 10th Edition, Pearson
The Norton Anthology of Drama, J. Ellen Gainor (Editor), Stanton B. Garner Jr. (Editor), Martin Puchner (Editor), Second Edition
The Essential Theatre, Oscar G. Brockett, Robert B. Ball, John Fleming, Andrew Carlson, Eleventh Edition
Theatre Plays (Plays will be announced by the instructor)

courses.name: History of Built Environment I
courses.purpose: This course introduces the concept of history and continuity in the built environment from prehistory to the end of the 17th century through selected examples from the Middle East, Anatolia, and Europe.
courses.description: Definition of history and concept of continuity in the built environment beginning from prehistory to the end of the 17th century by means of selected examples from the Middle-East, Anatolia and Europe, with a specific emphasis on the formation of architecture, interior architecture and landscape/urban design.

courses.name: Architectural Drawing
courses.purpose: A general overview of the relationship between architectural design and drawing. The course aims to develop students' ability to communicate design ideas through architectural drawing techniques.
courses.description: Introduction to basic principles and techniques of architectural drawings such as plan, section and elevation; three dimensional expression and rendering techniques for visual communication of design ideas.
learning_outcomes: Use drafting tools correctly/appropriately
Draw by using the three major architectural drawing techniques
Respond to specific architectural drawing tasks within an hour by using the textbook and class notes
Demonstrate a command of the three major architectural drawing techniques to arrange design presentations
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: objectives, contents and conduct of the course.
Hafta 2: Orthographic Drawings I
Hafta 3: Orthographic Drawings II
Hafta 4: Orthographic Drawings III
Hafta 5: Architecture and Landscape Drawings I
Hafta 6: Mid-Term I
Hafta 7: Republic Day Holiday, no classes
Hafta 8: Architecture and Landscape Drawings II (Term Project Introduced)
Hafta 9: Architecture and Landscape Drawings III
Hafta 10: Architecture and Landscape Drawings IV
Hafta 11: Oblique Drawings I
Hafta 12: Oblique Drawings II
Hafta 13: Mid-Term II
Hafta 14: Term Project Discussions (Juries Week)
resources: Architectural Graphics, Francis D. K. Ching, Wiley and Sons Inc, New Jersey
Drawing, a creative process, Ching, Francis D.K, 1990, New York: Van Nostrand Reinhold
Basics Technical Drawing, Bielefeld, Bert, and Isabella Skiba, 2013, Basel: Birkhäuser
Basics Freehand Drawing, Afflerbach, Florian, 2014, Basel: Birkhäuser
Drawing Perspective Methods for Artists : 85 Methods for Creating Spatial Illusion in Ar, Boerboom, Peter, and Tim Proetel, 2017, Mass: Rockport Publishers
Architectural Drawing, Dernie, David, 2014, London: Laurence King Publishing

courses.name: Contemporary Problems in Urban Sustainability
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop a critical understanding of sustainability in global urban contexts and to examine how the concept of sustainability is socially and politically constructed.
courses.description: Sustainability within global urban settings; socio-spatially situated understanding of what is 'sustainable'. Socio-political construction of 'sustainability'. Historical and contemporary examples in the North and South; management of natural resources (land, water...), socio-political equity; 'right to the city'. Use of case studies and contemporary examples.

courses.name: History of Built Environment II
courses.purpose: This course examines the historical development of the built environment through selected examples from Ottoman, Turkish, and Western architecture. It aims to help students understand how architecture, interior space, and landscape/urban design were formed and transformed over time through comparative analysis.
courses.description: Examination of selected examples beginning from the 18th century Ottoman, Turkish, and Western architectures by comparative analysis, with a specific emphasis on the formation of architecture, interior space, and landscape/urban design.
learning_outcomes: Able to acquire/provide knowledge of history of architecture to built environment
Demonstrate knowledge of history of architecture by analyzing specific case studies
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Indtroduction: Course overview, Syllabus and Policies
Hafta 2: Renaissance: Humanism or Power Rebranded?
Hafta 3: Baroque as a Global Language, Early Modern Global Exchanges and Networks
Hafta 4: Enlightenment: Age of Reason, Science, and Social Engineering
Hafta 5: Discovery of History, Heritage, and New Programs
Hafta 6: Industrialization, Mobilities, Colonies
Hafta 7: Avant-gardes: One Movement or Many
Hafta 8: Modernism and Gender
Hafta 9: Modern Urbanism and Regional Responses I
Hafta 10: Modern Urbanism and Regional Responses II
Hafta 11: Architectures of Independence
Hafta 12: Modernism deconstructed: Cold War and the Non-Aligned Movements & Postmodernists
Hafta 13: Counter cultures: Hippie-, Anti-, Radical modernity & Poststructuralist Turn
Hafta 14: Contemporary Architecture: Crisis & Agency
resources: The Future of Architecture Since 1889 A Worldwide History, Jean-Louis Cohen, 2016, Phaidon
A Global History of Architecture, Mark Jarzombek, Vikramaditya Prakash, Francis D. K. Ching, 2010, John Wiley & Sons
Modern Architecture: A Critical History 4th eds., Kenneth Frampton, 2006, Thames&Hudson
European Architecture 1750-1890, Barry Bergdoll, 2000, Oxford University Press
Architecture Since 1400, Kathleen James Chakraborty, 2006, University of Minnesota Press
Interior Design since 1900, Anne Massey, 2008, Thames and Hudson- World of Art
Turkey: Modern Architectures in History, Sibel Bozdogan, Esra Akcan, 2012, Reaktion Books

courses.name: Methods and Texts I
courses.purpose: This course gives students the critical methods necessary to interpret a variety of social texts.
courses.description: The primary mode of organization is around genre. A unit on poetry will teach students to identify poetic forms and to scan lines; a unit on fiction will address such issues as narrative voice, characterization, and symbolism; a unit on drama will examine the dimensions of staging and performance; a unit on film will teach students to approach texts in two and three dimensions; a unit on music will examine harmony, musical form, and performance; and a unit on nonfiction prose will examine the status of objectivity in source materials. Other major issues concern the distinction between literal and figurative language, terms for literary studies, and the linguistic diversity of the United States. Students will also learn how to develop a bibliography and how to cite sources using established styles.

courses.name: Designing with Digital Media
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the use of digital media in the design process and representation. It helps students develop basic skills for producing and communicating design ideas through digital drawing and modeling tools.
courses.description: Application of basic concepts and methods to the design process and representation; production of a digital set of drawings.
learning_outcomes: Use digital design media as a graphic communication tool
Evaluate capabilities of contemporary digital design tools in relation to design processes
Develop small-scale design projects using digital media
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction. (Module 1, HW1 Introduced)
Hafta 2: Module 1: 2D Representations
Hafta 3: Module 1: 2D Representations
Hafta 4: Module 1: 2D Representations
Hafta 5: Poster Design
Hafta 6: Mid Term
Hafta 7: Module 2: Modeling
Hafta 8: Module 2: Modeling
Hafta 9: Module 2: Modeling
Hafta 10: Module 3: Rendering
Hafta 11: Module 3: Rendering
Hafta 12: Module 3: Rendering
Hafta 13: Architectural Diagramming
Hafta 14: No Class (Final Jury Week)
resources: Architectural Drawing, Dernie, D., 2014 / Second Edition, London: Laurence King Publishing
Basics Technical Drawing, Bielefeld, B. & Skiba, I., 2013, Basel: Birkhäuser

courses.name: Introduction to American Studies I
courses.purpose: This course provides an introduction to the interdisciplinary study of American culture and literature. It aims to help students understand how different disciplines can be combined to interpret American studies.
courses.description: Through consideration of exemplary moments and problems in the history of the United States, students examine how history, cultural studies, textual criticism, political science, and sociology provide interpretive strategies for American studies. The course also establishes a foundation of knowledge in American geography, political systems, demography, institutions, economics, and social structure to support further study. Tutorial support is provided.

courses.name: Methods and Texts II
courses.purpose: This course continues the work of AMER 115 and develops students' ability to read, analyze, and discuss literary and cultural texts through a genre-based approach.
courses.description: The course is organized around genre. It includes a unit on drama focused on staging and performance, a unit on film focused on analyzing the formal development of a shot sequence, and a unit on the novel focused on the formal components of fiction.
learning_outcomes: Examine and critically evaluate some of the central terms and issues in economic and cultural theory at work in recent debates about globalization.
Situate American Studies and Cultural Studies, as academic disciplines, within broader contemporary social, political, and economic issues.
Sharpen their analytical skills by studying demanding theoretical texts in cultural theory and economic history.
Independently assess the influence of US, or US-dominated, cultural, economic and political institutions over contemporary international developments in the context of globalization.
Hone their written communications skills through in-class essays and assignments.
Refine their oral communication and public speaking skills through individual or group oral presentations and in-class debates.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course; brief introduction to, and recapitulation of, the historicity of temporality.

Hafta 2: The Opening of the Future: Narratives of Progress, Development, Individualism, and Unfettered Markets; From Local Self-Sufficiency to Increased Interdependence; The Centralisation of Power and the Monopolisation of Violence (Norbert Elias).
Hafta 3: The Advent of the Economic Revolution.
Hafta 4: Future Orientation and the Bourgeoisie.
Hafta 5: Legitimizing Capitalism: Social Darwinism.
Hafta 6: The Differentiation of Society and the Problem of Complexity Reduction.
Hafta 7: The Complexity of Modernity: Risk Society and the Contraction of Temporality.
Hafta 8: The Complexity of Modernity: Risk Society and the Contraction of Temporality (continued).
Hafta 9: Review for Mid-term exam; Mid term exam.
Hafta 10: Capitalism, Crises and Insecurity, and Their Effects on Temporality.
Hafta 11: Capitalism, Crises and Insecurity, and Their Effects on Temporality (continued).
Hafta 12: From Fordism to Flexible Accumulation: Temporal Acceleration and the Closing of the Future.
Hafta 13: Risk, Precariousness and Their Effects on Temporality and on Self-formation: Precariousness, neo-ethnic tribalism, and neo-eschatology.
Hafta 14: Let the markets decide! The temporal and Social Repercussions of Laissez-Faire on Emotional Well-Being.
resources: Heilbroner, Robert. The Worldly Philosophers. N.Y: Simon and Schuster, 1967.
Weber, Max. Selected passages from The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism.
Leccardi, Carmen. "Facing uncertainty: Temporality and Biographies in the New Century." Young: Nordic Journal of Youth Research 13.2 (2005): 123-146.
Williams, Raymond. "Social Darwinism", in Problems in Materialism and Culture. London: Verso, 1980.
Luhmann, Niklas. The Differentiation of Society. Translated by Stephen Holmes and Charles Larmore. New York: Columbia University Press, 1982.
Beck, Ulrich. Risk Society. Translated by Mark Ritter. London: Sage, 1992.
Reith, Gerda. "Uncertain times: The Notion of "Risk" and the Development of Modernity." Time and Society, vol 13, No 2/3, 2004.
Harvey, David. The Enigma of Capital and the Crises of Capitalism. Oxford University Press, 2010.
Leccardi, Carmen. "Redefining the Future: Youthful Biographical Constructions in the 21st Century." New Directions for Child and Adolescent Development 113.1 (2006): 37-48.
Harvey, David. The Condition of Postmodernity. Oxford: Blackwell, 1990.
Sennett, Richard. The Corrosion of Character: The Personal Consequences of Work in the New Capitalism. New York: W.W. Norton, 1999.
Ehrenberg, Alain. The Weariness of the Self. McGill-Queen’s University Press, 2010.

courses.name: Introduction to American Studies II
courses.purpose: This course continues the work of AMER 195 by building a foundation for the interdisciplinary study of the United States. It aims to deepen students' understanding of key themes in American Studies and to strengthen their ability to analyze cultural and social issues through primary documents.
courses.description: In this course, students study education, political systems, gender, race, and class in greater depth. The course emphasizes the examination of primary documents to develop understanding of these topics, and tutorial support is provided.
learning_outcomes: Produce a logically coherent argument about the diversity and dynamic quality of American cultures, including cultural identities, products, and everyday practices, based on evidence

Present clear textual and cultural analyses using appropriate methods and concepts pertinent to the field of American Studies, including textual analysis, ethnography, object analysis, and archival research
Theorize and write critically about the ways American experiences and institutions are shaped by and reflected in the products of culture
Discuss and defend their theories about American cultures and identities with others in a group/seminar setting

Express themselves clearly orally and in written English to demonstrate mastery in the following skills: close reading of texts; cultural interpretation; historical thinking; writing, research, and public speaking
Theorize and write critically about how American cultures and identities are understood and imagined in a global context
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction/ What is method?
Hafta 2: How to be a creative and critical thinker/Asking good questions
Hafta 3: What is a text? How to analyze a TV series
Hafta 4: Textual analysis in practices
Hafta 5: Material Culture/Theory of Things
Hafta 6: Primary and secondary sources
Hafta 7: Myth and Symbol
Hafta 8: Ethnography
Hafta 9: Archives/Special Collections at Bilkent Library
Hafta 10: Digital Archives/Assembling our own archives
Hafta 11: Ramadan
Hafta 12: Crafting an effective argument/How to write an abstract and project proposal
Hafta 13: Foucault
Hafta 14: Gramsci/Presentations

courses.name: American History II
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a broad understanding of the major developments in United States history from the Reconstruction era to the present. It emphasizes the political, social, economic, and cultural transformations that shaped modern America.
courses.description: A study of the history of the United States, beginning with the Reconstruction era and devoting particular attention to the Gilded Age, the Great Depression, the two World Wars, and the Cold War.
learning_outcomes: Students will identify the major events, developments, and problems in North American history between 1865 and the present.
Students connect primary sources and texts to broader issues in American social, cultural, and political history.
Students evaluate and assess the validity and meaning of primary written and visual sources.
Students develop analytical reading and writing skills through close readings, class discussions, group work, and writing exercises.
Students demonstrate an appreciation for the complexity of the past, and for the ways people and groups have worked to make social, cultural, and political change.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Capital and Labor (1880s-1910s) Readings: chapter 16 in The American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 2: Conquering the West (1870s-1910s) Readings: chapter 17 in the American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 3: Life in Industrial America (1880s-1910s) Readings: chapter 18 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 4: American Empire (1890s-1910s) Readings: chapter 19 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 5: The Progressive Era (1890s-1920s) Readings: chapter 20 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 6: World War I and Its Aftermath (1914-1920s) Readings: chapter 21 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 7: The New Era (1920s) Readings: chapter 22 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 8: The Great Depression Readings: chapter 23 in American Yawp and supplemental readings MIDTERM EXAM.
Hafta 9: World War II Readings: chapter 24 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 10: The Cold War (1940s-1960s) Readings: chapter 25 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 11: The Affluent Society (1950s-1960s) Readings: chapter 26 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 12: The Sixties Readings: chapter 27 in American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 13: The Unravelling (1960s-1970s) Readings: chapter 28 of American Yawp and supplemental readings
Hafta 14: The Triumph of the Right Wk 14: Readings: chapters 29 of American Yawp and supplemental readings
resources: The American Yawp: A Massively Collaborative Open U.S. History Textbook, 2019, Stanford

courses.name: American Theater
courses.purpose: This course examines selected 20th-century plays and major issues in the study of theater, including its relationship to mass cultural forms, state support, and documentary truth.
courses.description: A study of selected 20th century plays, movements, and issues raised by the study of theater, including its relationship to mass cultural forms, state support, and documentary truth. Writers will include Lorraine Hansberry, Arthur Miller, Eugene O'Neill, Marc Blitzstein, Arthur Arent, Luis Valdez, and Anna Deavere Smith.

courses.name: American Intellectual History I
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the intellectual history of the United States from the colonial era to the Civil War, helping them understand the major ideas, debates, and thinkers that shaped early American society and political culture.
courses.description: This course deals with the intellectual history of the United States from the colonial era to the Civil War. It explores the Puritan tradition, republican ideology in America, Tocqueville and democracy, the Transcendentalists, the debate over slavery, views on the role of women in the new republic, and antebellum social science. Primary texts by John Winthrop, Benjamin Franklin, Thomas Paine, Thomas Jefferson, James Madison, Alexis de Tocqueville, Ralph Waldo Emerson, Henry David Thoreau, William Lloyd Garrison, David Walker, Frederick Douglass, Sarah Grimke, Catharine Beecher, Margaret Fuller, Horace Bushnell, Henry C. Carey, and George Fitzhugh are read and discussed.
resources: Primary texts by John Winthrop, Benjamin Franklin, Thomas Paine, Thomas Jefferson, James Madison, Alexis de Tocqueville, Ralph Waldo Emerson, Henry David Thoreau, William Lloyd Garrison, David Walker, Frederick Douglass, Sarah Grimke, Catharine Beecher, Margaret Fuller, Horace Bushnell, Henry C. Carey, and George Fitzhugh

courses.name: American History I
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a broad understanding of the history of the United States and the major political, social, and economic developments that shaped the nation in its formative periods.
courses.description: A study of the history of the United States, with particular attention to the colonial era, the American Revolution, the early national period, and the Civil War.

courses.name: American Intellectual History II
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the intellectual history of the United States from 1865 to the present and to help them understand major thinkers, writers, and debates that shaped American thought in this period.
courses.description: This course deals with the intellectual history of the United States from 1865 to the present. It explores the pragmatist tradition, African Americans' struggle for civil rights and economic betterment, the American social sciences, intellectuals and the public sphere, feminist thought, trends in American literary studies and cultural studies, debates over multiculturalism, the controversy over the "clash of civilizations", and recent American thought on the philosophy and history of science.
learning_outcomes: Introduce students to the thought of major thinkers and writers in the U.S. from 1865 to the present, exploring the manner in which this thought can be contextualized within a broader trans-Atlantic tradition.
Provide a sense of historical context for the elaboration of the American intellectual tradition during the period since 1865.
Encourage students to think critically about historical issues and the manner in which they were treated by writers and thinkers in the past.
Encourage the close reading of texts.
Enhance students’ language skills, especially with regard to their ability to read, write, and speak using the English language.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: Sociology, the Impact of Darwin, and Approaches to Social Issues. Readings: Mike Savage & L. F. Ward.
Hafta 2: Women and Early 20th Century Feminism. Readings: Two selections from Charlotte Perkins Gilman.
Hafta 3: Race, culture, and society. Readings: W. E. B. Du Bois, 3 selections.
Hafta 4: Pragmatism. Readings: Charles Peirce & William James.
Hafta 5: Pragmatism and Democracy. Readings: John Dewey and Astra Taylor.
Hafta 6: The Social Scientist as Cultural Critic. Readings: Margaret Mead.
Hafta 7: Black Public Intellectuals Speak Out! Readings: M. L. King Jr. and Malcolm X.
Hafta 8: Midterm exam: week of March 25 to 29 during two-hour class session.
Hafta 9: Feminists Speak Out! Readings: Betty Friedan and Gloria Anzaldua.
Hafta 10: Capitalism and markets. Readings: Milton Friedman vs. Ha-Joon Chang.
Hafta 11: No classes on Monday & Tuesday, April 27 & 28. Reading: Michael Sandel, selection from "What Money Can't Buy."
Hafta 12: The Return of Inequality. Readings: Mike Savage.
Hafta 13: The Clash of Civilizations. Readings: Edward Said, Samuel Huntington.
Hafta 14: Intellectuals and the Cold War. Readings: N. Chomsky & W. W. Rostow.
resources: Amer 358 Readings available on Moodle

courses.name: American Poetry
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the social and formal development of poetic expression in the United States. It aims to help students understand major American poets and the historical, aesthetic, and cultural contexts that shaped their work.
courses.description: This course studies the social and formal development of poetic expression in the United States, with particular attention given to Bradstreet, Wheatley, Whitman, Dickinson, Longfellow, Crane, Pound, Eliot, Hughes, Brooks, Lowell, Frost, and Rich.
learning_outcomes: Students will evaluate works of American poetry in relation to various aesthetic, intellectual, and historical concerns.
Students will demonstrate an appreciation of the diversity and complexity of American poetry.
Students will consider the significance of American poetry within the larger purviews of American literature and American culture.
Students will conduct primary research through the analysis of texts in relation to their aesthetic, historical, cultural, and theoretical contexts.
Students will develop technical vocabulary necessary for poetic analysis and related critical discourse.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introductions / What is Poetry? / Poetic devices
Hafta 2: Early American Poetry / Love Poems
Hafta 3: American Gothic/Romantic Poetry
Hafta 4: Whitman, Dickinson & Frost
Hafta 5: Dickinson
Hafta 6: Frost
Hafta 7: Holiday / No Class
Hafta 8: American Poetry in Film
Hafta 9: American Modernism / Stein
Hafta 10: Williams
Hafta 11: T.S. Eliot
Hafta 12: Presentations / Holiday
Hafta 13: The Harlem Renaissance: Claude McKay, Langston Hughes, Countee Cullen
Hafta 14: Objectivists: Luis Zukofsky, George Oppen, Lorine Niedecker

courses.name: American Novel to 1900
courses.purpose: Introducing the novel as a literary form, the course offers a history of the development of the novel in America.
courses.description: Students are expected to apply various approaches to the reading of any selected novel or novels. Authors may include Hawthorne, Melville, Twain, S. Crane, H. James, R. Hall, K. Chopin, R.H. Davis, E. Wharton, C. Perkins Gilman, K.A. Porter, A. Smedley.

courses.name: Topics in Theory for American Culture
courses.purpose: This course offers an introduction to critical and cultural theory with specific reference to problems in American Studies.
courses.description: Students will be encouraged to think critically about issues concerning narrative form, knowledge and power, culture, "common sense," gender, race, and hegemony within the context of American culture and history. Selections from Barthes, Benjamin, Lyotard, Foucault, Gramsci, Bederman, Wiegman, Hardaway, Jackson Lears, Denning, and others will be read for the course.
resources: Selections from Barthes, Benjamin, Lyotard, Foucault, Gramsci, Bederman, Wiegman, Hardaway, Jackson Lears, Denning, and others

courses.name: American Novel From 1900
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to major developments in the American novel from 1900 to the present and to help them understand how novels reflect and shape literary, cultural, and historical change in the United States.
courses.description: A study of novels covering the period from 1900 to the present. Selections can include works by E. Hemingway, F.S. Fitzgerald, W. Faulkner, J. Steinbeck, W. Cather, T. Dreiser, J. Smiley, and "popular" novels, novels by women, African-American, Native American, and ethnic writers.
learning_outcomes: identify narrative techniques
identify primary features of genre
write critically about novels and novelistic themes
contextualize novelistic works
comprehend the importance of the novel to US culture in the twentieth century
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: Historical Contexts
Hafta 2: Modernism and the Novel
Hafta 3: Modernism and the Novel
Hafta 4: From Modernism to Postmodernism
Hafta 5: War and Satire
Hafta 6: War and Satire
Hafta 7: War and Satire
Hafta 8: Science Fiction
Hafta 9: Science Fiction
Hafta 10: Postwar Black Literature
Hafta 11: Postwar Black Literature
Hafta 12: New Voices in the 21st century
Hafta 13: New Voices in the 21st century
Hafta 14: New Voices in the 21st century

courses.name: The Thirties: the Great Depression and the New Deal
courses.purpose: This course examines the 1930s as a crucial decade in twentieth-century United States history, focusing on the causes and consequences of the Great Depression and the New Deal of President Franklin D. Roosevelt.
courses.description: The course examines the political, cultural, and intellectual developments of the nineteen-thirties in the United States. It takes a historical approach to the decade while also considering its literature, film, art, and music.

courses.name: Culture in its Historical Contexts
courses.purpose: To discuss culture as a cultural practice and to examine recent concerns about self-identity and social memory in their broader social and economic contexts.
courses.description: This is an interdisciplinary course that involves the reading of literary and philosophical texts. It situates cultural concerns about self-identity and social memory within their social and economic context.

courses.name: Topics in Cultural Studies
courses.description: Genres, topics, and academic skills not included in the curriculum, according to the needs of the students, instructor availability, and recent trends in American Studies and higher education.

courses.name: American Studies in a Global Context
courses.purpose: This course aims to help students understand the notion of America within a global context. It examines how the United States was globally constructed and how American culture has been globalized through historical and contemporary processes.
courses.description: The course covers the global construction of the United States with reference to European colonialism, the transatlantic slave trade, and the rise of the nation-state. It also examines contemporary theories of the globalization of American culture and traces the flow of American cultural products and ideas to regions such as West Africa, South Asia, and Turkey.
learning_outcomes: Examine and critically evaluate some of the central terms and issues in economic and cultural theory at work in recent debates about globalization.
Situate American Studies and Cultural Studies, as academic disciplines, within broader contemporary social, political, and economic issues.
Sharpen their analytical skills by studying demanding theoretical texts in cultural theory and economic history.
Independently assess the influence of US, or US-dominated, cultural, economic and political institutions over contemporary international developments in the context of globalization.
Hone their written communications skills through in-class essays and assignments.
Refine their oral communication and public speaking skills through individual or group oral presentations and in-class debates.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course.
Hafta 2: Globalization: Definitions, Recent debates and Current Developments.
Hafta 3: Effects of Globalisation on Cultural Identity.
Hafta 4: "Identity Politics" versus Economic Issues and Other Recent Trends in Cultural and American Studies.
Hafta 5: A. The Rise and Consolidation of the Nation-State; B. "National Culture" and the Imaginary Community.
Hafta 6: The Economic Revolution.
Hafta 7: The Liberal Economists.
Hafta 8: Keynesian versus Neo Liberal Economics.
Hafta 9: Neo-Liberalism and Neo-Colonialism.
Hafta 10: Neo-Liberalism and Neo-Colonialism (continued).
Hafta 11: Midterm Exam.
Hafta 12: The Acceleration of Time, the Compression of Space, and Problems of Identity Formation.
Hafta 13: The Multi-Culturalism Industry.
Hafta 14: The Cultural and Temporal Implications of The Conflict Between Industrial and Finance Capital.
resources: Held, David and Anthony McGrew. Globalization/Anti-Globalization. Cambridge: Polity, 2002.
Giddens, Anthony. Runaway World. How Globalization is Reshaping our Lives. London: Profile Books, 2002.
Fluck, Winfried. "The Modernity of America and the Practice of Scholarship." in Rethinking American History in a Global Age, edited by Thomas Bender. Berkeley: University of California Press, 2002.
Heilbroner, Robert. The Worldly Philosophers. N.Y: Simon and Schuster, 1967.
Harvey, David. A Brief History of Neo-liberalism. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2005.
Harvey, David. The New Imperialism. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2003.
Bauman, Zygmunt. The Individualized Society. New York: Polity Press, 2001.
Fraser, N. 2009. "Feminism, Capitalism and the Cunning of History," New Left Review 56 (March/April): 97-117.
Harvey, David. The Limits to Capital. (Revised and updated edition) London: Verso, 2006.

courses.name: American Pragmatism
courses.purpose: This course examines the contribution of American Pragmatism to intellectual thought and contemporary criticism.
courses.description: The course covers the writings of classical American philosophers William James and John Dewey, along with selected texts from Ralph Waldo Emerson as an intellectual forefather of American Pragmatism. It also studies key pragmatist ideas such as knowledge, ethics, social progress, democracy, the individual, social conflict, self-expression, the scientific method, culture, and education. In the final part of the semester, the course turns to contemporary pragmatist thinkers including Richard Rotry, Cornel West, and Richard Posner to compare classical and contemporary pragmatism and assess their contributions to social inquiry and criticism.

courses.name: History of American Presidential Campaigns
courses.purpose: This course examines the evolution of American presidential campaigns and the changing political culture of the United States. It aims to help students understand how campaigns have been shaped by media, technology, and broader social and political developments.
courses.description: The course looks at the history of American presidential campaigns, from the rough and often violent contests of the nineteenth century to the highly organized and media-driven campaigns of the present. It focuses on key texts and on the media of American politics, including newspapers, political cartoons, radio, television, and internet campaigning. The course also pays special attention to the contemporary presidential campaign in the United States.

courses.name: Race and Ethnicity in American Culture
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the cultural expression and identity formation processes of major ethnic groups in the United States through a comparative perspective.
courses.description: This course presents a comparative study of the culture and writings of major ethnic groups in the US. Students will be exposed to the cultural expression and the processes of identity formation for various groups, including Latino/as, Native Americans, African Americans and Italian-, Irish-, and Asian Americans. Students will study key texts in the field. Topics may include theories of assimilation, integration, and colonization, conceptual models such as "whiteness" and the "melting pot," and historical responses to immigration, such as ethnocide, eugenics, and repatriation, as backdrop to the complex roles played by race and ethnicity in the US.

courses.name: American Fiction in the 21. Century: Migration, Space, Identity
courses.purpose: This course explores how migrant narratives redefine identity in contemporary American fiction. It focuses on the ways multi-ethnic and immigrant experiences are represented through literature and related media.
courses.description: The course examines multi-ethnic literature of the Americas and the ways different ethnoracial groups inhabit urban spaces in contemporary America. It studies the painful transformations migrants experience when facing new realities and how these experiences are expressed in excerpts, stories, and novels by writers such as Bharati Mukherjee, Nami Mun, Junot Diaz, Wes Moore, Randa Jarrar, Dinaw Mengestu, Bich Minh Nguyen, Helena Maria Viramontes, and Chang-Rae Lee. The course also includes DVD interviews of some of the writers.

courses.name: Law in American Literature and Culture
courses.purpose: This course examines the role that law and theories of law played in American culture and literature from the 18th century through the present.
courses.description: The course studies the relationship between law, legal theory, and American literary and cultural production through literary works, legal theory, court cases, drama, and film. Readings are examined alongside contemporary developments in legal theory and landmark court cases, with possible authors including William Blackstone, Charles Brockden Brown, Nat Turner, George Lippard, Herman Melville, Charles W. Chesnutt, Susan Glaspell, and Barbara Johnson.
resources: William Blackstone
Charles Brockden Brown
Nat Turner
George Lippard
Herman Melville
Charles W. Chesnutt
Susan Glaspell
Barbara Johnson

courses.name: Colonialism and the Making of the New World
courses.purpose: This course examines the historical significance of the European 'discovery' of the Americas and the subsequent processes of exploration and colonization. It aims to develop a critical understanding of how colonialism shaped the New World and its peoples.
courses.description: The course explores the impact of European exploration and colonization of the Americas from a variety of historical and theoretical perspectives. It pays particular attention to the experiences of those exploited by the European colonial project through the critical analysis of historical and contemporary representations of the peoples and cultures of the New World.

courses.name: Selfhood and Emotional Life in American Culture and History
courses.purpose: This course examines the historical development of selfhood and emotional life in American culture, and more broadly in modern culture, through an interdisciplinary lens. It aims to help students understand how ideas about the self, emotion, and subjectivity have changed over time and across cultural contexts.
courses.description: Using texts drawn from cultural and social history as well as from cultural studies, social psychology, neuroscience, anthropology, and sociology, the course examines the experience of selfhood and manifestations of emotional life in American culture and in modern culture more generally from a historical perspective. Topics include the contrast between Western and East Asian senses of self; the shift from moral "character" to "personality" as the core of selfhood in the United States during the early twentieth century; the management of "negative" emotions such as anger and jealousy; the rise of "American cool"; the emergence of the culture of self-realization; and the growing importance of precarity and depression in recent decades.
resources: Hazel R. Markus
Antonio Damasio
Warren I. Susman
T. J. Jackson Lears
Peter N. Stearns
Lauren Berlant
Alain Ehrenberg
George Herbert Mead
David Riesman
Arlie R. Hochschild
Michel Foucault
