courses.purpose: This course aims to provide a historically conscious framework for analyzing and interpreting fundamental terminologies related to nature and culture across different disciplines. It also seeks to support comparative understanding of Ottoman and Turkish cultural traditions through these terms.
courses.description: Historically conscious analysis and interpretation of fundamental terminologies in the fields of anthropology, sociology, law, economics, linguistics, folklore and history; comparative study of Ottoman and Turkish cultural traditions in terms of the chosen terminology; study of the transformation and continuity in the oral and written traditions within this framework.

courses.name: The Turkish Short Story
courses.purpose: This course examines the development of the modern Turkish short story in its historical context, with particular attention to its generic transformation and to stylistic and thematic comparisons among works by modern authors.
courses.description: The origins of the contemporary short story in Turkish literature may be found in Aziz Efendi's Muhayyelât, a late 18th-century work that is considered a bridge between the storytelling tradition in the East and the modern short story. The course focuses on the historical development of the modern Turkish short story, its transformation as a genre, and comparisons of style and content among various modern works.

courses.name: Sources of Literature II: From Mimesis to Catharsis
courses.purpose: This course examines how art and literature express, perceive, and induce emotion in relation to the epistemological search associated with mimesis and human creativity. It also aims to explore how rituals, the spell of words, rhetoric, ethics, and aesthetics contribute to this inquiry.
courses.description: The course focuses on the thematic comparison of works from Eastern and Western cultures in relation to mimesis, creativity, and the expression of emotion in art and literature. It considers how individuals seek to conquer history and society in order to realize themselves ontologically.

courses.name: Modern Turkish Prose
courses.purpose: This course aims to help students understand the emergence of modern Turkish prose genres in late Ottoman and early republican contexts through the study of nineteenth-century and later prose texts. It also provides a comparative narrative studies background for students of Turkish literature.
courses.description: This course examines various prose texts from the nineteenth century onwards written in Turkish. It focuses on the early examples of short stories, novels, translated works, and theoretical discussions on narrative prose.

courses.name: Fictions of Migration
courses.purpose: This course examines how migration is represented in literature and film, with a focus on movements to and from major global centers such as London, Berlin, and Istanbul. It aims to help students think critically about migration beyond traditional national literary and cultural categories.
courses.description: The course covers fiction and film inspired by British postcolonial migration, Turkish gastarbeiter ('guest workers') in Germany, migration from rural Anatolia to Istanbul, and the recent migrant crisis. It explores the diverse ways migration shapes literature and film.

courses.name: Theories of Literary Translation
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the main theoretical frameworks developed from the early nineteenth century to the present to conceptualize literary translation as an intellectual endeavor, a literary practice, a mode of circulation, and a vector of cultural contact.
courses.description: The course familiarizes students with a broad range of major theoretical texts and key notions in translation studies. It also emphasizes the practice of literary translation in Turkish and Middle Eastern contexts, alongside seminal texts in the Western tradition, in order to support the study and criticism of translated literary texts.
learning_outcomes: Understand major theoretical frameworks in literary translation studies.
Become familiar with key concepts and seminal theoretical texts in the field.
Develop skills for studying and criticizing translated literary texts.
Gain awareness of literary translation practices in Turkish and Middle Eastern contexts.

courses.name: Applied Electromagnetics
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a solid understanding of electromagnetic field theory and its engineering applications. It prepares students to analyze and solve problems involving fields, waves, transmission lines, waveguides, antennas, and related devices.
courses.description: Review of static electric and magnetic fields. Dielectric and magnetic materials. Electrostatic and magnetostatic energy and forces. Magnetic circuits, transformers and electromagnetic energy conversion. Basic of electric machinery. Review of Maxwell's equations and plane waves. Reflection and transmission of plane waves at plane boundaries. Transmission lines, Smith chart and impedance matching. Waveguides and cavity resonators. Electromagnetic radiation and antennas. Antenna arrays, effective aperture, Friis transmission formula and radar equation.

courses.name: Introduction to CMOS VLSI Design
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and technologies used in CMOS VLSI design. It aims to provide students with the background needed to understand CMOS circuits and design strategies.
courses.description: Introduction to CMOS circuits, MOS transistor theory, CMOS processing technology, CMOS circuit characterization. CMOS VLSI circuit design, clocking strategies, case studies.

courses.name: Analog CMOS Integrated Circuits
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the fundamental analysis and design principles of analog CMOS integrated circuits. It also develops the ability to use CAD tools and practical methods needed for circuit characterization and implementation.
courses.description: Review of MOS device physics, single stage amplifiers, differential amplifiers, current mirrors, frequency response of amplifiers, Miller effect, noise in amplifiers, feedback, operational amplifiers, slew rate, power supply rejection, stability and frequency compensation, band gap references, switched capacitor circuits, nonlinearities, linearization, offset, oscillators, phase locked loops.
learning_outcomes: Ability to mathematically analyze an analog CMOS discrete/integrated circuit using large-signal models and small-signal parameters.
Ability to use computer-aided-design tools for the analysis of analog CMOS discrete/integrated circuits.
Ability to design an analog CMOS integrated circuit using computer-aided design tools satisfying the design-rules of the CMOS foundry.
Ability to characterize a CMOS discrete circuit such as an OPAMP using laboratory measuring equipment.
Ability to design the printed-circuit board suitable to use for an analog CMOS discrete circuit using computer-aided-design tools.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Single stage amplifiers
Hafta 2: Differential amplifiers
Hafta 3: Current mirrors, frequency response of amplifiers
Hafta 4: Operational amplifiers
Hafta 5: Slew rate, power supply rejection, stability and frequency compensation
Hafta 6: MOS Device physics
Hafta 7: IC Layout
Hafta 8: Project discussion
Hafta 9: Noise in amplifiers
Hafta 10: Bandgap references
Hafta 11: Switched capacitor circuits
Hafta 12: Nonlinearities, linearization, offset
Hafta 13: Oscillators
Hafta 14: Phase locked loops
resources: Design of Analog CMOS integrated circuits, Behzad Razavi, 1st or 2nd, McGraw Hill

courses.name: Power Electronics
courses.description: Analysis and design of linear regulators, inverters, DC-DC converters, different topologies of converters, efficiencies of power conversion circuits, transformers and magnetic design, power semiconductor devices, power factor and power factor correction.

courses.name: Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of microprocessors and microcontrollers and prepares them to design and implement embedded systems. It aims to build practical skills in assembly language programming, interfacing, and microcontroller-based system development.
courses.description: Introduction to microprocessors and microcontrollers. 8051 microcontroller. 8051 Assembly Language. Input/output interfacing. Timers. Serial Port. Interrupt programming. External Memory Interfacing.
learning_outcomes: Calculate the execution time of a subroutine written in assembly language and write a subroutine which generates a desired amount of delay
Generate binary waveforms with arbitrary frequency and duty cycles using timers
Generate arbitrary analog waveforms by interfacing a digital to analog converter to a microcontroller
Interface an analog to digital converter to a microcontroller so as to monitor a physical quantity such as temperature, distance, etc. by using sensors
Build interrupt-driven systems using the interrupt interface of the microcontroller
Design and build a microcontroller-based system
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to computing and microcontrollers
Hafta 2: Introduction to ARM Assembly language programming
Hafta 3: ARM Assembly – Arithmetic and Logic
Hafta 4: ARM Assembly – Branch & Call
Hafta 5: ARM Assembly – Signed Numbers
Hafta 6: ARM Addressing
Hafta 7: Introduction to C Programming
Hafta 8: GPIO
Hafta 9: LCD/Keyboard
Hafta 10: Timers
Hafta 11: Serial Port
Hafta 12: Interrupt
Hafta 13: Analog-to-digital (ADC) and Digital-to-analog (DAC) conversions
Hafta 14: Problem Solving
resources: The STM32F103 Arm Microcontroller and Embedded Systems: Using Assembly and C, Sepehr Naimi, Sarmad Naimi, and Muhammad Ali Mazidi, Microdigitaled

courses.name: Photonics
courses.description: Photon theory of light with matter. Spontaneous emission, absorption, and stimulated emission. Principles of laser amplifiers. Semi-conductor lasers, light emitting diodes, and light detectors. Electro-optics, accousto-optics, and their applications. Introduction to nonlinear optics.
learning_outcomes: Develop a basic understanding of quantum mechanical systems. Understand the principles of quantum measurement and the quantization of electromagnetic fields. Develop an understanding of number states, coherent states, and thermal light. Perform basic calculations regarding photon statistics.
Develop an understanding of atomic and molecular energy levels and thermal equilibrium occupancy statistics.
Calculate rates of spontaneous emission, absorption, and stimulated emission, using transition cross-sections and lineshape functions.
Calculate steady-state poulation dynamics in pumped atomic systems.
Calculate the gain coefficient and saturation properties of laser materials.
Calculate the output power and other porperties of various lasers. Describe properties of fundamental laser systems. Calculate properties of pulsed lasers.

Calculate carrier concentrations in semiconductor materials under various injection conditions. Calculate the rates of spontaneous emission, absorption, and stimulated emission in semiconductor materials.
Calculate properties of semiconductor laser amplifiers and lasers.
Calculate properties of semiconductor detectors.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Photons. Standing-wave and traveling-wave electromagnetic modes. Mode amplitude, mode energy.
Hafta 2: Overview of quantum mechanics. Quantum states and measurement of observables. Quantization of the electromagnetic field.
Hafta 3: Properties of photons and photon streams. Quantum states of light.
Hafta 4: Atomic systems and enegy levels.
Hafta 5: Interaction of electromagnetic modes with atomic systems. Spontaneous emission, absorption, stimulated emission.
Hafta 6: Blackbody radiation. Atomic rate equations.
Hafta 7: Principles of light amplifiers. Gain and gain saturation.
Hafta 8: Resonators and optical feedback.
Hafta 9: Principles of lasers.
Hafta 10: Properties of lasers. Mode-locking.
Hafta 11: Semiconductors. Photonic interactions in semiconductors.
Hafta 12: Semiconductor lasers and laser amplifiers.
Hafta 13: Semiconductor photodetectors.
Hafta 14: Introduction to elecro-optics and nonlinear optics.
resources: Fundamentals of Photonics, Saleh & Teich, 2nd Ed., 2007, Wiley

courses.name: Advanced Digital Communications
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a solid understanding of advanced digital communication principles and techniques used in modern communication systems. It prepares students to analyze, design, and evaluate digital transmission methods over bandlimited and wireless channels.
courses.description: Review of digital modulation techniques. Linear modulation with memory. Channel coding. Carrier and symbol synchronization. Intersymbol interference and the Nyquist criterion. Channel equalization techniques. Multicarrier modulation. Wireless communications. Diversity and multi-antenna techniques.
learning_outcomes: Review basic digital modulation techniques and develop an understanding of linear modulation with memory.
Investigate different ways of synchronization and timing recovery for digital communication systems.
Develop models for bandlimited channels and channel equalization algorithms required for signaling over these channels.
Recognize orthogonal frequency division multiplexing as a way to transmit over dispersive channels and determine parameters of OFDM systems according to specific channel characteristics.
Model wireless channels as time varying multipath channels and classify transmission schemes based on multipath and rapidity of channel variations.
Analyze performance of digital modulation techniques over wireless channels.
Simulate digital communication systems under different assumptions and implement timing recovery, synchronization, and channel equalization algorithms as examples.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Review of digital modulation, and channel capacity
Hafta 2: Linear modulation with memory
Hafta 3: Channel coding: linear block codes
Hafta 4: Channel coding: convolutional codes
Hafta 5: Channel coding: convolutional codes
Hafta 6: Synchronization: carrier phase and frequency estimation, time synchronization
Hafta 7: Synchronization: carrier phase and frequency estimation, time synchronization
Hafta 8: Bandlimited channels, intersymbol interference, power spectrum
Hafta 9: Nyquist ISI free condition
Hafta 10: Channel equalization
Hafta 11: Wireless channel characterization
Hafta 12: Digital transmission through fading channels, diversity techniques
Hafta 13: Diversity techniques, multiple antenna systems
Hafta 14: Multicarrier modulation and OFDM
resources: Digital Communications, John G. Proakis and Masoud Salehi, 2008 / 5th edition, McGraw Hill

courses.name: Advanced Mathematics for Signals, Systems, Control and Communications
courses.purpose: To provide the mathematical foundations needed to analyze and solve advanced problems in signals, systems, control, communications, and optimization.
courses.description: Theory and applications of mathematical methods in signals and systems theory and their applications in signal processing, communications, control and optimization. Topics include linear vector spaces and metric spaces; Hilbert spaces, including the projection theorem, approximations, and orthogonal expansions; Riemann and Lebesgue integration and properties; dual spaces and applications to constrained optimization; distribution theory, the Schwartz space, and the delta function; the discrete Fourier transform and the continuous Fourier transform; linear systems and their properties; optimization of functionals; and applications in signal processing and systems theory.

courses.name: Robust Feedback Theory
courses.purpose: To introduce the principles and methods used to analyze and design feedback systems with robustness considerations. The course aims to develop an understanding of stability, performance, and controller synthesis under uncertainty.
courses.description: Review of time and frequency domain analysis and design of feedback systems. Internal stability, asymptotic tracking, performance. Robust stability and robust performance. Stabilization. Parametrization of stabilizing controllers. Loop shaping. Model matching. Introduction to H-infinity control.

courses.name: Control and Optimization of Stochastic Systems
courses.description: Controlled and control-free Markov chains and stochastic stability; martingale methods for stability and stochastic learning; dynamic programming and optimal control for finite horizons, infinite horizons, and average cost problems; partially observed models, non-linear filtering and Kalman Filtering; linear programming and numerical methods; reinforcement learning and stochastic approximation methods; multi-agent (decentralized) stochastic control, and continuous-time stochastic control.

courses.name: Power System Analysis and Design
courses.purpose: Introduction to the analysis and design of power systems, with emphasis on the fundamental concepts and methods used in power engineering.
courses.description: Introduction to power systems. Phasors, instantaneous and complex power, three-phase circuits. Transformers, equivalent circuits, per-unit system. Transmission line parameters, conductor design, capacitance and inductance. Medium and short line approximations. Terminated line. Power flow solution techniques, Gaussian elimination, Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel. Iterative solutions, Newton-Raphson. Short-circuit analysis, contingency analysis. Design projects.

courses.name: Medical Imaging
courses.purpose: To introduce the fundamental principles and applications of major medical imaging modalities used in modern healthcare.
courses.description: Fundamentals and applications of four medical imaging techniques: magnetic resonance imaging, ultrasound, nuclear medicine, and X-ray computed tomography.

courses.name: Nonlinear Systems
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the fundamental concepts and analysis tools for nonlinear systems, which are essential for understanding real-world systems that cannot be adequately modeled by linear approximations. It also prepares students to analyze stability, oscillatory behavior, and nonlinear control-related phenomena.
courses.description: Linear versus nonlinear systems. Basic nonlinear analysis. Nonlinear ordinary differential equations. Second-order systems, Poincare-Bendixon theorem, limit cycles. Stability in the sense of Lyapunov. Input-output stability, passivity, small gain theorem. Singular perturbations. Differential geometric methods.
learning_outcomes: Develop basic skills to investigate second order systems, understand phase plane analysis, limit cycles and their predictions, utilize these ideas in controller design.

Understand the basic differences between the linear and nonlinear systems and the basic sources of nonlinearity, understand the basic concepts in analyzing nonlinear systems such as equilibrium points and linearization.
Understand the extensions of frequency domain techniques (e.g. describing functions) to nonlinear systems, their limitations and utilize them in controller design.

Understand various concepts of stability in nonlinear systems (e.g. Lyapunov Stability), utilize them in solving various stability problems in nonlinear systems, understand the relations between these concepts and their counterparts in linear systems.

Understand the input/output representation and related stability concepts (e.g. Lp stability) in nonlinear systems, utilize these ideas (e.g. small gain theorem, circle criteria) to solve stability problems.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction (Linear vs. Nonlinear)
Hafta 2: Second Order Systems
Hafta 3: Phase portraits
Hafta 4: Describing function Analysis-Theory
Hafta 5: Describing Function Analysis-Applications
Hafta 6: Mathematical Preliminaries
Hafta 7: Lyapunov Stability Analysis-Theory
Hafta 8: Lyapunov Stability Analysis-Applications
Hafta 9: Input-Output Stability
Hafta 10: Feedback System Analysis-Theory
Hafta 11: Feedback System Analysis-Applications
Hafta 12: Sliding Mode Control
Hafta 13: Singular Perturbation Theory
Hafta 14: Feedback Linearization
resources: Applied Nonlinear Control, J.J.E. Slotine, W. Li, 1991, Prentice-Hall

courses.name: Foundations of Magnetic Resonance Imaging
courses.purpose: This course introduces the basic principles of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), its instrumentation, and the main methods used in MRI. It also exposes students to current research directions in this active field.
courses.description: Basic principles of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), instrumentation, and various methods used in MRI. Various research areas in this highly active field are discussed.

courses.name: Advanced Optoelectronics: Innovative Design
courses.description: Review of physics of optoelectronic devices. Heterostructures. Optical absorption, emission and refraction processes. Junction theory. Light emitting diodes. Laser diodes. Photodetectors. Modulators. Photonic switches. Integrated photonic devices. Innovative optoelectronic devices.

courses.name: Medical Image Reconstruction and Processing
courses.purpose: To introduce the fundamental principles and applications of medical image reconstruction and processing, with emphasis on how medical images are formed, reconstructed, and improved for analysis and diagnosis.
courses.description: Fundamentals and applications of medical image reconstruction and processing. Reconstruction from non-uniformly sampled data, projection data, regularly/randomly undersampled data. Parallel imaging and compressed sensing for medical imaging. Improving image quality, denoising, deconvolution, off-resonance correction. Post-processing of images, image registration, image segmentation. Examples from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), X-ray computed tomography (CT), and magnetic particle imaging (MPI).
learning_outcomes: Develop an understanding of the fundamental applications of medical image reconstruction and processing.
Analyze the effects of uniform/non-uniform sampling/undersampling on the image.
Reconstruct images from non-uniformly sampled data, projection data, regularly/randomly undersampled data.
Implement parallel imaging and compressing sensing reconstructions for medical imaging.
Apply techniques to improve image quality.
Demonstrate post-processing of images for image registration and computer-aided diagnosis.
Acquire an ability to learn new medical image reconstruction or processing techniques.
Demonstrate sufficient knowledge to develop an advanced medical image reconstruction or processing technique.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction, Multi-dimensional Fourier Transform
Hafta 2: Imaging overview: MRI, CT, MPI
Hafta 3: Cartesian Sampling and Reconstruction, Data sampling and reconstruction in 1D, Data sampling and reconstruction in 2D/3D
Hafta 4: Image and Frequency Domain Reconstruction: Projection reconstruction (CT, MPI), Partial Fourier reconstruction in MRI
Hafta 5: Image and Frequency Domain Reconstruction: Non-Cartesian reconstructions: gridding, NUFFT, Examples from MRI, MPI and CT
Hafta 6: Improving Image Quality: Image denoising, Image deconvolution
Hafta 7: Improving Image Quality: Off-resonance correction in MRI, Correction of timing errors
Hafta 8: Parallel Imaging: Encoding in Image and Frequency Domains, Phased-arrays in MRI
Hafta 9: Parallel Imaging: SENSE and GRAPPA algorithms, Coil compression
Hafta 10: Compressed Sensing: Random undersampling
Hafta 11: Compressed Sensing: Sparsity/compressibility and nonlinear recovery, Model-based reconstructions
Hafta 12: Medical Image Registration: Rigid and non-rigid registration, Surface-based registration
Hafta 13: Medical Image Registration: Multi-modal registration and image fusion
Hafta 14: Medical Image Segmentation: Edge-based and region-based segmentation, Atlas-based segmentation, Computer-aided diagnosis
resources: Handbook of MRI Pulse Sequences, Matt Bernstein, Kevin King, Xiaohong Zhou, 1st Edition, Elsevier
Medical Image Analysis, Atam P. Dhawan, 2nd Edition, John Wiley & Sons

courses.name: Digital Signal Processing Laboratory
courses.purpose: Fundamentals of operating and analyzing real time digital signal processing systems, including the required theory, the hardware used to sample and process the signals, and real time software development environments. Implementation of a project that develops system-level design skills.
courses.description: Project work covering design and implementation of FIR and IIR filters, and applications of the discrete Fourier transformation.

courses.name: Oil and Gas Markets
courses.purpose: This course is designed to expose students to the workings of oil and gas markets.
courses.description: Dynamics and fundamentals of hydrocarbon markets will be discussed. The formation of market structure and the history of pricing of the commodities will be examined. Policy issues that affect, divert, and regulate the oil and gas markets will be elaborated within the framework of economics and energy security.

courses.name: Geopolitics of Energy in Eurasia
courses.purpose: This course is designed to expose students to energy security in Eurasia.
courses.description: Dynamics and fundamentals of oil and gas markets will specifically be discussed. The formation of market structures in the producing and exporting countries in Eurasia will be examined. Geopolitical issues in the region that affect energy markets will be elaborated within the framework of international political economy.

courses.name: Data Analysis in Energy Markets
courses.purpose: This course is designed to help students build a good understanding of analyzing short-, medium-, or long-term datasets in energy markets in order to build analysis or forecast tools.
courses.description: The course mainly focuses on the fundamentals of data reading and data manipulation, as well as combining and manipulating datasets to derive functional outcomes.

courses.name: Biomedical Signals and Instrumentation
courses.description: Biophysics of cell membranes, models of neuron membrane potential, Hodgkin-Huxley equations for the action potential, propagation of the action potential, neurocommunication, simple neural networks which explain behavior, volume conductor fields, theory of Electrocardiography (ECG), ECG amplifiers and instrumentation ECG signal processing, EEG, EMG, and other bioelectric signals, model of the cardiovascular system, model of the respiratory system, model of the neurocardiac control system, transducers for bioelectric, cardiovascular and respiratory measurements, precondition circuits and instrumentation techniques.

courses.name: Renewable Energy, Carbon Markets and Clean Technology
courses.purpose: The course is designed to help students understand how renewables, carbon markets and cleantech interconnect with each other to deliver the desired/expected outcomes in terms of low carbon energy growth and sustainable economic development.
courses.description: The course examines how renewable energy, carbon markets, and clean technology interact with one another and with the conventional energy system. It also addresses the complex decision-making involved in transitioning to a low-carbon economy.

courses.name: Designing Future Electricity and Gas Trading Markets
courses.purpose: The course is intended to provide an up to date orientation on how the present day traded markets for gas and electricity have evolved, the drivers of de-regulation and market competition.
courses.description: The course covers in detail the industry value chains and how these have become impacted by the developments in trading and the desire of policy makers to continually develop the architecture that is fit for purpose to support a level competitive playing field in electricity and gas marketing and wholesale trading.

courses.name: Commodity Derivatives and Risk Management
courses.purpose: This course provides an overview of commodity, particularly energy, derivatives markets and the pricing of commodity derivatives such as forwards, futures, swaps, and options. It also introduces the evolving practice of risk management in energy markets using these instruments.
courses.description: The course covers the actual working of derivatives and the analytical tools needed to understand them effectively. It balances theory and application, with emphasis on how to price derivatives. The course also discusses sources of risk in energy markets, including market, credit, and operational risk, with a main focus on market risk and related management techniques.

courses.name: Cyber Security and Energy Security: Understanding Paradigm Shift
courses.purpose: The main purpose of the lecture is to open a new dimension on the energy security phenomenon and create awareness about new risks and threats arising from the digitalization of energy infrastructures and security. It also aims to create an interactive environment where participants can bring their approaches and improve their skills through Table Top Exercises and Scenario Developments.

courses.name: Sustainable Energy and Energy Mix
courses.purpose: The course is intended to provide the student with a portfolio view of energy and an understanding of policy alternatives regarding the energy mix.
courses.description: The course develops a holistic view of the energy grid inclusive of all feasible resources and elaborates the grid in a multi-faceted manner. Sustainability, security, efficiency and equity issues are covered by means of proper scientific/technical models. A hands-on practice of analysis and learning is encouraged.

courses.name: Seminar in Energy Economics
courses.purpose: The purpose of the course is to make students get acquainted with the current problems/challenges of energy markets. The course will evolve around a single project that each group will carry their project throughout the semester on selected energy issues.
courses.description: At the end of the semester, the reported project should be able to demonstrate that students know the structure and workings of the energy market of their choice, understand interrelations with other energy and financial markets, and are aware of important problems and challenges of the selected market.
learning_outcomes: Know the structure and workings of the energy market of their choice.
Understand interrelations with other energy and financial markets.
Be aware of important problems and challenges of the selected market.

courses.name: Oral Expression Discussion and Presentation
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' oral expression skills through discussion and presentation. It also helps students practice organizing ideas clearly and speaking effectively in academic contexts.
courses.description: The course focuses on the relation between literature and the natural environment and approaches Victorian fiction, poetry, and essays through the lens of ecocriticism. It examines how Victorian writers responded to ecological questions and environmental degradation caused by the industrial revolution, with attention to issues such as pollution and deforestation.

courses.name: Economics of Regulation and Financing of Energy Investment
courses.purpose: The primary goal of the course is to understand the basics of energy markets, the economics of regulation, and the financing of energy investments.

courses.name: Introduction to Literary Studies
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the study of literature through major genres and methods of analysis. It aims to develop skills in interpretation, close reading, and the writing of literary criticism.
courses.description: Fiction, poetry, and drama; interpretation and close reading; writing literary criticism.

courses.name: Literature in its Contexts
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the historical, political, literary, religious, and mythic contexts necessary for the study of British Literature. It also aims to build contextual reading skills and a foundational understanding for further study in Literature in English.
courses.description: The course covers historical and cultural backgrounds important to the ELIT degree, methods for reading literature critically with contextual awareness, and key moments in Western literary history through major texts in British Literature. Excerpts from texts such as the Old and New Testaments, the Odyssey, The Aeneid, and the Metamorphosis are read alongside historical moments across the British Isles and important folk traditions, as well as modern and contemporary literary reactions to them.
learning_outcomes: Develop a basic grounding for further study of Literature in English.
Read literature critically with due contextual awareness.
Recognize important historical and cultural contexts relevant to British Literature.
Identify key moments in Western literary history through major literary texts.

courses.name: Research and Writing Techniques
courses.purpose: This course aims to help students develop the skills needed to research and write literary essays effectively. It also encourages students to reflect on and improve their existing writing practices.
courses.description: Students explore and practice the various steps involved in researching and writing literary essays. The course covers taking notes, analyzing primary texts, using secondary material, compiling a working bibliography, avoiding plagiarism, answering the question, preparing an outline, argumentation, and editing/proofreading.
learning_outcomes: Understand the requirements of researching and writing literary essays.
Apply note-taking, source use, outlining, argumentation, and editing techniques in practice.
Develop and improve existing research and writing practices.

courses.name: Topics in Literary and Cultural Studies
courses.purpose: This course offers students the opportunity to study one topic in an in-depth manner. It is designed to develop students' ability to make critical connections across literary texts and cultural contexts.
courses.description: The course may be organized around a specific theme, genre, critical paradigm, or particular aspect of British culture. Possible topics include monsters, falling in love, London, the Gothic, young adult fiction, journalism, globalization, print culture, performance, contemporary film, J. K. Rowling, and the Beatles.
learning_outcomes: Improve the ability to draw links among diverse literary texts.
Establish critical connections across literary and cultural materials.
Develop an in-depth understanding of a selected topic in literary and cultural studies.

courses.name: Introduction to Theory and Criticism
courses.purpose: The course aims to introduce students to major theoretical movements that shape contemporary critical practice and to help them understand how literary theory is applied in reading and interpreting texts.
courses.description: The course will analyze texts from at least three major theoretical movements of relevance to contemporary critical practice, including Marxism, psychoanalytical criticism, structuralism, post-structuralism, and theories of gender, sexuality, and culture. These theoretical texts will be studied in conjunction with selected primary texts in order to enable students to see how theory works in practice.

courses.name: Poetry and Poetics
courses.purpose: This course aims to promote students' understanding of poetry and poetics by developing their skills in reading poems of a wide variety of types from diverse historical periods.
courses.description: Students will explore various types of poetry, considering figurative language, prosodic features, sound patterns, verse and stanza forms, and the relationship between form and meaning in poetry.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate a clear understanding of the elements of poetry and poetics.
Apply this understanding to the interpretation of a given poem using appropriate critical vocabulary.

courses.name: Fiction and Narrative
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to think critically about fiction and storytelling by examining the historical development of narrative prose from its beginnings to later literary forms.
courses.description: In this course students encounter works of literary fiction of varying kinds and from a wide range of periods and contexts. Students explore both the beginnings of, and key developments in, narrative prose. The course also focuses on analyzing fiction in terms of language, themes, structure, characters, plot, setting, and narrative technique.
learning_outcomes: Student should be able to reflect on the history of the novel from its origins in the eighteenth century to its development in the twentieth- or twenty-first century.
Display familiarity with the various forms and subgenres that constitute the English novel.
Students should be able to analyze novels on their own.
Students should be able to employ terminology such as plot, character, setting, point of view in discussing novels.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course, terms and concepts. The Epistolary mode.
Hafta 2: Formal realism in fiction. Daniel Defoe's Robinson Crusoe (extracts)
Hafta 3: What is the Gothic? Horace Walpole: The Castle of Otranto (first half)
Hafta 4: Finish The Castle of Otranto
Hafta 5: Revise 17th and 18thc fictions, prepare for midterm. In-class Midterm exam I
Hafta 6: Social realism. Charles Dickens: Great Expectations (chapters 1-4)
Hafta 7: Great Expectations (chapters 5-8, additional extracts in class)
Hafta 8: multiple narrators: R. L. Stevenson: The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde (chapters 1-5)
Hafta 9: The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde (Chapters 6-10)
Hafta 10: Revise 19thc narrative fiction in-class Mid term exam II
Hafta 11: Modernist narrative fiction: Virginia Woolf essays and The Mark on the Wall
Hafta 12: Virginia Woolf "Time Passes" from To the Lighthouse.
Hafta 13: Postmodern narrative fiction: Jeanette Winterson The Passion (first 2 parts)
Hafta 14: Jeanette Winterson The Passion (last 2 parts)
resources: Robinson Crusoe [extracted parts], Daniel Defoe, 1719, [any edition]
The Castle of Otranto, Horace Walpole, 1764, [any edition]
Great Expectations [extracted parts], Charles Dickens, 1861, [any edition]
The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde, Robert Louis Stevenson, 1886, [any edition]
To the Lighthouse [extracted part], Virginia Woolf, 1927, [any edition]
The Passion, Jeanette Winterson, 1987, [any edition]

courses.name: Concepts in Literary Studies
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to key concepts and theoretical issues in literary studies. It aims to help students engage with major literary critics and theorists and to think critically about the relationship between literature and wider intellectual developments.
courses.description: The course covers key concepts and theoretical issues in literary studies, including representation, narrative, voice, genre, the author, gender, ideology, culture, and race. Students also explore the work of literary critics and theorists and the relationship between literature and broader intellectual developments.
learning_outcomes: Students should be able to analyze a literary text on their own using critical concepts such as form, subjectivity, gender, etc.
Students should identify and respond to major debates in theory over topics such as subjectivity and form.
Students should be able to employ literary terms such as narrative, poetics, rhetoric, etc.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: no class meetings (Week 1 begins on Thursday, and this is a Monday/Wednesday class)
Hafta 2: Close reading
Hafta 3: Close reading
Hafta 4: Narrative and trauma
Hafta 5: Narrative and trauma
Hafta 6: Representation
Hafta 7: Representation
Hafta 8: Representation
Hafta 9: Spring break
Hafta 10: Culture and civilization
Hafta 11: Culture and civilization
Hafta 12: Culture and civilization
Hafta 13: Culture and civilization
Hafta 14: WEEKS 14-16: Subjectivity, affect, and the post-human

courses.name: Introduction to Drama
courses.purpose: This course aims to explore drama as a form that embodies a wide range of political, social, and psychological issues across different time periods and countries. It also introduces students to the historical and formal conventions of drama and to different types of plays.
courses.description: This course is a study of drama as the embodiment of a wide range of political, social and psychological issues of various time periods and countries. Selected plays may be drawn from a variety of literatures, from those of ancient Greece to modern Europe, Japan and the United States. The course aims to explore the historical and formal conventions of drama as well as different types of plays.

courses.name: Drama and Performance
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to literary dramatic works from the beginnings of English drama in the Middle Ages through the Renaissance and the Restoration, and up to contemporary experiments in performative art. It aims to develop students' ability to analyze and interpret dramatic texts while understanding the relationship between performance and interpretation.
courses.description: In this course students encounter literary dramatic works of varying genres, from the beginnings of English drama in the Middle Ages to the Renaissance and the Restoration, up to the latest experiments in performative art. As well as developing techniques for analyzing and interpreting works of literary drama, students explore topics such as dramatic genres and dramatic conventions. They also explore the relationship between performance and interpretation and, in particular, the ways in which performance not only affects interpretation but also constitutes a form of interpretation.
learning_outcomes: Recognize the conventions of drama and be familiar with the history of drama.
Analyse plays on their own.
Demonstrate an awareness of varying genres from the beginnings of English drama in the Middle Ages to the Renaissance and the Restoration, up to the latest experiments in performative art.
Develop techniques for analysing and interpreting works of literary drama.
Recognize the relationship between performance and interpretation.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course introduction. Introduction to drama from antiquity to the middle ages.
Hafta 2: Theories of the theatre (1). The sixteenth century. Shakepeare. The Merchant of Venice, Act 1.
Hafta 3: The Merchant of Venice, Acts 2-5.
Hafta 4: The Merchant of Venice, the adaptation. The seventeenth century. Webster.
Hafta 5: The Duchess of Malfi, Acts 1-3.
Hafta 6: The Duchess of Malfi, Acts 4-5 and the adaptation.
Hafta 7: Theories of drama (2). The eighteenth century. Sheridan. The Rivals, Act 1.
Hafta 8: The Rivals, Acts 2-4.
Hafta 9: The Rivals, Act 5 and the adaptation.
Hafta 10: The nineteenth century. Shaw. Mrs. Warren's Profession, Acts 1-2.
Hafta 11: Mrs. Warren's Profession, Acts 3-4 and the adaptation.
Hafta 12: The twentieth century. Pinter.
Hafta 13: Betrayal.
Hafta 14: Betrayal, the adaptation.
resources: Course pack, Andrea Selleri (compiler)
Various documents uploaded on Moodle

courses.name: Introduction to the Novel
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the study of the novel in English and prepares them for more advanced literary analysis.
courses.description: The course emphasizes the formal qualities of the novel, including language, structure, plot, character, theme, and setting. It also serves as a bridge between earlier courses that introduce literary study more broadly and later courses that examine the novel in period and thematic contexts. Texts for study may be taken from any period or sub-genre of the novel.

courses.name: Poetry
courses.purpose: This course aims to promote students' understanding of poetry and to develop their skills in reading poems of a wide variety of types from diverse historical periods.
courses.description: Students will explore various types of poetry, considering figurative language, prosodic features, sound patterns, verse and stanza forms, and the relationship between form and meaning in poetry.

courses.name: Civil War, Restoration, Revolution
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the literature and culture of the long eighteenth century, situating major literary developments between the English Civil War and the French Revolution within their historical and cultural contexts.
courses.description: This course gives students a broad introduction to the literature and culture of the long eighteenth century, covering a period from the English Civil War of the 1640s to the French Revolution of 1789. Primary texts may be works of poetry, prose, or drama, and are studied in relation to their historical and cultural contexts. Topics covered may include literature of the English Civil War, Milton's Paradise Lost, Restoration drama, the flowering of satire, literature of the Enlightenment, English landscape writing, Neoclassicism and its emphasis on reason, harmony, and wit, the birth of the modern novel, the literature of sentiment and sensibility, the origins and development of the Gothic, and the emergence of the Industrial Revolution.
learning_outcomes: Understand how literature written between 1640 and 1789 responded to and shaped important historical events from the period.

Produce fine-grained and provocative close readings of literary texts from the period and situate those readings in relation both to the historical period and to literary criticism that focuses on the period.
Understand important literary phenomena from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, including the Miltonic epic, the rise of the novel, satire, wit, and spiritual biography and autobiography.
Understand how writers from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries saw literary language as related to political change.
Understand the language that critics use to talk about the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Civil War and Restoration Literature
Hafta 2: Introduction to Civil War and Restoration Literature
Hafta 3: John Wilmot, Earl of Rochester, ‘Against Constancy’ and ‘The Fall’
Hafta 4: The Libertine
Hafta 5: John Milton, selections from Paradise Lost, 10. 460–577
Hafta 6: John Milton, selections from Paradise Lost, 10. 460–577
Hafta 7: Introduction to Eighteenth-Century Literature
Hafta 8: Nature Wild and Tame
Hafta 9: Worlds of Beauty, Worlds of Crime
Hafta 10: Laurence Sterne, selections from Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman
Hafta 11: Laurence Sterne, selections from Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman
Hafta 12: Barry Lyndon
Hafta 13: Stings of Satire
Hafta 14: Stings of Satire

courses.name: Medieval and Renaissance Literature
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the study of Medieval and Renaissance literary texts and to some of the key genres and historical and cultural contexts of Medieval and Renaissance literature in English. It also encourages students to question periodising labels and common assumptions about these two periods and their literature.
courses.description: Using thematic and/or chronological approaches, the course explores themes such as monsters and villains, Medieval and Renaissance varieties of love, the problem of the epic, pastoral, romance, humanism, the Reformation, manuscript and print cultures, and the discovery of new worlds. The course focuses on key texts and on the social, political, and literary characteristics of the Medieval and Renaissance periods.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate a capacity to engage critically with the key course texts.
Show an understanding of significant social, political, and literary characteristics of the Medieval and Renaissance periods.

courses.name: The Short Story
courses.purpose: An introduction to the short story through selected examples from British, American, and world writing.
courses.description: An introduction to the short story through selected examples from British, American, and world writing.

courses.name: The English Novel (19th Century)
courses.purpose: A study of the major English novelists of the 19th century.
courses.description: A study of the major English novelists of the 19th century.

courses.name: Shakespeare
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' skills in textual analysis and criticism through the close study of Shakespeare's plays and poetry.
courses.description: The principle dramatic genres (Comedy, History, Tragedy) will be addressed, as well as the 'Late' or 'Problem' plays, the Sonnets, and other poems. Students will also learn to critically evaluate Shakespeare's dramatic texts as works of literature and works for the theatre, and to situate Shakespeare's poetry in terms of its contemporary contexts, classical precedents, and literary legacies.
learning_outcomes: Gain a basic knowledge of Shakespeare's relation to the world of theatre in his own time.
Critically evaluate Shakespeare's dramatic texts as works of literature and works for the theatre.
Situate Shakespeare's poetry in terms of its contemporary contexts, classical precedents, and literary legacies.

courses.name: Philosophy and Literature
courses.purpose: This course offers a philosophical examination of the concept of literature, the nature and distinctness of meaning in literature, the relations between literature and truth, and the values inherent in literature.
courses.description: The course examines philosophical questions about literature and literary criticism, including whether works of fiction can be philosophical, whether literature can be defined, what the criteria of validity in interpretation are, and what is distinctive about poetry as a mode of discourse.
learning_outcomes: Formulate the central philosophical issues regarding literature and literary criticism.
Explain and employ the fundamental concepts relating to literary criticism, including the concept of literature itself, literary meaning, and literary value.

courses.name: Plays and London since 1850
courses.purpose: This course aims to examine how plays represent the characteristics of London from 1850 to 1995 and to explore the relationship between British drama and social change in and about London in a historical perspective.
courses.description: This course offers a study of the plays that examine the characteristics of London city from 1850 to 1995. Along with the close analysis of the plays that reflect the manners of Londoners when they were written, the course probes into London productions, the efficiency of London theatres, and the ways in which plays contribute to social change in a historical perspective.
learning_outcomes: Be familiar with the work of a number of dramatists writing for British theatre from the 1850s onwards.
Consider the relationship between British drama and social change in and about London during the period.

courses.name: Victorian Literature
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the major prose and poetry of the Victorian period through thematic and chronological approaches. It aims to develop students’ ability to understand and critically engage with the social, political, and literary characteristics of the period.
courses.description: This course focuses on the prose and poetry of the Victorian period, using thematic and/or chronological approaches. Themes include the Condition of England Question, the Woman Question, morality, scientific and technological development, and the significance of race and Empire.
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to describe effectively the formal and thematic properties of texts typical of the most important genres of Victorian literature, that is, prose fiction, the essay, and poetry.
Students will be able to evaluate the dominant genres of Victorian literature.
Students will learn to situate and evaluate Victorian texts historically and theoretically.
Students will be able to consider and analyse how theoretical and practical issues shape and are shaped by modes of representation in Victorian texts.
Students will be able to formulate research questions about Victorian literary history in the light of insights and terminology derived from politics, history, philosophy, and other disciplines.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the Victorian Period.

Hafta 2: Industrialisation and the Condition of England. Thomas Babington Macaulay, ‘A Review of Southey’s Colloquies’ (1830); excerpt from ‘The Children’s Employment Commission: First Report’ (1842-43); Fredrich Engels, excerpt from The Great Towns (1845); Thomas Carlyle, excerpt from Past and Present (1843); Elizabeth Barrett Browning, ‘The Cry of the Children’ (1843); and excepts from Elizabeth Gaskell’s North and South (1855).

Hafta 3: The Angel in the House: Coventry Patmore, excerpts from The Angel in the House (1854); John Ruskin, excerpts from Of Queen’s Gardens (1864); Sarah Stickney Ellis, excerpts from The Women of England: Their Social Duties and Domestic Habits (1839); and Elizabeth Gaskell, ‘The Old Nurse’s Story’ (1852).

Hafta 4: Saints and Sinners: John Stuart Mill, The Subjection of Women (1869); Christina Rossetti, Goblin Market (1862); and Robert Browning, ‘My Last Duchess’ (1842), ‘Porphyria’s Lover’ (1842), ‘The Laboratory’ (1844).

Hafta 5: 'The Great Social Evil': Contagious Diseases Acts of the 1860s; Excerpts from Henry Mayhew’s ‘Prostitution in London’ (1862), Prime Minister William Gladstone and the Prostitutes of London, Anonymous, “The Great Social Evil”, Dante Gabriel Rosetti, ‘Jenny’, Oscar Wilde, ‘The Harlot’s House’.
Hafta 6: Midterm Week

Hafta 7: Medievalism: Clare Broome Saunders, ‘Guinevere: The Medieval Queen in the Nineteenth Century’, Women Writers and Nineteenth Century Medievalism (New York: Palgrave Macmillan, 2009), 103-132; William Morris, ‘The Defence of Guenevere’ (1858); Violet Fane, ‘Lancelot and Guinevere’ (1872).

Hafta 8: The Pre-Raphaelites: William Michael Rossetti, ‘The Pre-Raphaelite Manifesto’ (1895); Dante Gabriel Rossetti, ‘Nuptial Sleep’ (1881), ‘Soul’s Beauty’ (1881), ‘Body’s Beauty’ (1881); and Christina Rossetti, ‘In an Artist’s Studio’ (1896).

Hafta 9: Science and Sociology: Charles Darwin, Origin of the Species (1859); Gregory Claeys, ‘The “Survival of the Fittest” and the Origins of Social Darwinism’, Journal of the History of Ideas, 61.2 (2000), 223-240; and H.G. Wells, The Time Machine (1895).
Hafta 10: Science and Sociology: H. G. Wells, The Time Machine (1895) continued.
Hafta 11: Research Project Workshop

Hafta 12: Aestheticism and Decadence: Walter Pater, excerpts from Studies in the History of the Renaissance; Oscar Wilde, ‘The Preface to The Picture of Dorian Gray’, ‘Harlot’s House’, Charles Algernon Swinburne, ‘Hymn to Proserpine’.

Hafta 13: The Empire: Excepts from Stuart Laycock’s All The Countries We've Ever Invaded: And The Few We Never Got Round To (2012), Alfred, Lord Tennyson, “Opening of the Indian and Colonial Exhibition by the Queen, 1886”, Selected works from the Ladies’ Realm magazine, 1896-1901
Hafta 14: The Empire: Rudyard Kipling, “The White Man’s Burden” (1899), Ernest Crosby, “The Real White Man’s Burden”, H. T. Johnson, “The Black Man’s Burden”
resources: Norton Anthology: The Victorian Age
Course Pack, Ceylan Kosker Bevington

courses.name: Literature and Modernity
courses.purpose: This course aims to help students understand how literature in English responded to the major social, political, and cultural transformations of the first half of the twentieth century. It also develops students' ability to critically engage with literary texts in their historical context.
courses.description: This course focuses on literature written in English in the first half of the twentieth century. Primary texts may be works of fiction, poetry, or drama, and are explored in relation to their social, political, and cultural contexts. In particular, students will explore a range of British literary responses to modernity, in contexts such as those of European imperialism, world war, totalitarianism, and international and local varieties of modernism.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the key literary characteristics of writing from the first half of the twentieth century.
Critically engage with literary texts in relation to their historical and cultural contexts.

courses.name: Literary Theory
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to introduce students to some of the main contexts and concepts of modern literary criticism and theory; to examine, in different ways, a number of different traditions of thought and critical practice that have contributed to the formation of current debates about the nature of literature, literary criticism, and theory. By the end of the course, students are expected to have gained a critical sense of the presuppositions and principles of literary criticism, and issues of knowledge, value, tradition, and ideology arising from the practice of reading.
courses.description: This course examines major contexts and concepts of modern literary criticism and theory, along with a range of traditions of thought and critical practice that have shaped contemporary debates about literature, literary criticism, and theory. It also focuses on applying critical terminology and interpretive approaches to literary texts.
learning_outcomes: Be familiar with some of the central schools of thought and key ideas that have driven twentieth century and contemporary debates on literature.
Have gained a critical sense of the presuppositions and principles of literary criticism, and issues of knowledge, meaning, value, tradition, and ideology arising from the practice of reading.
Be able to apply theoretical paradigms and concepts to specific literary texts in nuanced and sophisticated ways.
Be able to engage in debates around the merits and shortcomings of particular arguments and ideas within the theoretical discourse, as well as in debates between different schools of theory.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Friedrich Nietzsche
Hafta 2: Existentialism (Martin Heidegger, Jean-Paul Sartre)
Hafta 3: No class
Hafta 4: Sigmund Freud and Psychoanalysis
Hafta 5: Freud, continued
Hafta 6: Critical Theory and Ideology Critique (Karl Marx)
Hafta 7: Critical Theory, continued (Theodore Adorno)
Hafta 8: Critical Theory, continued (Walter Benjamin)
Hafta 9: Michel Foucault: Power, Knowledge, Discourse
Hafta 10: Midterm Exam
Hafta 11: Orientalism and Postcolonialism (Edward Said, Homi Bhabha)
Hafta 12: Gender, Sexuality, and Feminist Theory (Simone de Beauvoir, Betty Friedan)
Hafta 13: Gender, Sexuality, and Feminist Theory (Judith Butler, Hélène Cixous)
Hafta 14: Contemporary Debates: Posthumanism (Donna Haraway)
Hafta 15: Contemporary Debates: Affect Theory and Postcritique (Siann Ngai, Lauren Berlant, Rita Felski)
resources: Course pack

courses.name: Transformations: Novels between Genres, Genders, and Species
courses.purpose: This course offers students the opportunity to explore late 19th and 20th century novels that cross boundaries related to genre, gender, and species.
courses.description: Extracts from a variety of theoretical texts related to topics such as Orientalism, postcolonial studies, the Gothic, the Uncanny, autobiography, and psychology will be studied in relation to the selected primary texts. The course focuses on late 19th and 20th century novels that cross boundaries related to genre, gender, and species.
learning_outcomes: Develop an understanding of the issues raised by generically hybrid literary texts.
Use theoretical material to enhance analyses of the selected novels.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Intro; Cortazar
Hafta 2: Woolf, Orlando
Hafta 3: Orlando
Hafta 4: Orlando
Hafta 5: Orlando
Hafta 6: Midterm 1
Hafta 7: The Vegetarian
Hafta 8: The Vegetarian
Hafta 9: The Vegetarian
Hafta 10: Midterm 2
Hafta 11: Zoo City
Hafta 12: Zoo City
Hafta 13: Zoo City
Hafta 14: Zoo City

courses.name: Selected Topics
courses.purpose: The course is designed to cover literary genres, movements, writers, or topics that are not included in the regular curriculum. It allows the department to address student needs and recent developments in literary studies.
courses.description: The course will cover any genre, movement, writer or topic not included in the curriculum. The coverage of the course will be determined according to the needs of the students and to the recent developments in literary studies.

courses.name: Postcolonial Literature
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to recent fiction written in English by authors from the former British Empire and to the major concerns of postcolonial literary study.
courses.description: A study of recent fiction written in English by authors from the former British Empire. Works by such authors as Chinua Achebe, Buchi Emecheta, V.S. Naipaul, Salman Rushdie, Hanif Kureishi, Anita Desai and Ngugi wa Thiong'o may be discussed. Post-colonial theory may also be studied through selected texts by authors like Edward Said, Frantz Fanon, Homi Bhabha, and Gayatri Spivak.

courses.name: Twentieth Century Poetry
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to major twentieth-century poems, poets, and poetic movements, with attention to how poetry develops in relation to historical, social, and cultural change.
courses.description: The course focuses on issues of language, style, and form in twentieth-century poetry. It examines the impact of Modernism on poetry in the British Isles, especially through poets such as Eliot and Yeats, and then considers later responses to that legacy, including the Movement of the 1950s and the British Poetry Revival of the 1980s.

courses.name: Advanced English Grammar I
courses.purpose: This course is designed specifically to help students further develop competency in grammar, vocabulary, and modes of written expression under timed conditions.
courses.description: Students work on improving their grammar, vocabulary, and written expression, with an emphasis on producing accurate writing under timed conditions. The course also supports self-editing through the maintenance of a portfolio of written work.

courses.name: Advanced English Grammar II
courses.purpose: This course is designed to build on the skills developed in ENG 117, specifically by helping literature students to improve their competency in grammar, vocabulary and modes of written expression under timed conditions.
courses.description: This course builds on ENG 117 and focuses on improving students' grammar, vocabulary, and written expression under timed conditions. It also emphasizes self-editing through the maintenance of a portfolio of written work.
learning_outcomes: Can identify sentence constituents and word order in complex sentences
Can use sentence constituents accurately in complex sentences
Can identify, analyse and correct errors related to sentence constituents and word order in self-produced prose
Can use appropriate meta-language to talk about the form and function of the structures covered in this course
Can edit their written work using the grammar knowledge they acquired during the course
Can effectively explain the form and function of sentence constituents orally
Can identify semantic, morphological, etymological features of vocabulary items that correspond to high B2 to C1 level of CEFR
Can use vocabulary at high B2 to C1 level of CEFR in a way that displays an awareness of abstract versus concrete/literal meaning
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course & expectations. Word order & verb patterns (sentence constituents, transitive-intransitive verbs, complement)
Hafta 2: Passive Constructions: Passives in reported speech / Passive –ing forms / Passive infinitives
Hafta 3: Passive Voice (cont’d): Causatives / Commands and Promises
Hafta 4: Subordinating Conjunctions. Finite adverbial clauses
Hafta 5: Non-finite Clauses
Hafta 6: Noun Modification (modifiers in noun phrases, adjective clauses, relative pronoun choice)
Hafta 7: Noun Modification (cont'd): Defining and non-defining relative clauses and phrases (omission, reduction)
Hafta 8: Gerunds and infinitives
Hafta 9: Gerunds and infinitives (cont'd)
Hafta 10: Unreal forms of past (wish/if only)
Hafta 11: Emphatic structures and inversion. The subjunctive
Hafta 12: Grammar presentations in pairs
Hafta 13: Grammar presentations in pairs
Hafta 14: Grammar presentations in pairs
resources: Advanced Learners' Grammar, Mark Foley & Diane Hall, 2010/10th Edition, Pearson/Longman
Real Grammar, Susan Conrad & Douglas Biber, 2009, Pearson/Longman

courses.name: Introduction to Creative Writing
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the fundamentals of creative writing and help them develop their own writing voice. It also encourages students to read, discuss, and critique short fiction with attention to craft, genre, and audience.
courses.description: Creative writing, fiction, writerly craft, short story, written sketches, creative process, inspiration techniques, expression in English, professional examples, critical audience, personal development, storytelling, literary elements, narrative structure, constructive critique.
learning_outcomes: Appreciate and evaluate short fiction with a focus on elements of craft and genre
Communicate about creative writing and processes clearly and appropriately for diverse audiences
Explore and develop an artistic process that is creative, productive, and personally meaningful
Use a range of techniques, tools, and resources appropriately to generate, develop, and refine creative ideas
Explore and develop a personal and effective writerly voice and literary style in English
Provide insightful and encouraging feedback on creative writing and processes
Craft enjoyable short fiction and creative writing that moves, educates, and inspires its readers
Use digital tools to polish and format writing and information appropriately and professionally
Develop awareness of professional practices, networks, and opportunities in creative writing
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course overview + Input on creativity and the elements of fiction
Hafta 2: Story Craft Discussions + writing exercises + Sketchbook #1 - Detail
Hafta 3: Story Craft Discussions + writing exercises
Hafta 4: Story Craft Discussions + writing exercises + Sketchbook #2 - Dialogue
Hafta 5: Story Craft Discussions + writing exercises
Hafta 6: Story Craft Discussions + writing exercises + Story Draft due
Hafta 7: Workshops + Peer Feedback + writing exercises
Hafta 8: Workshops + Peer Feedback + writing exercises
Hafta 9: Workshops + Peer Feedback + writing exercises
Hafta 10: Workshops + Peer Feedback + writing exercises
Hafta 11: Workshops + Peer Feedback + writing exercises
Hafta 12: Workshops + Peer Feedback + writing exercises
Hafta 13: Workshops + Peer Feedback + writing exercises + Sketchbook #3 + Final Story
Hafta 14: Writer Reflection
resources: Assorted classic and modern short stories

courses.name: Drawing I
courses.description: Exploration of the human figure, its parts and its form as far as structure and functions are concerned. Drawing fundamentals in figurative expression, proportions and compositional expressions by working directly from the model in various media.
learning_outcomes: Construct the basic drawing ability.
Develop practical skills on use of pencil, charcoal and ink drawing.
Develop analytical and creative drawing techniques.
Construct drawing skills based on observation.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course. Methods, list of tools and materials and rules for studio.
Hafta 2: Mark making exercises. Exploring Line
Hafta 3: Drawing with the both sides of the brain: Drawing the Line, Seeing the Line and Taking the Line for a Walk
Hafta 4: Contour drawing exercises: pure contour, blind contour and modified contour drawing.
Hafta 5: Perceiving shapes and spaces – exploring negative space
Hafta 6: Adding dimension – value: light and shading
Hafta 7: Adding accuracy and dimension to drawings
Hafta 8: Developing the line: Learning from the masters
Hafta 9: Expanding techniques: Pencil and Charcoal
Hafta 10: Expanding techniques: Pen and Brush
Hafta 11: Sketching -Tips and techniques & Composition - Types of composition
Hafta 12: Doodling, Sketching and Expressive Drawing and New Tools
Hafta 13: Introduction to Figure and Gesture Drawing
Hafta 14: Introduction to Still Life Drawing & Portfolio Review
resources: Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain, Betty Edwards, 2012/4th edition, TarcherPerigee
Keys to Drawing, Bert Dodson, 1990, North Light Books
Drawing for the Absolute Beginner: A Clear & Easy Guide to Successful Drawing, Mark Willenbrink, 2006, North Light Books
Pen And Pencil Drawing Techniques, Harry Borgman, 1989, Watson-Guptill Publications

courses.name: Post-War Literature
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to major literary developments since 1945 and helps them understand how post-war writing responds to historical, political, and cultural change. It also aims to develop students’ critical reading and analytical skills through engagement with fiction, poetry, and drama.
courses.description: This course focuses on literature published since 1945. Primary texts may be works of fiction, poetry, or drama, and are explored in relation to their social, political, and cultural contexts. Themes may include war and trauma, postmodernism, second-wave feminism, class, multiculturalism, and metafiction.
learning_outcomes: Have a clear sense of how the key historical developments during this period in British history, including the emergence of the welfare state, the Windrush generation and multicultural Britain, and Thatcherism, influenced literary production
Understand the vexed transformation in Britain’s self-conception as it transforms from Empire to welfare state, and how this tension is reflected in literature
Be familiar with the different aesthetic traditions and thematic concerns of writers from different parts of the British Isles, as well as those ‘voyaging in’ from the former colonies
Students should be able to employ appropriate critical terminology and draw on relevant critical sources and cultural contexts to support and frame analyses of post-war literature.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: George Orwell, "You and the Atom Bomb" (1945); JFK Commencement Address at American University (1963)
Hafta 2: Ian McEwan, Atonement (2001); Mark Rothko, "Aubade" (1944); Philip Larkin, "Aubade" (1977)
Hafta 3: Ian McEwan, Atonement (2001); Sylvia Plath, "Tulips" (1961)
Hafta 4: Ian McEwan, Atonement (2001); Ursula K. Le Guin, "The Ones Who Walk Away From Omelas" (1973)
Hafta 5: Ian McEwan, Atonement (2001); Albert Camus "The Fact of Absurdity" (1942)
Hafta 6: Samuel Beckett, Endgame (1957); Peter Howarth, "Why is it so difficult?" (2012)
Hafta 7: Samuel Beckett, Endgame (1957); excerpts from Marc Botha's A Theory of Minimalism (2017); JH Prynne "Moon Poem"
Hafta 8: Kazuo Ishiguro, An Artist of the Floating World (1986); Zadie Smith, "The Embassy of Cambodia" (2013)
Hafta 9: Kazuo Ishiguro, An Artist of the Floating World (1986); Jamaica Kincaid, "On Seeing England for the First Time" (1985)
Hafta 10: Kazuo Ishiguro, An Artist of the Floating World (1986); student presentations
Hafta 11: Kazuo Ishiguro, An Artist of the Floating World (1986); student presentations
Hafta 12: student presentations
Hafta 13: student presentations
Hafta 14: student presentations; course recap
resources: An Artist of the Floating World, Kazuo Ishiguro, 1986, Vintage International
Atonement, Ian McEwan, 2001, Penguin Random House
Endgame, Samuel Beckett
Course Pack

courses.name: Drawing and Visual Expressions
courses.purpose: Improvement of drawing skills by means of discovering the ways of how to look and what to see in the environment in which we live.
courses.description: Improvement of drawing skills through observing the environment and learning how to look and what to see.

courses.name: Drawing II
courses.description: Exploration of visual form, character and gesture of movements of the human figure. Searching, evaluating and applying the basic elements of life drawing: line, form, color, value, module and composition.
learning_outcomes: Learn and apply the principles of color theory to drawings through various exercises
Analyze the variations of different rendering techniques.
Know to use different and creative sketching and drawing techniques
Experiment with different tools and media
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Sketching: Tips and Techniques
Hafta 2: Sketching Exercises/ 30 Pages of Sketch Drawing, Homework
Hafta 3: Color Theory
Hafta 4: Exercises with Color, Homework
Hafta 5: Figure Drawing & Field Trip
Hafta 6: Color Pencils and Pastels, Homework
Hafta 7: Color Inks and Brushwork - Project 1
Hafta 8: Experimental Drawing and Painting Techniques
Hafta 9: Mixed Media Drawing and Painting Techniques, Homework
Hafta 10: Close-up Drawing
Hafta 11: Landscape Drawing, Project 2
Hafta 12: Sketching and Drawing in the City/Campus, Homework
Hafta 13: Field Trip to Ankara Castle - 1st week of May
Hafta 15: Evaluation of the Works and Preparation for the End of the Year Exhibition
resources: Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain, Betty Edwards, 2012, 4th edition, TarcherPerigee
Keys to Drawing, Bert Dodson, 1990, North Light Books
Drawing for the Absolute Beginner: A Clear & Easy Guide to Successful Drawing, Mark Willenbrink, 2006, North Light Books
Drawing on the Artist Within, Betty Edwards, 1986, A Fireside Book
The Colored Pencil Manual, Veronica Winters, 2018, Dover Publications
Pastel Innovations, Dawn Emerson, 2017, North Light Books

courses.name: Drawing III
courses.purpose: This course aims to improve students' drawing skills and visual understanding through the study of the human figure.
courses.description: Study of the figure to improve drawing skills and knowledge. Fast sketching, figural expression, transformation, drawing materials and techniques.

courses.name: Drawing IV
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to observe and represent the human body through drawing, with attention to form, color, and color values. It also seeks to support individual expression through model drawing based on the basic principles of art.
courses.description: Examining the human body in terms of form, color and color values. Studies leading to individual expression in model drawing based on the basic principles of art.

courses.name: Introduction to Art, Design and Culture I
courses.purpose: This undergraduate core course introduces students to diverse approaches to visual culture and the key theories and concepts that shape art, design, and architecture. It aims to develop students' understanding of the historical, social, and political contexts that regulate the production and consumption of visual culture.
courses.description: This undergraduate core course examines a diverse range of approaches to visual culture. Through lectures, class discussions and audio-visual media, this course introduces students to key theories and concepts on art, design and architecture. During the course, the students will develop an understanding of historical, social and political knowledge that regulate the production and consumption of visual culture. The students will be evaluated based on class participation, assignments, quizzes, oral presentation, a midterm and a final exam.
learning_outcomes: Strong historical and critical sensibility about artistic and cultural production.
Correlate Art and Culture, the terminology and institutions that define the arts, a historical background of the arts, paintings, sculpture, architecture, design, and popular culture.
Fair knowledge of and a critical perspective on concepts, values and the relationship of art and culture.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Art, Design and Culture I
Hafta 2: Images and Ideologies I
Hafta 3: Images and Ideologies II
Hafta 4: Debates on free will, subjecthood, and artistic production
Hafta 5: Power of the Image: Representation and Truth, Public Art and Monuments
Hafta 6: Film Screening and Discussion
Hafta 7: Visual Analysis on Avant-garde and Modern Art
Hafta 8: The Intersection of Art, Design and Architecture: Bauhaus
Hafta 9: Film Screening and Discussion
Hafta 10: Expanding the Canon: Feminism and Art History
Hafta 11: Spectatorship: The Gaze
Hafta 12: Art and Mass media I
Hafta 13: Art and Mass media II
Hafta 14: Group Presentations
resources: Ways of seeing., Berger, J., 1973, London: British Broadcasting
Reader; chapters and excerpts from selected books

courses.name: Artistic Anatomy
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop the ability to perceive and represent the perfection of the human body form through techniques and attitudes used in observing and drawing the skeleton and live models.
courses.description: The course focuses on observing and drawing the human skeleton and live models, with emphasis on understanding body form and anatomical structure through studio practice.

courses.name: Anatomical Figure Drawing
courses.purpose: Furthering the artistic and anatomical approach to figure drawing through critical interpretation and detailed study of the works of Renaissance great masters in studio sessions.
courses.description: The course focuses on figure drawing with an artistic and anatomical approach. It includes critical interpretation and detailed study of Renaissance masters’ works, supported by studio-based practice.
learning_outcomes: Have vision(s) in one’s mind by how and where to look, what to see in order to gain the ability of “eye to hand coordination”.

By drawing, have a better appreciation of the world around us and discover new horizons and dexterity, enjoy the enchanting side of doing so, and find out the artistic beauty of the human body by using simple instruments such as pencil and piece of paper.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: Art of Anatomy
Hafta 2: General aspects of Figure Drawing
Hafta 3: Renaissance Learning, Proportions
Hafta 4: G. Bernini and His Art. Studio Session I
Hafta 5: G. Bernini and His Art. Studio Session II
Hafta 6: Michelangelo and his Art. Studio Session III
Hafta 7: Michelangelo and His Art. Studio Session IV
Hafta 8: Raphael and His Art. Studio Session V
Hafta 9: Raphael and His Art. Studio Session VI
Hafta 10: A. Durer and His Art. Studio Session VII
Hafta 11: A. Durer and his Art. Studio Session VIII
Hafta 12: Sistine Chapel. Studio Sessions IX
Hafta 13: Sistine Chapel. Studio Session X
Hafta 14: Caravaggio. Studio Session XI
