pencils

courses.name: Introduction to Art, Design and Culture II
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' historical and critical knowledge of artistic and cultural production, with emphasis on the social function of art, modernism, post-modernism, art institutions, and media studies.
courses.description: This undergraduate course is part of the two-semester sequence (FA 171 and FA 172). It focuses on artistic and cultural production, the social function of art, modernism, post-modernism, art institutions, and media studies. Students apply concepts from lectures and readings to visual material in both theoretical and practical ways.
learning_outcomes: Have a historical and critical sensibility about artistic and cultural production.
Be sensitive on the social functions of art, develop an awareness on different art periods and various media such as photography, film, television, and digital media, and multimedia.
Have a keen awareness of their visual environment in a theoretical and practical way by applying the concepts given in the lectures and readings to their visual and symbolic environment.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Art and Culture II
Hafta 2: Social Function of Art
Hafta 3: Modernism
Hafta 4: Art and The Unconscious
Hafta 5: Institutions and History
Hafta 6: Pop Art and Mass Media

courses.name: Introduction to Painting I
courses.purpose: This course aims to build basic technical skills in painting and develop students' ability to observe, experiment, and express ideas through paint. It also introduces creative processes through practical exploration of color mixing and painting techniques.
courses.description: Building basic technical skills. Experiments with the use of paint. Conceptual and practical experiments on color mixing. Examining the creative processes through these experiments. (non-FA majors only).
learning_outcomes: Have personal understanding of the visual language, technical and observational skills.
Practice the foundations of acrilic painting techniques.
Have an improved sensibility and able to broaden their horizons what will be beneficial in any aspects of life - personal and professional.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to painting, tools and techniques. Additional Information: Buying painting supply, papers and canvases.
Hafta 2: Beginners: introduction to the colour wheel, color theory, basics of history of painting. Continuing students: reminding painting foundations, still life assignment.

Hafta 3: Beginners: priming papers, experiments with paints, different value and temperature of colours. Mixing colours, diversity of textures according to the changing tools and adding different mediums. Continuing: still life - follow-up.

Hafta 4: Beginners: different textures, lines, shades and shadows to be shown by using paints. Expressing contrasting emotions, using different techniques: glaze, textures, impasto, dry brush and knife, smooth blend/gradient of colors. Continuing students: still life - follow-up.
Hafta 5: Introducing proportions. Analysing still life as 3D construction to be turn into 2D composition.
Hafta 6: Follow-up.
Hafta 7: Follow-up: self-assesment.

Hafta 8: Set up a simple monochrome arrangement of a metal can or two on a neutral background. Use objects that do not have a lot of value contrast. Try to set things up so that edges of one object disappear against an object behind. Additional Information: follow-up.
Hafta 9: Beginners and continuing students: analysing soft and hard edges, warm and cool colours, still and vibrating surface, low and high contrast - all to be transformed from a nature onto canvas.
Hafta 10: Analysing soft and hard edges, warm and cool colours, still and vibrating surface, low and high contrast - all to be transformed from a nature onto canvas. Follow-up.
Hafta 11: Working with a model.
Hafta 12: Working with a model - follow-up.
Hafta 13: Working with a model - follow-up.
Hafta 14: Completing the portfolio - critics. Self assesment.
resources: Painting supply (acrilic)
An introduction to acrylics, Smith, Ray, 1998,c1993., New York. Dorling Kindersley in association with the Royal Academy of Arts.
An introduction to art techniques, Ray Smith, Michael Wright, James Horton., 1st pbk. ed, 1996., New York : Dorling Kindersley in association with the Royal Academy of Arts.
The Acrylic artist's bible: an essential reference for the practicing artist., Scott, Marylin, 2016., London : Chartwell Books.
Interaction of color, Albers, Josef, 50th anniversary edition ; 4th edition.

courses.name: Introduction to Painting II
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' skills in painting through guided practice and project-based work. It focuses on strengthening compositional ability and visual sensitivity by engaging with color, form, structure, volume, light, and sight relationships.
courses.description: Research on visual elements; form-color, structure, volume and composition. Relations between light, color and sight. Developing the skill of design, compositional capacity through working on certain projects. (non-FA majors only).
learning_outcomes: Posess developed skills of acrilic painting technique.
Posess an improved sensibility and develop inspiration with a flourished imagination.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to painting, tools and techniques
Hafta 2: Priming papers for the first introductory classes, introduction to the colour wheel
Hafta 3: Different value and temperature of colours. Mixing colours. Different textures, lines, shades and shadows to be shown by using paints - First excercise
Hafta 4: Analysing still life in comparison to great examples from the Art History.
Hafta 5: Introducing proportions. Analysing still life as 3D construction to be turn into 2D composition
Hafta 6: follow-up
Hafta 7: New still life introducing different textures, temperature of colors and expression.
Hafta 8: Follow-up
Hafta 9: Set up colour arrangement of objects with hard edges and very high contrasts - all to be transformed from a nature onto canvas
Hafta 10: Analysing soft and hard edges, warm and cool colours, still and vibrating surface, low and high contrast - all to be transformed from a nature onto canvas

Hafta 11: Set up a simple monochrome arrangement of a metal can or two on a neutral background. Use objects that do not have a lot of value contrast. Try to set things up so that edges of one object disappear against an object behind.
Hafta 12: Analysis of chosen master painting.

Hafta 13: Students will continue with their individual projects. During the course of remaining weeks, at least one new concept or technique will be presented each week with demos and exercises given as appropriate.

Hafta 14: Students will continue with their individual projects. During the course of remaining weeks, at least one new concept or technique will be presented each week with demos and exercises given as appropriate.

courses.name: Introduction to Printmaking I
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the fundamental techniques of water-based silkscreen printmaking and helps them develop independent and creative approaches to applying these processes.
courses.description: Introduction to the fundamental techniques of water-based silkscreen printmaking. Manual and digital processes. Independent work and application of processes in creative ways while mastering the applied techniques. All projects in small editions while the final project involving a multi-process, multi-layer edition. Individual and group critiques throughout the semester.
learning_outcomes: Be conversant with multiple introductory screen printing processes of paper stencil, hand-drawn films and photo emulsion.
Have a command of the technical aspects of all processes covered.
Attain the ability to mix and apply water based inks as well as photographic emulsion.
Possess an awareness of the historic traditions of the medium.
Have the discipline to develop initial preliminary ideas to create inventive, visually challenging works of art.
Think critically, communicate clearly and work creatively in intellectual pursuit.
Develop self-motivation, self-direction and a strong work ethic.
Have a completed portfolio of work that represents all assignments.

Hafta 2: Stencil Printing
Hafta 3: Drawing fluid and emulsion

Hafta 14: work week

Printmaking: History and Process, Sacilotto and Gilbert, 1978, Holt

courses.name: Introduction to Sculpture I
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the basic concepts, materials, and processes of sculpture. It aims to develop an understanding of perception and the representation of three-dimensional objects through studio practice.
courses.description: An introductory studio course on the basic concepts, materials and processes of sculpture, with an emphasis on the understanding of perception and representation of three dimensional objects for students from other disciplines. (non-FA majors only.)
learning_outcomes: Possess basic concepts, materials and processes of sculpture
Explore and realize two, three dimensional projects

courses.name: Introduction to Ceramics I
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the basic materials and techniques used in ceramics. It also helps students develop practical skills through studio work and connect ceramic practice with the life and work of an artist.
courses.description: Introduction to the basic material and techniques in ceramics including design, glazing, firing, loading and unloading kiln. Practice of what has been studied based on an artist's life and work. By the end of the course, writing of a research paper demonstrating knowledge of design, glazing and firing based on the artist chosen to work on.
learning_outcomes: Have a technical command of basic material and techniques in ceramics including design, glazing, firing, loading and unloading kiln.
Able to form functional and three-dimensional objects by using red, white and colored clay.
Able to design work with different texturing techniques.
Understand an artist’s life and work to see how ceramics principles are applied.

courses.name: Visual Perception and Color
courses.purpose: To examine the psychological basis of visual perception and the ways color is seen and interpreted. The course also aims to introduce fundamental concepts and theories related to color and color-form perception.
courses.description: Examination of subjects such as psychology of visual perception, seeing the color and perception of color-form, concepts and theories of color.

courses.name: Ancient Techniques and New Technology in Mosaic
courses.purpose: This course aims to explore experimental approaches in mosaic and ceramics by combining traditional techniques with new technologies. It encourages students to search for new ways of expression in color and to use clay as a creative surface.
courses.description: An experimental course, combining the current techniques and the new technology in ceramics such as using ceramic colors on fired tiles. Search for new ways of expression in color. Using the clay as the canvas.
learning_outcomes: Able to combine the current techniques and the new technology in ceramics such as using ceramic colours on fired tiles.
Able to search new ways of expression in colour.
Able to use the clay as the canvas.

courses.name: Introduction to Printmaking II
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the fundamental techniques of oil-based intaglio printmaking and to develop their ability to work independently in the medium. It also encourages experimentation with traditional and contemporary applications while building critique and portfolio skills.
courses.description: Introduction to the fundamental techniques of oil-based intaglio printmaking. Traditional and contemporary applications and experimentation. Working independently on monoprints, small editions, and a multi-layer, multi-plate final project. Individual and group critiques throughout the semester.
learning_outcomes: Have a technical command of multiple introductory printmaking and graveur processes including; monoprinting, drypoint, acid etching, aquatint, and chine colle.
Attain the ability to mix and apply oil based inks as well as the ability to handle and apply asphaltum and rosin.
Attain an understanding of the chemical processes involved with acid etching.
Understand and obey the health and safety regulations associated with graveur printing.
Possess an awareness of the historic traditions of the medium.
Explore and develop personal concepts in creative expression.
Develop self-motivation, self-direction and a strong work ethic.
Have a completed portfolio of work that represents all assignments.

courses.name: History of Art II
courses.purpose: To provide students with a chronological understanding of the major developments in Western art from the Renaissance to the 21st century.
courses.description: A chronological survey of the history of art from the Renaissance period to the 21st century.

courses.name: History of Art
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the history of art through a chronological survey from the Stone Age to the Byzantine World.
courses.description: A chronological survey of the history of art from the Stone Age to the Byzantine World.
learning_outcomes: Analyze art and its history within the frame of cultural studies.
Evaluate the history of art with its problems relating to political, theoretical, social and technical issues, participate in discussions.
Assess art history as a discipline with the capability of interpreting and reading an artwork within its historical context.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: Prehistoric Art
Hafta 3: The Art of the Ancient Near East
Hafta 4: The Art of the Ancient Egypt
Hafta 5: Early Asian Art
Hafta 6: Art of the Ancient Greek and The Aegean
Hafta 7: Introduction to Etruscan and Roman Art and Discussion before midterm

Hafta 9: Hellenistic and Roman Painting and Mosaics; Roman Architecture; Roman Sculpture; Late Antique Art; The Beginnings of Christian Art
Hafta 10: Byzantine Art; Christian Art in Northern Europe and in Western Europe; Romanesque Art; Gothic Art
Hafta 11: Discussion
Hafta 12: Giotto; The Italian Renaissance; Van Eyck; Alberti; Uccello; Botticelli
Hafta 13: Bellini; Dürer; Humanism; Bosch; The High Renaissance; Mannerism
Hafta 14: Discussion
resources: Art History, Marilyn Stokstad, Michael W. Cothren, 2017, 6th Edition, Pearson
The Visual Arts: A History, Hugh Honour, John Fleming, 2013, 7th Edition, Laurence King Publishing

courses.name: Special Problems in Drawing
courses.purpose: Studies of the human body concerning form, color and value. The course aims to develop individual expression through the use of models to build thought and imagery, with an emphasis on contemporary figural expression.
courses.description: Studies of the human body concerning form, color and value. Exploration of individual expression by using models to build up thought and imagery. Contemporary figural expressions.

courses.name: Analysis of Artwork
courses.purpose: To introduce students to the critical analysis of works of art through major theoretical debates in art theory since the 19th century.
courses.description: Introduction to critical analysis of works of art by concentrating on the theoretical debates developed around various issues on art theory since the 19th century.

courses.name: Analysis of Art Work II
courses.purpose: Having dealt with the nature of painting, photography and cinema in the first part of this course, this course aims to examine various postmodern approaches to art and to foreground the impact of postmodern theory on the arts.
courses.description: Study of various postmodern approaches to art, such as semiotic, psychoanalytic, social and political approaches, in order to foreground the impact of postmodern theory on arts.

courses.name: Business and Legal Considerations for Technology Startups
courses.purpose: To provide general knowledge on the organizational and legal aspects of innovation-based technological ventures, including how start-up ventures are formed and financed.
courses.description: General knowledge on organizational and legal aspects of innovation-based technological ventures (IBTVs), formation and financing mechanisms of start-up ventures, selection, design and R & D of IBTVs product or business model, legal aspects of patent and IBTV.

courses.name: Applied Sustainable Business Development
courses.purpose: This course aims to build students’ understanding of how sustainability is integrated into business decision-making and implementation. It focuses on the concepts, tools, and organizational practices needed to develop and sustain responsible business strategies.
courses.description: Business terms, jargon and vocabulary used in sustainability. Sustainable business atmosphere. Global, social and environmental events. Public policy and market forces in sustainable business decisions. Triple bottom line accounting for making a sustainable business case. Environmental impact by calculating a carbon footprint. Decisions in sustainable business through environmental and social factors on investment. Opportunities for philanthropy organizations' marketing. "Green" office opportunities. Backcasting for sustainability goals and vision. Stakeholders for sustainable business. Approach to global problems and sustainable solutions.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate the understanding of basic business terms, jargon, and vocabulary used in sustainability
Showing the ability to connect sustainable business decisions nationally and internationally to public policy, legal requirements, and market forces
Demonstrate the knowledge and understanding of the sustainable business applications through triple bottom line accounting for making a sustainable business case
Demonstrate the understanding of sustainability goals and vision through backcasting
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Get to know each other. Why to take this course? Introduction to the course. How to use the book. Assessment methods and how to use class hours.
Hafta 2: The Business Case: Market Forces, Public policy and regulations, The top line and competitive advantage, The interface example. Exercise: Protecting your brand. Lessons Learned.
Hafta 3: Defining sustainability: Why are we doing this? Defining sustainability for your company, What does this mean for your employees? Lessons Learned.

Hafta 4: Understanding your stakeholder, what is material to them? Why you need stakeholder engagement? Materiality and process for determining it. Best practices and common delivery mechanism. Communicating it back to stakeholders. The goal of 5 touches. Lessons Learned.

Hafta 5: Baselining sustainability to measure what matters. Measure what matters. Be inclusive. Using the gap and SWOT analysis. Baselining support other business decisions. Managing your sustainability data. Lessons Learned.

Hafta 6: Goals visions and your sustainability North Star. The North Star. Integrate your goals. Goals need to corporate, departmental and individual. Set the right goals. Align KPIs and sustainability goals with corporate goals. Assign responsibility and accountability. Lessons learned.
Hafta 7: Management support is essential. People need to see, hear and feel leadership support. Budget. Dedicate time and resources. Prove it out, small wins build momentum. Lessons learned.

Hafta 8: Understanding change management to guide implementation. Changes though and nobody wants to change. The Hagen-Wilhemn change matrix. Understand your company. Understanding your people. Breaking down silo and coordinating across departments. Don’t rest on your Laurels. Engaging the sceptics. Lessons learned.
Hafta 9: Midterm-project.

Hafta 10: Engaging Employees around sustainability. Introduction to employee engagement. Sustainability and employee engagement. Train and make sustainability part of the job. Make it personal. Recognize different learning styles. Empowered employees. Intrapreneurship. Lessons learned.

Hafta 11: Systems decision making and internal alignment. Prioritize your actions. Incorporate in decision making. Incorporate into systems. Investment and procurement policies. Align with philanthropy and community with sustainability. Operationalizing your green team. Lessons learned.
Hafta 12: Institutionalizing sustainability. Job description and onboarding. Evaluation and aligning benefits. Reward recognition and compensation. Gamification. Lessons learned.

Hafta 13: Communicating sustainability internally. Continuous communication reinforces your message. Share both the good and bad. Align your communication efforts. Use social media internally. External reporting. Transparency build trust. Lessons learned.
Hafta 14: Final project. Project presentations. Poster presentations.
resources: Making Sustainability Stick: The Blueprint for Successful Implementation., Wilhelm, K., 2014, Pearson FT Press ISBN: 978-0133445572
Creating a sustainable organization. Approaches for enhancing corporate value through sustainability, Peter A. Soyka, 2012, Pearson

courses.name: Philosophy of Art I
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to major philosophical questions concerning art, including how art is created, interpreted, and viewed. It also encourages critical reflection on key issues such as mimesis, aesthetic judgment, and the relationship between art, beauty, and goodness.
courses.description: Philosophical issues that arise concerning the creation, interpretation and viewing of art, since Plato. What is "mimesis"; who is the best judge of art; is art beautiful and good; should art be viewed dis-interestedly.

courses.name: Design Tools and Techniques
courses.purpose: Foundations for visualization tools and technical skills in graphic design.

courses.name: Conceptual Design
courses.purpose: This course investigates how abstract concepts can be applied to concrete design products to create specific moods and associations in design.
courses.description: An investigation of the application of abstract concepts to concrete design products, i.e., packaging, advertising and printed material, as a vehicle to create specific moods and associations in design.

courses.name: Web Design
courses.purpose: Introduction to the world wide web and the stages of the web design process.
courses.description: Introduction to the world wide web and the stages of web design process through image editor, html editor and browser.

courses.name: Lettering and Introduction to Typography
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of lettering and typography. It aims to build an understanding of typographic history, letterform anatomy, and compositional hierarchy, while developing basic typographic skills through studio practice.
courses.description: Evolution and principles of typography including anatomy of letterform and compositional hierarchy with typographic elements.
learning_outcomes: Have knowledge about the history, evolution and principles of typography.

Know and practice the anatomy and forms of letters, compositional hierarchy and typeface identification and are able to use the basic typographic rules, reach at a level that they can generate creative visual solutions through the techniques that they have learnt.

Designing with Type, Craig, James, 1980, New York: Watson. Gutil

courses.name: Typography I
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to typographic design systems and the aesthetic, functional, and conceptual use of lettering. It develops an understanding of typography as both a visual and technical discipline in graphic design.
courses.description: Typographic design systems, the aesthetic functional and conceptual use of lettering for printing, typographic uses of various visual media and technical methods.
learning_outcomes: Applying the rules and guidelines of typographic practice that are presented in the texts to the process of typographic design.
Analyzing the relationship between thought and form through elements of typography and production techniques.
Establishing necessary skillset to work with formal, functional and technical qualities of type and arrangements.
Identifying typographic terminology with accuracy.

courses.name: Typography II
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' understanding of typographic design as both a technical and artistic discipline. It focuses on how typography communicates ideas, organizes information, and supports visual expression through layout, letterforms, and typographic systems.
courses.description: The course covers the artistic and technical problems of typographic design, typographic layout techniques, letterform design, and portfolio studies on logos and signs.
learning_outcomes: Presenting typography as a mode of transmission of thoughts, ideas, and facts by investigating different modes of typographic communications ranging from direct (obvious) to indirect (subtle).
To identify the abstract properties inherent in existing typographic forms in order to develop an exploratory attitude toward space and visual organization.
To develop an understanding of the inventive potential of typographic form as a way to intensify and expand content and meaning.
Developing research skills and synthesizing large amounts of typographic information into an organized, consistent format and creating diversity within that format.

courses.name: Computer Graphics for Film and Television I
courses.purpose: This course aims to expose students to real-life graphic problems and solutions in the TV and theatrical movie world, from commercial graphics to main title design for films. It also seeks to develop students' ability to apply their prior skills in typography, lettering, drawing, and time-based thinking to entertainment graphics.
courses.description: This course covers graphic design problems and solutions related to television and theatrical film production, including commercial graphics and main title design for movies. It emphasizes both conceptual exploration and technical aspects of the process, with attention to typography, lettering, drawing, and the sense of time. The course also introduces the idea of what is considered sellable and rich in entertainment graphics.
learning_outcomes: Understanding and building the message hierarchy in a commercial or trailer.
Getting used to the concepts of 'rich' and 'production value' in entertainment graphics.
Training the eyes to be able to judge what is sellable.

Motion graphic design & fine art animation : principles and practice, Krasner, Jon S., 2004

The history of motion graphics : from avant-garde to industry in the United States, Betancourt, Michael, 2013

courses.name: Graphic Design for Non-Majors
courses.purpose: This course introduces non-majors to the basic elements, processes, and fundamentals of graphic design. It aims to develop an understanding of how visual communication works through image and text in both analogue and digital media.
courses.description: Extensive studio work. Basic elements, processes and fundamentals of graphic design, ways of efficient communication with image and text in analogue and digital media.
learning_outcomes: Construction of design thinking process (introductory)
Construction of creative thinking
Creation of a graphic design project from sketch to output, including transmitting analog to digital.
Evaluate the sense of layout and design principals
Evaluate the sense of typography and type at large
Evaluate the sense of image and text
Construct the basic notion of technical issues of printing, publishing, medium, formats, and software skills
Analyze a piece of design regarding historical and contemporary references
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Welcome. Lecture: What is graphic design
Hafta 2: Lecture: Grid System + Layout Classwork 1. Portrait Print / + Adobe Photoshop Demo
Hafta 3: Lecture: Calligraphy + Contemporary Lettering Classwork 2. Calligraphy ABC
Hafta 4: Project 1. Digital Calligraphy / + Adobe Photoshop Demo
Hafta 5: Lecture: Crystal Goblet + Typography Project 2. Type in Info / + Adobe Illustrator Demo
Hafta 6: Project 2. Type in Info
Hafta 7: NO CLASS - Field Trip Exhibition Walkthrough + Coffee
Hafta 8: Lecture: Semiotics Classwork 3. Vector Illustration / + Adobe Illustrator Demo
Hafta 9: Project 3. Vector Portrait / + Adobe Illustrator Demo / Presentation. (3)
Hafta 10: Project 3. Vector Portrait / Presentation. (3)
Hafta 11: Lecture: Visual Metaphore Project 4. Visual Metaphor
Hafta 12: Project 4. Visual Metaphor/ Mind Map + Concept / Presentation. (3)
Hafta 13: Project 4. Visual Metaphor/ Sketching / Presentation. (3)
Hafta 14: Project 4. Visual Metaphor/ + Template Execution Demo / Presentation. (3)
resources: Graphic Design Theory: Readings from the Field, Edited by Helen Armstrong, 2009, Princeton Architectural Press
Design Elements : Understanding the Rules and Knowing When to Break Them., Samara, Timothy, 2014, Gloucester : Rockport Publishers
Graphic Design, Referenced : A Visual Guide to the Language..., Gomez Palacio, Bryony. Vit, Armin., 2009, Beverly, Mass: Rockport Publishers.
The Graphic Design Idea Book : Inspiration From 50 Masters. , Heller, Steven. Anderson, Gail., 2016, London: Laurence King Publishing.
Design Elements Typography Fundamentals : A Graphic Style Manual for Understanding..., Cullen, Kristin. , 2012, Beverly, MA : Rockport Publishers.

courses.name: Computer Graphics for Film and Television II
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to create motion-based graphic work for film and television. It focuses on animation methods and the preparation of sketch boards and storyboards as part of the production process.
courses.description: Methods of animation, preparation of sketch board and storyboard. Studies with application of various graphic elements in motion.

courses.name: Motion Graphics
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the field of motion graphics and its role in time-based visual communication. It aims to build foundational understanding of motion graphics history, categories, techniques, and applications.
courses.description: Introduction to motion graphics, including history, categories, techniques and applications of motion graphics and animation basics as well as design and composition, storyboarding, sound and music adjustment on time based media.

courses.name: Essentials of Photography
courses.purpose: Introduction to the basic principles and techniques of photography, with emphasis on darkroom techniques and processes.
courses.description: Introduction to basic principles and techniques of photography covering darkroom techniques and processes.
learning_outcomes: Understanding the nature of light and camera for design applications.
Learn how to control depth of field on sensors with different formats.
Learn how to work with natural and artificial light sources. Acquire the ability to determine and set appropriate lighting method.
Gaining knowledge about darkroom essentials for design. Experience developing various film and paper types.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to History of Photography and Camera Obscura.
Hafta 2: Principles of Light and Camera.
Hafta 3: Principles of Light and Camera Phase II.
Hafta 4: Measuring Tools and Exposure Methods.
Hafta 5: Measuring Tools and Exposure Methods Phase II.
Hafta 6: Understanding Depth of Field.
Hafta 7: Understanding Depth of Field Phase II.
Hafta 8: Introduction to Artificial Lights Sources and White Balance.
Hafta 9: Introduction to Artificial Lights Sources and White Balance Phase II.
Hafta 10: Introduction to Artificial Lights Sources and White Balance Phase III.
Hafta 11: Introduction to Darkroom.
Hafta 12: Darkroom Tools and Technologies for Design.
Hafta 13: Darkroom Tools and Technologies for Design Phase II.
Hafta 14: Evaluation of Darkroom Project.
resources: Recommended - Hardware: Digital SLR Camera with lens.
Digital camera with a lens - PC / mac - PP

courses.name: Photographic Image Processing I
courses.purpose: To introduce students to processing techniques for digitized still images and the use of digital darkroom methods with related computer software.
courses.description: Processing techniques of digitized still images, including digital darkroom methods by using related computer software.

courses.name: Photographic Image Processing II
courses.purpose: Development of projects for media and video.

courses.name: Introduction to Visual Techniques
courses.purpose: An introductory course on vector and raster based graphic fundamentals and their applications to various 2-D graphic design items.
courses.description: An introductory course on vector and raster based graphic fundamentals and their applications to various 2-D graphic design items.

courses.name: Typographic Design and Theories
courses.purpose: Focus on using type to effectively communicate ideas by instilling an awareness in the dynamics and the creative potential of the Typography discipline.
courses.description: Exploration of new forms of visual expressions by using typographical syntax and semantics.

courses.name: Advanced Photography
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to advanced photography techniques and the use of image processing tools for professional photographic production.
courses.description: Introduction to advanced photography techniques such as advertisement, still-life and architecture photography and teaching image processing tools.
learning_outcomes: Understanding the nature of digital and analogue tools and mastering skills for final production.
Learn how to control depth of field on sensors with different formats.
Learn how to work with artificial light sources for professional production. Acquire the ability to determine and set appropriate lighting tools.
Gaining knowledge about darkroom workflow. Controlling contrast, brightness, color and aesthetic.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Contrast and Color Cast.
Hafta 2: Visualizing Contrast in Darkroom.
Hafta 3: Introduction to Medium and Large Format Cameras.
Hafta 4: Working with B&W Contrast Filters.
Hafta 5: Processing B&W Film.
Hafta 6: Processing B&W Film Phase II.
Hafta 7: Color Perception and Color Correction.
Hafta 8: Introduction to Scanning Film Based and Printed Photograph.
Hafta 9: Introduction to Scanning Film Based and Printed Photograph Phase II.
Hafta 10: Advanced Studio Lighting Techniques and Methods.
Hafta 11: Advanced Studio Lighting Techniques and Methods Phase II.
Hafta 12: Professional Photographic Production.
Hafta 13: Professional Photographic Production Phase II.
Hafta 14: Modern Printing Technologies and Archival Materials.
resources: Recommended - Hardware: Digital SLR Camera with lens.

courses.name: Information Design and Data Visualization
courses.purpose: Studio course focusing on expanding students' understanding of how graphic design can become an effective tool for organizing and presenting complex data and information.

courses.name: Interface Design Studio
courses.purpose: The course aims for a multi-layered understanding of what an interface means by bridging the gap between its physical and virtual properties. Sign theory is central to the course; it is introduced as a framework to implement functional web interfaces.
courses.description: The course focuses on interface design through sign theory, Material Design principles, and the development of functional web interfaces. It covers UI components, responsive layouts, typography, navigation, visual hierarchy, and the practical construction of web pages and interface systems.
learning_outcomes: Successfully planning out a website by learning how to design a responsive landing page. This knowledge can be fully translated into user friendly design for startups and small business web pages.

Breaking down ubiquitous user interface components (such as card, button, header, etc...) from a critical standpoint using sign theory as a philosophical guide. Rethinking and questioning these already established systems and realizing original work.
Associating key user experience concepts such as efficiency, accessibility, navigation, aesthetic unity and credibility.
Handling screen typography effectively with a clear understanding of the limitations of web typography.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction

Hafta 2: Introduction: Interactive lecture on Sign Theory. Indexical, iconic, and symbolic signs. De-mystifying user interface terminology using Material Design. Project introduction. Reading: The Interface Experience - A User’s Guide

Hafta 3: UI Component: Surface & Hero Image. Concepts of background and foreground as material surfaces. Opaque / semi-opaque surfaces. Browser window as a dynamic surface; fullwidth surfaces vs. fixed-width surfaces. The concept of a button including full functionality from the analog button. Reading: Material Design; intro, environment, material properties, elevation, and shadows. Intro to HTML.
Hafta 4: UI Component: Card lecture and tutorial. Cards as basic and flexible layout elements. Intro to CSS. Reading: Material Design, Components - Cards.

Hafta 5: UI Component: Card lecture and tutorial. Cards as basic and flexible layout elements. Intro to CSS. Reading: Material Design, Components - Cards. Integrating cards into wireframes & grid systems. Lessons from swiss style graphic design & modern examples in the light of swiss style. Work-in-progress: card study assignment.
Hafta 6: UI Component: Flexbox. Process Check. Reading: By-Design, Alice Rawsthorn

Hafta 7: UI Component: Hero & Media Queries. Further Header (or “Hero”) development. The idea of different sections serving different “goals and elements” purposes. Lecture and tutorial about how headers draw in a user, provide context about the content and reinforce the brand identity. Reading: Material Design, imagery & color.
Hafta 8: UI Component: Tables and Navbar. Improvements in readablity and aesthetics in a schedule design. Tables as data structures. Reading: Material Design, Data Styles.

Hafta 9: UI Component: Mark(s) & Modal Box Design. In this course, we use the term mark as a generic name for various logos, symbols and icons that enhances user experience and reinforces brand identity. Modal (or dialog) windows provide extra content and UI controls. They retain focus until dismissed. Tutorial on Pure CSS Modal windows. Reading: Material Design, Icons.
Hafta 10: UI Component: Location. Creating a map that fits the visual unity and consistency of a web page using various mapmaking tools.
Hafta 11: Process Check.

Hafta 12: Term Project Checklist: A comprehensive overview of attention points that are covered earlier, including typography, divs, spacings, margins, paddings, image quality and other technicalities. Getting ready for the Final Presentation.
Hafta 13: Term project work in progress.
Hafta 14: Term project work in progress.
resources: PDF Packet
HTML & CSS Design and Build Websites, John Duckett
The Interface Experience - A User’s Guide
Material Design
By-Design, Alice Rawsthorn

courses.name: Motion Design Studio
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' understanding of motion design concepts and their effective application in motion graphics production. It also helps students build practical skills in time-based media through software-based animation workflows.
courses.description: This course focuses on the concepts of motion design, effective use of motion graphics through storyboarding, sequential aspects of composition, kinetic images and type, and animation basics with today's software usage techniques on time-based media principles.
learning_outcomes: Clarify the categories, techniques and applications of motion graphics, namely composition, story-boarding, animation basics, animating text and kinetic typography techniques, expressions, keyframing and audio synchronization on time based media.
Advancement of software usage skills namely the fundamentals of shape layer animation, working with vector and raster-based layers in motion graphics within the scope of product promotion.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Weekly Schedule, Course Content & Introduction to Requirements.
Hafta 2: Introduction to Adobe After Effects: The Interface & Workspace, Composition settings, Timeline panel, Layer properties, Keyframe. Project 01: Kinetic Typography. The due date is Week 06.
Hafta 3: Classwork: Motion path, Layer duration, Nesting & Pre-compose, Type tool, Mask. Work in progress: Project 01 - Kinetic Typography.
Hafta 4: Classwork: Parent & Link, Null object, Timing & Spacing. Work in progress: Project 01 - Kinetic Typography.

Hafta 5: Classwork: Alignment, Time ruler & Trimming, 2D layers in 3D space, Navigation in 3D views, Camera tools, Keyframing 2D layers in 3D space, Expressions. Work in progress: Project 01 - Kinetic Typography.
Hafta 6: Classwork: Shape layer, The fill and stroke properties, Changing path over time & Shape morphing. Project 02: Logo Reveal. The due date is Week 10.

Hafta 7: Classwork: Trim paths, Set first vertex, Linear & radial wipe, Mask reveal on shape path, Importing from AI to AE, Converting AI layers to shape layers in AE, Animate a fill through a closed object using stroke effect. Work in progress: Project 02 - Logo Reveal.
Hafta 8: Classwork: Layer styles, Blending options. Work in progress: Project 02 - Logo Reveal.
Hafta 9: Work in progress: Project 02 - Logo Reveal.
Hafta 10: Classwork: Video Assets in Motion Graphics. Working with raster-based layers and video assets in AE. Project 04: Animated Promo Video - Final Project. The due date is final week.
Hafta 11: Lecture: The process of storyboarding. Work in progress: Project 04 - Animated Promo Video - Final Project.
Hafta 12: Work in progress: Project 04 - Animated Promo Video - Final Project.
Hafta 13: Work in progress: Project 04 - Animated Promo Video - Final Project.
Hafta 14: Work in progress: Project 04 - Animated Promo Video - Final Project. Extra Classes.
resources: Motion Graphic Design Applied History and Aesthetics., Krasner, J., 2008, Amsterdam; Boston: Focal Press.
Creating motion graphics with After Effects., Meyer, T., Meyer, C., 2008, Amsterdam; Boston: Elsevier/Focal Press.
Motion graphic design & fine art animation : principles and practice., Krasner, J., 2004, Boston: Elsevier/Focal Press.
Creative motion graphic titling for film, video, and the web., Braha, Y., Bryne, B., 2010, Oxford: Focal.
The history of motion graphics : from avant-garde to industry in the United States., Betancourt, M., 2013, [Rockville, Md.]: Wildside Press.

courses.name: Photographic Practice
courses.purpose: This course is designed for non-majors to introduce the basic principles and techniques of photography, along with darkroom techniques and processes.
courses.description: The course covers the basic principles and techniques of photography, darkroom techniques and processes. It also includes camera and light principles, camera obscura, early photographic techniques and history, large format camera and movements, iconography and indexicality in photography, contemporary debates including photography and AI, and photographic art installation and documentation.
learning_outcomes: Understanding nature of light and operational system of camera.
Gaining knowledge about the history of photography and being able to interpret contemporary debates.
Acquire the ability to recognize the correct techniques and printing materials in preparation for the exhibition.
Knowing primary and secondary qualities of tools of the medium and distributing them in exhibition space.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Principles of Camera and Light.
Hafta 2: Camera Obscura.
Hafta 3: Early Techniques and Brief History of Photography.
Hafta 4: Early Techniques and Brief History of Photography Phase II.
Hafta 5: Introducing Large Format Camera and Movements.
Hafta 6: Introducing Large Format Camera and Movements Phase II.
Hafta 7: Large Format Camera in Contemporary Photography.
Hafta 8: Iconography and Indexicality in Photography.
Hafta 9: Iconography and Indexicality in Photography Phase II.
Hafta 10: Photography, AI and Contemporary Debates.
Hafta 11: Photography, AI and Contemporary Debates Phase II.
Hafta 12: Photographic Art Installation.
Hafta 13: Installation and Visual Documentation of Photographic Work.
Hafta 14: Installation and Visual Documentation of Photographic Work Phase II.
resources: Camera Lucida, Roland Barthes, 1980
Photography as Contemporary Art, Charlotte Cotton, 2009, Themes&Hudson
The Original Copy: Photography of Sculpture, 1839 to Today, MoMA
Picture Industry - A Provisional History of The Technical Image, 1844-2018, Luma

courses.name: Packaging
courses.purpose: This course focuses on the role of package design in brand identity and its function as a stylized form of functionalism. It aims to develop students' understanding of how packaging carries, protects, and presents a product while delivering the brand promise and enriching the user experience.
courses.description: Package design plays an important role in brand identity. The course examines packaging as a functional and visual communication medium, with attention to typography, illustration, color, material, and 2D and 3D structures.

courses.name: History of Graphic Art
courses.purpose: Introduction to the twentieth century graphic design by exploring various movements, philosophies and pioneering figures using a collaborative, thought-provoking format.

courses.name: Psychology of Advertisement
courses.purpose: An introduction to the psychological aspects of the current consumer market and their relation to cultural and sociological phenomena, with the aim of helping determine an effective policy in advertising.
courses.description: An introduction to the psychological aspects of the current consumer market. Its relation to cultural and sociological phenomena as an aid in determining an effective policy in advertising.

courses.name: Logos, Symbols and Signs
courses.purpose: Exploration of the languages and functions of logos, symbols and sign systems in visual communication.

courses.name: Design Issues
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to contemporary issues in graphic design and related professional contexts. It aims to help students develop awareness of the debates, challenges, and conditions they will encounter throughout their careers.
courses.description: A hybrid lecture/participation studio that introduces students to the contemporary issues they will encounter throughout their careers.

courses.name: Introduction to Video Production Techniques I
courses.description: Development of basic video production skills such as pre-production planning, lighting for video, shooting with a video camera and sound recording.

courses.name: Illustration I
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to interpret subjects visually through illustration. It also encourages creative thinking, personal expression, and the use of both traditional and digital tools.
courses.description: Ways of interpreting a subject through pictures. Black and white and color illustrations. Interpretation of graphic subjects through pictures.
learning_outcomes: Assess various concept and image generating techniques.
Develop illustrative skills using various tools and methods of illustration.
Development of personal visual style.
Initiate the skill of creative and lateral thinking.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction & Visual Thinking. Overview of course objectives, tools, expectations. What is Illustration? (functions, history, contemporary fields). Traditional Tools and Digital Tools.
Hafta 2: Drawing Foundations. Focus on line, mark-making & texture, form, proportion, gesture drawing. Expressing yourself in Drawing. Abstract composition using only line/texture.
Hafta 3: Value & Light. Shading, contrast, light sources, atmosphere. Tonal studies. Illustration with dramatic lighting.
Hafta 4: Composition & Visual Storytelling. Composition principles, rule of thirds, balance, focal point, negative space, storytelling through images. Re-illustrate a short fable in 3 panels.
Hafta 5: Color Theory for Illustration. Color psychology, palettes, harmony, mood. Create an illustration using a limited color palette.
Hafta 6: Medium Exploration I (Traditional). Experimentation with watercolor, colored pencil, or markers. Same subject illustrated in two different traditional mediums.
Hafta 7: Medium Exploration II (Digital). Basics of digital illustration software. Digital Collage.
Hafta 8: Character Design. Personality through shapes, silhouettes, expressions, poses. Design a unique character with 3 expressions and 3 poses.
Hafta 9: Illustration for Print & Editorial. Spot illustrations, magazine covers, visual metaphors. Create an editorial illustration for an article headline.
Hafta 10: Narrative & Sequential Art. Storyboarding, comics, visual pacing. 1–2 page short comic strip or storyboard sequence.
Hafta 11: Developing a Personal Style. Exploration of influences, experimenting with techniques, building consistency. Create a set of 3 related illustrations in your emerging style.
Hafta 12: Final Creative Personal Projects. Planning, sketches, feedback, revisions. Work on a self-directed final illustration project.
Hafta 13: Final Hybrid Illustration Experiment. Combine traditional and digital techniques. Explore mixed media approaches.
Hafta 14: Presentation. Write a short reflective statement and present work to the class. Participate in critique.

courses.name: Professional Practice
courses.purpose: Preparation of fourth-year students for a professional graphic design career.
courses.description: Working fields of graphic design, the design firm, design market in Turkey, design ethics, copyright and licensing, presentation of design work, and preparing a resume and portfolio.
learning_outcomes: Professionally aware of design related issues such as advertisement agency dynamics, copyright and labor rights, design ethics, game industry, freelance discussions, etc.
Have knowledge about what to face when in a professional environment.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Frame & Professional Practice Landscape
Hafta 2: Creative Entrepreneurship & Positioning Foundations
Hafta 3: Understanding The Job and Giving an Offer
Hafta 4: CV, Portfolio & LinkedIn as Positioning Kit
Hafta 5: Value-Based Communication & Client Insight
Hafta 6: Writing the Value Letter
Hafta 7: Designing Messages That Move People
Hafta 8: Digital Presence & One-Page Funnel
Hafta 9: Building the One-Page Funnel
Hafta 10: Portfolio System & Case Study Architecture
Hafta 11: Developing Case Studies & Visual Narrative
Hafta 12: AI in Creative Practice & Portfolio Refinement
Hafta 13: Offer Interview Prep & Final Portfolio Polish
Hafta 14: Roll-Up Lecture & Offer Interviews
resources: A Designer's Art, Paul Rand, 2016, Princeton Architectural Press
Show your work, Austin Kleon, 2014, Workman Publishing Company
How to Be a Graphic Designer Without Losing Your Soul, Adrian Shaughnessy, 2010, Princeton Architectural Press
Burn Your Portfolio: Stuff They Don't Teach You in Design School, Janda Michael, 2013, New Riders
The Graphic Designer's Guide to Portfolio Design, Debbie Rose Myers, 2013, Wiley

courses.name: Introduction to Research Skills
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop the essential academic skills needed for successful university study. It also introduces students to research-oriented thinking through engagement with a theme related to the art, architecture, and archaeology of the Mediterranean basin and the Near East.
courses.description: Examination of a theme concerning the art, architecture, and archaeology of the Mediterranean basin and the Near East, with emphasis on developing the skills essential for successful university study: reading scholarly works, taking notes, identifying research problems, writing papers (both short reports and longer research papers), giving oral presentations, and taking part in discussions. The topic examined will vary from year to year, depending on the interests of the instructor.

courses.name: Introduction to Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course provides background material essential for any student of archaeology.
courses.description: The course covers the history of archaeological research and excavation, major archaeological discoveries of past centuries, modern excavating, surveying and recording techniques, dating methods, identification of sites, and the topographical factors involved in locating ancient settlements.

courses.name: Illustration II
courses.purpose: To develop students' ability to realize and print an illustrated book project in a chosen format, such as a technical book, children's book, story book, poetry book, or an illustrated book in another field.
courses.description: Realization and printing either a technical book, children's book, story book, poetry book or illustration of a book written in another field.

courses.name: Ways of Seeing: Approaches to Art and Architectural History
courses.purpose: This course provides a thematic introduction to major works of art and architecture and to the techniques and methods used to analyze and interpret them. It aims to help students develop a broad visual and conceptual framework for understanding art and architectural history across different cultures and periods.
courses.description: A thematic introduction to great works of art and architecture, techniques of analysis, and methods of interpretation. Examples studied will be taken from cultures around the world, ancient, medieval, and modern, with the majority coming from the western tradition. Themes explored will include form, style, iconography, images of the sacred, architecture and ritual, politics and art, landscapes and natural settings, private architecture, and perspectives from sociology, Marxism, feminism, and semiotics.
learning_outcomes: Develop a visual repertoire of works of art and architecture from the Ancient Near East, Egypt, Greece, Rome, the Byzantine Empire, the Islamic world, India, China, and Japan.
Compare and contrast art and architectural works in a systematic way.
Grasp broad issues in art and architectural history.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course: preview of the themes to be covered. The basic terminology. What is ‘art’? Why study art history?
Hafta 2: What about prehistoric ‘art’? Prehistoric art in Europe and Anatolia: Forms and meanings of cave paintings and megalithic architecture in the light of social cultural changes - Chapter1
Hafta 3: What about prehistoric ‘art’? Prehistoric art in Europe and Anatolia: Forms and meanings of cave paintings and megalithic architecture in the light of social cultural changes - Chapter1.
Hafta 4: The Art of the Ancient Near East: Art as the mirror of prestige and power - Chapter2.
Hafta 5: The Art of the Ancient Near East: Art as the mirror of prestige and power - Chapter2.
Hafta 6: The Art of Ancient Egypt: Pictorial and architectural conventions and their changes during the Middle and New Kingdom - Chapter3.
Hafta 7: The Art of Ancient Egypt: Pictorial and architectural conventions and their changes during the Middle and New Kingdom - Chapter3.

Hafta 8: MIDTERM TEST (The date and time of the exam will be announced in due course) & Art of Ancient Greece and the Aegean World: Phenomena of the Bronze Age cultures, beginnings of the Greek civilization and the development of art and architecture between the Classical and Hellenistic Period - Chapter5

Hafta 9: Art of Ancient Greece and the Aegean World: Phenomena of the Bronze Age cultures, beginnings of the Greek civilization and the development of art and architecture between the Classical and Hellenistic Period-Chapter5.

Hafta 10: Art of Ancient Greece and the Aegean World: Phenomena of the Bronze Age cultures, beginnings of the Greek civilization and the development of art and architecture between the Classical and Hellenistic Period-Chapter5.
Hafta 11: Etruscan and Roman Art as the celebration of the human existence, building program of Roman government, portraiture and techniques - Chapter6.
Hafta 12: Etruscan and Roman Art as the celebration of the human existence, building program of Roman government, portraiture and techniques - Chapter6.
Hafta 13: Late Antiquity and Byzantine World. Transformation of the Roman Art and Architecture, illuminated manuscripts, icons and iconoclasm-Chapter7.
Hafta 14: Gothic Art and Architecture: Origins, characteristics and developments in France, England and Germanic Islands-Chapter11.
resources: Art, A Brief History, M. Stokstad and M. Cothren, 6th edition, Prentice Hall / Pearson
A World History of Art, Hugh Honour and John Fleming, Fourth Edition, Fleming-Honour Ltd.
Gardner's Art Through the Ages, Fred Kleiner et al., Tenth Edition, Harcourt Brace College Publishers

courses.name: Archaeological Illustration
courses.purpose: This course provides students with the basic principles and practical skills of archaeological illustration, from the elementary field level, for record purposes, leading up to drawings made to final publication standard.
courses.description: The coursework includes preparing and finishing drawings of the usual range of artefacts recovered during an excavation, such as pottery, metalwork, stonework, and modified animal bone.

courses.name: Human Evolution and World Prehistory
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the major stages and debates in human prehistory, helping them understand how humans evolved and how early societies developed across different regions of the world.
courses.description: This course is a survey of the human prehistory of Africa, Europe and Asia from the first hominids to the Mesolithic period, with particular emphasis on morphological evolution, environment and cultural developments.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate a general understanding of the principles and mechanisms of evolution as a theorem.
Develop an understanding of the evolution of human physical and cognitive abilities, technical skills, artistic expression and imagination.
Master the ability to summarize and synthesize the information provided in readings, lectures, and through assigned work.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to course goals and evaluation method; review of syllabus and course material. Fundamental question: WHO ARE WE, AND HOW DID WE GET HERE?
Hafta 2: Darwin's ghosts - evolutionary thought before Darwin; 19th century revolutions and revelations; 20th century and beyond: The Hand of Man; genes in the space age.
Hafta 3: Defining hominines (bipedalism, manual dexterity, behaviour) African environmental conditions at 6mya
Hafta 4: Earliest hominines (Sahelanthropus, Orrorin and Ardipithecus) impact on current thought about early hominine evolution.
Hafta 5: The gracile Australopithecine genera
Hafta 6: 3 million years ago – Climate and Transition; gracile extinctions; emergence of genus Homo
Hafta 7: Homo ergaster and Homo erectus: the emergence of the modern body form
Hafta 8: Review and mid-term
Hafta 9: Homo ergaster and Homo erectus: the first human radiation Multiregional continuity and Population and/or Genetic Replacement models.
Hafta 10: Emergence of Homo sapiens (Homo heidelbergensis [Archaic Homo sapiens] Homo neanderthalensis) gradualism and hybridization
Hafta 11: Homo neanderthalensis, Denisova hominins, and their relationship with modern humans
Hafta 12: Homo sapiens: the emergence of modern anatomy and of modern behaviour
Hafta 13: "The biggest mistake in the history of humankind" - homo sapiens, the end of the Pleistocene, and the beginning of famine and epidemics
Hafta 14: Where do we go from here? Humankind, the anthropocene, or: is Covid19 here to stay?
resources: CARTA symposia, 2010-2019

courses.name: Fieldwork Methodologies in Archaeology
courses.purpose: The course aims at providing students with the fundamental tools to navigate their first experiences in archaeological fieldwork, both in survey and excavation contexts. It is designed to build the conceptual background and practical awareness needed for fieldwork research and daily project activities.
courses.description: The course covers the fundamentals of archaeological fieldwork in both survey and excavation contexts. It introduces the conceptual background of fieldwork research, the technical aspects of daily project activities, and the use of digital approaches to data documentation and analysis.
learning_outcomes: Identify surveying and planning tools, equipments, and methods used in the documentation of archaeological field work.
Master the correct and efficient implementation of the tools, equipments and methods used in the documentation of archaeological field work.
Prepare survey drawings and plans that comply with field standards by utilizing the tools, equipments and methods introduced in the course.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction
Hafta 2: The history of archaeological fieldwork - how we ended up here
Hafta 3: What is an archaeological site?
Hafta 4: Archaeological survey - archaeology without digging 1: remote sensing
Hafta 5: Archaeological survey - archaeology without digging 2: Field survey
Hafta 6: Archaeological survey - archaeology without digging 3: data processing and interpretation
Hafta 7: MIDTERM: Archaeological survey write-up
Hafta 8: Excavation - the practicalities of running and recording an excavation
Hafta 9: Manual recording of archaeological sites 1
Hafta 10: Manual recording of archaeological sites 2
Hafta 11: Recording three-dimensional space - surveying in the 21st century
Hafta 12: The photographic site record, on the ground and from the air
Hafta 13: After the excavation / survey - processing what is found
Hafta 14: Final presentations
resources: Field Archaology: An INtroduction, Peter Drewett, 2011/second, Routledge

courses.name: Introduction to Classical Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course introduces the major aspects of the material cultures of Ancient Greece and Rome and situates them within their historical development and scholarly study.
courses.description: This course surveys major aspects of the materials cultures of Ancient Greece and Rome, beginning with the Iron Age of Greece and concluding with the decline of the Western Roman Empire. It covers their architecture, arts, economic and social development in urban and rural settings, as well as the ways scholars have approached the field over its history. Equal weight is given to the development of Classical Greece, its Hellenistic expansion, and the Roman domination of the Mediterranean.

courses.name: Roman Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the origins and development of Roman art and architecture within their archaeological context, and to show how these forms functioned in Roman society, especially as political propaganda.
courses.description: This course examines the origins and development of Roman art and architecture in its archaeological setting from the Etruscan period through to the reign of the emperor Constantine. The course focuses on the evidence from Rome and Italy, but also looks at the impact and influence of Rome on local art and architecture in the various provinces of the Roman Empire. Throughout the course an emphasis is placed on understanding the role of art and architecture in the Roman World in general, and in particular its use as a form of political propaganda.
learning_outcomes: Analyse and explain a specific example of art or architecture from the Etruscan or Roman periods in its present form
Analyse a specific example of art or architecture from the Etruscan or Roman periods in its historical and/or cultural context

Describe and assess in their historical and cultural context a selection of example of art and architecture from the Etruscan and Romans as discussed in class and in the required and in the recommended course readings
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction: Italy - the land & Italy before Rome
Hafta 2: The Art and Archaeology of the Etruscans
Hafta 3: The Origins and Early History of Rome
Hafta 4: Republican Rome
Hafta 5: Republican Rome - 2
Hafta 6: The Age of Augustus
Hafta 7: Rome under the Early Julio-Claudian Emperors
Hafta 8: Midterm
Hafta 9: The Roman World of the Flavians
Hafta 10: The Roman World under Trajan and Hadrian
Hafta 11: Roman Art and Archaeology under the Antonines
Hafta 12: Roman Art and Archaeology during the Third Century
Hafta 13: The Roman World from Diocletian to Constantine
Hafta 14: Conclusion
resources: Roman Art, Nancy H. Ramage, Andrew Ramage, 2015 (6th Edition), Pearson
A History of Roman Art, Steven L. Tuck, 2015, Wiley-Blackwell
A Companion to the Archaeology of the Roman Republic, Jane DeRose Evans, 2013, Wiley-Blackwell
A Companion to the Archaeology of the Roman Empire (2 vols.), B. Burrell, 2024, Wiley-Blackwell
Imperial Rome and Christian Triumph, Jas Elsner, 1998, OUP

courses.name: Mythologies of the Ancient Mediterranean World
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the mythologies of the Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and Greco-Roman civilizations from a comparative and cultural perspective.
courses.description: The course surveys major myths about gods, goddesses, heroes, and heroines, and examines the role these myths played in the societies that created them. Lectures are supplemented by visual material and ancient written sources.

courses.name: Anatolia I-Hunters,Farmers,Merchants in Prehistoric Anatolia
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the pre- and early historic archaeological heritage of Turkey and helps them understand major developments from the Early Neolithic to the advanced Iron Age. It also aims to familiarize students with key sites, archaeological evidence, and broader patterns of human interaction in ancient Anatolia.
courses.description: An introduction to the pre- and early historic archaeological heritage of Turkey, from the Early Neolithic (ca. 10.000 BC) to the advanced Iron Age (ca. 500 BC). Besides iconic places like Gobekli Tepe, Troia, Hattusa and Gordion, also less well known arenas of human action and interaction are surveyed.
learning_outcomes: Examine the material culture of ancient Anatolia, from the point of view of history and archaeology.
Utilize material evidence to address questions in Anatolian history and archaeology.
Critically evaluate and synthesize the information from class lectures and weekly readings in written assignments and exams.
Critically evaluate and synthesize information given in class lectures and in the assigned scholarly articles in written and oral assessments.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction – discussion of syllabus – Deconstructing the “Anatolia is a bridge from East to West”-paradigm
Hafta 2: History of exploration – adventurous travellers, Ottoman pioneers, early Republican scholars, contemporary researchers
Hafta 3: The Walking Dead I – early genus homo in Western Asia
Hafta 4: The Walking Dead II – surveying Middle and Upper Palaeolithic findspots in Turkey
Hafta 5: The Epipalaeolithic/ Early Holocene transition – The Revolution is called off: different life- and foodways in the Near East around 10.000 BC
Hafta 6: Circling the Square – the early Holocene in Southeast Turkey: Göbekli Tepe, Karahan Tepe, Hallan Çemi, Gre Filla and their contemporaries
Hafta 7: Squaring the Circle – later Pre-Pottery Neolithic sites in Upper Mesopotamia (Çayönü, Nevalı Çori, Cafer Höyük and their peers)
Hafta 8: MIDTERM I
Hafta 9: Go West! The earlier and later Neolithic in Central and Western Turkey (Boncuklu Höyük, Çatalhöyük and their contemporaries)
Hafta 10: The Later Neolithic/ Chalcolithic in Central and Western Turkey: Mobile-sedentary oscillation, and Cannibal Corpse(s) at Gedikkaya
Hafta 11: The Waking Dread – Late Chalcolithic Upper Mesopotamia, Anatolia and the Black Sea littoral – Migrants, Warfare, Metals, and Human Sacrifice
Hafta 12: The Rise of Merchandise I: The Early Bronze Age in Western Turkey and the Aegean
Hafta 13: The Rise of Merchandise II: Early Bronze Age Central Anatolia and the Great Caravan Route
Hafta 14: The Rise of Merchandise III – Crisis and Collapse? Late 3rd millennium developments in Ancient Turkey
resources: Ancient Turkey, Sagona, A. & Zimansky, P., 2009, Routledge
The Oxford Handbook of Ancient Anatolia, Steadman, R.S. & McMahon, G. , 2011, Oxford University Press
Various readings shared as pdf's

courses.name: Great Discoveries from the Ancient World
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the origins and development of archaeology through the study of major discoveries from the ancient world. It also helps students understand how archaeological evidence and scientific methods contribute to interpreting the past.
courses.description: The course examines how archaeology emerged as a discipline and how it has developed to the present day by analyzing a series of major discoveries, from famous examples such as Troy and Tutankhamun's tomb to less well-known cases such as the Hoxne hand-axes and the grave of Richard III. It also presents the wide range of scientific methods used in archaeology today.
learning_outcomes: Explain how archaeology has changed our understanding of the past.
Discuss research advances in archaeological techniques and knowledge about the past.
Assess the value of archaeology in social terms.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: What is archaeology?
Hafta 2: History of Archaeology I: A playground for the wealthy; History of Archaeology II: Archaeology, colonialism and nationalism

Hafta 3: History of Archaeology III: Archaeology and history - deciphering languages; Modern Archaeology as Science I: Digging Up the Past; Modern Archaeology as Science II: Beyond the Excavated Trenches; Presentations
Hafta 4: Modern Archaeology as Science III - Dating in Archaeology; Modern Archaeology as Science IV - Interpreting the past; Lucy and the beginning of the human story; Presentations
Hafta 5: The Java Man and the early ancestors of humanity; Altamira and the discovery of ancient creativity; Quiz 1; Presentations
Hafta 6: Göbeklitepe and the beginnings of ritual life; Çatalhöyük and the beginnings of modern lifestyles; Presentations
Hafta 7: The cold case of Ötzi, the Ice Man; Small circles, big circles – Sacred British landscapes; Presentations
Hafta 8: The Pyramids of Egypt: aliens or smart architects?; At the gates of Troy; Presentations
Hafta 9: Holiday
Hafta 10: Hattusa, an imperial capital; Presentations; Midterm
Hafta 11: Ulu Burun, a window into ancient maritime trade; At the court of the Mycenaean kings; Knossos between myth and reality; Presentations
Hafta 12: The Amarna Letters and the First Age of Diplomacy; Tutankhamun - the most famous discovery ever?; The Nineveh reliefs and Assyrian politics of fear; Presentations
Hafta 13: Crystallized in the past - the Hallstatt salt mines; Bog Bodies - sacrificing humans in Northern Europe; The Emperor’s eternal guards; Presentations
Hafta 14: Antikythera - the oldest known computer; Under the ashes - Pompeii and the Vesuvian eruption of 79 CE; Presentations; Quiz 2
Hafta 15: Sutton Hoo - the King in the boat; Richard III - the King in a parking lot; Custer’s ‘Last Stand’ – nationalist mythmaking in the American West; Presentations
Hafta 16: North America before the European invasion; Templo Major - Aztecs, colonists and the ‘discovery’ of new worlds
resources: The Story of Archaeology, J. Pollard, 2007

courses.name: From Villages to Empires in Ancient Mesopotamia
courses.purpose: The goal of the course is to provide students with a long-term perspective on the change and continuity of Mesopotamian societies between the late Epipalaeolithic and the Iron Age (ca 11000-300 BCE).
courses.description: Topics include fundamental themes of Near Eastern archaeology such as sedentism, animal and plant domestication, power ideology, urbanization, state formation, and social stratification, as well as key archaeological sites, material culture and technology.

courses.name: Greek Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course surveys major aspects of the material culture of Ancient Greece, from the Iron Age through the Roman Empire, with attention to architecture, the arts, and economic and social development in both urban and rural settings.
courses.description: The course covers the material culture of Ancient Greece, beginning with the Iron Age and concluding with the Roman Empire. It includes architecture, the arts, and the economic and social development of Greek society in urban and rural contexts, along with new interpretations in light of recent discoveries.

courses.name: Cultural Anthropology
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the field of cultural anthropology and its central questions. It aims to develop an understanding of how anthropologists study culture, social life, and cultural change through ethnographic and analytical approaches.
courses.description: An introduction to cultural anthropology. Topics examined include the concept of culture; ethnographic research, its history, methods, and theory; language and communication; social identity, personality, and gender; subsistence patterns; economic systems; sex, marriage, and family; kinship and descent; grouping by age, common interest, and social status; political systems; religion and spirituality; the arts; and processes of cultural change.
learning_outcomes: Summarize the contents of a scholarly article, evaluating the key points of its argumentation.
Apply techniques of anthropological analysis to present an original example of culture change in Turkey, based on interviews etc.
Demonstrate mastery of terms, concepts, and ethnographic examples, and their broader anthropological significance.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the Course
Hafta 2: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
Hafta 3: Research Methods in Anthropology
Hafta 4: Core concepts: Culture
Hafta 5: Core Concepts: Race and Ethnicity
Hafta 6: Religion and Rituals
Hafta 7: Kinship, Family, Social Organization
Hafta 8: Gender and Sexuality
Hafta 9: Midterm Exam and also Language and Communication
Hafta 10: Politics, Power, Violence
Hafta 11: Politics, Power, Violence contd.
Hafta 12: Economic Systems and Exchange
Hafta 13: Applied Anthropology: Cultural Heritage
Hafta 14: Anarchist Archaeology
resources: Cultural Anthropology: The Human Challenge, Haviland, W. A., Prins, H. E. L., McBride, B., and Walrath, D., 2017/15th Ed., Cengage Learning
TBA in class

courses.name: Latin I
courses.purpose: Introduction to Latin for beginning students. The course aims to build a foundation in Latin grammar and reading.
courses.description: The course will emphasize both grammar and reading.

courses.name: Ancient Greek II
courses.purpose: This course continues the development of Ancient Greek language skills begun in Ancient Greek I. It aims to strengthen students' command of grammar and vocabulary so they can read and translate Greek texts with increasing accuracy.
courses.description: Continuation of the grammar and vocabulary acquisition started in Ancient Greek I.
learning_outcomes: Can analyze a Greek text in order to demonstrate mastery of the intricacies of Ancient Greek syntax.
Can recognize the vocabulary and grammar of Ancient Greek and can translate texts.
Takes a proactive part in the learning process by analyzing the grammar and syntax of Ancient Greek.
On midterm and final examinations, can recognize the vocabulary and grammar of Ancient Greek and can translate texts.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Athenaze, lesson 10
Hafta 2: Athenaze, lesson 10 cont'd; lesson 11
Hafta 3: Athenaze, lesson 12
Hafta 4: Athenaze, lesson 13
Hafta 5: Athenaze, lesson 13 cont'd; lesson 14
Hafta 6: Athenaze, lesson 14 cont'd
Hafta 7: Athenaze, lesson 15
Hafta 8: Athenaze, lesson 15 cont'd; lesson 16
Hafta 9: Athenaze, lesson 16 cont'd; mid-term (Friday, 3 April)
Hafta 10: Athenaze, lesson 17
Hafta 11: Athenaze, lesson 17 cont'd; lesson 18
Hafta 12: Athenaze, lesson 18 cont'd; lesson 19
Hafta 13: Athenaze, lesson 19 cont'd
Hafta 14: Athenaze, lesson 20
resources: Athenaze, book 1, Maurice Balme & Gilbert Lawall, 2003, Oxford University Press
Athenaze, book II, Maurice Balme & Gilbert Lawall, 2003, Oxford University Press
Oxford Grammar of Classical Greek, James Morwood, 2001, Oxford University Press
Pocket Oxford Classical Greek Dictionary, James Morwood & John Taylor, 2002, Oxford University Press

courses.name: Ancient Greek I
courses.purpose: Introduction to ancient Greek for beginning students.
courses.description: The course will emphasize vocabulary acquisition, grammar, and reading.

courses.name: Latin II
courses.purpose: This course completes the grammar and vocabulary acquisition begun in Latin I.
courses.description: Completion of the grammar and vocabulary acquisition begun in Latin I.
learning_outcomes: Recognize the etymology of vocabulary items.
Demonstrate knowledge of the elements of Latin taught in class.
Analyze word formation and the use of vocabulary in the Latin language.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: I-stem nouns of the Third Declension, Ablatives of Means, Accompaniment, Manner XIV
Hafta 2: Numerals and third declension adjectives (Wheelock's Latin Caput XV-XVI)
Hafta 3: The relative pronoun (WL XVII)
Hafta 4: First and second conjugations: present system passive; ablative of agent (WL XVIII)
Hafta 5: Perfect passive system; interrogative pronouns and adjectives (WL XIX)
Hafta 6: Fourth declension; ablatives of place from which and separation (WL XX); Third and fourth conjugations: present system passive (WL XXI)
Hafta 7: Fifth declension; ablative of place where and summary of ablative uses (WL XXII)
Hafta 8: Review and MIDTERM
Hafta 9: Participles (WL XXIII)
Hafta 10: Ablative absolute; passive peripherastic; dative of agent (WL XXIV)
Hafta 11: Infinitives; indirect statement (WL XXV)
Hafta 12: Comparison of adjectives; ablative of comparison; irregular comparison of adjectives (WL XXVI)
Hafta 13: Subjunctive mood; present subjunctive; Jussive and purpose clauses (WL XXVII)
Hafta 14: Imperfect subjunctive; present and imperfect subjunctive of sum and possum; result clauses (WL XVIII)
resources: Wheelock's Latin 7th Edition, Frederic M. Wheelock & Richard A. LaFleur, 7th Edition/ 2011, USA
Workbook for Wheelock's Latin 3rd Revised Edition, Paul T. Comeau and Richard A. LaFleur, 2000, Harper Collins

courses.name: Greek Sculpture
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with an understanding of the development of Greek sculpture and to introduce them to current research, new interpretations of images, and methods of interpretation.
courses.description: The course concentrates on the development of Greek sculpture, with special emphasis on the state of research, new interpretations of images, and the methodology of interpretation.
learning_outcomes: Develop a visual repertoire of Greek sculpture.
Summarize the contents of scholarly articles and evaluate the key points of their argumentation.
Write a short research paper on a problem in the description and interpretation of Greek sculpture.
Demonstrate mastery of the forms, styles, techniques, functions, and social significance of Greek sculpture.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction and early marbles
Hafta 2: Kouroi and Korai
Hafta 3: Sanctuary votives
Hafta 4: Funerary memorials
Hafta 5: Sculpture for temples and treasuries
Hafta 6: Late Archaic, 525-480 BC
Hafta 7: 'Classical’ statue culture and the early fifth-century revolution
