Hafta 8: Athletes, gods, and heroes, 480-430 BC
Hafta 9: Olympia, Temple of Zeus
Hafta 10: Parthenon
Hafta 11: Athens, 430–400 BC, and the Nike of Olympia
Hafta 12: Eastern dynasts: Lycia, Caria, Phoenicia
Hafta 13: Athletic and divine statuary, 400-300 BC
Hafta 14: The good citizen: portraits and grave stelai
resources: Greek Sculpture: The Archaic Period, J. Boardman, 1991, Thames & Hudson
Greek Architecture and its Sculpture in the British Museum, I. Jenkins, 2000, London
Handbook of Greek Sculpture, O. Palagia, 2019, Berlin
A History of Greek Art, M. Robertson, 1975, Cambridge
Greek Sculpture: An Exploration, A.F. Stewart, 1990, New Haven

courses.name: Byzantine and Islamic Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide a broad understanding of the art, architecture, and archaeology of the Mediterranean and Near East from the time of Constantine the Great to the 15th century. It focuses on the historical development and cultural context of Late Roman, Byzantine, and early Islamic artistic traditions.
courses.description: A survey of art, architecture, and archaeology of the Mediterranean and Near East in the medieval and early modern periods, from Constantine the Great to the 15th century. Focus will be on the art and architecture of the Late Roman and Byzantine empires in Italy and the eastern Mediterranean basin and of early Islamic states.

courses.name: Hellenistic and Roman Sculpture
courses.purpose: This course provides an analytical survey of Hellenistic and Roman sculpture from the time of Alexander the Great to that of Constantine the Great. It aims to help students understand the development of sculptural techniques, materials, subjects, and styles across this period.
courses.description: An analytical survey of Hellenistic and Roman sculpture from the time of Alexander the Great to that of Constantine the Great (ca. 300 BC-AD 300). The course examines the techniques and materials sculptors used during the period, the range of topics they depicted from portraiture to historical narratives, and the stylistic changes and approaches made in this time span.

courses.name: Monumental Painting
courses.description: The arts of wall painting and mosaics in the Greek world and Italy from the Archaic period to the third century AD.

courses.name: Introduction to Digital Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course introduces the role of digital tools and methods in archaeology. It aims to provide students with a broad understanding of how digital approaches are used to represent, analyze, and interpret the non-digital past.
courses.description: Digital is omnipresent in all aspects of our lives and so it is also in all the archaeological disciplines. This course offers an overview of digital tools and methods used for the representation of our non-digital past, including basics of data organization and analyses, 3D modelling and virtual reality.

courses.name: GIS Applications in Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course provides an in-depth conceptual and practical introduction to Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for archaeological research and fieldwork.
courses.description: The course covers the use of GIS to digitally record excavation and survey projects, analyze remote sensing data such as satellite, drone, and geophysical data, and study human-environment interaction, relations between communities, and intra-site distribution of artefacts. It also introduces digitalization of data, mapping tools, and analytical tools for research applications.

courses.name: European Prehistory
courses.purpose: To provide an overview of European prehistory from the time of archaic humans to the first millennium BC.
courses.description: Survey of European prehistory, from the time of Archaic humans to the first millennium BC. Paleolithic and Mesolithic cultures; Neolithic and Bronze Age cultures.

courses.name: Greek Architecture
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the development of Greek architecture from ca. 700 BC to the 1st century BC, with emphasis on major architectural orders and building traditions.
courses.description: A survey of Greek building from ca. 700 BC to the 1st century BC. The Greek architectural tradition and its historical development will be emphasized (the history and nature of Doric and Ionic orders and of "Aeolic" and Corinthian styles). Materials, techniques and procedures of construction will also be covered. Sanctuary architecture provides the core material of the course but military, funerary, and ceremonial monuments will also be considered.

courses.name: Greek Vase Painting
courses.description: A survey of Greek vase painting from the Geometric period (ca. 9th century BC) to the 4th century BC with special attention to Attic vase painting of the Archaic and Classical periods.

courses.name: Roman Architecture
courses.description: A survey of Roman architecture from its origins in the Iron Age to the Constantinian period (ca. 500 BC - AD 300), the course examines in detail the materials and methods used by Roman architects and the buildings they made in Rome and Italy and in the provinces of the Roman Empire.

courses.name: Archaeology of Troy
courses.purpose: A broad-based investigation into the problem of the origin and development of the Homeric city of Troy.
courses.description: The written and archaeological sources and the extent and the limitations of the data will be emphasized. The class will discuss the relationships between epic, history and archaeological evidence.

courses.name: Archaeology of Syria and Palestine
courses.purpose: To introduce the archaeology of the Levant and its relationship with surrounding cultures from the beginning of urban civilization to ca. 1200 BC.
courses.description: The archaeology of the Levant and its relationship with surrounding cultures from the beginning of urban civilization to ca. 1200 BC.

courses.name: The Aegean Bronze Age
courses.purpose: To introduce the prehistoric cultures of the Aegean area, with emphasis on major Bronze Age civilizations and their archaeological significance.
courses.description: The prehistoric cultures of the Aegean area, concentrating on Minoan Crete, Troy, the Aegean Islands, and Mycenaean Greece.

courses.name: Egyptian Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the art and archaeology of ancient Egypt and examines how major monuments and artistic forms developed over time.
courses.description: A survey of the art and archaeology of ancient Egypt, from the Predynastic Period to the end of the New Kingdom (4000-1100 BC). The course will emphasize major monuments of architecture, sculpture, relief and painting. Questions of stylistic change and historical context will be considered, as well as cultural relations with neighboring civilizations.

courses.name: Ancient Greek IV
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to read and interpret Ancient Greek texts at an advanced level. It also introduces students to epigraphy as a field of study.
courses.description: Selections from Greek literature, including works by Homer, Hesiod, Xenophon, Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides, Plato, Herodotus, and Thucydides. The course also includes an introduction to epigraphy.

courses.name: Anatolia II- The World of Hittites
courses.purpose: The course aims to provide an impressionistic understanding of the Hittite world in Anatolia between ca. 1950 BCE and 700 BCE. It also seeks to connect major historical events with archaeological, historical, and philological evidence in order to reconstruct the Hittite past.
courses.description: The course examines the deeds of important historical figures and their relations with neighboring and often adversary states such as Mycenae, Egypt, Mitanni, and Babylon. It discusses events including the Battle of Qadesh, the Trojan War, the Sea People, and the collapse of the Hittite Empire. It also combines archaeological, historical, and philological research to reconstruct daily life in the Hittite kingdom, from craft and farming to medicine and religious festivals.
learning_outcomes: Develop a visual repertoire and analytical competence in Hittite material culture, including architecture, ceramics, small finds, and art.
Master and appropriately use key terminology and concepts in Hittite archaeology and history, including those derived from ancient textual sources.
Demonstrate an understanding of the main phases and trajectory of Hittite history, from its Middle Bronze Age foundations through the Late Bronze Age and into the post-Hittite Iron Age.
Assess major archaeological and historical issues relevant to the Hittite world within the broader context of Anatolian archaeology.
Demonstrate skills in bibliographical research and critical evaluation of archaeological and historical data related to the Hittites.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course. Hittite scholarship, chronology, and sources.
Hafta 2: Who were the Hittites? Anatolian landscapes and Late Bronze Age societies.
Hafta 3: Hittite political geography. Before the Hittite Kingdom – the Early Bronze Age.
Hafta 4: The Old Assyrian Trade period. The trade center of Kültepe/Kanesh.
Hafta 5: Other major Middle Bronze Age centers. The dawn of the Hittite State.
Hafta 6: The Land of Thousand Gods: religion, magic and ritual between archaeology and texts, ca 2500-1200 BCE. Hittite cult festivals.
Hafta 7: Imperial beginnings. Language and multilingualism in Late Bronze Age Anatolia.
Hafta 8: Introduction to essay writing. Midterm exam.
Hafta 9: Hattusa, an imperial capital. Hittite administration.
Hafta 10: Late Bronze Age ceramics: production, empire and identity. Major Hittite centers.
Hafta 11: At center stage: the Hittite Empire and international diplomacy. Late Bronze Age metallurgy.
Hafta 12: The role of women in Hittite society. Monuments in stone: royal ideology and propaganda in the Hittite world.
Hafta 13: A clash of kings: the Tarhuntassa affair. The Sea People: Collapse and transformation.
Hafta 14: Continuing Hittite legacies: the Kingdom of Carchemish in the Early/Middle Iron Age. Phrygia and Tabal.
resources: Insights into Hittite History and Archaeology, H. Genz and D.-P. Mielke (eds.), 2011, Peeters
selected readings
The Kingdom of the Hittites, Bryce, T., 2006, Oxford University Press
Hittites: An Anatolian Empire / Hititler: Bir Anadolu İmparatorluğu, Alparslan, M.D & Alparslan, M., 2012, Yapı Kredi Yayınları
Hattusha Guide: A Day in the Hittite Capital, Seeher, J., 2005, Ege Yayınları
Hititler ve Hitit İmparatorluğu 1000 Tanrılı Halk/Die Hethiter und Ihr Reich Das Volk Der 1000Götter, Jacob, W., 2002, Kunst - und Ausstellungshalle

courses.name: Monuments of Babylon
courses.purpose: This course examines major aspects of the ancient city of Babylon, one of the most important urban centers of the ancient world. It aims to balance written sources and archaeological evidence in understanding the city.
courses.description: Babylon, known as the Gate of the Gods, was by 600 BC the ancient world's largest city and home to two of its ancient wonders. The course focuses on Babylon's urban plan, palaces, temples, museums, housing, and lifestyle, drawing on Greek historical accounts, the Old Testament, Babylonian texts, and archaeological excavations.

courses.name: Hittite Grammar I
courses.purpose: Hittite Grammar I introduces students to the grammar of Hittite, the oldest attested Indo-European language.
courses.description: Students will learn to read uncomplicated Hittite sentences and passages in transliteration and become familiar with the research tools of the field.

courses.name: Latin III
courses.purpose: This course aims to complete the grammar points of Latin and prepare students to read original texts. It also introduces students to epigraphy.
courses.description: Completion of the grammar points of the Latin language and an introduction to original texts. Also, an introduction to Epigraphy.

courses.name: Daily Life in Ancient Greece
courses.purpose: This course examines the daily life of ancient Greeks across the life cycle and throughout the day, highlighting variations by gender, age, and social class.
courses.description: The daily life of ancient Greeks is examined, from birth and childhood to death, from morning to evening into night, with differences according to gender, age, and social class. Houses and other buildings, sculpture, vase painting, mosaics, and miscellaneous objects from houses and tombs provide the evidence for this look at ancient Greek society.

courses.name: Monuments of Athens
courses.purpose: To introduce students to the monuments of Athens and their development from the Archaic period through the Hellenistic and Roman periods, with attention to stylistic change and historical-cultural context.
courses.description: The monuments of Athens from the Archaic period through the Hellenistic and Roman periods, considering stylistic developments and historical and cultural context.

courses.name: Monuments of Rome
courses.purpose: To provide a detailed study of the main monuments of ancient Rome from its origins to the early Christian period, with attention to their characteristics and their role and function in everyday life.
courses.description: A detailed study of the main monuments of ancient Rome from its origins to the early Christian period (ca 500 BC - AD 500), with an emphasis on their characteristics and their role and function in everyday life.

courses.name: Introduction to Akkadian
courses.purpose: Introduction to Akkadian for beginning students. It is designed to provide an entry point into the language for students with no previous foreign language knowledge, especially those interested in Bronze Age Mesopotamia and Anatolia.
courses.description: The course introduces Akkadian to beginning students. It is particularly recommended for archaeology students whose interests lie in Bronze Age Mesopotamia and Anatolia.

courses.name: Latin IV
courses.purpose: To develop students' ability to read Latin with greater fluency and accuracy through sustained reading of Roman literary texts.
courses.description: Readings and discussions of many of the works of Roman literature. Emphasis will be on correct translation of the Latin, with attention to genre and narrative technique, and to building facility in reading Latin. Selections from writers such as Plautus, Vergil, Cicero, Caesar, Lucretius, Petronius and Ovid will be read in the original.

courses.name: Ancient Mesoamerican Civilizations
courses.description: A survey of the civilizations of Mesoamerica from earliest human settlement to the Spanish conquest, with emphasis on the art and archaeology of the great states: Olmec, Teotihuacan, Maya, Toltec, and Aztec.

courses.name: Ancient Cities
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the development of urbanism from the Neolithic period onward and to help them understand the historical significance of ancient cities in different civilizations.
courses.description: A study of urban developments from the Neolithic period onwards, covering in particular cities of the Near East, Egypt, the Aegean, Greece and the Roman world.

courses.name: The Buried Cities of Campania
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide a detailed understanding of Pompeii and Herculaneum as key archaeological and historical sites in Campania. It also seeks to help students interpret how these cities illuminate art, architecture, and everyday life in the Republican and early Imperial periods.
courses.description: This course provides a detailed analysis of Pompeii and Herculaneum, two Italian cities in the Campania region of Italy buried by the volcanic eruption of Mount Vesuvius in AD 79. Re-discovered in the 18th century, excavations at both places have provided much evidence regarding the art and architecture and the everyday life of the region in the Republican and early Imperial periods. The first part of the course will examine the history of both cities up to the time of the eruption in AD 79 and will introduce you to their public and private architecture. The second part will examine in more detail what these sites tell us about everyday life there at the time of the eruption, and in particular the ways in which the residents lived, worked and played, which will allow you to better conceptualise patterns of life in the two places in the period between c. 150 BC - AD 79.

courses.name: Hittite I
courses.purpose: An introduction to Hittite, the oldest attested Indo-European language.
courses.description: Overview of the discovery of the Hittite language and civilization, and presentation of the basics of Hittite grammar and cuneiform script.

courses.name: Hittite III
courses.purpose: This course will offer a survey of the Hittite textual corpus.
courses.description: Students will read and translate selected historical and religious texts.

courses.name: Hittite II
courses.purpose: This course aims to build students' competence in reading, transliterating, and translating Hittite texts. It also introduces the grammatical and orthographic foundations needed for working with Hittite cuneiform and related research tools.
courses.description: A detailed survey of Hittite grammar and orthography. Beginner level excerpts from Hittite texts will be read and analyzed.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate ability to read and transliterate Hittite cuneiform.
Demonstrate ability to read and translate Hittite texts into English.
Demonstrate familiarity with the research tools of the field, such as grammars, dictionaries, sign-lists, and online databases.
Demonstrate ability to recognize Hittite vocabulary and grammar on quizzes and exams.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: EH Lesson 7
Hafta 2: EH Lesson 7
Hafta 3: EH Lesson 8
Hafta 4: EH Lesson 8
Hafta 5: EH Lesson 9
Hafta 6: EH Lesson 10
Hafta 7: Readings from selected Hittite texts
Hafta 8: Review and MIDTERM
Hafta 9: Readings from selected Hittite texts
Hafta 10: Readings from selected Hittite texts
Hafta 11: Readings from selected Hittite texts
Hafta 12: Readings from selected Hittite texts
Hafta 13: Readings from selected Hittite texts
Hafta 14: Readings from selected Hittite texts
resources: Elements of Hittite, Theo van den Hout, 2011, Cambridge University Press
Hethitisches Zeichenlexicon, Christel Rüster and Erich Neu, 1989, Harrassowitz Verlag

courses.name: Iron Age Sculpture of the Near East
courses.purpose: This course focuses on the major sculptural groups of the Anatolians, Assyrians, Neo-Babylonians, and Persians.
courses.description: Attention is focused on the major sculptural groups of the Anatolians, Assyrians, Neo-Babylonians and Persians.

courses.name: Archaeology of Phoenicia
courses.purpose: To provide a detailed survey of Phoenician art and archaeology within its historical and economic context, with special attention to the Phoenicians' interactions with the wider Mediterranean and Near Eastern world.
courses.description: Detailed survey of Phoenician art and archaeology in its historical and economic context. Particular emphasis is placed on the influences and relationships, especially in the fields of trade and art, between the Phoenicians and the rest of the Mediterranean and Near Eastern world.

courses.name: Greek Sanctuaries
courses.purpose: This course examines the religious, political and social uses of one of the most important institutions of the Greek world.
courses.description: This course examines the religious, political and social uses of one of the most important institutions of the Greek world, together with the architecture, sculpture, pottery and offerings found in sanctuaries.

courses.name: Museum Practices and the Preservation of Cultural Heritage
courses.purpose: To introduce students to the various aspects of museum work and the role of museums in preserving cultural heritage. The course emphasizes core museum management principles and professional practices.
courses.description: Study of various aspects of museum work. Management principles, cataloging and care of art objects, exhibitions and acquisitions, administrative procedures, and museum architecture will be emphasized. Specialist lecturers and visits to museums and their facilities.

courses.name: Readings in Prehistory
courses.purpose: Senior seminar on current debates in prehistory.
courses.description: Senior seminar on current debates in prehistory, with topic to be set by the instructor.

courses.name: Religion and Society in the Ancient Near East
courses.purpose: This course examines how religion functioned in the ancient Near East and how it shaped social life in major ancient civilizations.
courses.description: This course will examine formal and private religion practiced by the ancient civilizations of Mesopotamia, the eastern Mediterranean, Anatolia and Egypt. Topics to be covered include religious settings (temples, shrines and outdoor cult places), iconography referring to deities and cults, and ancient texts that explain cult practices and religious beliefs. Lectures, class discussions and student presentations.

courses.name: Anatolia III-Life in Greek and Roman Anatolia (Classical Anatolia)
courses.purpose: This course examines the archaeological remains of Classical Anatolia to understand the region's changing social and economic systems between roughly 600 BC and AD 300. It aims to show how architecture and site development reflect prosperity and historical change in Greek and Roman Anatolia.
courses.description: The course focuses on major and lesser-known sites in Anatolia, including Priene, Pergamum, Ephesus, Patara, Oinoanda, and Ankara. It takes a chronological approach to explore what archaeological and architectural remains reveal about social and economic developments in the Classical period.

courses.name: Special Topics in Art, Architecture and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course provides specialized teaching on a specific topic in art, architecture and archaeology. Each semester, the topic is selected by the instructor to emphasize a particular theoretical approach and/or regional and chronological focus.
courses.description: The course covers specialized topics in art, architecture, and archaeology chosen each semester by the instructor. It is designed to highlight a particular theoretical approach and/or regional and chronological focus, and to address contemporary issues in the field.
learning_outcomes: Train students in contemporary issues in the field of art, architecture, and archaeology.
Provide venues for interdisciplinary thinking.

courses.name: Ancient Technologies and Materials
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a general understanding of the contribution of the natural sciences, mainly chemistry and physics, to the enhancement of traditional archaeological methodologies. It also introduces the role of ancient technological innovations and engineering advances in contemporary materials science.
courses.description: The course covers scientific approaches used in archaeology, including 14C-dating, dendrochronology, sediment analysis, X-ray analysis, lead isotope analysis, spectral analysis, laser-Raman spectroscopy, and FTIR spectroscopy. The second part of the course focuses on ancient technological innovations, engineering advances, and their contribution to materials science.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate mastery of scientific dating and analysis techniques in archaeological research.
Demonstrate knowledge of crucial intellectual and technological innovations in World Archaeology.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course
Hafta 2: Science in Archaeology
Hafta 3: Isotopes – palaeoenvironment reconstruction
Hafta 4: Isotopes - diet
Hafta 5: Isotopes - mobility
Hafta 6: Dating
Hafta 7: aDNA
Hafta 8: Organic residue analysis
Hafta 9: Oral Presentations
Hafta 10: Proteomics
Hafta 11: Enamel peptide analysis
Hafta 12: ZooMS
Hafta 13: Use-wear analysis
Hafta 14: Soil and sediment micromorphology

courses.name: Readings in Near Eastern Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course will investigate the most recent issues and scholarship on a topic in the field of Near Eastern art and archaeology.
courses.description: Subject to be announced in the schedule of classes.

courses.name: The Archaeology of Cyprus in the Bronze Age
courses.purpose: This course introduces the richly textured cultures of Cyprus during the Bronze Age and explains how the island's resources and strategic location shaped interaction with neighboring regions.
courses.description: The course examines Cypriot archaeological culture, social organization, technology, and maritime economy during the Bronze Age (ca. 3500-1100 BC). It is taught through class lectures, discussions, and student presentations.

courses.name: The Eastern Roman Provinces
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide a detailed, regionally focused understanding of the historical and architectural development of Anatolia and the Levant under Roman control, from the earliest period to the beginning of Late Antiquity. It also seeks to situate settlements and their architecture within their broader political, military, economic, and cultural contexts.
courses.description: A detailed and regionally focussed study of the history and architecture of Anatolia and the Levantine zone in the period the relevant areas came under the control of Rome from the earliest days to the beginning of Late Antiquity (ca AD 300-400). Settlements and their architecture will be examined in their political, military, economic and cultural context.

courses.name: Selected Topics in Anatolian Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course provides a focused seminar on selected specialized topics in Anatolian Art and Archaeology. It is designed to deepen students' understanding of specific cultural periods, regions, or issues through readings, reports, and a research paper.
courses.description: Weekly 3-hour seminar with assigned readings, reports and a research paper on a specialized topic to be selected by the instructor. It will cover specific cultural periods, regions or issues in Anatolian Art and Archaeology.

courses.name: Neolithic and Chalcolithic Periods in Anatolia
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the major prehistoric cultural developments in Anatolia, with particular emphasis on the transition from aceramic Neolithic to ceramic Neolithic and Chalcolithic societies.
courses.description: An examination of the aceramic Neolithic cultures of Southeast Anatolia and the ceramic Neolithic cultures of the South and Southwest Anatolian plateau and their Chalcolithic successors.

courses.name: Archaeological Method and Theory
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the analytical approaches that are fundamental to archaeology and to help students understand the major interpretive models that have shaped archaeological practice since the discipline emerged in the 19th century.
courses.description: This course discusses analytical approaches that are fundamental to the discipline of archaeology. It focuses on the sequence of major interpretive models that have directed archaeological practice since its emergence as a scientific discipline in the 19th century.
learning_outcomes: Master the ability to identify and define technical terminology and concepts in archaeological research.
Grasp and discuss broad issues in archaeological research and analytical paradigms.
Discern theoretical perspectives embedded in archaeological discourse, and evaluate their impact.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course
Hafta 2: History of the Discipline
Hafta 3: Culture-Historical Approach
Hafta 4: Processual Archaeology
Hafta 5: Post-Processual Archaeology
Hafta 6: Current Trends in Archaeological Thought I
Hafta 7: Current Trends in Archaeological Thought II
Hafta 8: Oral presentations
Hafta 9: Documenting Place
Hafta 10: Documenting Time I
Hafta 11: Documenting Time II
Hafta 12: Documenting Humans I
Hafta 13: Documenting Humans II
Hafta 14: Conclusive remarks
resources: Archaeology The Key Concepts, C. Renfrew and P. Bahn, editors, 2005, Routledge
A History of Archaeological Thought, B.G. Trigger, 2006/2nd, Cambridge UP
Various readings, on reserve and listed in syllabus

courses.name: Landscape Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course offers an in-depth introduction to a major branch of today's archaeology that involves the investigation of ancient landscapes and the dynamics of human-environment interaction across time.
courses.description: Topics include discussion regarding the Anthropocene, resilience theory, political ecology, human-driven environmental and climatic change, sensory archaeology, palaeoclimatology and geoarchaeology, remote sensing and GIS-led landscape analysis.

courses.name: Readings in Anatolian Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course is designed to investigate current issues and scholarship in Anatolian art and archaeology. It aims to familiarize students with recent academic debates and research approaches in the field.
courses.description: The course covers recent issues and scholarship on a topic in Anatolian art and archaeology, with the specific subject announced in the class schedule. It is organized as a lecture course with weekly readings and discussion of selected monuments, regions, and scholarly problems.
learning_outcomes: Discuss information presented in the lectures and readings.
Present in well-organized fashion an analysis of scholarly articles.
Analyze a research problem in the light of information learned from lectures, presentations, and readings.
Apply information learned in lectures and readings to specific questions, using a critical approach.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: overview, question, methods, materials
Hafta 2: Sardis and Persepolis: Lydians and Persians
Hafta 3: Xanthos: Lion Tomb and Harpy Monument
Hafta 4: Hellespontine Phrygia: Polyxena Sarcophagus and Çan Sarcophagus
Hafta 5: Xanthos: Nereid Monument
Hafta 6: Limyra: Monument of Perikle
Hafta 7: Trysa and Xanthos: Merehi and Payava Sarcophagi
Hafta 8: Caria: Mausolea at Halikarnassos and Mylasa
Hafta 9: Sidon: Abdalonymos Sarcophagus and others
Hafta 10: Pergamon: Great Altar
Hafta 11: Kommagene: Nemrud Dağ and Monuments of Antiochos I
Hafta 12: Ephesos and Aphrodisias: Late Hellenistic monuments
Hafta 13: Sebasteion at Aphrodisias and Parthian Monument at Ephesos
Hafta 14: Celsus’ Library and Carian Gergakome
resources: The Lydians and Their World, N. Cahill, 2010
City Reliefs of Lycia, Childs, W.A.P., 1978
Art in the Hellenistic Age, Pollitt, J.J., 1986
The marble reliefs from the Julio-Claudian Sebasteion, Smith, R.R.R., 2013

courses.name: Readings in Greek Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course will investigate the most recent issues and scholarship on a topic in the field of Greek art and archaeology.
courses.description: Subject to be announced in the schedule of classes.

courses.name: Palaeolithic Archaeology in Eurasia and the Near East
courses.purpose: This seminar is designed to make students familiar with prominent finds and features of the Eurasian and Near Eastern Palaeolithic.
courses.description: The course covers the science of the most ancient cultural period of our ancestors (ca. 1,000,000 - 10,000 BCE), focusing on the dynamic development of archaeology and new insights into the dawn of humankind, its dispersal, and biological and socio-cultural evolution. Topics include lithic and bone working technologies, artwork, and the archaeology of caves and open-air shelters, with special attention to the contribution of neighboring sciences such as geology, geochemistry, palaeozoology, palaeoanthropology, and genetics.

courses.name: Introduction to Sumerology
courses.purpose: The course introduces students to the study of Sumerian language, documents, history, and culture. It also aims to provide a broader understanding of the significance of Sumerology within related fields and the role of the Sumerians in the history of civilizations.
courses.description: A history of the discovery of the Sumerian civilization is followed by an overview of Sumerian history and the main features of Sumerian society. The course familiarizes students with the corpus of Sumerian texts in the fields of literature, mythology, religion, economy, and political history. The significance of Sumerology for other fields of study, especially Assyriology and Hititology, is explained. The question of Sumerian origins is explored alongside an assessment of the role of the Sumerians in the history of civilizations.

courses.name: Readings in Byzantine Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course will investigate the most recent issues and scholarship on a topic in the field of Byzantine art and archaeology.
courses.description: Subject to be announced in the schedule of classes.

courses.name: Neo-Assyrian Art and Archaeology
courses.description: The Neo-Assyrian Empire (10th-7th century BC) represents a high moment in the long history of Ancient Near Eastern civilizations. This course covers notable aspects of its architecture, arts and settlement systems as preserved in the archaeological record.

courses.name: Supervised Study
courses.purpose: Independent research under the supervision of a faculty member whose special competence coincides with the area of a student's interest.
courses.description: Independent research under the supervision of a faculty member whose special competence coincides with the area of a student's interest. Consent of the supervising faculty member and of the major advisor is required.

courses.name: Readings from Near Eastern Texts
courses.purpose: This course will focus on the translation of Near Eastern texts.
courses.description: This course will focus on the translation of Near Eastern texts.
prerequisites: Knowledge of Akkadian will be a prerequisite.

courses.name: Readings in Roman Art and Archaeology
courses.purpose: This course will investigate the most recent issues and scholarship on a topic in the field of Roman art and archaeology.
courses.description: Subject to be announced in the schedule of classes.

courses.name: Readings From Greek/Latin Texts
courses.purpose: This course will focus on selected readings from original sources.
courses.description: Selected readings from original Greek and Latin texts.

courses.name: Readings From Greek/Latin Texts II
courses.purpose: To enable students to read ancient Greek or Latin authors in their original language and deepen their ability to work with authentic classical texts.
courses.description: Reading ancient Greek or Latin authors in their original language. This course requires familiarity with the ancient language beyond the department's 2-year ancient language curriculum.

courses.name: Classical Arabic II
courses.description: Basic grammar of Classical Arabic. Irregular grammatical forms. Reading of simple religious and legal texts.

courses.name: Readings in Classical Archaeology
courses.purpose: This graduate tutorial is designed to develop students' ability to engage critically with scholarly literature in Classical Archaeology. It also supports discussion-based learning around a topic selected by the instructor.
courses.description: Graduate tutorial in Classical Archaeology, on a topic to be chosen by the instructor.
learning_outcomes: Present in well-organized fashion an analysis of scholarly articles
Analyze a research problem in the light of information learned from class discussions and from readings.
Apply information learned in class discussions and from readings to the analysis of specific questions.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Hellenistic art: visual cultures from Macedonia to Bactria, 330-30 BC
Hafta 2: Introduction
Hafta 3: Alexander Mosaic
Hafta 4: Macedonia & Hellenistic kingship
Hafta 5: Greek cities
Hafta 6: Seleukid Syria & Babylonia
Hafta 7: Seleukid & Parthian Iran
Hafta 8: Bactria & India
Hafta 9: Attalid Pergamon
Hafta 10: The Great Altar
Hafta 11: Ptolemaic Egypt
Hafta 12: Ptolemaic Alexandria
Hafta 13: Hellenistic coins
Hafta 14: Delos to Rome
Hafta 15: First century BC, east & west
resources: The Hellenistic World from Alexander to the Roman Conquest, M.M. Austin, 2nd ed. 2006, Cambridge
The architectural development of the Greek stoa, J.J. Coulton, 1977, Oxford
A Companion to the Hellenistic World, A. Erskine (ed.), 2003, Oxford
Brill’s Companion to Ancient Macedon, R.J. Lane Fox, 2011, Leiden
Art in the Hellenistic Age, J.J. Polit, 1986, Cambridge
Hellenistic Sculpture, R.R.R. Smith, 1991, London
The Hellenistic Age: A very short introduction, P. Thonemann, 2018, Oxford
From Samarkhand to Sardis, S. Sherwin White & A. Kuhrt, 1993, London

courses.name: Advanced Classical Arabic I
courses.description: Reading and grammatical interpretation of Classical Arabic texts.

courses.name: Advanced Classical Arabic II
courses.description: Reading and grammatical interpretation of Classical Arabic texts.

courses.name: Ottoman Turkish and Paleography I
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the Arabic script and the basics of Ottoman grammar, preparing them to read and work with Ottoman Turkish sources.
courses.description: Course on Arabic script and Ottoman grammar.

courses.name: Classical Arabic I
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the basic grammar of Classical Arabic and to build a foundation for reading and understanding simple texts.
courses.description: Basic grammar of Classical Arabic. The main tenses.

courses.name: Ottoman Turkish and Paleography II
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to read and interpret Ottoman Turkish texts written in Arabic script. It also strengthens their understanding of Ottoman grammar and vocabulary needed for more advanced historical texts.
courses.description: Course on Arabic script and Ottoman grammar. Reading exercises on printed Ottoman texts.
learning_outcomes: Achieve an improved level of reading and interpretation of Ottoman texts based on the use of printed text in Ottoman script.
Acquire an improved level of understanding of Ottoman grammar and vocabulary required for Ottoman texts of more advanced content.
Recognize certain Persian and Arabic elements in Ottoman grammar and vocabulary to enhance reading and interpretation skills.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: Derivative Triliteral Infinitives I: İf'âl and Tef’îl
Hafta 3: Derivative Triliteral Infinitives II: Tefe''ül and Tefâ‘ül
Hafta 4: Derivative Triliteral Infinitives III: Müfâ‘ale and İnfi‘âl
Hafta 5: Derivative Triliteral Infinitives IV: İfti‘âl, İf ‘ilâl and İstif ‘âl
Hafta 6: Arabic Prepositions
Hafta 7: Arabic Prepositions
Hafta 8: Midterm
Hafta 9: Arabic Pronouns and the Ottoman Transcription Alphabet
Hafta 10: Persian prefixes and suffixes
Hafta 11: Persian verbs used in word construction
Hafta 12: Persian verbs used in word construction
Hafta 13: Reading Rika in Printed Texts
Hafta 14: Reading Rika in Printed Texts

courses.name: Ottoman History: 1300-1600
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the classical period of the Ottoman Empire and to help them understand the main political, economic, and religious structures of the empire.
courses.description: Classical period of the Ottoman Empire. Economic, political and religious institutions. Ottoman economic system. Relations with its European neighbors.

courses.name: Ottoman History: 1600-1914
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a broad understanding of the major political, economic, and ideological developments that shaped the Ottoman Empire from the seventeenth century to the First World War. It also seeks to help students interpret the causes and consequences of Ottoman decline, reform, and transformation.
courses.description: Ottoman Empire in decline. Political, economic and ideological developments at the capital as well as in the provinces. Nationalism among non-Muslims and Muslims. Administrative and political reforms during the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.
learning_outcomes: Discuss major events and problems during this period that led to the decline and collapse of the Ottoman Empire.
Acquire a general understanding of the political, economic and ideological developments in the capital as well as in the provinces.
Analyse certain issues such as nationalism among Muslims and non-Muslims, and administrative and political reforms, based on primary and secondary sources.
Present arguments in written and oral forms.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: the 17th / 18th / 19th Centuries: Historiographical questions and periodization
Hafta 2: The “Classical” Age (1300-1600), an overview: the “Classical” Age and the Ottoman Society and Economy during the “classical” period
Hafta 3: The 17th century Crisis and Transformations: Military and Fiscal Transformation and the Agrarian crisis and the rebellions
Hafta 4: The decline and political changes: The myth of decline and political crisis and changes, the Second Empire

Hafta 5: The late 17th century and the turn of the 18th century: The political situation right after the Treaty of Karlowitz (1699) and the Administrative decentralization and tax-farming (iltizam/malikane)
Hafta 6: The 18th century and the age of ayans: the 18th century, a historiographical perspective and the challenge of the periphery, the ayans
Hafta 7: The New Order (Nizam-ı Cedid)
Hafta 8: The Tanzimat Era
Hafta 9: Emergence of Nationalism
Hafta 10: The Hamidian Period
Hafta 11: The Second Constitutional Era and Ittihat ve Terakki
Hafta 12: The End of the Empires: The Balkan Wars and the First World War
Hafta 13: Revisiting the 19th century transformations I: Modernization, Modernization and the question of Secularism and Modernization of the State
Hafta 14: Revisiting the 19th century transformations II: Nations, Imagined Communities and the Invention of Traditions, Nationalism in the Balkans

courses.name: Medieval British History
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the formation of the medieval English state from its Anglo-Saxon beginnings to the 14th century and to examine the major political, social, and governmental developments of the period.
courses.description: Formation of the medieval English state from its Anglo-Saxon beginnings to the 14th century, tracing the developments in central and local government, its politics, social structure and its interaction with the rest of the British Isles and the Continent.

courses.name: Medieval Europe (500-1500)
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide an overview of the history of Western Europe from the fall of the Roman Empire to the Renaissance, focusing on the major political, social, and religious transformations of the medieval period.
courses.description: This course traces the history of western Europe from the fall of the Roman Empire to the Renaissance, and deals with the main political, social and religious changes during that period.

courses.name: Byzantine History II: 1025-1453
courses.purpose: This course aims to examine the major political, social, economic, and cultural developments of the Byzantine Empire from 1025 to 1453. It focuses on the empire’s transformation, its relations with neighboring powers, and the factors that led to the fall of Constantinople.
courses.description: The course covers the deterioration of the Byzantine land regime, feudalization, the penetration of Turks into Anatolia and its impact on Byzantium, the Latin conquest of Constantinople, relations between Byzantium and Anatolian Turkish principalities, and the conquest of Constantinople. It also addresses the Komnenian and Palaiologan periods, the Fourth Crusade, successor states, and the rise of the Ottomans.
learning_outcomes: Discuss recurrent themes of this period — namely deterioration of the Byzantine land regime, feudalization, the settlement of the Turks in Anatolia, the Latin conquest of Constantinople and the fall of Constantinople.
Analyse interactions between the Byzantine Empire and Turkish principalities.
Compose a research essay based on primary and secondary sources and introduce the study in an oral presentation.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course. Wrap up session: what is Byzantium? For a history of the Byzantine empire between the 4th and the 9th century.

Hafta 2: Byzantium and its neighbors; the Land and Peoples in and beyond Byzantium. Byzantium and the Balkans; Bulgaria and Russia; Byzantium and the Arabs (the Empire strikes back); a New Aristocracy on the Rampage.
Hafta 3: Byzantine Court Ceremonies and Ottonian Period. A) Byzantine Economy. B) Imperial Ceremonies: Constantine VII’ De Caerimoniis and Liutprand ‘ Embassy. C) Ottonian Age and Ottonian Italy.

Hafta 4: Basil II and the 11th century crisis of The Byzantine Empire; The Normans; the Byzantine Church. 1) Basil II, "The Bulgar-Slayer". 2) Eleventh Century crisis. 3) 1054 A.D.: the Great Schism. 4) The Normans from West to East.
Hafta 5: Failing Emperors.
Hafta 6: Changes in government under the Komnenoi; Alexios I and the Alexiad: a cosmopolitan society. A) The Komnenian Era and the West. B) Anne Komnene and the Alexiad. C) A Cosmopolitan society.
Hafta 7: John II Komnenos and Byzantine Women.
Hafta 8: MID TERM EXAMINATION and ASSESSMENT (in class).
Hafta 9: Venice and Byzantium: the Fourth Crusade. 1) Venice: the raise and raise of a maritime republic. 2) Fourth Crusade: the Fall of Constantinople.

Hafta 10: The Successor States and the Recovery of Constantinople (1261); Byzantine Cyprus. A) The Towers of Trebizond, Arta, Nicaea and Thessaloniki. B) Mistra, Piero Della Francesca, Byzantium and the Western Medieval world in the thirteen and fourteenth century. C) Cyprus, from Byzantine to the Arabs and back again; Crusader Cyprus.
Hafta 11: The Palaiologoi: from Re-conquest to Civil War (1261-1355); the Rise of the Ottomans.
Hafta 12: The Siege and the Fall of Constantinople (1453).
Hafta 13: Special Seminar: The Turks in Anatolia.
Hafta 14: Special Seminar: Venice and the Trukic Maritime Beyliks.
resources: A History of Byzantium, T. Gregory, 2010, Blackwell

courses.name: Byzantine History I: 324-1025
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the formation and development of the Eastern Roman Empire and the major political, institutional, cultural, and religious transformations of the Byzantine world.
courses.description: Foundation of the Eastern Roman Empire, development of Byzantine institutions, relations of the Byzantine Empire with the East and West. Cultural and religious developments.

courses.name: Modern Europe (1453-1914)
courses.purpose: The course seeks to deal, selectively, with the historiography of major themes in the political, social, intellectual, and religious history of Europe from the Renaissance to the eve of the emergence of the national state system characteristic of the twentieth century.
courses.description: This course examines major themes in the political, social, intellectual, and religious history of Europe from the Renaissance to the early twentieth century. It focuses on the historiography of modern Europe, including the development of modernity, the Reformation, Enlightenment, revolution, state formation, industrialization, and imperial change.
learning_outcomes: Acquire an outline knowledge of the main developments in modern European history
Define pivotal historiographical terms such as 'Renaissance', 'Reformation', 'Enlightenment', 'Absolutism', 'Modernity' etc
Critically analyse relevant literature
Present and discuss in written and oral forms key questions relating to modern European history
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: General Introduction with Presentation of Course Program and Assessments
Hafta 2: Introduction: The Peculiarity of (European) Modernity, or, “What Number to Dial.”
Hafta 3: Rediscovering the Myth of Europe Part I: Mediterranean Civilization after the Conquest of Constantinople – Ending the Christian Fairytale of Unity
Hafta 4: Rediscovering the Myth of Europe Part II: Converting the Mission of Crusade into Voyages – A New History of the Age of Exploration(s)
Hafta 5: The Shadow of Modernity: Machiavelli, the Reformation, and the Changing Currents in European Political Thought
Hafta 6: Confession and War: Redefining Sovereignty through the “Grand Tragedy of Europe” – A Reassessment of the Thirty Years’ War
Hafta 7: Old Government, Part II [Spring Break Special]: The Squadron of Political Consciousness: A Story between Sweden, France, and Rome
Hafta 8: Old Government, Part III: The Cosmos of Europe in Louis XIV’s Versailles and the Organization of the Ancien Régime
Hafta 9: The Blaze of Modernity? – Part I: England and the Liberal Imagination
Hafta 10: The Blaze of Modernity? – Part II: The Grand Design of the French "Lumières" and its Limits
Hafta 11: Dialectical Enlightenment: Revolutions, Terror, and the Birth of the Nation from the Failure of the Enlightenment
Hafta 12: The Archive of Modernity: Re-Imagining the Imperial Engine of Statecraft in the Age of Napoleon
Hafta 13: The Opium of Modernity and the Industry of Modernity in Europe: Perspectives on the Age of Industrializations from the Other End of the World and the Ghost of Capital
Hafta 14: The Problem of “Global Modernity”: Bismarck’s "Behemoth" and the Ticking Clock of the Decay of Empire
resources: The Oxford History of Modern Europe, T. C. W. Blanning, 2000, Oxford University Press
The Legitimacy of the Modern Age, Hans Blumenberg, 1985, MIT Press
The Birth of the Modern World, C. A. Bayly, 2004, Wiley/Blackwell
Liberty as Independence: The Making and Unmaking of a Political Idea, Quentin Skinner, 2025, Cambridge University Press

courses.name: History of the United States until the Reconstruction
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a basic historical framework of the United States from the colonial period up to the Civil War and Reconstruction era. It is designed to orient students to more detailed study of early American history.
courses.description: Basic history of the United States from the colonial period up to the Civil War. The course focuses on the characteristic problems of early American political, economic, and intellectual history during the nation-building process.

courses.name: History of US Foreign Relations: the Cold War and Beyond
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with an overview of the major developments in American foreign relations from the end of World War II to the present, with particular emphasis on US-Middle East relations.
courses.description: A survey of the major developments in American foreign relations from the end of World War II to the present. The course will include considerable emphasis on US-Middle East relations.

courses.name: History of the United States from the Reconstruction
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a broad understanding of the history of the United States from the end of Reconstruction to the contemporary period. It also seeks to orient students toward more detailed study by highlighting major political, economic, and intellectual developments in the rise of the USA as a world power.
courses.description: Basic history of the United States from 1865 up to the modern times, designed to orient students to more detailed information. Characteristic problems of American political, economic and intellectual history in the process of its emergence as a world power.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate a basic understanding of the general history of the United States from 1865 to the contemporary period.
Identify characteristic problems of American political, economic and intellectual history in the process of the USA's emergence as a world power.
Critically appraise relevant literature.
Produce written and oral presentations.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: General Themes American nationalism—Industrial power—Paradoxes of Globalism
Hafta 2: End of Reconstruction Political realignment—the Solid South—Boom & Bust
Hafta 3: The West Railroads—Immigrants—Industrialization
Hafta 4: Turn of the Century Robber barons—Populism—Progressivism
Hafta 5: Wartime Imperialism—The Great War—Wilsonianism
Hafta 6: “Normalcy” Isolationism—the Roaring 20s—Nativism
Hafta 7: Midterm examination
Hafta 8: Depression & Recovery Market Crash—FDR—New Deal
Hafta 9: World War II
Hafta 10: Cold War I 1950s Consensus—Eisenhower—Red Scare
Hafta 11: Cold War II: Glorious 60s—Civil Rights Movement--Vietnam
Hafta 12: Cold War III “Stagflation”—Carter—Reagan
Hafta 13: End of empire
Hafta 14: Review session
resources: The American Mind, Henry Steele Commager
The Search for Order, Robert Wiebe
Nature's Metropolis, William Cronon
American Populism, Robert McMath
In Search of Progressivism, Daniel Rogers
Globalization and the American Century, Eckes and Zeiler
The Perils of Prosperity, William Leuchtenberg
The Age of McCarthyism, Ellen Schrecker
America's Lost War: Vietnam, Charles Neu
The Rise of Neoconservatism, John Ehrman

courses.name: Latin for Medieval and Early Modern History II
courses.purpose: This course is designed to further develop students' Medieval Latin skills for those who wish to specialize in Medieval and Early Modern History. It aims to strengthen grammatical competence and reading ability in order to support work with historical Latin sources.
courses.description: Further Medieval Latin for students who wish to specialize in Medieval and early Modern History. Emphasis on both grammar and reading.
learning_outcomes: Acquire a complete grasp of Latin grammar and an extensive vocabulary
Translate and interpret extracts from a variety of kinds of medieval and early modern Latin sources
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: Ecclesiastical Latin and Neo-Latin Adaptations of Roman Classical Latin – Changes and Alterations from a Cultural Perspective
Hafta 2: The Subjunctive System I: ut/cum clauses; Vergil’s Aeneid and its Reception
Hafta 3: Participium Coniunctum [PC]; Livy, Ab Urbe Condita, and the Foundations of Rome
Hafta 4: Ablativus Absolutus with Participle of Simultaneity; Livy’s History of Rome and its Ecclesiastical Heritage; 1st Mid-Term Examination
Hafta 5: The Passive System I: Present & Imperfect
Hafta 6: The Passive System II: Perfect and Pluperfect
Hafta 7: Construction of Participle Sentences: PC and Abl. abs. with Participle of Anteriority; Ovid’s Metamorphoses and the Aftermath of Roman Poetry
Hafta 8: Gerundives with esse and other “-ND-“ Forms; Chronicles and Historiography; 2nd Mid-Term Examination
Hafta 9: "Visio Tnugdali" & "Historia de Sancto Silvestro": Temporal Profiles of Sentence Structures
Hafta 10: The Subjunctive System II: Irrealis (Conditional Clauses) and the Subjunctive in Main Clauses; 2nd Mid-Term Exam
Hafta 11: Foundations of Ecclesiastical Latin I: Biblical and Patristic Latin; The Augustinian Tradition in the Development of the Latin Language
Hafta 12: Foundations of Ecclesiastical Latin II: The Language of the Medieval Inquisition
Hafta 13: Theology and Church Government: The Donation of Constantine
Hafta 14: Letter Writing and Scholarship: The Revival of Ciceronian Latin in Petrarchan Epistolary Culture and the Confrontations with the Augustinian Reception
resources: Reading Medieval Latin, Keith Sidwell, 1995, Cambridge University Press
A Primer of Ecclesiastical Latin, John F. Collins, 1985, The Catholic University of America Press

courses.name: Peter the Great: The Birth of Modern Russia (1682-1725)
courses.purpose: This course is designed to survey and evaluate the reign of Peter the Great (1682-1725), the Tsar who made Russia a European power, in detail. It will also help students understand how Peter I reorganized Russia and how his legacy shaped the course of Russian history.
courses.description: The course covers Peter the Great's wide range of reforms, the resurgence of Russia as an Empire, the wars he fought, and the foreign relations conducted in his era, with references to the social and cultural traits of his time.

courses.name: Humanities and Social Science Honors Seminar
courses.purpose: This seminar is designed to introduce students to basic concepts and theoretical approaches that are common to advanced research in the humanities and social sciences, and to develop their ability to engage with interdisciplinary academic work at an advanced level.
courses.description: This seminar is restricted to students with a cumulative GPA of 3.30 or higher. It presents important readings and texts from several disciplines, such as philosophy, psychology, sociology, and literary theory. The specific content changes from year to year, but the course includes seminar discussions, one-on-one meetings with the seminar leader, and a term paper on a topic related to the seminar issues.
learning_outcomes: Understand basic concepts and theoretical approaches used in advanced humanities and social science research.
Engage critically with readings and texts from multiple disciplines.
Discuss academic work in seminar settings and in individual meetings.
Produce a polished term paper on a chosen topic related to the seminar.
Develop academic writing that can be used in graduate school applications.

courses.name: Latin for Medieval and Early Modern History I
courses.purpose: Introduction of Medieval Latin to graduate students who wish to specialize in Medieval as well as Early European History.
courses.description: Emphasis on both grammar and reading.

courses.name: Democracy and Empire in the Ancient World
courses.purpose: This course examines the political ideas and institutions of ancient Greece and Rome in order to understand how democracy, dictatorship, republic, and empire developed and interacted in the ancient world.
courses.description: Democracy, dictatorship, republic, and empire in ancient Greece and Rome.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: NOTE: Readings will be no more than 15-20 pages: The pre-democratic polis: Possible readings include: Aeschylus’ Eumenides, Herodotus’ Persian Council, Thucydides’ Archaeology and/or Periclean, Funeral Oration
Hafta 2: The Government in Crisis: Possible readings include: Thucydides’ Mytilenean and/or Melian Debates, Lysias, Against Eratosthenes; Plato’s Apology

Hafta 3: The State in Crisis: Possible readings include: Aristophanes, Women of the Assembly/Lysistrata/Birds; Thucydides’ Corcyraean stasis and corresponding analysis of war, Xenophon, Constitution of Sparta
Hafta 4: Alexander: Possible readings include: Plutarch’s Alexander, Arrian's Anabasis
Hafta 5: Alexander and the Hellenistic Kingdoms: Selected secondary reading as well as artwork and inscriptions
Hafta 6: Between Myth and Truth-The Origins of Rome: Possible readings include: Livy (Romulus, Numa, Superbus), Dionysius of Halicarnassus
Hafta 7: The Roman Republic: Possible readings include: 12 Tablets, Polybius Book 6
Hafta 8: The Problems of the Republic: Possible readings include: Cicero, Against Verres 2; On the Command of Pompey
Hafta 9: The Problems of the Republic: Possible readings include: Cicero, Against Verres 2; On the Command of Pompey
Hafta 10: Civil War: Possible readings include: Sallust, On the Catilinarian Conspiracy
Hafta 11: The death of the Republic: Possible readings include: Suetonius, Augustus. Field trip to the Res Gestae in Ankara
Hafta 12: The Epic Empire and the Pax Augusta: Possible readings include: Vergil, Aeneid 1, 6
Hafta 13: Empire in Verse: Possible readings include: Ovid, Horace, Martial; Lucan, Pharsalia
Hafta 14: Imperialism and its discontents: Possible readings include: Tacitus, Agricola, Germania, Annals
resources: Ancient Greek Democracy: Readings and Sources, Robinson, E.W. (ed.), 2008, Wiley-Blackwell
Ancient Rome: A Sourcebook, Dillon, M. and Garland, L., 2013, Routledge
Course Pack with supplementary readings

courses.name: Media for Representation
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to represent design ideas accurately and effectively through computer-based media. It supports the communication of design concepts, dimensions, materials, and processes using 2D drawing and 3D visualization tools.
courses.description: Computer generated 2D drawings of the design, its dimensions, process and materials and introduction to 3D visualization technology to create and communicate design.
learning_outcomes: Students can analyze and develop appropriate design solutions, applying previously learned information, using modern software systems and tools.
Students can propose an appropriate design solution within a reasonable time frame and demonstrate knowledge related to previously covered topics.
Students can practice with the new tool on their own, solving a given design problem successfully, while reflecting on and demonstrating knowledge related to previously discussed topics.
Students can clearly demonstrate advanced proficiency as an interior architect, using the computer media as a primary design and presentation tool to communicate design ideas.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction
Hafta 2: Basics Concepts / Drawing Setting / Drawing Concepts in 2D
Hafta 3: Using and Specifying Coordinates
Hafta 4: Annotation Commands
Hafta 5: Basics of Layers and related Properties
Hafta 6: Types of 3D Modeling
Hafta 7: MIDTERM
Hafta 8: Object Properties / 3D Space / 3D Views / Coordinate System
Hafta 9: Render / Materials / Light
Hafta 10: Drawing Complex Objects
Hafta 11: Plotting and Printing
Hafta 12: Final
Hafta 13: Representation Tools
Hafta 14: Representation Tools

courses.name: Human Factors
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce human factors and ergonomics as key determinants in the design of interior environments. It helps students develop a user-centered and universal design approach grounded in applied anthropometry.
courses.description: Human factors/ergonomics as a determinant in the design of interior environments. User-centered approach to design. Universal design. Principles of applied anthropometry. Public spaces. Residential spaces.
learning_outcomes: Apply the accurate, appropriate and related human factors criteria and universal design principles to design problems
Analyze and evaluate components of the built environment in accordance with human factors criteria and universal design principles
Design and create products and spaces in accordance with human factors criteria and universal design principles
Apply appropriate written,visual and verbal communication techniques and technologies in individual and group work
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction, principles of anthropometry
Hafta 2: Static anthropometric data
Hafta 3: Workspace design
Hafta 4: Human diversity, universal design
Hafta 5: Universal design, seating
Hafta 6: Seating, analysing a kiosk
Hafta 7: Offices, Midterm (11 March 2026, for both sections)
Hafta 8: Bathrooms
Hafta 9: Kitchens
Hafta 10: Project: Case study presentations
Hafta 11: Living rooms, bedrooms; Project: Case study presentations
Hafta 12: Residential Examples
Hafta 13: Quiz: Accidents in the home, auditoriums
Hafta 14: Design jury week
resources: Bodyspace, Stephen Pheasant, 2000, Taylor and Francis
Ergonomics of the Home, Etinenne Grandjean, 1973, Taylor and Francis

courses.name: Lighting Design
courses.description: Physics of light. Vision and Perception. Lighting design principles. Artificial light sources. Natural light. Lighting applications. Color and color design.
learning_outcomes: Design a lighting project during the semester for a small-scale interior space.
Apply theoretical and technical knowledge of artificial and natural lighting to given conditions of interior spaces within one to two hours.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction, Vision and Perception
Hafta 2: Physics of Light
Hafta 3: Lighting objectives, Lighting parameters
Hafta 4: Lighting design principles
Hafta 5: Application examples
Hafta 6: Artificial light sources
Hafta 7: Illuminance calculation
Hafta 8: Spring Break
Hafta 9: Natural light I
Hafta 10: Natural light II
Hafta 11: Colour I
Hafta 12: Colour II
Hafta 13: Term project
Hafta 14: Term project
resources: Architectural Lighting, Egan, M.D. and Olgyay, V., 2002/2, McGraw-Hill
The Design of Lighting, Peter Tregenza & David Loe

courses.name: Architectural Acoustics
courses.purpose: This course introduces the principles of acoustics as they apply to architectural and interior environments, with emphasis on noise control and fire prevention in buildings.
courses.description: Acoustics, noise control and fire prevention in buildings.

courses.name: People and Environment
courses.description: Environment and social behavior. Environmental perception and cognition. Environmental appraisals. Environmental psychology research methods. Implications for design.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of issues related to environment-behavior studies
Observation of environment-behavior relations in real life settings
Discuss observations
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: Human and environment relationship
Hafta 2: Scope / Overview
Hafta 3: Environmental perception and cognition
Hafta 4: Approaches; Research methods
Hafta 5: Activity patterns; Territoriality
Hafta 6: Examination – part 1; Review
Hafta 7: Personal space
Hafta 8: Crowding + Privacy
Hafta 9: Environmental appraisals and assessments
Hafta 10: Implications for design
Hafta 11: Examination – part 2; Review
Hafta 12: Presentations of project
Hafta 13: Presentations of project
Hafta 14: Review; Project submission

Designs for the Pluriverse, Escobar Arturo, 2018, Duke university press, pp. 25-48
Anthropocene Psychology, Matthew Adams, 2020, Routledge, pp. 17-25
Spatializing Culture, Setha Low, 2017, Routledge, pp. 14-23
Feminism and the Mastery of Nature, Val Plumwood, 1993, Routledge, pp. 19-40

courses.name: Detailing Studio
courses.description: Interior architectural detailing. Use of materials. Producing application drawings and within project referencing.

courses.name: Sustainable Design for Interiors
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the principles of sustainable design and their application in interior architecture. It focuses on developing awareness of environmental performance, health, and resource efficiency in interior environments.
courses.description: Concepts of sustainable design. Water use, energy conservation, thermal comfort and indoor air quality. Sustainable services of cold and hot water supplies. Interior design applications of heating, ventilation, sanitation and sewage disposal. Principles of alternative energy.
learning_outcomes: Develop a comprehensive portfolio of design case studies of innovative water use, energy conservation, thermal comfort and indoor air quality systems.
Discuss and explain sustainable criteria, through which environmentally friendly, healthy and socially inclusive interior environments could be created.
Design self-sufficient interiors with self-sufficient energy and water use; analyze, identify and apply design principles of sustainable design and create own sustainable design solutions.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction and description of the course
Hafta 2: What is sustainable design?
Hafta 3: Water systems: Cold and hot water supply - Homework
Hafta 4: Toilet and bath design
Hafta 5: Energy conservation: Heating of interiors - Homework Submission
Hafta 6: Indoor environmental quality: Daylighting, ventilating and cooling
Hafta 7: Fire Safety Seminar
Hafta 8: Term Project
Hafta 9: Term Project + Midterm
Hafta 10: Term Project
Hafta 11: TERM PROJECT Crit (Initial ideas + first sketches + conceptual development)
Hafta 12: TERM PROJECT Crit (design + development)
Hafta 13: TERM PROJECT Crit (design + development+detailing)
Hafta 14: TERM PROJECT Crit (design + development+detailing)

courses.name: Computerized Presentation Techniques
courses.purpose: Advanced usage of computerized programs for representation of student projects by integrating classical and computer media.
courses.description: Advanced usage of computerized programs for representation of the student projects by integrating classical and computer media.

courses.name: Product Detailing
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to resolve detailing problems in product design and interior architecture contexts. It focuses on how products are detailed for function, constructability, and material suitability.
courses.description: Detailing problems of products. Detailing of furniture, cabinetry, upholstery, fixtures, etc. Problems related to the nature of materials and production methods.

courses.name: Special Topics in Interior Design I
courses.purpose: Topics of special interest. The course is intended to support research and investigation of specific design issues.
courses.description: Research and investigation of specific design issues.

courses.name: Special Topics in Interior Design II
courses.purpose: Topics of special interest are explored to extend students' understanding and competency in interior design knowledge.
courses.description: Application of understanding to competency level of design knowledge.

courses.name: TV Set Design
courses.purpose: Application of theory into practice. Developing interior design considering TV studio cameras and TV studio light.
courses.description: TV Set Design.

courses.name: Architectural Photography
courses.purpose: To introduce photography as a tool for analyzing and documenting architecture and for visualizing design ideas.
courses.description: Photography for analyzing and documenting architecture. Basics of photography. Photography for visualizing design ideas.

courses.name: Color Theory and Applications
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental principles of color and their role in interior architecture and environmental design. It aims to develop students’ understanding of color perception, color systems, and the effective use of color in design applications.
courses.description: Basic color terminology. Color systems. Color vision and perception. Color interactions and contrasts. Harmony of colors, color schemes and compositions. Color planning and applications in design and architecture. Color research.

courses.name: Advanced Detailing Studio
courses.description: Building systems and materials. Modular interior systems. Comprehensive and detailed project development.

courses.name: Visionary and Future Environments
courses.purpose: To introduce students to the challenges of living in space and to develop their ability to analyze the needs and requirements of physical and social environments in space.
courses.description: Challenges of living in space. Needs analysis. Requirements of the physical and social environments in space. Project development for space settlements in digital medium.

courses.name: Leisure Practices and Spaces
courses.purpose: To provide a comprehensive understanding of leisure issues, including the transformation in the definition and practices of leisure.
courses.description: The course focuses on the transformation in the definition and practices of leisure and develops a basis for the critical analysis of new leisure spaces through extensive reading and observation on relevant sites.

courses.name: Interior Design: Professional Practice
courses.purpose: To introduce students to the professional practice of interior design and the administrative framework of managing a major interior design project. The course aims to prepare students for client relations, trade relations, and the legal and regulatory aspects of the profession.
courses.description: Administration of a major interior design project. Client and trade relations. Codes, government and legal requirements.
learning_outcomes: Having the capability of using the administrational information for an interior architectural design project
Ability of performing commercial components within the business life; client and trade relations
Examining productivity within Interior Architecture and Environmental Design
Analyzing skills and ways for getting governmental codes and legal requirements
Analyzing ethical standards of the profession of “Interior Architecture and Environmental Design”
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course
Hafta 2: Philosophical framework of the profession. Qualifications for the professional efficiency
Hafta 3: Historical background of the interior architecture. Professional divisions and current legal organizations of interior architecture
Hafta 4: Analysis of professional services, and management for a design project
Hafta 5: Office types, partnerships, and associations. Study on professional and bidding documents, work proposals, contracts, and letters of agreement.
Hafta 6: Interior Architecture in the world
Hafta 7: Pricing considerations. Quantity surveying. Bill of quantities
Hafta 8: Business concerns and firm types
Hafta 9: Project managing, and methods of time estimations
Hafta 10: Project managing, and methods of time estimations / Mid-term
Hafta 11: Office establishments and office managing
Hafta 12: Governmental codes. Legal requirements
Hafta 13: Critiques for final project
Hafta 14: Critiques for final project

courses.name: Environmental Management and Sustainable Development
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to sustainable systems and techniques and to develop innovative systems thinking for sustainable development. It also seeks to build practical skills for environmental management while addressing the economic and organizational dimensions of environmental decisions.
courses.description: Sustainable systems and techniques. Innovative system thinking for sustainable development. Practice skills for environment management. Economic and organizational dimensions of environmental decisions.

courses.name: Issues and Themes in Industrial Design
courses.purpose: Raising awareness on the profession of industrial design and developing research skills.
courses.description: Production of design solution on an industrial design project.

courses.name: Current Issues in Interior Design I
courses.purpose: To introduce students to innovative planning approaches and current design issues in interior design, while supporting the development of advanced design skills.
courses.description: Innovative planning. Current design issues. Development of advanced design skills.

courses.name: Current Issues in Interior Design II
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to engage with current issues in interior design through contemporary techniques, materials, and problem-solving approaches.
courses.description: Specialized issues related to contemporary techniques and materials. Special interior design problem solving.

courses.name: Introduction to Modeling and Optimization
courses.description: Introduction to mathematical modelling. Linear and integer models. Linear programming. Graphical method. Simplex method. Duality and sensitivity analysis. Network models. Network simplex method.
learning_outcomes: Construct linear and integer models of common processes in engineering and management
Solve linear programs using the simplex method
Do sensitivity analysis
Use linear programming software such as Excel and Xpress
Do effective teamwork
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Operations Research
Hafta 2: Linear Programming Models
Hafta 3: Linear Programming Models
Hafta 4: Geometry of Linear Programming
Hafta 5: Simplex method
Hafta 6: Simplex method
Hafta 7: Duality in Linear Programming
Hafta 8: Duality in Linear Programming
Hafta 9: Sensitivity Analysis
Hafta 10: Sensitivity Analysis
Hafta 11: Network Flow Models
Hafta 12: Network Flow Models
Hafta 13: Network Simplex
Hafta 14: Integer Programming Models
resources: Operations Research: Algorithms and Applications, W. L. Winston, 4th edition, 2004, Brooks/Cole

courses.name: History of Furniture
courses.purpose: Understanding historical evolution of furniture design and industry. Developing research skills. Raising awareness of and seeing relations between history and today.
courses.description: Understanding historical evolution of furniture design and industry. Developing research skills. Raising awareness of and seeing relations between history and today.
learning_outcomes: Define and describe the period of a specific furniture style, movement and designer successfully
Explain the relation between social context of a specific period and, furniture design and production
Demonstrate a comprehensive knowledge on a specific designer, methods of design and designs
Develop a contemporary design based on the knowledge in the historical context.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction and decription of course
Hafta 2: Ancient Furniture & Middle Ages & 16th Century Furniture
Hafta 3: 17th Century Furniture & Quiz 1
Hafta 4: 18th Century Furniture & 19th Century Furniture
Hafta 5: Film screening & Quiz 2
Hafta 6: Industrial Revolution and the beginning of the 20th century Furniture

Hafta 14: Project discussion
