courses.name: Introduction to Complex Geometry
courses.description: Vector bundles. Sheaf theory and sheaf cohomology. Kahler manifolds. Chow rings. Lefschetz (1,1)-theorem. The Hodge conjecture, i.e. the (p,p) version of Lefschetz's theorem.

courses.name: Introduction to Algebraic Geometry
courses.description: Plane curves, conics and cubics. Affine varieties, Hilbert basis theorem, Zarisky topology, Hibert's null stellennsatz. Coordinate rings, morphisms. Projective varieties, briational morphism. Smoothness, dimension and the tangent space. The 27 lines on a cubic surface.

courses.name: Partial Differential Equations
courses.description: Pfaffian systems, linear and nonlinear PDE's of first order. Second order PDE, characteristic curves and characteristic equations. Laplace equation, wave equation, heat equation. Method of integral transforms, Fourier series, Green's function.

courses.name: Analysis on Manifolds
courses.description: The algebra and topology of Rn, review of differentiation and integration, inverse and implicit function theorems, change of variables, tensors and differential forms, integration on chains, integration on manifolds, and Stokes' theorem.

courses.name: Introduction to Financial Mathematics
courses.purpose: This course introduces the basic knowledge and techniques used in financial mathematics, with emphasis on the Black-Scholes model and its extensions. It aims to provide the mathematical foundation needed to analyze pricing and hedging problems for financial derivatives.
courses.description: Basic knowledge and techniques in financial mathematics: basic Black and Scholes model and its sophistications. Theory of discrete and continuous martingales, semimartingales. Stochastic integral, Ito formula, construction and solution of stochastic differential equations. Applications to option pricing and hedging problems for the European options. American options and the numerical problems of the solutions of second order elliptic equations.

courses.name: Dynamical Systems
courses.purpose: To introduce the geometric theory of differential equations and the main qualitative tools used to analyze dynamical behavior of systems.
courses.description: Geometric theory of differential equations. Existence and uniqueness theorems; continuous dependence on initial conditions and parameters. Flows for autonomous and non-autonomous systems. Topological conjugacy and equivalence, Hartman-Grobman theorem. Stability theory. Non-autonomous linear systems and Floquet theory. Periodic solutions and Poincaré maps. Global methods, Liapunov theory and LaSalle invariance principle. Stable, unstable, and center manifolds. Structural stability and bifurcation theory. Symbolic dynamics and the shift map. Homoclinic phenomena.

courses.name: Principles of Biology
courses.purpose: This course is for students from the Physics, Chemistry and Mathematics Departments.
courses.description: Molecules of life, organization of the cell, chromosomes and cell division, patterns of inheritance, evolution, recombinant DNA technology, genetic diseases.

courses.name: Experimental Approaches in Molecular and Cellular Biology
courses.purpose: This course introduces a substantial theoretical basis to understand key experimental techniques used in modern molecular biology research.
courses.description: The course focuses on techniques and concepts involving genetic engineering, restriction enzymes, cloning DNA plasmid constructs, analysis of DNA/RNA samples, creating and screening a library, probe labeling and hybridization, PCR, q-RT-PCR, DNA sequencing techniques, manipulating gene expression in prokaryotes, heterologous protein production in eukaryotes, cell growth and cytotoxicity assays, cell adhesion, invasion, motility, apoptosis assays, molecular diagnostics, protein therapeutics, nucleic acids as therapeutics, and transgenic animals. It also covers the physical-chemical and biological principles underlying the basic techniques and procedures used in molecular biology, with examples on how to understand and interpret results generated by these methods.

courses.name: Molecular Biology of the Cell I
courses.purpose: This course aims an in-depth understanding of cell signaling. We start by studying the signal/receptor families individually, and move on to a synthesis by studying examples from developmental biology and cancer.
courses.description: Selected original articles that from the foundations and principles of cell signaling are analyzed and criticized thoroughly as part of the coursework.

courses.name: Science and Ethics
courses.description: Scientific concepts, history of science, the birth of modern science, science and society, ethics of science.

courses.name: Physiology
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with an understanding of how the human body maintains homeostasis and how major organ systems are regulated and integrated to support normal function.
courses.description: Homeostatic and biological control mechanisms in major body systems, including the skeletal system, muscle system, nervous system, circulatory system, respiratory system, digestive system, excretory system, reproductive system and immune system.
learning_outcomes: Correctly demonstrate how vertebrate cells are organized into tissues, organs, and organ systems and give examples of each
Explain the reasons why homeostasis is a vital function of the human body
Identify and describe the functions of the hormones and know where these hormones are synthesized
Differentiate among the different types of neurons in CNS and PNS
Describe how the brain controls higher functions like learning and memory
Prepare well-designed presentations on physiological disorders
Differentiate among smooth, striated, and cardiac muscle in terms of derivation, location, and initiation of contraction
Evaluate the primary functions of the vertebrate circulatory system
Explain how the mammalian heart beats
Differentiate between the neural and hormonal regulation of digestion
Describe the metabolic and digestive activities of the liver.
Compare anabolic and catabolic processes in energy metabolism
Understand the events associated with human fertilization
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction, Compartmentation: Cells and Tissues, Communication, Integration, Homeostasis
Hafta 2: Neurons: Cellular and Network Properties
Hafta 3: Central Nervous System
Hafta 4: Peripheral Nervous System, Sensory Physiology and Muscles
Hafta 5: Control of body movement
Hafta 6: Midterm 1
Hafta 7: Endocrine and Reproductive Systems and Metabolism
Hafta 8: Cardiovascular Physiology
Hafta 9: Respiratory Physiology
Hafta 10: Renal Physiology, Fluid and electrolyte balance
Hafta 11: Digestive Physiology
Hafta 12: Homework and quiz
Hafta 13: Midterm-2
Hafta 14: Pathophysiology
resources: Human Physiology, Dee Unglaub Silverthorn, 6th Ed., Pearson

courses.name: Practical Biology
courses.purpose: This course is for students from faculty of Education. It aims to provide practical techniques for the biology laboratory and support students in biological experiments and problem solving in biology.
courses.description: The course covers practical techniques for the biology laboratory, biological experiments, and problem solving in biology. Students carry out practical projects and participate in one hour of discussion and four hours of laboratory work per week.

courses.name: Genomics
courses.description: High throughput methods for the analysis of DNA and RNA. Repetitive sequences, comparative genomics, transcriptomics, metagenomics, epigenomics, evolutionary genomics, systems biology.
learning_outcomes: Identify and compare critical aspects of next generation sequencing technologies
Compare and contrast classical vs. novel methods of genomic analysis
Analyze data generated by genome and epigenome research
Discuss the power of newer technologies in genome research
Discuss the utilization and power of newer in vitro and in silico technologies in genome research
Compute and thus generate answers to pre-prepared questions by performing various in silico analyses - Laboratory
Interpret data variations
Characterize principal epigenetic mechanisms that influence gene expression
Integrate biological and statistical knowledge gained in previous years into a comprehensive synthesis
Apply knowledge of life sciences, basic sciences and technology with a particular emphasis on molecular biology and genetics to solve problems.
Identify, formulate, solve biological problems and utilize computers and technical/scientific software including bioinformatics approaches.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Genomes, Genome Browsers, Databases, Syllabus; Lab1a: GNOMAD
Hafta 2: DNA/Protein variation and Next Generation Sequencing Technologies; LAB 1b: Ensembl Biomart
Hafta 3: Next Generation Sequencing Analysis Basics; LAB2a: UCSC Genome Browser Tracks and Galaxy Basics I
Hafta 4: Prokaryotic, Viral and Eukaryotic Genomes, Metagenomics; MIDTERM 1 (in class 50 mins); LAB2b: Galaxy Basics II
Hafta 5: Metagenomics Analysis I; LAB3a: Metagenomics 16S fastq paired end data and quality control and data processing
Hafta 6: Metagenomics Analysis II: LAB3b: metagenomics analysis clustering and visualization
Hafta 7: Comparative Genomics/Transcriptomics; Spring Break; LAB4a: NGS I in galaxy
Hafta 8: Human Genome Variation GWAS; MIDTERM 2-3 in class (100 mins); LAB4b: NGS II in galaxy
Hafta 9: Cancer Genomics; LAB5a: GWAS Catalog and VEP; LAB QUIZ 1 (labs 1-3); LAB 4c: NGS III
Hafta 10: Epigenomics and Cistrome; LAB5: VEP and IGV
Hafta 11: Systems Biology; LAB6: The Cancer Genome Atlas TCGA cbioportal.org
Hafta 12: Presentations; LAB QUIZ 2 (Labs 4-6)
Hafta 13: Presentations; MIDTERM 4-5 (in class 100 mins)
Hafta 14: Presentations
resources: Introduction to Genomics, Lesk, 4th edition
Weekly Lecture Notes, Ilyas Chachoua
Bioinformatics and Functional Genomics, Pevsner
Genomes 4

courses.name: Biochemistry II
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a solid understanding of the major biochemical pathways and molecular processes that underlie cellular metabolism. It also helps students develop the ability to connect individual pathways to the overall physiology of organisms.
courses.description: Introduction to intermediary metabolism, polysaccharides, energy storage, lipids and membrane structure, nucleic acid structure and nucleotide metabolism.
learning_outcomes: Describe how a cell extracts energy and reducing power from its environment to synthesize macromolecules and determine ATP as the universal currency of free energy in biological systems.
Perceive glycolysis as a tightly controlled energy conversion pathway in many organisms and explain how glucose can be synthesized from non-carbohydrate precursors.
Define gluconeogenesis and explain that it is not simply the reverse of glycolysis.

Understand the citric acid cycle as the final common pathway for the oxidation of fuel molecules and as a source of building blocks for biosyntheses by locating the enzymes involved in eukaryotic cells.
Describe how oxidative phosphorylation depends on electron transfer and takes place in mitochondria by comprehending the subunit structure and the function of ATP synthase in eukaryotic cells.
Recognize how the regulation of cellular respiration is controlled by ATP demand.
Understand the function of the pentose phosphate pathway and the key role of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase in protection against reactive oxygen species.
Describe glycogen as the readily mobilized storage form of glucose and understand why it is preferred and where it is stored.
Explain how glycogen is synthesized and degraded by different, reciprocally regulated pathways and the role of allosteric responses and hormones in this regulation.
Examine the use of fatty acids as fuel and understand how the catabolism of unsaturated and odd-chain fatty acids requires additional steps.
Describe the elongation cycle of fatty acid synthesis and understand the accessory enzyme systems required for elongation and unsaturation to contrast fatty acid oxidation and fatty acid synthesis.
Understand the degradation of proteins to amino acids and how protein turnover is tightly regulated.
Trace the carbon atoms of degraded amino acids in major metabolic intermediates.
Appreciate the importance of nitrogen fixation and the biosynthesis of amino acids from intermediates of the citric acid cycle and other major pathways.
Describe de novo and salvage pathways in nucleotide biosynthesis.
Describe the key steps in the biosynthesis of membrane lipids and steroids.
State the biological significance of cholesterol.
Understand how the separately examined pathways are integrated and examine large-scale biochemical interactions that constitute the physiology of organisms.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course
Hafta 2: Glycolysis and Gluconeogenesis
Hafta 3: Glycolysis and Gluconeogenesis
Hafta 4: The Citric Acid Cycle / Paper presentations
Hafta 5: Oxidative Phosphorylation / Paper presentations
Hafta 6: Oxidative Phosphorylation / Paper presentations
Hafta 7: The Calvin Cycle and Pentose Phosphate Pathway / Paper presentations
Hafta 8: The Calvin Cycle and Pentose Phosphate Pathway / Paper presentations
Hafta 9: Glycogen Metabolism / Paper presentations
Hafta 10: Glycogen Metabolism / Paper presentations
Hafta 11: Fatty Acid Metabolism / Paper presentations
Hafta 12: Fatty Acid Metabolism / Paper presentations
Hafta 13: Case studies
Hafta 14: Case studies
resources: Biochemistry, Jeremy M. Berg, John L. Tymoczko, Gregory D. Gatto Jr., Lubert Stryer, 8th Edition, W. H. Freeman

courses.name: Biomolecules, Biomaterials and Bioprocesses
courses.description: Utilization of biological macromolecules as a biomaterial, principles applied to harness these biological complex molecules in biology, medicine and pharmaceutical biotechnology discussion of the selected cutting edge research papers.

courses.name: Immunology
courses.purpose: This course will provide students with a fundamental understanding of immunobiology.
courses.description: Topics will include basic concepts in immunology, innate and adaptive immunity, regulation of immune system, immune system in health and disease, and manipulation of the immune response.
learning_outcomes: Learn basic concepts in immunology.
Gain detailed knowledge of the development and function of the immune system.
Learn the key characteristics of innate and adaptive immunity.
Learn how the immune system is regulated at molecular and cellular levels.
Show fundamental understanding of diseases associated with defects and dysfunction of the immune system.
Learn how the immune response can be manipulated to treat diseases: immune modulators, immunotherapy, and vaccines.
Understand immunological terminology and learn major modern techniques used in immunology research.
Improve skills with oral and written scientific communication.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the Course
Hafta 2: Basic Concepts in Immunology
Hafta 3: Innate Immunity: The First Lines of Defense
Hafta 4: The Induced Response of Innate Immunity
Hafta 5: Antigen Recognition by B-cell and T-cell Receptors
Hafta 6: The Generation of Lymphocyte Antigen Receptors
Hafta 7: Antigen Presentation to T Lymphocytes
Hafta 8: Lymphocyte Receptor Signaling
Hafta 9: The Development of B and T lymphocytes
Hafta 10: T-cell-Mediated Immunity
Hafta 11: The Humoral Immune Response
Hafta 12: Presentations and case studies
Hafta 13: Presentations and case studies
Hafta 14: Presentations and case studies
resources: Required - Textbook: Janeway`s Immunobiology, Kenneth Murphy & Casey Weaver, 8th or newer, Garland Sciences

courses.name: DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms
courses.purpose: To introduce the major types of DNA damage and the cellular mechanisms that detect and respond to them. The course aims to build an understanding of how cells maintain genome integrity through DNA repair pathways.
courses.description: Types of DNA damage and the cellular response to it. DNA repair mechanisms: direct reversal of repair, base-excision, nucleotide-excision, mis-match repair, homologous recombination repair, non-homologous end joining.

courses.name: Introduction to Human Genetics
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental principles of human genetics and their application to understanding inherited traits and genetic disease.
courses.description: Principles of human genetics, patterns of single gene inheritance, human molecular genetics, the human gene map, cytogenetics, the molecular and biochemical basis of genetic disease, genetic counseling, prenatal diagnosis.

courses.name: Introduction to Systems Engineering
courses.purpose: To introduce engineering problem solving from a systems perspective and to provide a unified application of mechanical engineering principles. The course also aims to familiarize students with CAD systems and group project work.
courses.description: Treatment of engineering problems from a systems perspective and a unified application of mechanical engineering principles, introduction to and use of CAD systems and group projects.

courses.name: Mechanical Vibrations
courses.description: Free and forced vibrations of single- and multi-degree-of-freedom systems. Fundamentals of modal analysis, damping and vibration control methods. Vibration measurement and analysis.

courses.name: Numerical Methods for Engineers
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide engineering students with the fundamental numerical tools needed to solve mathematical problems that are difficult or impossible to handle analytically. It also helps students understand the theory behind numerical algorithms and their practical limitations.
courses.description: Taylor series and approximation errors, solving systems of linear and nonlinear equations, curve fitting and interpolation, numerical differentiation and integration, and applications to systems of ordinary differential equations.
learning_outcomes: Enhancing problem solving skills: Numerical methods are extremely powerful problem-solving tools. They are capable of handling large system of equations, non-linearities, and complicated geometries that are not uncommon in engineering practice, and that are often impossible to solve analytically.

Giving knowledge of the basic theory: the intelligent use of “pre-packaged” or “canned” computer programs that involve Numerical methods is often predicated on the knowledge of the basic theory underlying these methods.

Designing computer programs: Many engineering problems cannot be approached using “canned programs”. Being conversant with numerical methods and being adept at computer programming, gives the capability of solving problems without having to buy or commission expensive software.and global climate change, and will be able to assess the validity of energy conversion claims made in popular media.

Exhibit power and limitations of computers: Successfully implementation of numerical methods on a computer and then their application to solve otherwise intractable problems, provides a dramatic demonstration of how computers can serve one’s professional development, and helps acknowledge and control the errors of approximation that are part and parcel of all large-scale numerical calculations.

Reinforce understanding of mathematics: Because one function of numerical methods is to reduce higher mathematics to basic arithmetic operations, they get at the 'nuts and bolts' of some otherwise obscure topics. Enhanced understanding and insight can result from this alternative perspective.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Taylor Series, Approximations and Errors
Hafta 2: Roots of Equations: Bisection, False Position, and Iteration Methods
Hafta 3: Newton-Raphson and Secant Methods, System of Non-linear Equations
Hafta 4: System of Linear Algebraic Equations: Gauss Elimination, Matrix Inversion, Gauss-Seidel Iteration
Hafta 5: One-dimensional Unconstrained Optimization
Hafta 6: Curve Fitting, Least Squares Regression
Hafta 7: Polynomial Interpolation
Hafta 8: Spline Interpolation
Hafta 9: Numerical Differentiation
Hafta 10: Numerical Integration, Newton-Cotes Formulae
Hafta 11: Romberg’s and Gauss-Quadrature Integration
Hafta 12: Solution of Ordinary Differential Equations
Hafta 13: Euler, Runge-Kutta, Multi-Step Methods
Hafta 14: System of ODE’s, Initial-Value Problems, Boundary-Value Problems
resources: Numerical Methods for Engineers, S.C. Chapra and R.P. Canale, 7th edition / 2014, McGraw Hill

courses.name: Special Techniques in Molecular Genetics
courses.description: Principles of specific methods used in molecular genetics, laboratory tools for molecular genetic applications, restriction enzymes, cloning vectors, gene analysis methods, DNA cloning, gene libraries and library screening, PCR and other applications, expression of cloned genes in cultured cells, isolation and characterization of gene transcripts, RNA analysis, microarray analysis, proteomics, RNA world and RNA knockdown strategies, gene manipulations in animals, gene and cellular therapies. Discussions of selected research papers. (No lab session.)
learning_outcomes: Understand the basic knowledge regarding some of the most common experimental methods used in molecular biology and recombinant DNA research.
Learn critical molecular biology methods by lecture presentations and reading papers.
Understand how the methods work.
Understand limitations for these methods.
Learn how to analyze data by reading lots of primary literature and to learn to doubt what you read.
Study how molecular biology is used to answer a wide range of biological questions.
Learn that there are several experimental methods that can be used to get to the same endpoint.
Observe how basic research can lead to unexpected applications.
Design the next experiments.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Laboratory tools for molecular genetic applications
Hafta 2: Gene Analysis methods
Hafta 3: Gene Analysis methods II
Hafta 4: Expression of cloned genes in cultured cells
Hafta 5: Expression of cloned genes in cultured cells
Hafta 6: Isolation and characterization of gene transcripts
Hafta 7: RNA world and RNA knockdown strategies
Hafta 8: Microarray types, analysis and its applications
Hafta 9: Proteomics
Hafta 10: Gene manipulations in animals
Hafta 11: Gene and cellular therapies
Hafta 12: Class presentations
Hafta 13: Class presentations
Hafta 14: Class presentations
resources: Principles of Gene Manipulations and Genomics, Eds: S.B. Primrose, R.M. Twyman, 2006, Blackwell Publ

courses.name: Measurement and Instrumentation
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the principles and methods used in measurement systems and instrumentation. It prepares students to understand how physical quantities are sensed, processed, analyzed, and interpreted in engineering applications.
courses.description: Principles of measurement and measurement systems, basic principles of signals and frequency domain representation, Fourier transform, mechanical modeling of measurement systems, probabilistic and statistical methods for measurement data analysis, measurement uncertainty calculation, basics of experiment design, measurement, recording and analysis of force, strain, temperature, humidity, flow, velocity, vibration and acceleration. Use of typical laboratory equipment such as oscilloscopes, frequency analyzers, operational amplifiers, and thermocouples.
learning_outcomes: Able to identify types of uncertainties in a measurement.
Able to do unit conversion
Able to calculate total measurement system errors
Perform statistical analysis on measurement data
Able to solve, design and identify basic circuits used in measurement
Able to determine transfer functions of filters
Able to measure temperature, pressure and viscosity
Able to measure dimension, velocity, acceleration, rotation
Able to measure mass, force, and torque
Able to measure vibration
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Signal Transduction and Sensors
Hafta 2: Static Characteristics of Measurement Systems
Hafta 3: Data Acquisition
Hafta 4: Uncertainty Analysis
Hafta 5: Uncertainty Analysis (Cont.)
Hafta 6: Dynamic Characteristics of Measurement Systems
Hafta 7: Intro. to Signal Analysis - amplitude, SNR, dynamic range, resolution
Hafta 8: Frequency Domain Representation of Signals and Noise
Hafta 9: Frequency Domain Representation of Signals and Noise (Cont.)
Hafta 10: Frequency Domain Representation of Signals and Noise (Cont.)
Hafta 11: Op-Amps for Instrumentation
Hafta 12: Filtering and Matching Circuits
Hafta 13: Bridge Circuits & Data Acquisition
Hafta 14: Data Acquisiton - Revisited
resources: Theory and Design for Mechanical Measurements, Richard S. Figliola, Donald E. Beasley, International Adaptation, 7th Edition or 6th Edition, Wiley
Principles of Measurement and Instrumentation, A. S. Morris, 3rd edition, Butterworth Heinemann
Principles of Measurement Systems, John P. Bentley, 2005/4, Pearson

courses.name: Finite Elements
courses.purpose: This course introduces the finite element method as a numerical tool for solving mechanics problems governed by partial differential equations. It aims to build the theoretical and computational foundation needed to model, analyze, and simulate engineering systems.
courses.description: Solving partial differential equations of mechanics numerically. Fundamentals of the finite element method including weak form, shape functions, isoparametric approximation, Gauss quadrature, element types, assembly operation, sparsity pattern with application to 2D problems. Self-written finite element code in MATLAB. Computational simulations of elastic materials and stress analysis using the MATLAB code. Domain discretization, pre-processing and post-processing aspects.
learning_outcomes: To compute the deformation fields for a truss structure both analytically and using the finite element method.
To compute stress and strain fields using the finite element method for a given geometry and boundary conditions in 1D.
To compute stress and strain fields using the finite element method for a given geometry and boundary conditions in 2D.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Understanding finite elements through springs combinations
Hafta 2: Truss elements and assembly of 1D objects in 2D and 3D space
Hafta 3: Programming assembly of truss structures
Hafta 4: Strong from, weak form, energy minimization
Hafta 5: Approximation using shape functions
Hafta 6: Integration via Gauss quadrature
Hafta 7: Formulation of FEM in 1D adopting isoparametric concept
Hafta 8: Programming 1D FE code and sparsity patterns
Hafta 9: Strong and weak form for 2D problems
Hafta 10: Domain discretization in 2D and pre-processing
Hafta 11: Derivation of shape functions and Gauss quadrature in 2D
Hafta 12: Formulation of FEM in 2D adopting isoparametric concept
Hafta 13: Programming 2D FE code
Hafta 14: Post-processing and visualization aspects
resources: A First Course in Finite Elements, Jacob Fish and Ted Belytschko, 2007, Wiley
Concepts and Applications of Finite Element Analysis, Robert D. Cook et al., 2001, Wiley
The Finite Element Method: Linear Static and Dynamic Finite Element Analysis, Thomas J. R. Hughes, 2000, Dover

courses.name: Design and Manufacturing
courses.description: Engineering materials, bulk deformation processes, material removal processes and machines, sintering, polymeric materials processes, additive manufacturing, economic and quality considerations, design principles, machine elements.
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to calculate the deformation forces and energy requirements in forming processes
Students will be able to calculate the cutting forces and energy requirements in machining processes
Students will be able to set the optimum cutting conditions in machining processes
Students will be able to perform the conceptual design of products
Students will be able to select the machine elements from catalogs
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction. Engineering materials
Hafta 2: Metals
Hafta 3: Plastics, Ceramic materials, Composite materials
Hafta 4: Classification of manufacturing processes, Casting.
Hafta 5: Bulk deformation processes
Hafta 6: Bulk deformation processes
Hafta 7: Metal removal machines and processes
Hafta 8: Metal removal machines and processes
Hafta 9: Sintering, Polimeric materials processes Additive manufacturing
Hafta 10: Economic considerations in manufacturing, Quality consideration and SPC
Hafta 11: Design principles, machine elements
Hafta 12: Machine elements
Hafta 13: Machine elements
Hafta 14: Mahine elements
resources: Manufacturing Engineering and Technology, S. Kalpakjian and S. R. Schmid, 2006/5, Prentice Hall

courses.name: Acoustics and Noise Control
courses.purpose: Fundamentals of generation and radiation of sounds. Sources of noise and control methods. Sound and vibration measurement and analysis.
courses.description: Fundamentals of generation and radiation of sounds. Sources of noise and control methods. Sound and vibration measurement and analysis.

courses.name: Introduction to Computational Fluid Dynamics
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental ideas and numerical tools used to solve fluid dynamics problems computationally. It aims to build the background needed to understand finite volume methods, numerical stability, and error control in fluid mechanics and heat transfer applications.
courses.description: Finite volume methods (FVM) as a means of solving different types of differential equations that arise in fluid dynamics. Fundamentals of numerical analysis, ordinary differential equations and partial differential equations related to fluid mechanics and heat transfer. Error control and stability considerations. Numerical solutions to the Navier-Stokes equations.

courses.name: Heat Exchanger Design
courses.purpose: To introduce the principles of design, manufacture, and use of major types of heat exchangers.
courses.description: Applications of thermodynamics, heat transfer, fluid mechanics laws, and empirical relations for common heat exchangers including boilers, condensers, evaporators, and cooling towers.

courses.name: Industrial Fluid Power
courses.purpose: This course introduces the basic principles of hydraulic and pneumatic power systems and their industrial applications.
courses.description: Basic principles, introduction to hydraulic and pneumatic power systems. Hydraulic power systems: hydraulic fluids; distribution system; energy input and transfer devices; energy modulation devices; energy output and transfer devices; other components such as reservoirs, filters, and accumulators; system design and circuit analysis. Pneumatic power systems. Case studies.

courses.name: Mechatronic Systems
courses.purpose: To introduce the fundamental components and principles of mechatronic systems and show how mechanical, electronic, sensing, actuation, and control elements are integrated into a working system.
courses.description: Introduction to analog electronics, semiconductor electronics, operational amplifiers and analog signal processing, digital electronics, microcontrollers, actuators, sensors, data acquisition, and systems integration.

courses.name: Applied Thermodynamics
courses.purpose: Design and analysis of devices and thermal processes using principles of thermodynamics.
courses.description: Examples include nuclear power stations, jet engines, energy conversion, internal combustion engines, desalination and others.

courses.name: Renewable Energy Systems
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the fundamental principles of energy conversion and storage relevant to renewable energy technologies. It also provides a basis for understanding direct energy conversion devices and the physical principles behind them.
courses.description: Principles of energy conversion among different states of energy including heat, electricity and optical energy. Direct energy conversion devices for thermic, thermo-ionic, magnetohydrodynamic, photovoltaic energy. Principles and theories of chemical and mechanical energy storage devices.

courses.name: Automotive Engineering
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental engineering principles used in the design and analysis of automobiles. It aims to help students understand how major vehicle subsystems affect performance, safety, and handling.
courses.description: Automotive components. Modeling of vehicle dynamics. Maximum speed and acceleration calculations. Wheels and tires. Brake systems and performance. Suspension system and geometry. Steering system and geometry. Aerodynamics drag and effects on vehicle performance.

courses.name: Machine Elements
courses.description: Stress analysis in 3-D. Stress concentration, factor of safety. Static design criteria; theories of failure for ductile and brittle materials. Fatigue design criteria under mean and combined stresses. Design of shafts. Design of permanent joints; riveted joints, welded joints. Design of bolted joints. Design of springs. Power transmission. Kinematics of gear drives; spur gears, helical gears, bevel gears, worm gears. Design of spur and helical gears. Friction, wear and lubrication. Design of sliding bearings. Selection of rolling element bearings. Design of couplings, clutches and brakes. Design of belt drives; flat belts, V-belts.

courses.name: Applications of Solid Mechanics
courses.description: Mathematical preliminaries, strain and compatibility, stress and equilibrium, displacement- and stress-based solutions of boundary value problems in isotropic linear elasticity, miscellaneous topics.

courses.name: Principles of Turbomachinery
courses.purpose: This course introduces undergraduate students to turbomachinery and helps them transition from thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, and heat transfer to this subject.
courses.description: The course covers several types of turbomachines by combining theory and applications. It explains how gas turbines, pumps, and compressors function, and also relates turbomachines to wind power and three-dimensional effects in axial turbomachines.

courses.name: Mechanical Engineering Design I
courses.purpose: Development of a mechanical engineering project, including design procedures, design selection, and the engineering statement of customer preferences. The course also emphasizes ethical and professional responsibilities of engineers.
courses.description: Projects lead to products. The course covers the development of a mechanical engineering project, design procedures, design selection, and the engineering statement of customer preferences, with attention to ethical and professional responsibilities.

courses.name: Machinery Dynamics
courses.purpose: To introduce the synthesis and analysis of mechanisms used in machinery, with emphasis on understanding how linkages, cams, and gear trains are designed and evaluated.
courses.description: Introduction to the synthesis and analysis of linkages, cams and gear trains. Degree of freedom analysis of mechanisms. Kinematic analysis of mechanisms (position, velocity and acceleration). Graphical and analytical synthesis of mechanisms. Quasi-static and dynamic force analysis of mechanisms. Cams and gear trains.

courses.name: Mechanical Engineering Design II
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to apply mechanical engineering design principles in a group project setting. Students work from conceptual development to a working product while practicing design, analysis, reporting, and presentation skills.
courses.description: Group project stressing the mechanical engineering design principles. Projects spanning from conceptual development to working products. Reports explaining the projects, design steps, analyses complemented by oral presentations.
learning_outcomes: Implement individual designs and analyze their performance based on simulated and real measurements.
Fabricate the designed product by manufacturing and purchasing the individual components and integrating these components to obtain a working machine.
Conduct tests for the resulting product to validate and verify the product’s compatibility to the requirements and constraints of the design.
Prepare informative presentations/reports to communicate project progress and results.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course introduction, Interviews
Hafta 2: Interviews, Guest Seminar: Manufacturing
Hafta 3: Interviews
Hafta 4: Interviews, Guest Seminar: Project Management
Hafta 5: Interviews
Hafta 6: Interviews
Hafta 7: Feedback Meetings, Assignment#1, Peer Evaluation
Hafta 8: Interviews
Hafta 9: Progress presentations
Hafta 10: Interviews
Hafta 11: Interviews, Booklet & Video Assignment
Hafta 12: Interviews
Hafta 13: Interviews
Hafta 14: Final project presentations, Assignment#2

courses.name: Undergraduate Research in ME
courses.purpose: This course provides students with an opportunity to engage in independent, faculty-supervised research in mechanical engineering and to gain familiarity with basic research techniques.
courses.description: Introduction to research techniques in mechanical engineering. Independent study on a research topic under the supervision of a faculty member. Documentation of the results for evaluation by the supervisor.
learning_outcomes: Students demonstrate potential to contribute to research in mechanical engineering
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: This is an independently pursued, research-oriented course with no specific weekly syllabus.

courses.name: Dynamics of Aerospace Vehicles
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts needed to analyze and understand the motion of aerospace vehicles. It aims to build a basis for studying flight dynamics, stability, and control.
courses.description: Introduction to basic aerospace engineering. Introduction to flight and basic aerodynamics. Reference frames and coordinate systems. Frame rotations and Euler angles. Aerospace dynamics: translational and rotational equations of motion. Longitudinal dynamics. Lateral dynamics. Discussion of static stability, trim and dynamic stability. Approximate and linear equations of motion. Basic longitudinal and lateral controls.

courses.name: Notation
courses.purpose: To provide students with a detailed overview of conventional and contemporary notational techniques and to develop the ability to write idiomatically for instruments.
courses.description: A detailed overview of conventional and contemporary notational techniques, writing idiomatically for instruments.

courses.name: Theory I: Fundamentals
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts of music theory and the basic tools needed to analyze and understand tonal music.
courses.description: Fundamentals of music theory: elements of pitch and rhythm, triads and seventh chords, chord-labeling. Fundamentals of voice-leading and consonant harmony: principles of voice-leading, part-writing for triads in root-position and inversion, harmonic progression and the sequence. Cadences, small formal units, and non-chord-tones.

courses.name: Instrumentation
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' knowledge and skills in instrumentation and orchestration, with emphasis on the characteristics and practical use of orchestral instruments and ensembles. It also supports the ability to write effectively for different instrumental groupings through applied scoring work.
courses.description: Orchestrational styles and techniques of the 18th and early 19th centuries. Representative scores from the literature and assignments in scoring for various instrumental ensembles.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate theoretical, technical and practical knowledge, instrumentational and notational skills pertaining to the woodwind and brass instruments in following areas: structure and acoustical preconditions, historical evolution, members of corresponding instrumental family and their peculiarities, conventional and extended playing techniques, sound production and parts of instruments operative therein, properties of sounding, tessitura etc

Devise original submissions on a weekly basis that demonstrate thorough knowledge of key ideas, instrumentation-practical and textural skills, ability to think up and formulate appropriate parts for individual instruments and their groupings, and engagement with material and visions presented in projects.
Work progressively towards a deadline with regular partial submissions of an extensive project handling choirs, large ensembles or orchestras of wind instruments.

Show ability to draw on acquired knowledge of instrumentation, music-practical experiences, critical thinking and creativity, applying music theory and analytical skills critically, linking and synthesizing diverse fields such as harmony and voice-leading, form and style imaginatively.

Explore thoroughly creative works, i.e. music scores and their executions, scholarly and music-theoretical sources to discover others’ ideas and skills about course contents and refer to those correctly, providing as well specific examples to allow illustration and support of own ideas effectively.

Communicate and justify findings, ideas and creations concerning own instrumentational designs through sufficiently sophisticated, coherent and logical ways of music notation and further verbal explanations
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course
Hafta 2: Physical and psychoacoustic aspects of instrumental sound production
Hafta 3: Voice (Speech & Singing)
Hafta 4: Flute
Hafta 5: Clarinet, Saxophone
Hafta 6: Oboe, Bassoon
Hafta 7: Trumpet, Trombone, Horn, Tuba
Hafta 8: Violin, Viola
Hafta 9: Cello, Double Bass
Hafta 10: Percussion (Unpitched)
Hafta 11: Percussion (Unpitched, continued)
Hafta 12: Percussion (Pitched)
Hafta 13: Piano, Accordion, Synthesizer
Hafta 14: Harp
Hafta 15: Guitar (Classical and Electric)
Hafta 16: Final Project Discussion
resources: The Study of Orchestration 4th ed., Samuel Adler, 2016, W.W. Norton & Company
The Technique of Orchestration, Kennan, Kent W. and Grantham, Donald, 2001/6., Pearson
Orchestration, Piston, Walter, 1955, W. W. Norton & Company, Inc.
Behind bars : the definitive guide to music notation, Gould, Elaine, 2011, Faber Music

courses.name: Theory II: Harmony and Voice Leading
courses.purpose: This course develops students’ ability to understand, analyze, and realize tonal harmony beyond the basics, with emphasis on chromatic harmony, voice leading, and harmonic function. It prepares students to work with more advanced harmonic progressions and formal structures in Western art music.
courses.description: Dissonant harmony: the dominant seventh chord and other types of seventh chords in context, secondary functions and modulation. Harmonic analysis of Bach chorales. Overview of larger forms. Chromaticism: mode mixture and the Neapolitan, augmented-sixth chords, enharmonicism. Further elements of harmonic vocabulary: substitution, alteration, dominant ninth chord, common-tone diminished seventh chord, elements of post-romantic vocabulary and beyond, etc.
learning_outcomes: Aurally recognize and analyze simple harmonic progressions in diatonic environment with triads in root position and inversion.

Identify, analyze and write harmonic progressions with chromatic alterations, including secondary dominants and leading-tone chords, distinguishing tonicization, modulation and techniques pertaining to them.

Harmonize melodies and bass lines applying chromatic relationships with triads and 7th chords in root position and inversion, exercising principles of proper voice-leading, part-writing and chord-spacing in four-part choral medium.
Demonstrate knowledge of basic phrase types and some larger formal structures typical of 18th century styles.
Develop working knowledge of the structure of music, demonstrated through written, applied, and aural mediums, based on fundamental theoretical principles.
Able to apply knowledge of art and classical music tradition
Harmonically analyze a four-part chorale texture distinguishing between non-chord tones and chord tones, naming each non-chord tone and labeling chords, and identifying cadences.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Triads in Second Inversion and Cadences (review): Musical form (phrases, theme) • Cadences and harmonic rhythm • Motive, phrase, period, and sentence • Bass and soprano harmonization, formal considerations • Ear-Training (ET)
Hafta 2: Review of bass and soprano harmonization with triads in second inversion • ET

Hafta 3: Nonchord Tones (1): Harmonic analysis - Identifying chord and nonchord tones, chord labeling • Classification of nonchord tones (1) : Passing tones, neighboring tones, suspensions and retardations • Embellishing a simple texture • Analysing Bach’s Chorales • ET

Hafta 4: Nonchord Tones (2): Harmonic analysis - Classification of nonchord tones (2) : Appogiaturas, escape tones, the neighbor group, anticipations, the pedal point • Special problems in the analysis of nonchord tones • ET
Hafta 5: Review of nonchord-tone and harmonic analysis • ET

Hafta 6: Secondary Functions (1) / Introduction to chromaticism and altered chords • Secondary dominant chords • Spelling secondary dominants • Recognizing secondary dominants • Secondary dominants in context • ET

Hafta 7: Secondary Functions (2) / Secondary leading-tone chords • Spelling secondary leading-tone chords • Recognizing secondary leading-tone chords • Secondary leading-tone chords in context • Sequences involving secondary functions • Deceptive resolutions of secondary functions • Other secondary functions • Review of secondary functions and modulation with bass harmonization and harmonic analysis for the Midterm Exam • ET • Midterm Exam : secondary functions and modulation with bass harmonization, harmonic analysis
Hafta 8: Secondary functions and modulation with soprano harmonization and harmonic analysis • ET

Hafta 9: Modulations (1) Using Diatonic Common Chords / Modulation and change of key • Modulation and tonicization • Key relationships • Common-chord modulation • Analyzing common-chord modulation • ET
Hafta 10: Modulations (2) Using Some Other Techniques / Altered chords as common-chords • Sequential modulation • Modulation by common-tone • Monophonic modulation • Direct modulation • ET
Hafta 11: Review of modulations with soprano harmonization • ET

Hafta 12: The V7 Chord / Considerations of voice-leading • The approach to the 7th • The V7 in root position • The V7 in three parts • Other resolutions of the V7 • The inverted V7 chord • The V5/6 chord • The V3/4 chord • The V2 chord • ET
Hafta 13: The II7 and VII7 Chords / Considerations of voice-leading • The II7 chord • The VII7 chord in major • The VII7 chord in minor • ET
Hafta 14: Review of seventh-chords with bass and soprano harmonization for the Final Exam • ET • Final Exam : bass and soprano realization involving 7th-chords, harmonic analysis
resources: Tonal harmony : with an introduction to twentieth-century music, Kostka, S. M.; Payne, D.; Almén, B., 2013/7th, McGraw-Hill
Workbook for tonal harmony : with an introduction to twentieth-century music, Kostka, S. M.; Payne, D.; Almén, B., 2013/7th, McGraw-Hill
Tonal harmony : with an introduction to twentieth-century music (2 CDs), Kostka, S. M.; Payne, D.; Almén, B., 2013/7th, McGraw-Hill
Traité d'harmonie théorique et pratique, Dubois, T., 1921, Heugel
Harmony, Piston, W.; DeVoto, M., 1987/5th, Norton
Harmonic practice in tonal music, Gauldin, R., 2004/2nd, Norton
Classical form : a theory of formal functions for the instrumental music of Haydn, Mozart, and Beeth, Caplin, W. E., 1998, Oxford University Press

courses.name: The Culture and Basics of Music Making
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a broad understanding of the cultural, historical, and structural foundations of music making. It introduces major genres, performance practices, and compositional approaches across diverse musical traditions.
courses.description: The course focuses on the definitions, compositional techniques, performance practices, and structural materials of major music genres. It covers a wide range of historical and cultural contexts and discusses styles including classical music, pop music, jazz, funk, electronic music, song, concerto, opera, madrigal, symphony, and ballet.
learning_outcomes: Analyze aural examples of a varied repertoire of music, representing diverse genres and cultures, by describing the uses of elements of music and expressive devices
Evaluate a performance, composition, arrangement, or improvisation by comparing it to similar or exemplary models
Classify by genre or style and by historical period or culture unfamiliar but representative aural examples of music and explain the reasoning behind their classification
Engage with music creatively by designing instruments and notation systems
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Categories of Music; brief definitions through history, concert venues, instrumentation, audience, performance practice, and short examples; ancient music, folk/ethnic music, classical music, classical Turkish music, jazz, pop, electronic music; homework essay comparing the music in terms of the relationship between the music and the listener
Hafta 2: Brief genre definitions; song, concerto, symphony, opera, oratorio, cantata, music theater, multimedia, installation
Hafta 3: Parameters of music; rhythm, tempo, melody, harmony, and form
Hafta 4: Song; concept, history, and form; listening examples including medieval forme fix, madrigal, Schubert Lied, Hacı Arif Bey, jazz standards, Beatles, and contemporary examples
Hafta 5: Song (continued); analyses of 19th century Lied, Beatles, and jazz standard; assignment essay on popular music concert review
Hafta 6: Orchestra and its instruments
Hafta 7: General review for midterm examination
Hafta 8: Midterm examination
Hafta 9: Symphony and extended composition; concept, history, and form; listening examples including Beethoven Symphony, extended jazz/big band composition, and extended rock pieces

Hafta 10: Concerto and virtuosity; concept, history, and form; listening examples including Baroque concerto, 18th/19th century examples, jazz concerto, new music concerto, virtuosity, and rock music and virtuosity; assignment essay on classical music concert review
Hafta 11: Music and drama; concept, history, and genres; listening examples including Handel opera example, Wagner opera example, new music opera example, and rock opera/concept album example

Hafta 12: Music and drama; concept, history, and genres; listening examples including Handel opera example, Wagner opera example, new music opera example, and rock opera/concept album example; assignment essay on analytical paper

Hafta 13: Music and dance; concept, history, and genres; listening examples including Baroque stylized dances, Tchaikovsky ballet example, Le Sacre du Printempts, and funk examples by James Brown/Michael Jackson
Hafta 14: General review for the final examination
resources: The Enjoyment of Music, Machlis & Forney, Tenth Edition, W.W. Norton
Resonances: Engaging Music in Its Cultural Context, Esther M. Morgan-Ellis, 2020, University of North Georgia

courses.name: Origins of Western Music: From Antiquities to Baroque
courses.purpose: This course provides a general view of the content and boundaries of different periods in Western Music History.
courses.description: Music in Antiquity. Roman Liturgy. The genesis of polyphony. Ars nova. Ars subtilior. Burgundy in the Fifteenth Century. Franco-Flemish Composers. Reformation. Instrumental music during the age of Renaissance.

courses.name: Orchestration
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' understanding of orchestration techniques and conventions, with a particular focus on 19th-century practices.
courses.description: Advanced topics in orchestration focusing on 19th century techniques and conventions.

courses.name: Opera and Instrumental Music in the Baroque Period
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the development of opera and instrumental music in the Baroque period. It aims to build historical understanding of major genres, composers, and stylistic changes in Western art music.
courses.description: Florentine Camerata. Birth of Opera. Early Baroque vocal music genres. Early Baroque instrumental music genres. Monteverdi Operas. Opera in Rome, Venice and Naples in seventeenth century. Development of keyboard music in the late seventeenth century. The evolution of sonata. Genesis of Orchestral Music during the late seventeenth century. The evolution of concerto.
learning_outcomes: Able to acquire and apply knowledge of the history of classical music tradition through analytical skills
Able to identify different periods of the history of classical music
Able to classify specific structural and stylistic attributes of different periods of the history of classical music
Able to recognize the social and political context of music in history
Able to convey analytical and critical ideas relevantly based on a coherent outline before an professional audience
Able to develop writing skills in an academic format to expose analytical and critical ideas relevantly based on a coherent outline
Able to develop skills of research and writing about the history, traditions, practices, and cultures of Western art-music
Develop a working knowledge of all appropriate aspects related to the classical music history and its relation to other arts.
Recognize the need for, and an ability to engage in life-long learning
Develop skills on how to access to the authoritative sources of classical music history
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: A general review of the first semester and introduction to HWMII/ General view to Baroque and Birth of Opera
Hafta 2: Early Operas and Monteverdi’s Orfeo
Hafta 3: Concertato Medium and the Grand Concerto
Hafta 4: Cantata, Oratorio and Other Early vocal chamber music genres. Instrumental Music of the Early Baroque: Genres with continuous texture
Hafta 5: Instrumental Music of the Early Baroque: Genres with discontinuous texture Variations and Dance Music, French Lute and Keyboard Music
Hafta 6: Midterm Presentations
Hafta 7: Opera in Rome and Venice in the 17th century Opera in France and England in the late 17th century
Hafta 8: The North German Tradition: Toccata, fugue and genres based on chorale
Hafta 9: Sonata during 1660-1710
Hafta 10: Corelli and Trio Sonata
Hafta 11: Ensemble Music and the Concerto Grosso
Hafta 12: Music of Vivaldi
Hafta 13: Music of Vivaldi
Hafta 14: Discussions on Baroque and Classical as concepts Study for final exam
resources: Opera and Instrumental Music in the Baroque Period, Onur Türkmen, 2015, https://bilkentcomposition.wordpress.com/
A History of Western Music, 10th Edition, J. Peter Burkholder, Donald J. Grout, Claude V. Palisca, 2019, W.W.Norton &Company
Norton Anthology of Western Music 8th Edition, Vol.1, J. Peter Burkholder, Claude V. Palisca, 2019, W.W.Norton &Company
A History of Western Music, J. Peter Burkholder, Donald J. Grout, Claude V. Palisca, 2019, W. W. Norton & Company, Inc.

courses.name: Theory III: Modal Counterpoint
courses.description: Sixteenth century polyphony with a module for harmony review. Advanced chromatic harmony: bass and soprano harmonization with larger and more difficult examples, review of chromaticism, enharmonicism, and alteration in more diversified and complex environments. Counterpoint: two-and three-part species and free counterpoint, imitation.

courses.name: Music in Europe during the Age of the Enlightenment
courses.description: Handel. Rameau. J.S.Bach. The age of enlightenment and early classical period. Italian Opera Buffa in the early classical period. Opera Seria. Gluck and the opera reform. Haydn. Mozart.

courses.name: Theory IV: Classical Form
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' analytical understanding of classical instrumental forms and selected contrapuntal techniques, while also introducing key ideas in twentieth-century music theory. It supports the ability to recognize formal functions, harmonic organization, and stylistic features in music from the classical and post-tonal traditions.
courses.description: Instrumental forms in Classical Style with a short introduction to twentieth century music. Form and analysis: some contrapuntal forms and the fugue. Formal elements in the instrumental music of the classical era. Tight-knit themes, looser formal regions, full-movement forms such as sonata form, slow-movement forms, minuet/trio form, rondo forms, and concerto form. Some vocal forms and the lied. Ostinato forms, variations, suite. Overview on the practices of twentieth century music: new techniques and materials, post-tonal theory.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate aural analysis of chorales by J. S. Bach, i.e. four-part homophonic textures including non-chord tones, cadences, tonicization and modulation, etc. using proper analytical symbols.

Identify and analyze, vis-a-vis written source and/or aurally, conventional theme types in the classical style examining involved harmonic progressions, formal functions, segments, and subjacent structural relationships.
Add 1 or 2 part/s to a given cantus firmus in accordance with the conventions of strict (species), free, and imitative counterpoint respectively.
Harmonize larger excerpts implementing non-chord tones, with an improved insight on textural, formal and stylistic relationships, and a reference to the acquired skills in counterpoint.
Develop working knowledge of the structure of music, demonstrated through written, applied, and aural mediums, based on fundamental theoretical principles.
Able to apply knowledge of art and classical music tradition
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Two-part Counterpoint / Third and fourth species (review) • Ear-Training (ET)
Hafta 2: Review of third and fourth species • ET
Hafta 3: Fifth species preparatory exercises • Rules • Non-chord tones • The Cambiata figure • The counterpoint • ET

Hafta 4: Two-part Free Counterpoint: Rules • The problem of text-setting and prosody • Examples and exercises • Two-part Imitative Counterpoint: The “manner of writing” • Imitation vis-à-vis melodic independence • Types of imitation: strict, free, tonal, and real • Styles and histories • Examples and exercises • ET
Hafta 5: Review of fifth species, free counterpoint, and imitative counterpoint • ET

Hafta 6: The Classical Form : A theory of formal functions by Caplin // Preliminaries / Some basic formal functions: An overview • Sentence • Period • Small ternary • Framing functions • Interthematic functions: A sonata exposition • Fundamental progressions of harmony: Harmonic functions and progressions • Tight-Knit Themes (1) : Sentence • Presentation phrase: Basic idea, repetition of basic idea, tonic prolongation, etc. • Continuation phrase: Continuation function, fragmentation, acceleration of harmonic rhythm, cadential function, etc. • Deviations from the norm
Hafta 7: Review of sentence, etc., for the Midterm Exam • Midterm Exam : Harmonic and formal analysis with sentence, etc.
Hafta 8: Overview of sentence and period in comparison

Hafta 9: Tight-Knit Themes (2) : Period • Antecedant phrase: Contrasting idea, weak cadential closure, etc. • Consequent phrase: harmonic organization, modulating consequent and cadential strength, etc. • Deviations from the norm
Hafta 10: Period (continued)
Hafta 11: Review of sentence and period in comparison

Hafta 12: Tight-Knit Themes (3) : Hybrid Themes • Hybrid 1: antecedent + continuation • Hybrid 2: antecedent + cadential • Hybrid 3: compound basic idea + continuation • Hybrid 4: compound basic idea + consequent / Tight-Knit Themes (4) : Compound Themes • Sixteen-measure period and its three categories • Antecedent = presentation + continuation • Antecedent = compound basic idea + continuation • Antecedent = antecedent + continuation • Sixteen-measure sentence: the model form, compressed continuation

Hafta 13: Tight-Knit Themes (5) : Small Ternary • Exposition • Contrasting middle • Recapitulation • Tight-knit versus loose organization / Small Binary • First Part • Second Part • Presence of contrasting middle • Contrasting middle + continuation • Contrasting middle + cadential • Contrasting middle + consequent • Contrasting middle + further dominant prolongation • Absence of contrasting middle

Hafta 14: Review of sentence, period, hybrid, compound, small ternary, and binary, etc., for the Final Exam • Final Exam : Harmonic and formal analysis with sentence, period, hybrid, compound, small ternary, and binary, etc.
resources: Counterpoint : The Polyphonic Vocal Style of the Sixteenth Century, Jeppesen, K., 1992, Dover
Classical form : a theory of formal functions for the instrumental music of Haydn, Mozart, and Beeth, Caplin, W. E., 1998, Oxford University Press
Tonal harmony : with an introduction to twentieth-century music, Kostka, S. M.; Payne, D.; Almén, B., 2013/7th, McGraw-Hill
Workbook for tonal harmony : with an introduction to twentieth-century music, Kostka, S. M.; Payne, D.; Almén, B., 2013/7th, McGraw-Hill
Tonal harmony : with an introduction to twentieth-century music (2 CDs), Kostka, S. M.; Payne, D.; Almén, B., 2013/7th, McGraw-Hill

courses.name: 19th Century Music and Modernity
courses.description: Beethoven. Opera trends in the early nineteenth century. Hector Berlioz. Chamber and orchestral musical innovations of the nineteenth century. Nineteenth century Italian Opera. Verdi Operas. Birth of romantic German Opera. Richard Wagner. The German, Austrian and French composers of the late nineteenth century. French naturalism in Opera. Verismo opera. Nationalism in music and its new trends during late nineteenth century. Debussy.
learning_outcomes: Able to acquire and apply knowledge of the history of classical music tradition through analytical skills
Able to identify different periods of the history of classical music
Able to classify specific structural and stylistic attributes of different periods of the history of classical music
Able to recognize the social and political context of music in history

Able to recognize analytical associations between specific structural and stylistic attributes of different periods of the history of classical music and the social, political context of the same eras
Able to recognize associations between specific structural and stylistic attributes of different periods of the history of classical music and the performance practices of the same eras
Able to convey analytical and critical ideas relevantly based on an analytical context through a coherent outline before an professional audience
Able to develop writing skills in an academic format to expose analytical and critical ideas relevantly based on a coherent outline
Will develop critical and analytical thinking and skills of writing about the history, traditions, practices, and cultures of the Western art-music tradition
Able to develop skills of research and writing about the history, traditions,practices, and cultures of Western art-music
Able to function on multidisciplinary teams
Develop skills on how to access to the authoritative sources of classical music history
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Beethoven’s early period Beethoven’s middle period – Symphonies
Hafta 2: Beethoven’s middle period – Piano Sonatas and Concertos Beethoven’s late period – Piano Pieces
Hafta 3: Beethoven’s late period – 9th Symphony and late quartets Beethoven and his contemporaries
Hafta 4: Definition of Romanticism Orchestral Music in the first part of the 19th Century
Hafta 5: Piano Music of Mendelssohn, Chopin, Liszt (early period) Piano Music of Schubert and Schumann
Hafta 6: Lied
Hafta 7: Midterm Presentations
Hafta 8: Chamber Music in the 19th century French Opera in the 19th century
Hafta 9: Italian Opera in the 19th century Verdi Operas
Hafta 10: German Opera and Wagner Wagner Operas
Hafta 11: Late Romantic Opera in France and Italy War of The Romantics (F.Liszt)
Hafta 12: War of The Romantics (J.Brahms) Late Romantic Music in Russia –Mighty Five
Hafta 13: Late Romantic Music in Russia –Tchaikovsky Late Romantic Music in France
Hafta 14: Music of Claude Debussy Turn of the century and Expressionism
resources: Required - Textbook: A History of Western Music, 10th Edition, J. Peter Burkholder, Donald J. Grout, Claude V. Palisca, 2019, W.W.Norton &Company
Recommended - Lecture: 19th Century Music and Modernity, Onur Türkmen, 2005, bilkentcomposition.wordpress.com [[download](http://bilkentcomposition.files.wordpress.com/2011/12/hwm-iv.pdf)]
Course Book - Textbook: Norton Anthology of Western Music 8th Edition Vol. 2, J. Peter Burkholder, Claude V. Palisca, 2019, W.W.Norton &Company
Recommended - Web Link: A History of Western Music , J. Peter Burkholder, Donald J. Grout, Claude V. Palisca, 2019, W. W. Norton & Company, Inc. [[download](http://digital.wwnorton.com/hwm10)]

courses.name: Advanced Orchestration II
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop advanced orchestration skills by focusing on 20th-century techniques, conventions, and contemporary approaches to writing for instruments and ensembles. It also supports students in applying orchestration knowledge creatively and analytically in larger-scale musical contexts.
courses.description: Advanced topics in orchestration focusing on 20th century techniques and conventions.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate advanced theoretical and technical knowledge alongside practical skills in transcribing music and analyzing scores from literature for double and triple orchestra
Work progressively towards a deadline with regular partial submissions of extensive projects handling medium- to large-sized orchestral powers idiomatically.

Illustrate intensive engagement, intellectually as well as concerning compositorial craft, with presented course materials and visions next to rather abstract fields of tension such as style, modernism, cultural location, etc.

Show advanced ability to draw on both previously learned and recently acquired knowledge of instrumentation, music-practical experiences, critical thinking and creativity, applying music theory and analytical skills critically, linking and synthesizing diverse fields such as harmony and voice-leading, form, style, historical and genre-typical characteristics imaginatively.

Explore thoroughly creative works, i.e. music scores and their executions, scholarly and music-theoretical sources to discover others’ ideas and skills about course contents and refer to those correctly, providing as well specific examples to allow illustration and support of own complex original ideas effectively.

Communicate and justify findings, ideas and creations concerning own orchestral designs through sufficiently sophisticated, coherent and logical ways of music notation and further verbal explanations.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the Course
Hafta 2: Writing for Voice; Guitar (Classical and Electric); Accordion
Hafta 3: Parametric Decoupling and Prescriptive Notation; Instrumental Timbre Revisited
Hafta 4: Computer-assisted Orchestration I: Tools and Techniques
Hafta 5: Computer-assisted Orchestration II: Creating a Workspace; Computer-assisted Orchestration III: Static Sounds
Hafta 6: Computer-assisted Orchestration VI: Dynamic Sounds
Hafta 7: Sound Mimesis: A Historical and Cultural Overview; Instrumental Imitation of the Human Voice I
Hafta 8: Instrumental Imitation of the Human Voice
Hafta 9: Computer-assisted Orchestration in Practice: Speakings; Orchestrating Natural Acoustic Phenomena I: Le Lac Revisited
Hafta 10: Orchestrating Natural Acoustic Phenomena II; Orchestrating Animal Vocalizations
Hafta 11: Found and Prepared Instruments; Orchestrating Industrial Sounds I
Hafta 12: Orchestrating Industrial Sounds II; Computer-assisted Orchestration in Practice II
Hafta 13: Orchestrating Synthetic Sounds; Computer-assisted Orchestration in Practice III
Hafta 14: Computer-assisted Orchestration with Live Electronics
resources: The Study of Orchestration, 4th ed., Samuel Adler, 2016, W.W. Norton & Company
Max 7 or 8 with the Orchidea computer-assisted orchestration package
OpenMusic
Praat – computational phonetics software
DAW (GarageBand, Logic, Ableton Live etc.)
Bärenreiter's “Contemporary instrumental and vocal techniques” book series
Contemporary compositional techniques and OpenMusic, Hirs, Rozalie and Gilmore, Bob (ed.)
The Technique of Orchestration, Kennan, Kent W. and Grantham, Donald, 2001/6., Pearson
Orchestration, Piston, Walter, 1955, W. W. Norton & Company, Inc.

courses.name: Tonal Counterpoint
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' understanding of contrapuntal writing in the Baroque tradition, with particular focus on imitation techniques and the style of J. S. Bach. It also prepares students to apply these techniques through composition and analysis.
courses.description: Contrapuntal practices of the Baroque era, with special emphasis on imitation techniques and the "Art of Fugue". The style of J. S. Bach, his predecessors, contemporaries, and followers. Regular assignments, including composition exercises, as well as the analysis of short compositions in the related styles are compulsory.
learning_outcomes: Apply the knowledge of five contrapuntal species in the 16th century polyphonic style
Integrate invertible counterpoint within exercises in the 18th century polyphonic style
Compose pieces (i.e., inventions, passacaglias, chorales, sections of fugues, complete fugues, etc.) in the polyphonic style of 18th century
Identify the fundamental aspects of the polyphonic style of the baroque period
Able to analyze the structure of fugue, i.e., form, elements, specific sections, tonal plan, écriture, etc., and compose parts of it with the corresponding writing skills
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction and the principles of the melodic line
Hafta 2: Principles of 2-voice counterpoint: 1:1 and 2:1
Hafta 3: Chromaticism in 2-voice counterpoint
Hafta 4: 2-voice counterpoint cont. 3:1, 4:1, syncopation, suspension etc…
Hafta 5: Canon
Hafta 6: Invertible counterpoint
Hafta 7: 2-part Invention
Hafta 8: 3-voice counterpoint
Hafta 9: Imitation in 3-voice counterpoint
Hafta 10: Fugue - Exposition
Hafta 11: Fugue - Episodes, middle entries etc..
Hafta 12: Fugue - double and triple fugues
Hafta 13: Fugue - Analyzing fugues from WTC
Hafta 14: Final
resources: Counterpoint, Kent Kennan, 4th Edition

courses.name: Theory and Analysis of Sonata Forms
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' analytical writing skills in music and their ability to critically evaluate major approaches to sonata form analysis.
courses.description: Focus on the Classical style: Haydn, Mozart, Beethoven. A development of skills in analytical writing in music and a critical assessment of two analytical methodologies pertaining to sonata form: Hepokoski and Darcy's Sonata Theory and William Caplin's Formal Functions. Concepts developed through analysis and model composition.

courses.name: History of 20th Century Music
courses.description: Mahler and Strauss in comparison. Igor Stravinsky. Post-modernism. Atonality. Serialism. Neo-classicism. Integral serialism. Indeterminacy. Timbre. Minimalism. Spectral music.

courses.name: Introduction to Composition
courses.purpose: For students with little or no previous experience in composition, this course introduces fundamental ways of thinking about and organizing musical material. It aims to develop basic compositional skills through guided feedback, listening, and practical exercises.
courses.description: Exploration of ways of thinking about and organizing basic material elements such as melody, harmony, rhythm and timbre, as well as developing skills of score preparation and analytical listening. Exposition to a variety of ideas and techniques, based very broadly on Western "art" music of the 20th century, but not attempting to guide towards any particular style. The primary emphasis is on learning through feedback, via individual and group meetings as well as frequent opportunities to hear compositional exercises performed and recorded.

courses.name: Post - Tonal Theory
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a deep understanding of post-tonal compositional styles and analytical techniques from the first half of the 20th century. It develops the ability to analyze music using traditional pitch-centered methods, set theory, and approaches that focus on rhythm, timbre, texture, and related musical elements.
courses.description: In-depth survey of compositional styles and techniques of the first half of the 20th century. Traditional pitch-centered analysis, including set theory, as well as approaches focusing on rhythm, timbre, texture, and other elements. Concepts developed through intensive analysis and model compositions.
learning_outcomes: Students will develop a critical understanding of set theory analysis pertaining to the early 20th century music.
Students will be able to apply the analytical techniques in an analysis.
Students will develop skills in analytical writing in music through a final term essay.
On successful completion of the course, students will be able to prepare a comprehensive presentation applying set theory analysis methods to a specific piece of music from the repertoire.
On successful completion of the course, students will be able to compose three different pieces of music within the early 20th century conventions and practice.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Set Theory; Analysis: Webern op. 11/III
Hafta 2: Normal and Prime Forms; Analysis: Berg: Op. 2/ii; Reading: Perle (1977). p. 2-4, p. Jarman (1987). p. 285-288
Hafta 3: Set Theory cont.; Analysis: Schoenberg: Op.11/1; Reading: Wittlich (1974) p. 41-46, Ogdon (1981), p. 169-82
Hafta 4: Set Theory cont.; Analysis: Webern Op. 5 IV; Reading: Beach (1979). p. 19-22, Burkhart (1980)
Hafta 5: Set Theory cont.; Analysis: Schoenberg: Op. 16/III “Farben”; Reading: Burkhart (1974), Forte (1973). p. 166-177
Hafta 6: UNIT 2: TWELVE TONE TOPICS; Webern and symmetry; Analysis: Webern op. 21; Readings: Bailey (1983). p. 184-195, Goldthwaite (1969)
Hafta 7: Schoenberg and hexachordal combinatoriality; Analysis: Schoenberg: Op. 33a; Readings: Graebner
Hafta 8: Stravinsky and rotational array; Analysis: Stravinsky: Requiem Canticles; Readings: TBA
Hafta 9: UNIT 3: Centricity; Introduction; Analysis: Scriabin: Op. 74 / Debussy: Preludes; Readings: NA
Hafta 10: Debussy and Centricity; Analysis: Les Jeux; Readings: Eimert, Pasler (1982). p. 60-75, Kramer (1978)
Hafta 11: Bartok and complementary Z relations; Analysis: String Quartet No. 4; Readings: Antokoletz (1984)
Hafta 12: Stravinsky and octatonicisim; Analysis: Rite of Spring; Readings: Berger
Hafta 13: Stravinsky cont.; Analysis: Rite of Spring; Readings: Van den Toorn, Tymoczko
Hafta 14: Student Presentations
resources: Introduction to Post-Tonal Theory, Straus, Joseph

courses.name: Score Reading III
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' ability to read and realize full orchestral scores, especially from the late Romantic period. It also builds practical skills in understanding orchestration, texture, timbre, balance, and score reduction at the piano.
courses.description: Realization of full scores up to late romantic period. Emphasis on developments in orchestration in the romantic period. Orchestral texture, timbre, balance and aural expectancy. Omission of orchestral doublings in piano realization and voice leading according to hand position. Textural transfer and playing a full score on piano. Applications on transcription.

courses.name: Form and Chromatic Harmony in 19th Century Music
courses.purpose: To explore chromatic harmony in 19th-century instrumental and vocal forms and to examine the development of sonata form after Beethoven.
courses.description: Exploration of chromatic harmony in 19th-century instrumental and vocal forms. Developments of sonata form after Beethoven. Concepts developed through analysis and model compositions.

courses.name: Score Reading IV
courses.purpose: To develop advanced score reading skills for late Romantic and modern orchestral works, with emphasis on understanding orchestral texture, timbre, sound balance, and aural expectancy.
courses.description: Realization of advanced full scores starting from the late Romantic period. Extended information on orchestral texture, timbre, sound balance and aural expectancy. Executing modern scores with extended technique on the piano. Transcriptions and prima vista practices.

courses.name: Theory and Analysis of Contemporary Music
courses.purpose: To provide an in-depth survey of the compositional styles and techniques that emerged in the second half of the 20th century.
courses.description: Topics include post-serialism, New Complexity, Spectralism, Musique Concrete Instrumental, Micropolyphony, Aleatoric music, Chance music, Stochastic music, and more recent developments.

courses.name: Conducting III
courses.purpose: To develop the student's conducting technique at an advanced level and strengthen the ability to communicate musical ideas through gesture. The course also aims to improve expressive control in performance situations.
courses.description: Conductor as a performer. Expressive gestures implying qualities of sound and silence. Performance gestures of dynamics, kinetics, agogics. Transitions of time beating gestures and expressive gestures. Comparative discussions of schools of N. Malko and I. Music on conducting performance technique.

courses.name: Contemporary Turkish Music
courses.purpose: To examine the sociopolitical, cultural, and stylistic background of New Turkish Music and to understand how music life in Turkey developed under European influence from the late Ottoman era onward.
courses.description: Sociopolitical, cultural, and stylistic background of New Turkish Music. European influenced music life, its styles and institutionalization in Turkey beginning with the late Ottoman era. Culture politics, statist music institutionalization, polyphonic styles in the early republican and the post-war Turkey. Acting on the fault lines: stylistic polarization among first two composer generations, alaturka-alafranga and monophony-polyphony debates, nationalization-globalization dilemma, battling paradigms of the Turkish-traditional versus the international-modernized.

courses.name: Music of Igor Stravinsky
courses.description: Studies on the Stravinsky's works with consideration of four different periods: Early pieces, Russian Phase, Neo-Classicism and Serialism.

courses.name: Studies on Modality
courses.purpose: To review the theory and ear training of modes.
courses.description: Review of the theory and ear training of modes. Analyzing examples from Machaut, Dufay, Josquin, Greig, Mussorgsky, Debussy, Bartok, Samuel Barber.

courses.name: Contemporary Notational Techniques
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop students' understanding of how musical notation relates to composition and performance, and to improve their communication skills through notation.
courses.description: The course covers a brief historical overview of music notation, basic notation principles, and contemporary notation styles. It emphasizes handwriting skills and does not use notation software.

courses.name: Conducting IV
courses.purpose: This course aims to develop the student's ability to function as an interpreter of musical works through conducting. It focuses on interpretative analysis, gesture choice, and the planning and execution of rehearsals and performances.
