courses.description: Conductor as interpreter. Interpretative analysis. Analysis of work and choice of gestures. Interpretative aural imagination and its application. Completion and molding of conducting gesture techniques including unmetrical gestures. Planning timing and conducting the rehearsal week and the performance.
learning_outcomes: Will develop working knowledge of the structure of music, demonstrated through written, applied, and aural mediums, based on fundamental theoretical principles.
Analyze and learn scores.
Read the score with inner hearing.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 2: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 3: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 4: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 5: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 6: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 7: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 8: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 9: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 10: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 11: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 12: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 13: Lecture and individual performance
Hafta 14: Lecture and individual performance
resources: The Modern Conductor, Green, Elizabeth A.H. and Mark Gibson, 2004, Seventh Edition, Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Prentice-Hall
The Conductor and His Baton, Malko, Nikolai, 1950, Copenhagen: Wilhelm Hansen
The Conductor and His Score, Malko, Nikolai and Elizabeth A. H. Green, 1975, Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Prentice-Hall
The Techniques of Orchestral Conducting, Musin, Ilia. Translated by Oleg Proskurnya, 2014, Lewiston, NY: Edwin Mellen Press
The Grammar of Conducting, Rudolf, Max, 1994, third edition, New York: Schirmer Books
The Score, the Orchestra, and the Conductor, Meier, Gustav, 2009, New York: Oxford University Press
Conducting Bibliography, Journal of the Conductors Guild, Green, Jonathan D., 2011, Leesburg, VA, The Conductors Guild

courses.name: Source Readings in Music
courses.purpose: This course aims to engage students with important writings and speeches in music history, helping them understand how musical ideas and concepts developed from ancient Greece through the twentieth century.
courses.description: Discussion group based on important writings and speeches in music history from ancient Greece through the twentieth century. Anthological readings on music and musical concepts throughout the ages. Active participation is required.

courses.name: Modern Music Before 1945
courses.purpose: To examine the music of the fin de siècle and the pre-war period in Europe and the U.S.A., and to understand the artistic and socio-cultural environment of the period.
courses.description: Music of fin de siecle and pre-war period in Europe and U.S.A. Observation of the artistic and socio-cultural environment of the related period. Samples of a broad spectrum of musical life in the twentieth century, including orchestral, choral, band, chamber music, and solo repertoire, both instrumental and vocal.

courses.name: Modern Music After 1945
courses.purpose: To provide a socio-historical understanding of post-war music in Europe and the U.S.A., with emphasis on the major artistic trends and technical developments that shaped the period.
courses.description: A socio-historical view on the music of the post-war generation in Europe and the U.S.A. The course focuses on major trends such as avant-gardism and technical developments in music such as serialism in the related period.

courses.name: Piano Literature
courses.purpose: To survey the historical development of the piano and its repertoire with emphasis on cornerstone works. The course also aims to develop listening and interpretive awareness through comparative study of master artists' performances.
courses.description: Survey of the piano's historical development and its repertoire with emphasis on cornerstone works. Comparative listening to master artists' renditions, their interpretations, and style practices. Building programs for concerts, recitals, recordings, and competitions.

courses.name: Nanocomposites
courses.purpose: This course is designed to provide students with a general understanding of the preparation, structure, and properties of nanocomposite materials, to extend their knowledge of materials science and nanomaterials in relation to nanocomposites, and to discuss the importance of nanocomposite materials used in nanotechnology.
courses.description: The course introduces metal, polymer, and ceramic materials as matrices for nanocomposites. It covers approaches for preparing nanocomposite materials and presents their chemical, mechanical, electrical, and optical properties. The course also discusses applications of nanocomposites in industry and biosciences, and includes laboratory demonstrations and experiments on the synthesis, preparation, and characterization of selected nanocomposite materials.

courses.name: Introduction to Nano- and Micro-Scale Quantum Devices
courses.description: Introduction to mesoscopic solid-state physics; what is mesoscale; electronic transport in solids; transport in ballistic, diffusive and quantum regimes; metal-insulator transition; quantum Hall effect; quantum objects; electronic phase coherence; single electron tunneling; superconductivity; experimental methods; cryogenics; electronic measurement.

courses.name: Self- Organized and Self-Assembled Systems From Nanoscience to Biotechnology
courses.description: Complex, adaptive, dynamics systems; pattern formation; self-organization; self-assembly; nonlinear dynamics; equilibrium thermodynamics; nonequilibrium thermodynamics; information theory; networks; scaling; power laws; evolutionary dynamics.

courses.name: Contemporary Turkish Piano Music
courses.purpose: To provide general information on the musical styles of Rey, Erkin, Saygun, and Akses.
courses.description: Observation on the development of the piano literature among these national composers. Detailed analysis and discussion group on the style and performance of the selected repertoire.

courses.name: Introduction to Logic
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the basic notions and methods of logic. It aims to develop the ability to analyze arguments, understand logical consequence and proof, and translate ordinary language into symbolic form.
courses.description: A self-contained introduction to the basic notions of logic, including language, truth, argument, consequence, proof, and counter example. Both propositional logic and predicate logic are studied (their syntax plus semantics), with an emphasis on translating English sentences into logical symbols. A contemporary software package such as Tarski's World may be used to construct derivations of valid arguments.
learning_outcomes: Students demonstrate proficiency in critical thinking and competence in basic set theory.
Students translate English arguments into symbolic form (and vice versa).
Students identify and assess the validity (and invalidity) of simple deductive arguments by means of Venn diagrams and truth tables.
Students apply rules of inference to determine the validity of deductive arguments in propositional and predicate logic, via tree proofs within these symbolic formal systems.
Students demonstrate proficiency in elementary model theory by constructing models (and countermodels) showing validity (respectively, invalidity) of an argument in predicate logic.

Students analyze, by reading it from its original source, an important technical contribution by a major logician and reformulate it in contemporary terminology and symbolism (while appreciating the contribution in its historical context)
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Propositions and Arguments
Hafta 2: The Language of Propositional Logic
Hafta 3: Semantics of Propositional Logic
Hafta 4: Uses of Truth Tables
Hafta 5: Logical Form
Hafta 6: Connectives: Translation and Adequacy
Hafta 7: Trees for Propositional Logic
Hafta 8: The Language of Monadic Predicate Logic
Hafta 9: Semantics of Monadic Predicate Logic
Hafta 10: Trees for Monadic Predicate Logic
Hafta 11: Models, Propositions, and Ways the World Could Be
Hafta 12: General Predicate Logic
Hafta 13: Identity; Metatheory
Hafta 14: Other Methods of Proof
resources: Logic: The Laws of Truth, Nicholas J. J. Smith, 2012, Princeton University Press
An Introduction to Formal Logic, Peter Smith, 2003, Cambridge University Press
The Logic Manual, Volker Halbach, 2010, Oxford University Press

courses.name: Introduction to Philosophy I
courses.purpose: The course raises and examines central problems in theoretical philosophy and investigates them through close reading of influential texts in the history of philosophy.
courses.description: The course raises and examines central problems in theoretical philosophy such as: Is there a world of things that exists independently of human thought and sensation? How can we know the difference between appearance and reality? How does our mind and the physical world relate? How can we know whether there are other minds? Do we freely choose our actions or are they pre-determined? Those problems are investigated through a close reading of influential texts in the history of philosophy.

courses.name: Ancient Philosophy
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the thought of ancient philosophers and the central questions that shaped early philosophical inquiry.
courses.description: This course introduces the thought of ancient philosophers focusing on questions about the purpose of philosophy, the nature of knowledge, virtue and the good life.
learning_outcomes: Students can identify important Greek thinkers and theories of this period, including Pre-socratic Ionian philosophy, Plato, Aristotle and the Hellenistic Schools
Students can describe the social, cultural, intellectual and political context in which these philosophies emerged.
Students can analyze the philosophical theories of important thinkers of the time, and to provide detailed statements and analyses of their argumentation.
Students can conduct themselves responsibly, meet their professional obligations, and contribute to class discussion.
Students can compose effective essays which illustrate their ability to recognize and critique the various philosophical theories studied in this class.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction (Readings, Guthrie, Homer) Presocratics (Melesians AGP 1-14)
Hafta 2: Presocratics Melesians (Readings, AGP21-41, Heraclitus, Parmenides)
Hafta 3: Parmenides, Xenophanes, Intro to Fifth Century (Readings, Euthyphro AGP80-85, 97-114)
Hafta 4: Plato's Euthyphro (Readings, Meno AGP 203-228)
Hafta 5: Plato's Meno (Readings Republic AGP 467-528)
Hafta 6: Plato's Republic (Readings, Republic AGP 467-528)
Hafta 7: Plato's Republic, Parmenides (Readings, Parmenides, AGP 604-613)
Hafta 8: Plato's Apology, Crito (Readings, AGP 115-153)
Hafta 9: Plato's Crito
Hafta 10: Aristotle Intro (Categories AGP 645-662)
Hafta 11: Aristotle, Metaphysics, Physics (AGP 694-726)
Hafta 12: Aristotle, Metaphysics, Physics (AGP 758-808)
Hafta 13: Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics AGP (832-891)
Hafta 14: Hellenistic Overview
resources: Readings in Ancient Greek Philosophy: From Thales to Aristotle, Cohen et al. (Editors), 2005, Hackett

courses.name: Epistemology
courses.description: This course addresses several of the central problems of contemporary epistemology, such as: conceptions of epistemic justification; skeptical arguments and responses to them; foundationalism and coherentism; externalism and internalism; causal theories of knowledge; rationality and cognitive relativism; naturalized epistemology.

courses.name: Rationalists
courses.purpose: This course introduces the rationalist tradition in philosophy through the works of Descartes, Leibniz, and Spinoza.
courses.description: We will look at these philosophers' responses to questions about substance, perception, thought, identity and causality.

courses.name: Ethics
courses.purpose: This course critically examines major accounts of morality, including moral sense, deontological, utilitarian, and intuitionist approaches. It also raises fundamental questions about the basis of value judgments, the role of reason and passion, and the relation between consequences, motives, and individual worth.
courses.description: This course covers central theories and problems in philosophical ethics. It focuses on moral sense theory, deontological ethics, utilitarianism, and intuitionism, and discusses questions about objective versus subjective value, ultimate values, reason and passion, altruism and egoism, and the moral evaluation of actions and persons.
learning_outcomes: Students can identify and explain arguments from a range of key texts in philosophical ethics.
Students can assess arguments, critically evaluate them and apply them to contemporary ethical problems.
Students can explain their own views and the views of other philosophers and to argue for them clearly in both written and oral form.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 2: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 3: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 4: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 5: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 6: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 7: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 8: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 9: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 10: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html

Hafta 13: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
Hafta 14: https://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/html/ethics/syllabus.html
resources: Required - Reading: Course Reader, YS Berkovski, 2026 [download](http://sandyber.github.io/philosophy/teaching.html)

courses.name: Empiricists
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the major works and arguments of the empiricist philosophers Locke, Berkeley, and Hume. It aims to develop students' ability to analyze central problems in modern philosophy, especially those concerning substance, perception, thought, identity, and causality.
courses.description: This course introduces the works of empiricist philosophers Locke, Berkeley, and Hume focusing on the nature of substance, perception, and thought, and philosophical problems about identity and causality.
learning_outcomes: Examine key arguments in the texts in the history of modern philosophy
Construct and defend one's own arguments
Identify the main problems in the given text, suggest possible solutions to the problems
Effectively use class discussions to identify key problems in the given text
Students can compare and evaluate basic arguments and positions of key authors in empiricist philosophy.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction, Locke
Hafta 2: Locke
Hafta 3: Locke
Hafta 4: Locke
Hafta 5: Berkeley
Hafta 6: Berkeley
Hafta 7: Berkeley
Hafta 8: Hume
Hafta 9: Hume
Hafta 10: Hume
Hafta 11: Hume
Hafta 12: Hume
Hafta 13: Hume
Hafta 14: Hume
resources: An Essay concerning Human Understanding, Locke, J., 2008, OUP
Philosophical Writings, Berkeley, G., 2008, CUP
A Treatise of Human Nature, Hume, D., 2000, OUP

courses.name: Introduction to Philosophy II
courses.purpose: The course introduces and examines central problems in practical philosophy, including morality, moral conventionalism, right action, moral obligation, and the nature of a good life. It aims to develop students' ability to think critically about these issues through engagement with influential philosophical texts.
courses.description: The course raises and examines central problems in practical philosophy such as: Is there a single true morality? To what extent is morality conventional? How can we know what is the right and wrong thing to do? Why should I do the right thing? What is it to live one's life well? Those problems are investigated through a close reading of influential texts in the history of philosophy.
learning_outcomes: Students can interpret and evaluate the arguments presented in influential texts in moral philosophy and the philosophy of action.
Students can effectively use structured group work to examine a theoretical problem and evaluate responses to that problem.
Students can present their ideas in a professional fashion, including correct citations and footnotes.
Students can engage critically with a text and present a charitable interpretation of it.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: Metaethics 1
Hafta 3: Metaethics 2
Hafta 4: Metaethics 3
Hafta 5: Morality and Human Nature 1
Hafta 6: Morality and Human Nature 2
Hafta 7: Philosophy of well-being
Hafta 8: Hedonism 1
Hafta 9: Hedonism 2
Hafta 10: Virtue Ethics 1
Hafta 11: Virtue Ethics 2
Hafta 12: Virtue Ethics 3
Hafta 13: Applied Ethics 1
Hafta 14: Applied Ethics 2
resources: Selected Articles via Moodle

courses.name: Political Philosophy
courses.purpose: This course examines central questions in political philosophy, such as when coercion is justified, when disobedience is justified, and what freedom means within a political community.
courses.description: The course offers a close reading of influential philosophical texts on political philosophy, focusing on major debates about coercion, freedom, justice, obedience, and political authority.
learning_outcomes: Students can interpret and evaluate the ideas and arguments presented in influential texts in political philosophy.
Students can independently read and interpret challenging texts.
Students can effectively use structured group work to examine a theoretical problem and evaluate responses to that problem.
Students can identify, clarify and assess the strengths and weaknesses of influential arguments in political philosophy.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Mutual Advantage I
Hafta 2: Mutual Advantage II
Hafta 3: Mutual Advantage III
Hafta 4: Rawls I
Hafta 5: Rawls II
Hafta 6: Rawls III
Hafta 7: Nozick I
Hafta 8: Nozick II
Hafta 9: Nozick III
Hafta 10: Marx I
Hafta 11: Marx II
Hafta 12: Marx III
Hafta 13: Review
Hafta 14: Review
resources: A Theory of Justice, John Rawls, 1971, Havard University Press
Anarchy, State and Utopia, Robert Nozick, 1974, Basic Books
Morals by Agreement, David Gauthier, 1986, Oxford University Press
The Portable Karl Marx, Marx, 1983, Penguin
Justice, Gender, and the Family, Susan Okin, 1991, Basic Books

courses.name: Kant
courses.purpose: This course is based around a close and critical reading of Kant's Critique of Pure Reason. It aims to examine Kant's response to the possibility that the world studied by science is in some sense mind-dependent and/or mind-constructed.
courses.description: The course covers Kant's distinction between a priori and a posteriori knowledge and analytic and synthetic judgments, his argument for synthetic a priori truths, his transcendental deduction of the categories, and his transcendental idealism.

courses.name: Social and Legal Philosophy
courses.purpose: This course examines key issues in legal and social philosophy through selected historical and contemporary texts.
courses.description: The course covers central topics in legal and social philosophy, including the nature of law and its relation to morality, legitimate political authority, rights, the limits of state authority over the individual, the justification of punishment, and arguments for free speech.
learning_outcomes: Students can interpret and critically analyze arguments in a philosophical text, present their results in a clearly structured written form.
Students can apply different legal-theoretical approaches to concrete problems in legal and social philosophy.
Students can identify and critically discuss concepts and arguments in the assigned readings.
Students can demonstrate their understanding of scholarly texts and contemporary case-materials in legal and social philosophy and communicate the contents clearly to an audience.
Students can answer questions on the material.
Students can describe, compare, and evaluate basic arguments and positions in the different areas of legal and social philosophy.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: The Nuremberg trial and the limits of legal authority
Hafta 2: Natural law vs legal positivism I: What is law? Validity and authority
Hafta 3: Natural law vs legal positivism II: Unjust law and the rule of law
Hafta 4: Natural law vs legal positivism III: Responsibility and authority
Hafta 5: Legal obligation I: The principle of fair play
Hafta 6: Legal obligation II: Consent, coercion, and disobedience
Hafta 7: Rights I: Will theory vs. interest theory
Hafta 8: Rights II: Children's rights
Hafta 9: Harm and liberty I: Harm principle
Hafta 10: Harm and liberty II: Freedom of expression
Hafta 11: Harm and liberty III: Hate speech
Hafta 12: Global justice and international law I: Authority, war, and intervention
Hafta 13: Global justice and international law II: Collective responsibility and responsibility to protect (R2P)
Hafta 14: Review
resources: Selected Articles will be available on the moodle page for the course weekly
The Philosophy of Law: Classic and Contemporary Readings with Commentary, Frederick Schauer and Walter Sinnott-Armstrong (eds.), 1995, Oxford University Press

courses.name: Intermediate Logic
courses.purpose: This course builds on PHIL 101 - Introduction to Logic and examines the uses and limitations of formal techniques in the study of language and argument.
courses.description: Topics to be covered include further study of propositional and predicate calculus, including completeness, soundness and decidability results; set-theoretic and semantic paradoxes; introduction to modal and intuitionistic logic; and logic and computability.

courses.name: Philosophy of Language
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to central issues in the philosophy of language and the main philosophical debates surrounding meaning, reference, truth, and related concepts. It also aims to develop students' ability to critically evaluate major theories about language and truth.
courses.description: We discuss key concepts such as truth, meaning, reference, logical form, speech act and metaphor. In addition we critically assess various theories that aim to show what it is for a statement to be true. As preparation, the course commences with a brief recap of key aspects of logic.
learning_outcomes: Students can identify, assess and distinguish philosophical views about language.
Students can present their philosophical ideas and criticism in a clear fashion.
Students can assess the soundness of views propounded in philosophical works.
Students can creatively address and tackle philosophical problems.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: the course, requirements, logic.
Hafta 2: On Sense and Reference, G. Frege
Hafta 3: Naming and Necessity, S. Kripke
Hafta 4: The Causal Theory of Names, G. Evans
Hafta 5: FIRST EXAM (2 hours, open book)
Hafta 6: On Denoting, B. Russell
Hafta 7: Reference and Definite Description, K. Donnellan
Hafta 8: The Meaning of ?Meaning?, H. Putnam
Hafta 9: Frege on Demonstratives, J. Perry
Hafta 10: SECOND EXAM (2 hours, open book)
Hafta 11: Two Dogmas of Empiricism, W.V.O. Quine
Hafta 12: Radical Translation (chapter 2 of Word and Object), W.V.O. Quine
Hafta 13: On The Very Idea of a Conceptual Scheme, D. Davidson
Hafta 14: A Guide to Naturalizing Semantics, B. Loewer
resources: Required - Reading: Will be provided by email

courses.name: Metaphysics
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to central questions and debates in contemporary metaphysics through the study of selected key texts.
courses.description: Focusing on a selection of key texts, this course examines core topics in contemporary metaphysics, such as truth, existence, universals and particulars, causality, modality, perception, knowledge, the a priori, identity, anomalous monism, supervenience, vagueness, and time.

courses.name: Philosophy of Mind
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to key issues in contemporary philosophy of mind and helps them examine major approaches to the mind/body problem. It also aims to develop students’ ability to compare philosophical accounts of mind with broader questions in metaphysics and cognitive science.
courses.description: The course covers dualist, materialist, and functionalist responses to the mind/body problem, along with related topics such as personal identity, consciousness, intentionality, mental causation, behaviorism, identity theory, and machine functionalism. It also considers whether and in what ways the human mind can be compared to a computer.
learning_outcomes: Examine key arguments in the texts in philosophy of mind and evaluate them.
Students can interpret and critically analyze arguments in a philosophical text, present their results in a clearly structured written form.
Students can relate crucial arguments in the philosophy of mind to contemporary views in metaphysics, the philosophy of mind and Cognitive Science.
Present philosophical ideas and criticisms in a clear fashion.
Identify the key problems in the given text and debate possible solutions to them.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction and warm-up: What is Philosophy of Mind? What is Physical? What is Mental? Is there a mark of the mental?
Hafta 2: Mind as a Distinct Substance/Property: Dualism
Hafta 3: Dualism (cont.)
Hafta 4: Mind as Behavior: Behaviorism
Hafta 5: Mind as the Brain: Identity Theory
Hafta 6: Identity Theory (cont.)
Hafta 7: Midterm Exam
Hafta 8: Mind as a Computing Machine: Machine Functionalism
Hafta 9: Machine Functionalism(cont.)
Hafta 10: Consciousness
Hafta 11: Consciousness (cont.)
Hafta 12: Mental Causation
Hafta 13: Mental Causation (cont.)
Hafta 14: Special Sciences
resources: Philosophy of Mind, Jaegwon Kim, 2010, 3, Westview
Phil 308 Course Pack, Collection, Will be made available online

courses.name: History and Philosophy of Science
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to major conceptual developments in the history and philosophy of science, helping them understand how scientific ideas, methods, and standards of explanation have evolved over time.
courses.description: This course offers an introduction to some of the key conceptual developments in the history and philosophy of science, from ancient Greece through to the present day. Topics covered include the logic of scientific discovery, Scientific Revolutions, pseudo-science, philosophical problems of modern physics and biology, laws and explanation.
learning_outcomes: Examine key arguments presented in texts in the philosophy of science.
Students can construct and defend their own arguments.
Identify the key problems in the given text and debate possible solutions to them.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Science and Philosophy
Hafta 2: Aristotle: The First Scientist
Hafta 3: The Scientific Revolution
Hafta 4: The Problem of Induction
Hafta 5: Falsification, Testing, Demarcation, Pseudoscience
Hafta 6: Problems with Falsificationism
Hafta 7: Midterm Exam
Hafta 8: Revolutions and Rationality
Hafta 9: Incommensurability and Relativism
Hafta 10: Theories and Frameworks
Hafta 11: Science and Society
Hafta 12: Science and Genderr
Hafta 13: Holism and Naturalism
Hafta 14: Realism and Anti-Realism
resources: Theory and Reality: An Introduction to the Philosophy of Science, 2nd Edition, P. Godfrey-Smith, 2021, University of Chicago Press
Selected chapters from A Historical Introduction to the Philosophy of Science, 4th Edition, J. Losee, 2001, Oxford University Press
Selected chapters from Philosophy of Science: An Historical Anthology, T. McGrew, M. Alspector-Kelly, F. Allhoff, 2009, Blackwell-Wiley
Short selections from Latour and Woolgar, Keller and Longino

courses.name: Aesthetics
courses.purpose: This course examines key debates in the Philosophy of Art.
courses.description: This course examines key debates in the Philosophy of Art, such as the definition of art, the ontology of artworks, the nature and scope of the aesthetic, expression, representation, interpretation, appreciation, aesthetic value and the value of art, creativity, art and ethics.

courses.name: Advanced Philosophy of Language
courses.purpose: This course continues PHIL 306 Philosophy of Language and introduces more advanced issues and material in the philosophy of language.
courses.description: A continuation of PHIL 306 - Philosophy of Language, delving into advanced material.

courses.name: Advanced Philosophy of Mind
courses.purpose: This course is a continuation of PHIL 308, Philosophy of Mind, and is intended to deepen students' understanding of advanced issues in the philosophy of mind.
courses.description: A continuation of PHIL 308 - Philosophy of Mind, delving into advanced material.

courses.name: Nineteenth Century Philosophy
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the major developments in nineteenth-century philosophy and to the central debates that shaped modern thought. It focuses on the historical and conceptual transitions from Kant to later idealist, existential, and pragmatic traditions.
courses.description: Kantian revolution, German idealism, Schopenhauer's idealism, Kierkegaard's existentialism, British idealism, and American pragmatism.

courses.name: History of Analytic Philosophy
courses.purpose: This course examines the historical development of analytic philosophy, beginning with the foundational contributions of Frege and Russell.
courses.description: The course involves a close reading of key texts from the history of analytic philosophy, including Frege’s Foundations of Arithmetic, Russell’s The Philosophy of Logical Atomism, Wittgenstein’s Philosophical Investigations, and Anscombe’s Intention.

courses.name: Phenomenology
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to central figures and themes in 20th-century phenomenology, helping them understand how phenomenological thinkers approach consciousness, experience, and meaning.
courses.description: An introduction to major figures (Husserl, Heidegger, Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, Scheler, Stein) and topics (intentionality, anti-psychologism, embodiment, intersubjectivity, transcendental idealism, the lifeworld) in 20th century phenomenology.
learning_outcomes: Students can analyze various philosophical problems raised by the phenomenological method.
Students can present their philosophical ideas and criticism in a clear fashion.
Students can assess the soundness of views propounded in philosophical works.
Students can creatively address and tackle philosophical problems.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Husserl: Psychologism, Arithmetic, and the Frege Review
Hafta 2: Husserl: The Structure of Intentionality and Knowledge
Hafta 3: Husserl: The Phenomenological Reduction
Hafta 4: Husserl: Transcendental Idealism and the Lifeworld
Hafta 5: Sartre: The Ego
Hafta 6: Sartre: Imagination and Emotions
Hafta 7: Embodiment
Hafta 8: Embodiment
Hafta 9: Empathy and Intersubjectivity
Hafta 10: Empathy and Intersubjectivity
Hafta 11: Scheler: Value Perception
Hafta 12: Scheler: Value Perception
Hafta 13: Phenomenology of Art
Hafta 14: Phenomenology of Art
resources: Logical Investigations, Edmund Husserl, Routledge
Ideas I, Edmund Husserl, Hackett

courses.name: What is a Mind?
courses.purpose: A topical introduction to philosophy of mind guided by the following questions: What are the major properties and functions of a mind? And in what terms do we think of minds?
courses.description: The course offers a systematic survey of philosophical theories of mind. We begin with the legacy of earlier centuries -- mind/body dualism, consciousness, self, and free will -- then turn to the first scientific response to this legacy -- behaviorism and the rise of scientific psychology -- and examine the major theoretical positions and debates it generated in the 20th century, such as reductive physicalism, functionalism and the computer model of the mind, eliminative materialism, instrumentalism, and commonsense psychology.

courses.name: Medieval Philosophy
courses.purpose: This course gives an overview of philosophy during the middle ages (500-1400) while situating it within a broad social and cultural context.
courses.description: We consider major figures such as St. Augustine, St. Anselm, and St. Thomas, and study some of the main themes of medieval philosophy: the relationship between faith and reason, the existence of God and abstract entities, and the nature of human knowledge.

courses.name: Philosophy of Mathematics
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce and examine major classical and contemporary issues in the philosophy of mathematics.
courses.description: This course covers several classical and contemporary problems in the philosophy of mathematics, such as mathematical truth, the nature of proof, mathematical intuition, the foundations of mathematics, and mathematical knowledge.

courses.name: Foundations of Cognitive Science
courses.purpose: This course introduces the major paradigms in contemporary cognitive science and examines how they differ in their understanding of mind and information processing.
courses.description: We start from two major paradigms in contemporary cognitive science -- the wide and the narrow paradigms. The narrow paradigm, which has been the more popular, is concerned with how information is encoded and computed, particularly in human minds. The main rival theories within the narrow paradigm are the symbol-system view and connectionism. The wide paradigm takes minds to be more than information processors, to come in a variety of kinds, and to operate relative to a variety of parameters -- teleological, regulatory, environmental, and social. According to the wide paradigm even information processing has to be reexamined in the light of such parameters.

courses.name: Consciousness
courses.purpose: This course examines the central philosophical and scientific questions surrounding consciousness, including what consciousness is, why it is puzzling, and why it matters. It aims to introduce students to major debates about the nature, structure, and evolution of consciousness.
courses.description: The course addresses questions such as whether consciousness is one phenomenon or many, the distinction between the relatively easy problem of how consciousness works and the hard problem of how it feels, and the mechanisms and competencies that underpin it. It also considers where consciousness is located in the brain, who possesses it, whether it comes in degrees, and how it develops and evolves. The class is interdisciplinary and examines both philosophical positions and arguments as well as the latest scientific theories and data about consciousness.

courses.name: From the Kitchen to the Streets: An Introduction to Feminism
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to major issues in feminism and to encourage them to develop their own arguments about real-life social and ethical questions.
courses.description: In this course we will critically examine key topics in feminism, including abortion, sexual harassment, pornography, and the politics of work and family. We will also investigate the impact of feminism on language, science, morality, and the way we interact with other cultures. Philosophers have fundamentally contributed to our understanding of what it means to be a woman. So a part of the course will be devoted to studying the place of women in the history of ideas.
learning_outcomes: Develop their own arguments with respect to real life issues.
Critically examine key topics in feminism.
Understand the impact of feminism on language, science, morality, and intercultural relations.
Study the place of women in the history of ideas.

courses.name: Philosophy of Cognitive Science
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to central debates and key texts in the philosophy of cognitive science. It examines how philosophical questions arise around cognition and the scientific study of mind.
courses.description: Focusing on a selection of key texts, the course covers core topics in contemporary philosophy of cognitive science, including memory, theory of mind, modularity, innateness and empiricism, neuroethics, animal cognition, and consciousness.

courses.name: Embodied Cognition
courses.purpose: This course examines major developments in 4E cognitive science and philosophy of mind through a selection of historical and recent texts.
courses.description: Topics include Merleau-Ponty's philosophy of perception, Gibson's ecological psychology, embodied cognition, the extended mind hypothesis, enactivism, and embodied intersubjectivity.

courses.name: Basic Physics: Mechanics
courses.purpose: The course aims to present the basic concepts and laws of mechanics at a level where the relevant mathematical laws do not require prior knowledge of calculus.
courses.description: The topics studied include vectors; translational and rotational kinematics and dynamics of particles and systems; work and energy; collisions; harmonic oscillations.

courses.name: Moral Psychology
courses.purpose: This course aims to use philosophical ethics together with empirical findings from cognitive and behavioral sciences to investigate central questions in ethical theory.
courses.description: This course combines the theoretical resources of philosophical ethics and the empirical resources of cognitive and behavioral sciences. Empirical evidence from the human sciences will be used to examine core problems in ethical theory.
learning_outcomes: Understand how philosophical ethics and empirical research can be combined in the study of moral psychology.
Analyze core problems in ethical theory using evidence from cognitive and behavioral sciences.
Evaluate questions about moral judgment, sentiment and reason, situation and character, evolution, cooperation, moral disagreement, and free will.

courses.name: Basic Physics II
courses.purpose: The course aims to present the basic concepts and laws of electricity and magnetism at a level where the relevant mathematics does not require a priori knowledge of calculus.
courses.description: The topics studied include charge and matter; Coulomb's law; electric field and potential; DC circuits; magnetic field; Ampere's and Faraday's laws.

courses.name: Physics for Poets
courses.purpose: This course aims to present basic ideas of modern science to non-science majors with very little background in mathematics and physics.
courses.description: Scientific objectivity, science of mechanics, Newton's laws, electricity and magnetism, waves, special and general relativity, cosmology, atoms, particles and waves, uncertainty principle, quantum theory, fundamental constituents of matter, forces of nature.

courses.name: Analytical Mechanics
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the analytical tools needed to formulate and solve classical mechanics problems beyond Newtonian methods. It emphasizes the use of generalized coordinates, variational principles, and Hamiltonian/Lagrangian formalisms to describe mechanical systems efficiently.
courses.description: Generalized coordinates, constraints, variational principles, Lagrange's equations, central force problem, motion in non-inertial frames, rigid body dynamics, Hamilton's equations, theory of small vibrations.
learning_outcomes: Apply Newton's laws to analyze complicated mechanical systems, including planetary orbits and rigid body rotations.
State the principle of least action and use calculus of variations to obtain Newton's second law.
Recognize conservation laws for energy, momentum, and angular momentum and relate them to symmetries.
Describe mechanical systems with few degrees of freedom using the Lagrangian formulation.
Construct the Hamiltonian for simple mechanical systems and contrast the Hamiltonian description with the Lagrangian.
resources: Classical Dynamics of Particles and Systems, International Edition 5e, Stephen T. Thornton and Jerry B. Marion, 2008/5e, Cengage Learning

courses.name: Introductory Astronomy
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts of astronomy and the structure of the universe. It aims to build a basic understanding of celestial objects and large-scale cosmic phenomena.
courses.description: Introducing Astronomy; Planets and Moon; Stars and Stellar Evolution; Galaxies and Cosmology.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introducing Astronomy
Hafta 2: Planets and Moon
Hafta 3: Stars and Stellar Evolution
Hafta 4: Galaxies and Cosmology

courses.name: Advanced Calculus for Applications in Physics
courses.purpose: This course aims to equip students with advanced mathematical tools used in physics and to strengthen their ability to formulate and solve physical problems mathematically. It builds the foundation needed for later work in classical and quantum physics, differential equations, and mathematical methods.
courses.description: Special functions of mathematical physics, hypergeometric functions, Sturm-Liouville theory, Green's functions, integral transforms, integral equations, probability.
learning_outcomes: Solve advanced problems in single variable calculus regarding series, derivatives and integrals.
Use cartesian, cylindrical and spherical coordinate systems to describe three dimensional space with vector calculus.
Solve first and second order linear ordinary differential equations, contrast homogeneous and particular solutions.
Calculate Fourier series representations of periodic functions.
Generalize calculus to complex numbers, identify the properties of analytic functions.
Use Cauchy's residue theorem to evaluate integrals.
Use special functions and orthogonal polynomials to solve physics problems.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Review of linear algebra for physics: inner products, norms, orthogonality, and projections. Eigenvalues and eigenvectors as tools for decoupling physical systems.

Hafta 2: Linear operators and eigenvalue problems. Diagonalization and normal modes. Degeneracy and simultaneous diagonalizability of commuting operators. Defective operators and Jordan canonical form. Physical consequences of non-diagonalizability. Self-adjoint operators and reality of spectra.
Hafta 3: Coordinate systems and transformations. Jacobians and metric factors. Orthogonal curvilinear coordinates and physical interpretation.
Hafta 4: Vector calculus in curvilinear coordinates. Gradient, divergence, curl, and Laplacian operators. Integral theorems and physical meaning.
Hafta 5: Classification of partial differential equations. Boundary and initial conditions. Physical examples from diffusion, waves, and electrostatics.
Hafta 6: Separation of variables as an eigenvalue problem. Orthogonality and completeness of modes. Fourier series in bounded domains.
Hafta 7: Fourier transforms and integral transforms. Convolution theorem and Parseval’s identity. Physical applications to wave and diffusion equations.
Hafta 8: Sturm–Liouville theory. Weight functions, eigenfunction expansions, and physical boundary-value problems.

Hafta 9: Green’s functions for ordinary and partial differential equations. Operator inversion and physical response functions. Linear response theory. Conformal mapping methods for two-dimensional Laplace problems.
Hafta 10: Bessel functions. Radial equations arising from separation of variables in cylindrical geometries. Zeros and orthogonality. Applications to waveguides and circular membranes.
Hafta 11: Legendre polynomials and associated Legendre functions. Spherical harmonics and angular eigenvalue problems.
Hafta 12: Hermite polynomials and Hermite functions. Harmonic oscillator and Gaussian weight functions.
Hafta 13: Laguerre and associated Laguerre polynomials. Radial problems and applications to quantum systems.
Hafta 14: Common operator-based and spectral formulation of boundary-value problems in classical and quantum physics. Synthesis of Arfken Chapters 6–10 and 14–20.
resources: Mathematical Methods for Physicists, G. B. Arfken, H. J. Weber, and F. E. Harris, 7th edition, Academic Press
Mathematical Methods in the Physical Sciences, M. L. Boas, Wiley
Mathematical Physics: A Modern Introduction to Its Foundations, S. Hassani, Springer
Mathematical Methods for Physics and Engineering, K. F. Riley, M. P. Hobson, and S. J. Bence, Cambridge University Press
Mathematical Methods for Physicists : A concise introduction, Tai L. Chow, Cambridge University Press

courses.name: Physics for Administrators
courses.purpose: This course intends to impart to the student knowledge of physics relevant to everyday life decisions. It is designed for students with a minimal background in mathematics and science, with emphasis on understanding basic concepts and making order-of-magnitude computations.
courses.description: The course covers advanced topics in physics, including energy and power; atoms and heat; gravity, force, and space; nuclei and radioactivity; nuclear reactors and weapons; electricity and magnetism; waves; light; invisible light; climate change; alternative energy; and quantum physics.

courses.name: Electromagnetic Theory I
courses.description: Electrostatics; Coulomb's and Gauss' laws, the scalar potential. Solutions to the Laplace equation in rectangular, spherical and cylindrical coordinate systems with various boundary conditions. Poisson's equation; energy in the electric field; electrostatics of materials; capacitance. Magnetostatics: Biot-Savart and Ampere's laws, the field vector potential; energy in the magnetic field; magnetostatics of materials; Faraday's law; inductance.

courses.name: Quantum Physics
courses.description: Old quantum theory; elementary introduction to quantum physics; Schrodinger equation, uncertainty principle, correspondence principle; one dimensional problems; angular momentum; hydrogen atom.

courses.name: Quantum Mechanics I
courses.description: Wave packets and uncertainty; the postulates of quantum mechanics; eigenfunctions and eigenvalues; simple problems in one dimension; general structure of wave mechanics; operator methods in quantum mechanics; harmonic oscillator; path integral formulation of quantum mechanics; systems of many degrees of freedom; symmetry; rotational invariance and angular momentum; hydrogen atom.

courses.name: Electromagnetic Theory II
courses.purpose: This course aims to build a deeper understanding of classical electromagnetism and its applications. It prepares students to analyze electromagnetic waves, radiation, and relativistic aspects of electric and magnetic fields.
courses.description: Maxwell's equations; electromagnetic waves; reflections from boundaries; propagation in waveguides; radiation from accelerating charges; Lorentz transformations of electric and magnetic fields.
learning_outcomes: Recognize Maxwell's equations as the basis of all electromagnetic phenomena.
Use vector calculus tools to describe Electric and Magnetic fields in time dependent situations.
Derive the electromagnetic wave equation from Maxwell’s equations.
Identify different regions in the electromagnetic spectrum from Gamma rays to Radio waves.
Connect material properties of susceptibility and permeability to wave propagation and speed of light in materials.
Recognize the electromagnetic wave equation as the basis of optical phenomena including reflection, refraction and polarization.
Analyze Maxwell’s equations for a single source to derive Lorentz transformations of special relativity.
Use the concept of retarded potentials to express electromagnetic fields in the far field.
Qualitatively describe the properties of radiation from a classical emitter, such as an antenna.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Brief survey of Magnetostatics, Maxwell's Equations and Conservation Laws
Hafta 2: EM Waves -- in Vacuum
Hafta 3: EM Waves -- in Vacuum (Cont'd)
Hafta 4: EM Waves -- Reflection & Refraction
Hafta 5: EM Waves -- Waveguides
Hafta 6: EM Cavities & MT-1
Hafta 7: EM Waves -- Material Absorption & Dispersion
Hafta 8: Potentials & Radiation
Hafta 9: Radiation
Hafta 10: Radiation (Cont'd)
Hafta 11: Special Theory of Relativity -- Postulates & MT-2
Hafta 12: Holiday & STR -- Space-time
Hafta 13: STR -- Relativistic Kinematics & Dynamics & Holiday
Hafta 14: STR -- Relativistic Electrodynamics
resources: Introduction to Electrodynamics, Griffiths
Detailed course syllabus for Spring 2025: www.fen.bilkent.edu.tr/~yalabik/ders/phys_316_25/

courses.name: Quantum Mechanics II
courses.description: Spin; addition of angular momenta; approximation methods in quantum mechanics; atoms and molecules; scattering theory; quantum theory of electromagnetic radiation.
learning_outcomes: Recognize the Hamiltonian evolution and projective measurements as the basis of quantum phenomena.
Develop methodologies to attack these cases
Perform required operations related to methodologies
Produce and interpret results related to these problems
Discuss how outcomes may be related present and future technologies
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: General theory of angular momentum. Spin states.
Hafta 2: General theory of angular momentum. Spin states.
Hafta 3: Direct-product spaces for composite systems. Entangled states. Coupling of angular momenta.
Hafta 4: Permutational symmetry for systems of identical particles.
Hafta 5: Introduction to quantum statistical mechanics. Fermi-Dirac and Bose-Einstein distributions.
Hafta 6: Time independent Perturbation Theory - The non-degenerate case
Hafta 7: Time independent Perturbation Theory - The non-degenerate case
Hafta 8: Time independent Perturbation Theory - The degenerate case
Hafta 9: Applications of perturbation theory
Hafta 10: Variational method
Hafta 11: Applications of variational method: Helium atom, Hartree-Fock equations.
Hafta 12: Scattering Theory.
Hafta 13: Scattering theory.
Hafta 14: Time-dependent perturbation theory and applications
resources: Required - Textbook: Introduction to Quantum Mechanics, Griffiths, Latest edition
Recommended - Textbook: Introduction to Quantum Mechanics, Griffiths and Schroeter
Required - Web Link: See detailed syllabus for Spring 2025: www.fen.bilkent.edu.tr/~yalabik/ders/phys_326_26/

courses.name: Numerical Methods in Physics
courses.description: Solutions to linear systems of equations; roots of polynomials and other nonlinear functions; statistical applications; determinants, eigenvalues, and eigenvectors, solutions to differential equations; applications of FFT; utilization of scientific software packages. Emphasis will be placed on physical applications.

courses.name: Methods in Computational Physics
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce advanced numerical methods used to solve scientific problems and to develop students' ability to apply these methods in practice through project-based work.
courses.description: Advanced topics in numerical methods for scientific problems. The course emphasizes student project work.

courses.name: Statistical Physics
courses.description: The laws of thermodynamics; applications of thermodynamics; basic probability concepts; elementary kinetic theory; classical microcanonical, canonical and grand canonical ensembles; classical ideal gas; equipartition of energy; quantum mechanical ensembles; ideal Fermi and Bose systems; black body radiation, phonons, the electron gas; magnetism; introductory nonequilibrium statistical physics.
learning_outcomes: Recognize that the microscopic basis of thermodynamics can be formulated in terms of the laws of classical or quantum mechanics
Explain the connection between statistical descriptions and macroscopic observables using microcanonical, canonical and grand canonical ensembles.
Use numerical calculations to obtain macroscopic variables for simple model systems in statistical physics
State the meaning of Boltzmann factors for microscopic states and relate them to the partition function.
Generalize statistical description of classical gas to quantum ideal gases.
Use the concept of entropy to classify reversible and irreversible changes.
Interpret a given phase diagram, identifying critical points and first and second degree phase transitions.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Nature and scope of thermodynamics, extensive and intensive properties, equilibrium states, quasi-static processes, modes of energy transfer, first law of thermodynamics.
Hafta 2: Exact differentials, thermodynamic derivatives and response coefficients, entropy and second law of thermodynamics. Postulates of Gibbsian Thermodynamics.Fundamental equation.
Hafta 3: Maximum entropy principle. Legendre transforms, free energies, minimum principles. Stability conditions.
Hafta 4: Basic notions of probability, statistical entropy, entropy maximum principle. Combinatorics.
Hafta 5: Postulates of Statistical Mechanics: Ergodic hypothesis, principle of a priori equal probabilities. Ensembles. Microcanonical ensemble.
Hafta 6: Einstein model of crystals, simple model of paramagnetism, negative absolute temperature. Density of states. Classical phase-space calculation of density of states.
Hafta 7: Canonical ensemble.
Hafta 8: Quantum-mechanical and classical calculation of canonical partition function for simple models.
Hafta 9: Grandcanonical ensemble. Simple models of adsorption.
Hafta 10: General ensemble theory. Fluctuations and thermodynamic equivalence of ensembles.
Hafta 11: Systems of identical particles. Permutational symmetry. Symmetric and anti-symmetric states.
Hafta 12: Classical ideal gases: vibrational and rotational partition functions for diatomic and polyatomic gases. Nuclear-spin statistics.
Hafta 13: Quantum statistics. Fermi-Dirac and Bose-Einstein distributions. Classical limit of quantum statistics.
Hafta 14: Quantum ideal gases
resources: Required - Textbook: Thermal Physics, Ralph Baierlein, 2001, Cambridge University Press
Recommended - Textbook: An Introduction to Thermal Physics, Daniel V. Schroeder, 2000, Addison Wesley Longman
Recommended - Textbook: Concepts in Thermal Physics, S. J. Blundell and K. M. Blundell, 2006, Oxford University Press

courses.name: Theory of General Relativity
courses.description: The Spacetime of Special Relativity; Lorentz Transformations; Manifolds and Coordinates; Vector and Tensor Calculus on Manifolds; Electromagnetism; The Equivalence Principle and Spacetime Curvature; The Gravitational Field Equations; The Schwarzscild Geometry; Experimental Tests of General Relativity; Schwarzschild Black Holes; The Friedman-Robertson-Walker Geometry; Cosmological Models; Linearized General Relativity; Gravitational Waves.

courses.name: Classical Thermodynamics
courses.purpose: To introduce the macroscopic description of physical systems and the fundamental laws governing thermal phenomena. The course develops the concepts and tools needed to analyze equilibrium, energy transfer, entropy, and thermodynamic potentials.
courses.description: Scope and nature of macroscopic description and thermodynamics. Types of thermodynamic systems, extensive and intensive thermodynamic variables and state functions, equilibrium states. Quasi-static processes, modes of energy transfer, distinction between heat and work, the first law of thermodynamics. Entropy and second law of thermodynamics. Gibbsian concept of fundamental equation, maximum entropy principle (EMP). Deduction of conditions for thermal, mechanical and chemical equilibrium from MEP. Reversible processes and heat engines. Legendre transforms and thermodynamic potentials (Enthalpy, Helmholtz free energy, Gibbs Free energy and grand potential). Thermodynamic derivatives and response coefficients, Maxwell relations and other relations and inequalities between thermodynamic properties. Stability of thermodynamic systems, LeChatelier principle. Phase transitions and critical phenomena. Gibbs phase rule. Phase diagrams.

courses.name: Optics
courses.description: Survey of optics, covering electromagnetic waves, interference, diffraction, reflection and refraction, laser gain. This will be followed by discussions of selected advanced topics, such as optical resonators, cw and pulsed lasers, including the interaction of light with matter, nonlinear optics, and fiber optics. The particular selection of the advanced topics can vary from year to year.

courses.name: Nanoscience and Nanotechnology I
courses.description: General survey of nanoscience and nanotechnology, atomic scale characterization and processes, scanning probe microscopies such as STM/AFM and atomic manipulation, nanofabrication, carbon nanotubes, nanowires, transport in nanostructures, nanoelectronics, nanomagnetism, and spintronics.

courses.name: Nanoscience and Nanotechnology II
courses.description: Electronic structure of nanostructures, carbon nanotubes, nanowires, transport in nanostructures, Landauer-Buttiker formalism, nanoelectronics, molecular electronics, nanodevices, nanomagnetism, spintronics.

courses.name: Introduction to Quantum Transport Phenomena in Solids
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental physical principles governing charge transport in solids, especially in regimes where quantum effects become important. It aims to build understanding of how equilibrium, non-equilibrium, and mesoscopic phenomena shape transport behavior in condensed matter systems.
courses.description: Equilibrium and non-equilibrium states in ultrasmall systems; fluctuation dissipation theorem; Kubo formula for linear response; electron-phonon interaction; electron conduction in a magnetic field; quantum localization and metal-insulator transition; Aharonov-Bohm effect; mesoscopic space and charge quantization effects; macroscopic quantum phenomena; Coulomb blockade of tunneling.

courses.name: Condensed Matter Physics I
courses.description: Crystal diffraction; crystal binding; phonons and lattice vibrations; thermal, acoustic and optical properties; free electron model; energy bands, electron-phonon interactions; semiconductors; transport properties.

courses.name: Quantum optics
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and methods of quantum optics, focusing on how light is quantized and how it interacts with matter at the quantum level. It prepares students to analyze modern quantum optical phenomena and models used in research.
courses.description: Electromagnetic field quantization. Coherent and squeezed states. Quantum distributions, first- and second-order coherences. Atom-photons interactions: Rabi and Jaynes-Cummings models. Cavity quantum electrodynamics. Three-Level quantum optics. Open systems and decoherence.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate the understanding of quantized electromagnetic field and its interactions with quantum systems
Use state-of-the-art technological resources for the quantum optics projects
Analyze and comment on results, write technical reports
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Electromagnetic Field Quantization
Hafta 2: EM Field Quantization (Cont'd)
Hafta 3: Coherent States
Hafta 4: Coherent States (Cont'd)
Hafta 5: Squeezed States
Hafta 6: Quantum Distributions
Hafta 7: 1st and 2nd Order Coherences
Hafta 8: Atom-Field Interactions: Perturbative Approaches
Hafta 9: Atom-Field Interactions (Cont'd)
Hafta 10: Rabi Model and Photon Echo
Hafta 11: Jaynes-Cummings Model and Cavity-QED
Hafta 12: Three-Level Quantum Optics: CPT, STIRAP, EIT
Hafta 13: Decoherence: Theoretical Background
Hafta 14: Decoherence: Master Equations
resources: Introductory Quantum Optics, C. C. Gerry and P. L. Knight, 2005, Cambridge
Introduction to Quantum Optics, G. Grynberg, 2010, Cambridge

courses.name: Atomic Molecular and Optical Physics
courses.description: Hydrogen-like ion, fine and hyperfine structures, multielectron atom, LS and jj couplings. Atoms in static magnetic and electric fields. Radiative transitions, selection rules, Einstein coefficients, rate equations, resonance fluorescence, Hanle effect, optical pumping. Molecules: rotational and vibrational motion, Franck-Condon principle, infrared and Raman spectra.

courses.name: Condensed Matter Physics II
courses.description: Dielectric properties; diamagnetism and paramagnetism; ferromagnetism and anti-ferromagnetism; magnetic resonance; electron-phonon interactions; super-conductivity; optical properties.
learning_outcomes: Develop the concept of Fermi surface and its electrical and thermal consequences.
Describe optical properties of solids in terms of microscopic polarization.
Examine the magnetic behavior of solids and physical effects resulting in different types of magnetism.
Analyze electron-electron interaction with and without phonon exchange, and electron-phonon interaction.
Give the phenomenological description of superconductivity and identify different types of superconductors.
Analyze the effects of defect states on physical properties of solids.
Describe the utility of semiconductors as the basis of modern information technology.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Semiconductor devices
Hafta 2: Optical properties of solids, polarization
Hafta 3: Frequency and wave vector dependent dielectric constant, polarons
Hafta 4: Magnetism in solids: Diamagnetism, paramagnetism, Pauli paramagnetism, Hund's rules
Hafta 5: Ferromagnetis, ferrimagnetism, antiferromagnetism, spin waves and magnons
Hafta 6: Electron-phonon interactions.
Hafta 7: Transport Theory, quantized conductance,
Hafta 8: Superconductivity, type-I and II superconductors
Hafta 9: BCS Theory, Tunneling junctions
Hafta 10: Ferroelectric and piezoelectric materials
Hafta 11: Metal-semiconductor, semiconductor-semiconductor junctions, semiconductor heterostructures and devices.
Hafta 12: Vacancies, voids, interstitials, substitution of foreign atoms, defect states, dislocations
Hafta 13: Surfaces, relaxation and reconstruction of surfaces, surface states, chemisorption theory
Hafta 14: Theory of diffusion, alloys
resources: Recommended - Textbook: Introduction to Solid State Physics, Charles Kittel
Recommended - Textbook: Solid State Physics, Ashcroft/Mermin
Recommended - Textbook: Solid State Basics, S. H. Simon

courses.name: Optical Properties of Solids
courses.purpose: To introduce the macroscopic and fundamental theory of optical properties of solids, with emphasis on the relationship between electronic structure and optical behavior.
courses.description: Macroscopic theory; fundamental theory with emphasis on the relationship between electronic structure and optical properties of solids. Representative semiconductors, insulators and metals; impurities and defects in solids; surface and interface states; optical properties of quantum well structures; photoemission; luminescence.

courses.name: Magnetic Properties of Solids
courses.description: Theory of magnetism; diamagnetic and paramagnetic behavior of solids; ferromagnetic, antiferromagnetic, and ferromagnetic solids; magnetic properties under the alternating field.

courses.name: Group Theory
courses.description: Abstract group theory; theory of group representations; physical applications of group theory; full rotation groups and angular momentum; applications in molecular and solid state physics.

courses.name: Elementary Excitations in Solids
courses.description: Interacting electron gas; Plasmons; electron-hole interaction and excitons; phonons; spin waves and magnons; interaction processes; transport phenomena; virtual phonons and superconductivity; interaction with photons; thermal properties.

courses.name: Nuclear and Particle Physics
courses.purpose: To introduce the basic concepts and phenomena of nuclear and particle physics, and to provide a foundation for understanding the structure and interactions of matter at the subatomic level.
courses.description: Introduction to subatomic particles; properties of nuclei and nucleons; spin and magnetic moments; nuclear reactions; radioactivity; alpha and beta decays; nucleon interactions and nucleon scattering at low energies; nuclear models; elementary particles.

courses.name: Special Topics in Theoretical Physics
courses.description: Classical Statistical Mechanics, Quantum Statistics, Fermi gas, Electronic specific heat, The Richardson effect, Photoelectric effect, White dwarf starts, Transport problem, Pauli paramagnetism Landau diamagnetism, De Haas-Van Alphen effect, Quantum Hall effect, Base gas, Phinins, photons, Base-Einstein condensation. Interacting electron gas, Interacting Base gas. A few exactly soluble models, Mean-field approximation. Diffusion and Brownian motion, Fokker Fokker-Planck equation, Fluctuation-dissipation theorem.

courses.name: Introduction to the Physics of Low-Dimensional Systems
courses.purpose: To introduce the fundamental concepts and methods used to study physical systems in one and two dimensions, with emphasis on the distinctive behavior that emerges in low-dimensional settings.
courses.description: Many-body physics in one and two dimensions, classical two-dimensional models, strategies towards exact solutions, quantum lattice models, magnetism, second quantization, symmetries in quantum physics, specifics of low-dimensional physics, exact solutions based on the Bethe ansatz, geometric phases in physics, topology in physics, polarization and conductivity, quantum Hall effect, topological insulators.

courses.name: Introduction to Many Body Theory
courses.description: Exchange Symmetry, Fermions and Bosons, Second Quantization Formalism, Free Bosons, Bose-Einstein Condensation, Free Fermions, Temperature Dependence, Interactions, Hartree-Fock and Random Phase approximation, BCS theory, Gross-Pitaevskii equation, Bogoluibov deGennes equation. Green's functions, diagrammatic perturbation theory.

courses.name: Optical Trapping and Optical Manipulation
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the theoretical and experimental tools needed to understand optical trapping and optical manipulation. It also introduces the main application areas where these techniques are used.
courses.description: The course covers the principles, methods, and experimental aspects of optical trapping and optical manipulation. It includes an overview of various fields in which these techniques have been applied.

courses.name: Semiconductor Device Physics
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a foundation in the physics and fabrication principles underlying semiconductor devices. It prepares students to understand how processing steps and material properties influence device behavior and performance.
courses.description: Review of semiconductor technology. Crystal growth and doping. Oxidation. Diffusion. Ion implantation. Epitaxy. Review of MBE and MOCVD technology. Deposited films. Lithographic processes. Lateral device geometries and layout. Device and circuit fabrication. Characterization. Ohmics and Schottky barrier formation. p-n junction.

courses.name: Quantum Electronics
courses.description: Propagation of optical beams; optical resonators; interaction of radiation with matter; laser oscillations; specific laser systems; Q-switching and mode-locking; laser amplifiers; noise and modulation in lasers; non-linear optics.

courses.name: Ultrafast Lasers and Nonlinear Optics
courses.purpose: General introduction to the field of ultrafast optics and nonlinear optics.
courses.description: Nonlinear and dispersive pulse propagation, optical solutions, laser dynamics, mode-locking, ultrafast lasers, commonly used nonlinear optical processes.

courses.name: Field Theory
courses.description: Classical field theory; canonical quantization; quantization of scalar, spinor and vector fields; interacting fields and perturbation theory; symmetries; Feynman graphs.

courses.name: Theory of Relativity
courses.purpose: To introduce the fundamental concepts of space and time in classical mechanics and to develop an understanding of the principles of special and general relativity.
courses.description: The concepts of space and time in classical mechanics; relativity principle of Galilee; special relativity; Lorentz transformations; introductory concepts in general relativity; experimental evidence for special and general relativity.

courses.name: Semiconductor Device Physics
courses.purpose: To introduce the physical principles underlying semiconductor devices and their operation. The course aims to build a foundation for understanding how semiconductor materials and device structures determine electrical behavior.
courses.description: Semiconductor theory and semiconductor properties; equilibrium and nonequilibrium statistics, Boltzmann transport equations and transport properties, p-n junction diodes; metal-semi-conductor junctions; MOS capacitors; bipolar transistors; field-effect transistors; heterostructures.

courses.name: Elementary Particles
courses.purpose: To introduce the basic properties and interactions of elementary particles and to provide a conceptual framework for understanding the subnuclear world.
courses.description: Properties of elementary particles: spin, parity, hypercharge, etc.; interactions of elementary particles; group theory of subnuclear world; quark theory; experimental status of elementary particles.

courses.name: Politics II: How Does It Work?
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the functioning of political systems around the world and help them understand how political institutions and processes operate in practice.
courses.description: Functioning of political systems around the world, focusing on political culture, party systems, electoral politics, interest groups, governance structures, public policy, and bureaucracies.
learning_outcomes: Have command of fundamental concepts in political science
Identify deficiencies in knowledge and practice related to the field.
Have raised awareness for lifelong learning and questioning
Communicate field related knowledge and skills in writing and speaking.
Analyse theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflections in practice.
Learn how to receive feedback and make progress accordingly
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Chapter 1: What is science? What is politics? What is political science?
Hafta 2: Chapter 9: Political Culture and The Media
Hafta 3: Chapter 9: Political Culture and The Media (continued)
Hafta 4: Chapter 10: Representation, Elections and Voting
Hafta 5: Chapter 11: Parties and Party Systems
Hafta 6: Chapter 11: Parties and Party Systems (continued)
Hafta 7: Chapter 12: Groups, Interests and Movements
Hafta 8: Chapter 12: Groups, Interests and Movements (continued)
Hafta 9: Chapter 13: Constitutions, Law and Judges
Hafta 10: Chapter 14: Political Executives/Presidential Systems
Hafta 11: Chapter 15: Assemblies/Parliamentary Systems
Hafta 12: Chapter 16: Public Policy and Bureaucracy
Hafta 13: Chapter 20: Is Politics Broken?
Hafta 14: Review
resources: POLITICS, Andrew Heywood, 5th Edition, Macmillan Red Globe Press, 2019

courses.name: Turkish Political History II
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the necessary information regarding Turkish politics and the analytical tools needed to assess historical phenomena through the study of modern Turkish political history.
courses.description: This course is a survey of Turkish political history between 1923-1990, focusing on the main themes, people, events, and institutions in modern Turkish history.

courses.name: Fundamentals of Social Research
courses.purpose: This course introduces the philosophical bases and goals of social research. It aims to develop students' understanding of the logic underlying scientific analysis and the issues involved in selecting and formulating a research question.
courses.description: Introduction to philosophical bases and goals of social research. The logic underlying a scientific analysis. The problems and considerations arising in the selection and formulation of a research question.
learning_outcomes: Have command of fundamental concepts in social research and of relationships between concepts.
Communicate field related knowledge in writing and speaking.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Introduction and Overview
Hafta 2: Science and Research (Chapter 1)
Hafta 3: Science and Research (Chapter 1) - continued
Hafta 4: Basic Methodological Approaches in Social Research (Chapter 4)
Hafta 5: Basic Methodological Approaches in Social Research (Chapter 4) - continued
Hafta 6: Beginning the Research Process: Research Question and the Literature Review. (Chapter 5) + Qualitative and Quantitative Research Designs. (Chapter 6)
Hafta 7: Survey Research (Chapter 10)
Hafta 8: Survey Research (Chapter 10) + Experimental Research (Chapter 9)
Hafta 9: Non-reactive Research and Secondary Analysis (Chapter 11)
Hafta 10: Content Analysis (Chapter 11)
Hafta 11: Field Research (Chapter 13)
Hafta 12: Ethnography, Ethnomethodology and Focus Groups (Chapter 13)
Hafta 13: Quantitative and Qualitative Data Analysis (Chapter 12)
Hafta 14: Review
resources: Social Research Methods, 7th edition., W. Lawrence Neuman, 2011, Pearson
SUPPLEMENTARY READINGS WILL BE AVAILABLE ON MOODLE

courses.name: Negotiation and Mediation in Politics
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the theory and practice of negotiation and mediation in legal and political conflicts. It also seeks to develop students' applied negotiation and mediation skills through simulations, role plays, and case analysis.
courses.description: This course covers theories of bargaining, negotiation outcomes and processes, psychological dynamics in negotiations, communication strategies, the role of language, culture, and power, and the role of third parties. It also includes negotiation simulations, role plays, and detailed case discussions such as the Turkish Constitution, Cyprus, and Jerusalem.

courses.name: Social Transformation in Turkey
courses.purpose: This course aims at providing an insight into the social and cultural aspects of social transformation in Turkey.
courses.description: The transformation of spaces, divisions of labor, social stratification, life-styles and cultural values are explained through sociological and anthropological studies carried out in Turkey since the 1960s.

courses.name: Comparative Politics of Advanced Industrialized Countries
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the comparative study of political development in advanced industrialized countries. It helps students understand how historical and contemporary political processes shape institutions, democracy, and state-society relations across different national contexts.
courses.description: Historical and contemporary political developments in the USA, UK, France, Germany, and Japan are examined in comparative perspective. The course covers the modern state, democracy, and the political systems and transformations of these countries, with attention to empirical cases and comparative analysis.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of field-related concepts and theories.
Reflect upon and critically discuss causes and consequences of transformations of polities, economies and societies both as a team member and as an independent individual.
Apply course concepts to empirical cases.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: The Modern State
Hafta 3: Democracy
Hafta 4: Politics in Britain in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 5: Politics in Britain in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 6: Politics in France in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 7: Politics in France in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 8: Politics in Germany in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 9: Politics in Germany in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 10: Politics in Japan in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 11: Politics in Japan in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 12: Politics in the United States in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 13: Politics in the United States in Comparative Perspective
Hafta 14: Conclusions and Review

courses.name: Turkish Political History I
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with an analysis of late Ottoman and Turkish socio-political concepts, events, and institutions, with an emphasis on continuity and change. It also seeks to equip students with the analytical tools needed to assess historical phenomena.
courses.description: This course is a survey of Ottoman/Turkish political history from its late Ottoman roots until the foundation of the Republic of Turkey in 1923.

courses.name: Turkish Political Development
courses.purpose: This course examines the historical development of political systems in Turkey from the Ottoman period to the present, with special emphasis on the multi-party era.
courses.description: The development of the political systems from the Ottoman period to the present with special emphasis on the multi-party period.
learning_outcomes: Have basic knowledge of causes and consequences of transformations of societies
Analyze theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflections in practice.
Have the capacity to share knowledge and learn collectively.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: Ottoman Empire I
Hafta 3: Ottoman Empire II
Hafta 4: Early Republic I
Hafta 5: Early Republic II
Hafta 6: Multiparty Politics I
Hafta 7: Multiparty Politics II
Hafta 8: Multiparty Politics III
Hafta 9: Multiparty Politics IV
Hafta 10: Special Themes I
Hafta 11: Special Themes II
Hafta 12: Special Themes III
Hafta 13: The 1980 Coup
Hafta 14: Wrap-Up Session

courses.name: Contemporary Turkish Politics
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a theoretical understanding of state, politics, and society in contemporary Turkey, with special emphasis on the problems of and prospects for democracy.
courses.description: State, politics and society in contemporary Turkey are examined from a theoretical perspective, with special emphasis on the problems of and prospects for democracy.
learning_outcomes: Have basic knowledge of causes and consequences of transformations of societies
Analyse theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflections in practice
Have the capacity to share knowledge and learn collectively
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Politics in the Third Republic
Hafta 2: Elections and Voting Behavior
Hafta 3: Political Parties and Party System
Hafta 4: Civil-Military Relations in the Third Republic
Hafta 5: A New Era in Turkish Civil-Military Relations
Hafta 6: Islam and Politics I
Hafta 7: Islam and Politics II: The Case of the AKP
Hafta 8: MIDTERM
Hafta 9: Ethnic Minorities I
Hafta 10: Ethnic Minorities II
Hafta 11: Religious Minorities
Hafta 12: Civil Society and Social Movements
Hafta 13: Europeanization and its Implications for Domestic Politics
Hafta 14: Concluding Remarks: The Fault Lines of Turkish Politics

courses.name: Comparative Politics of Developing Countries
courses.description: Historical and contemporary political developments in Russia and her neighboring countries (the Commonwealth of Independent States), Eastern Europe, and the Peoples Republic of China.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of field-related concepts and theories (e)
Reflect upon and critically discuss causes and consequences of the key aspects of polities, economies, and societies both as a team member and as an independent individual (e) (c)
Apply course concepts to empirical cases (a)
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course introduction and overview
Hafta 2: INDIA, Chapter 7 in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
