Hafta 3: INDIA, Chapter 7 (cont.) in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 4: INDIA, Chapter 7 (cont.) in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 5: CHINA, Chapter 15 in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 6: CHINA, Chapter 15 (cont.) in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 7: CHINA, Chapter 15 (cont.) in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 8: --- MIDTERM EXAM ---
Hafta 9: RUSSIA, Chapter 13 in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 10: RUSSIA, Chapter 13 (cont.) in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 11: RUSSIA, Chapter 13 (cont.) in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 12: MEXICO, Chapter 10 in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 13: MEXICO, Chapter 10 (cont.) in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
Hafta 14: MEXICO, Chapter 10 (cont.) in Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A. 2018. Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Boston: Wadsworth, Cengage.
resources: Introduction to Comparative Politics: Political Challenges and Changing Agendas, Kesselman, M. Krieger, J. and Joseph, W.A., 2018 (8th edition), Cengage

courses.name: Political Anthropology
courses.purpose: The course examines how power and authority operate in human societies through social and cultural phenomena. It aims to provide students with an anthropological perspective on political organization and change.
courses.description: The course is concerned with the social and cultural phenomena related to how and why power and authority operate in human societies. Some of the topics covered are: types of preindustrial political systems; the evolution of the state; religion in politics; political succession; women and power; political symbolism and the influence of macrolevel polities on local authorities and polities.

courses.name: Research Methods
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the logic and practice of social science research. It helps students learn how to formulate research problems and choose appropriate research designs.
courses.description: Quantitative and qualitative research methods. Formulating research problems and selecting appropriate research designs. General problems of measurement, data collection techniques, analysis and interpretation of social science data.

courses.name: European Society and Culture
courses.purpose: The course offers a general overview of European society and culture through a critical discussion of its historical and contemporary political and cultural contexts.
courses.description: It covers European countries, including Eastern Europe and the European Union member countries. The course explores cultural identities, political economy, class, urbanization, ethnic minorities, nationalism, gender issues, religion, and immigrants.

courses.name: State and Society in Israel
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to acquaint students with the basic characteristics of the Israeli society and political system.
courses.description: The course covers the evolution of a self-governing Jewish community from its origins in Ottoman Palestine until the present day. It addresses issues such as the governmental system, political parties, foreign policy, military, nationalism, religion, citizenship and ethnicity from a comparative perspective.

courses.name: Turkish Political Thought I
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the major political and intellectual trends in Ottoman-Turkish history. It aims to help students understand continuity and change in the late Ottoman period and the early Turkish Republic through the study of key ideologues and primary sources.
courses.description: This course is a survey of political/intellectual trends in Ottoman-Turkish history. It focuses on the late Ottoman period until the establishment of the Turkish Republic, with an analysis of political and intellectual trends and their main ideologues, using both secondary and primary sources.
learning_outcomes: Attain basic understanding of causes and consequences of historical transformation.
Acquire basic knowledge pertaining to the field.
Organize, evaluate and express information in a systematic and analytical manner.
Asses/evaluate primary material with a critical approach.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: General Introduction to the Course
Hafta 2: Classical Ottoman Institutions and 'Traditional' Ottoman Reform
Hafta 3: From 'Traditional' to 'Modern Ottoman Reform'
Hafta 4: Sultan Selim III, The New Order and Sultan Mahmud II
Hafta 5: The Tanzimat Era
Hafta 6: The Tanzimat Era continued
Hafta 7: The Hamidian Era
Hafta 8: Midterm Examination
Hafta 9: The Hamidian Era continued
Hafta 10: The Second Constitutional Era, Westernism
Hafta 11: The Second Constitutional Era, Islamism
Hafta 12: The Second Constitutional Era, Turkism
Hafta 13: The Second Constitutional Era, Materialism
Hafta 14: The Second Constitutional Era, Feminism

courses.name: Turkish Political Thought II
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the necessary information concerning Turkish political thought and to teach them how to analyze discourse through careful analysis of both primary and secondary sources.
courses.description: This course is a survey of political and intellectual trends in modern Turkish history between 1923 and 1990.

courses.name: Social Theory: Past and Present
courses.purpose: The aim of the course is to enable students to understand the changes in social theory as a response to transformations in modern societies, and to develop an awareness of diverse theoretical perspectives that emerged since the late nineteenth century.
courses.description: A select survey of classical and contemporary social theories. After a review of classical macro and micro theories, the focus will be on contemporary perspectives such as systems theory, critical theory, feminism, structuralism and post-structuralism.

courses.name: Ethnographic Research and Politics
courses.purpose: To introduce students to ethnographic approaches and techniques and to show how ethnographic research can be used in the social sciences.
courses.description: Different ethnographic approaches and techniques and different field research practices through the works of some prominent social anthropologists. A more experimental understanding of using ethnographic research in social sciences by practicing various ethnographic methods such as participant observation, ethnographic writing and research design. Practice through "individual ethnographic research projects" concerning their immediate social and political environment as well as debates on the ethics and politics of ethnographic research itself.

courses.name: Cosmopolis: From the Roman to the Ottoman and British Empires
courses.purpose: This course examines political thought on empire and cosmopolis across the last two thousand years. It asks why so little theory was written about empires and explores how major thinkers understood imperial rule and universal political order.
courses.description: The course studies concepts of empire and cosmopolis through Roman, Byzantine, Holy Roman, Ottoman, Austrian, and British contexts. It combines political thought with selected literature and history, and includes both writers who lived within empires and canonical thinkers who reflected on empire from outside.
learning_outcomes: Have knowledge of fundamental concepts of imperial and cosmopolitan thought.
Have knowledge of the historical developments of concepts.
Have a philosophical grasp of the shape of political concepts, their variations, and the difficulties of applying them to the world.
Have the ability to relate a complicated sense of conceptual development to modern situations.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: INTRODUCTION. Philosophical ideals of polis, imperium, cosmopolis.

Hafta 2: Objective geography. On the geography of the fertile crescent. Garth Fowden, part of ch. 1 of Empire to Commonwealth: Consequences of Monotheism in Late Antiquity. Miscellaneous maps and an account of Eratosthenes’s calculation of the size of the earth. Transition: languages. Nicholas Ostler, sect. on Turkish etc in Empires of the Word.

Hafta 3: Subjective geography. On Roman and Greek geography. Claude Nicolet, ch. 3 of Geography and Politics in Early Roman Empire. Carl Schmitt, The Nomos of the Earth. Historical consciousness. G.W.F. Hegel, ‘The Natural Context of the Geographical Basis of World History’.

Hafta 4: From polis to what? Hans Kelsen, ‘The Philosophy of Aristotle and the Hellenic-Macedonian Policy’. Peter Green, ‘The Metamorphosis of the Barbarian: Athenian Panhellenism in a Changing World’.
Hafta 5: Cosmopolis: Cynics and Stoics. Excerpts from texts on Alexander, Zeno etc. ‘Diogenes Cosmopolitanism’. Malcolm Schofield, ‘Social and Political Thought’.
Hafta 6: Imperium: Roman Republic and imperium. Richard Koebner, ch 1 from Imperium. Ernest Barker, ‘The Conception of Empire’. Imperium: Roman Empire. Augustus, Res Gestae.

Hafta 7: Imperium/Cosmopolis: The Israelite tradition. Various texts (Daniel, the Gospel of John, Pauls’ Letter to the Romans). Christianity. Reinhold Niebuhr, ‘Augustine’s Political Realism’. Byzantium, Islam, Russia etc.
Hafta 8: Imperium: Holy Roman Empire. Walter Ullmann, ‘Reflections on the Medieval Empire’. States and Overseas Empires. G.V. Scammell, ‘The New Worlds and Europe in the Sixteenth Century’.
Hafta 9: Imperium: The shift in the meaning of ‘empire’. Richard Koebner, ‘From Imperium to Empire’. Robert Lowe, ‘Imperialism’.

Hafta 10: Cosmopolis: Enlightenment and Kant. Immanuel Kant, ‘Perpectual Peace (1795)’. Modernity. Carl Schmitt, ‘The New Nomos of the Earth’. Danilo Zolo, ‘Hans Kelsen: International Peace through International Law’.
Hafta 11: Imperium: Modern Imperialism. Richard Koebner, ‘The Concept of Economic Imperialism’. Chapters from Koebner and Schmidt, Imperialism.
Hafta 12: Lenin, ‘Imperialism: The Highest Stage of Capitalism’. Niall Ferguson, ‘The Limits of American Empire’. Samuel Huntington, Who are we?
Hafta 13: Cosmopolis: Modern Cosmopolitanism. ‘Cosmopolitanism’ in International Encyclopaedia of the Social Sciences. David Held, ‘Principles of Cosmopolitan Order’.
Hafta 14: Jeremy Waldron, ‘What is Cosmopolitan?’

courses.name: Liberty and Liberalism
courses.purpose: This course examines liberty as a political ideal and liberalism as the modern attempt to make that ideal the foundation of politics. It also aims to clarify the difficulties of trying to establish political ideals through political parties.
courses.description: The course engages with historical views of liberty, classical liberalism since the early nineteenth century, and the criticisms made of liberalism since that time. It contrasts an ideal with an ideology and explores the problems involved in turning political ideals into party programs.

courses.name: Foundations of Modern Political Theory
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the historical foundations of modern political theory by examining key intellectual developments from the Renaissance and Reformation periods.
courses.description: This course historically focuses on the Renaissance and Reformation period and particularly analyzes Republicanism, humanism, Lutherism, constitutionalism, and absolutism. It addresses such questions as how and why one defends liberty, under what circumstances the right to resist is justified, what are different responses to constitutionalism, and what is state sovereignty with and without absolutism.

courses.name: Issues of Urbanization
courses.purpose: This course investigates the social and political outcomes of rapid urbanization, with a focus on squatter settlements and their residents. It also aims to develop a critical approach to the integration of migrants into their new urban environment.
courses.description: Rural-to-urban migration, accompanied by squatter settlements in the physical realm, has been transforming many Asian, African and Latin American societies since the 1950s. The course covers theories that seek to explain the social and economic transformation of modernizing societies and their urban development. It focuses mainly on the Turkish case and addresses gender, ethnicity, religion and regional identity.

courses.name: Turkish Nationalism :Politics and Ideology
courses.purpose: This course aims to examine the emergence of Turkish nationalism as a political ideology in the late nineteenth century and its spread and influence in the Republic of Turkey. It also focuses on the political institutionalization of Turkish nationalism and its role in modern Turkish politics. Finally, it discusses future prospects, including nationalist responses to Turkey's domestic and global interests, commitments, and problems.
courses.description: The course covers the history of ideas surrounding Turkish nationalism, including the environment in which it was born and flourished, its influence in the early republican era, and its evolution afterward. It also examines how the Republic adopted nationalism as the core ideology of the nascent state, how various branches of the nationalist movement found political expression, and how they participated in politics in modern Turkey. Contemporary nationalist voices and their theoretical openings are also considered.

courses.name: Ethics and Morality in Daily Life
courses.purpose: This course aims to equip students who are not students of philosophy with some basic philosophical approaches to ethics and morality in simplified form, and to encourage them to apply these approaches to some practical issues in politics, science, law and other aspects of social life.
courses.description: Ethics and morality are usually either confined to the realm of religion or taught, learned and discussed in the area of philosophy. However, in today's world, people are faced with ethical dilemmas and moral issues in many areas of daily life. The lines between "good" and "evil", and between "right" and "wrong" are becoming increasingly blurred, yet individuals are generally left without a guide for solving these issues. There is therefore a need to discuss such issues as part of the education of young people in every field.

courses.name: Contemporary Political Ideologies
courses.purpose: This course will deal with ideologies as a unitary problem and also as a plural condition.

courses.name: Gender and Politics
courses.purpose: The aim of the course is to gain a deeper understanding of the ways in which gender and gender inequality shape institutions, policies, and political processes, as well as the way states affect gender relations, the political construction of gender, and political mobilization based on gender.
courses.description: Classical and contemporary views on gender and politics will be examined with a focus on three topics within the broad area of the relationship of gender and politics in some depths: women in politics; state social policy, and gender and ideology.

courses.name: Issues in Modern Political Thought
courses.purpose: The object of this course is to critically analyze the project of modernity through the prisms of democracy, industrialization, science and cultural representation. The first half of the course focuses on the idea of progress in modern thought and politics. In the second half, the main question under investigation is the political impact of the project of modernity on different social forces.

courses.name: Civil Society in Turkey
courses.purpose: The course aims at studying the development of civil society in Turkey. It introduces students to the concept of civil society from a comparative and historical perspective and examines its relationship with the state.
courses.description: The course focuses on the notion of civil society at the global level and then shifts to the national level in Turkey. It deals with the distinction between civil society and state, the characteristics and dynamics of contemporary civil society in Turkey, and related case studies. Topics include media, labor unions, non-governmental organizations, political parties, and interest groups in contemporary Turkey.

courses.name: Politics and Society in Turkey
courses.purpose: This course examines the major debates and controversies that shaped the modern Turkish Republic, especially modernization, westernization, nationalism, and secularism. It aims to help students understand how these themes influenced political life and social norms in Turkey.
courses.description: This course explores the debates and controversies over modernization, westernization, nationalism and secularism as they become the main themes of the constitutive norms of the modern Turkish Republic. After briefly tracing the historical developments around these themes since the founding of the Republic, the course examines different dimensions of Turkish nationalism and its aspirations for a West-oriented modernity in various contexts of politics and daily life such as the use of public spaces, urban planning issues, differentiation of gender roles, or trends in popular culture.

courses.name: Politics and Literature
courses.purpose: This course examines literature as a social product and explores how literary works represent reality, reflect on it, and react to it. It also investigates the political aspirations of major literary paradigms and their role as forms of social critique and political imagination.
courses.description: Focusing on the modern period from the nineteenth century to the present, the course analyzes the political aspects and potential of different literary paradigms from realism to postmodernism. It considers how these paradigms function as modes of social critique and how they imagine social and political alternatives.
learning_outcomes: Have advanced level of fundamental conceptual knowledge so as to consider its reflections in practice.
Have basic knowledge on the operation of interpersonal and inter societal relations.
Have basic knowledge of causes and consequences of transformations of societies.
Analyse theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflections in practice.
Access to authentic resources for information in the fields of social and behavioural sciences using written and visual records.
Have awareness to fulfill undertaken duties and responsibilities.
Plan the process and schedule of team projects in the field and related sub fields, and execute assigned duties at the required level within deadlines.
Aware of what and how to learn.
Have the capacity to share knowledge and learn collectively.
Communicate field related knowledge and skills in writing and speaking.
Have sufficient awareness about the issues of universality of social rights, social justice, quality and cultural values, together with environmental protection, and occupational health and safety.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: Literature and Politics
Hafta 3: Realism
Hafta 4: Realism (cont.) and Modernism
Hafta 5: Modernism (cont.)
Hafta 6: Realism vs. Modernism
Hafta 7: Realism vs. Modernism (cont.)
Hafta 8: MIDTERM EXAM
Hafta 9: Surrealism
Hafta 10: Existentialism
Hafta 11: Colonialism
Hafta 12: Postmodernism
Hafta 13: Minimalism
Hafta 14: Project: final essay

courses.name: Theory and Modern Society
courses.purpose: This course critically examines the development of social theory from its founding figures to contemporary debates. It focuses on how modern and post-modern perspectives address questions of social order, power, knowledge, and social change.
courses.description: Elaborate examination of the efforts to address, evaluate, and extend questions posed by founding fathers of social theory (Durkheim, Marx, Weber, Nietzsche) with particular attention to current positions in social and political theory within the dynamic axis of modernity vs. post-modernity. This seminar-type course develops upon these debates with a special emphasis on social and intellectual contexts, conceptual frameworks and methods, and contributions to modern society and its theorizing. Works: Darwin, Spencer, Parsons, Dahrendorf, Saussure, Habermas, and Foucault.
learning_outcomes: Have command of fundamental concepts in Social and Behavioural Sciences and of relationships between concepts.
Analyse theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflections in practice.
Evaluate the knowledge acquired in the field with a critical approach by investigating causal relationships.
Communicate the field-related knowledge and skills in writing and speaking.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: an overview of the course.
Hafta 2: The Enlightenment and the Birth of Modern Social Theory.
Hafta 3: Working definitions: Modernization, Modernity and Modernism.
Hafta 4: The birth of modern technologies of social control and a brief introduction to Michel Foucault’s re-conceptualization of “power relations.”
Hafta 5: Power/Knowledge: Techniques of normalization and the formation of knowledge on human subjects in modern society.
Hafta 6: TBA: Film and Discussion of the film. // Guidelines for final papers.
Hafta 7: From technologies of power to technologies and practices of the self.
Hafta 8: Review for Midterm#1
Hafta 9: Transformations in work life in the modern and late modern era.
Hafta 10: Transformations in culture and social thought in the twentieth century: Modernism and Postmodernism.
Hafta 11: New Theoretical Issues in the Twentieth Century: Actor-network theory: a new look at the interactions between nature, science, technology and society.
Hafta 12: Understanding the impact of modern communication technologies and the Internet on social relations.
Hafta 13: Algorithms, AI and the Future of Society
Hafta 14: Review of the Course/Guest Lecture
resources: AVAILABLE ON MOODLE

courses.name: Political Concepts
courses.purpose: This course aims to consider a range of fundamental concepts in political theory, not for the purpose of introduction, but for the purpose of reflection. It focuses on intensive study and discussion of writings on concepts such as democracy, justice, ideology, liberty, party, politics, power, public opinion, representation, revolution, rights, rule, and state.
courses.description: The course examines fundamental concepts in political theory through close reading and discussion of texts. The basic text is Political Innovation and Conceptual Change edited by Ball, Farr, and Hanson.
resources: Political Innovation and Conceptual Change, eds. Ball, Farr and Hanson

courses.name: State Society and Citizenship in Turkey
courses.purpose: This course aims to analyze the issue of citizenship in Turkey in relation to the state and society, and to situate it within broader theories and contemporary debates on citizenship.
courses.description: The course covers theories of citizenship, its emergence and development, and various aspects of citizenship in the current literature. It also examines contemporary debates on citizenship in Turkey, including identity politics, multiculturalism, and constitutional citizenship, along with the historical background, construction attempts, legal process, and current situation in Turkey.

courses.name: Nationalism and Citizenship in Comparative Perspective
courses.purpose: This course examines the nation as a key unit of analysis and explores how nations, national identities, nation-building processes, and national belonging are formed and contested in a global context.
courses.description: The course investigates different dimensions of the making of nations and national identities, with attention to nation-building and issues of national belonging. It situates these themes in relation to gender, race, ethnicity, colonialism, and class.

courses.name: Interculturalism and Europe
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the conceptual and practical dimensions of interculturalism in Europe from an interdisciplinary perspective. It also seeks to help students understand how cultural diversity is perceived and experienced, as well as the role of immigration policies and politics in the European Union.
courses.description: This course will cover the conceptual and practical aspects of interculturalism in Europe from an interdisciplinary perspective. It will focus on how cultural diversity is perceived and experienced by individuals (based upon anthropological studies) and the immigration policies and politics of the European Union since the end of Cold War.

courses.name: Governance and Public Policy
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the basic concepts, analytical tools, and theoretical approaches used in the study of public policy and governance. It also seeks to help students understand how policy variation and policy change occur across countries and policy sectors in comparative perspective.
courses.description: This course covers basic concepts, analytical tools, theoretical approaches and empirical information in studying public policy and governance in comparative perspective. It studies causal theories of policy variation and policy change across different countries and policy sectors. By drawing on a sample of policy areas, the course examines policy, politics and polity-level variables in phases of policy cycle. It traces emergence of 'governance' as distinctive mode of policymaking emphasizing sharing of competences among different public and private actors. It contextualizes policy variation and change by studying impact of domestic, regional and global interactions.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of field-related concepts and theories
Solve a field-related problem both as a team member and as an independent individual.
Apply course concepts to empirical cases
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Public Policy
Hafta 2: The State of the Sub-discipline
Hafta 3: Policy Types and Dimensions
Hafta 4: Institutions in Public Policy I
Hafta 5: Institutions in Public Policy II
Hafta 6: Approaches to Public Policy I
Hafta 7: Approaches to Public Policy II
Hafta 8: Politics of the Policy Process I
Hafta 9: Politics of the Policy Process II
Hafta 10: Politics of the Policy Process III
Hafta 11: Politics of the Policy Process IV
Hafta 12: Politics of Policy Change
Hafta 13: Domestic-level and International-level Interaction
Hafta 14: Conclusions

courses.name: Issues in Political Theory
courses.purpose: This course examines the relationship between aesthetics and ethics in political theory and explores how aesthetic perspectives shape political and moral judgments.
courses.description: The course first examines different connotations of aesthetics and its conceptual interdependence on ethics. It then analyzes the impact of aesthetics on the moral and political distinctions between good and evil. In the final part, it reviews the relationship between aesthetics, ethics, and different conceptualizations of politics, using historical case studies such as wars, Auschwitz, communist experimentations with reality, and twenty-first-century episodes of suicide bombing, torture, and the dismembering of human sensibilities.

courses.name: Conflict, Violence, and Peace
courses.purpose: This seminar course aims to introduce students to major theoretical approaches to social and political conflict, violence, peace, and reconciliation. It helps students understand how different social science disciplines and classical philosophical texts explain conflict dynamics and peace processes.
courses.description: This is a seminar course that dwells on various theories of social and political conflict, violence, and peace. The course brings together different theoretical approaches developed in sociology, social psychology, cultural anthropology, and political science in addition to some classical texts in philosophy with regard to conflict, violence, and peace. Some of the issues covered are: the origins of social conflict, functions of conflict in a society, different types of social conflict, structure-agency debate with regard to conflict, escalation of conflict, psychological dynamics of social conflict and violence. The course will also examine different theoretical approaches that developed in various social science disciplines with regard to peace and reconciliation.
learning_outcomes: Have command of fundamental concepts in social and behavioral sciences and of relationships between concepts.
Have basic knowledge of causes and consequences of transformations of societies.
Provide interdisciplinary interpretation by establishing relationships between subfields of the major and other fields of social sciences, have interdisciplinary analysis skills of these.
Analyse theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflections in practice.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Defining social and political conflict: political and economic approaches
Hafta 2: Nature and sources of conflict: psychology of human needs
Hafta 3: Nature and sources of conflict: Culture
Hafta 4: Group dynamics and inter-group conflict
Hafta 5: On Violence
Hafta 6: Gender and violence
Hafta 7: Dynamics of conflict escalation and intractability
Hafta 8: Modes of conflict: Non-violence as a conflict strategy
Hafta 9: Peace negotiations
Hafta 10: Peacebuilding
Hafta 11: Dialogue and interactive conflict resolution
Hafta 12: Recovering from war: Reconciliation
Hafta 13: Bottom-up peacebuilding: Local ownership and inclusivity
Hafta 14: presentations

courses.name: Politics of Security
courses.purpose: This course is designed as an advanced level introduction to the politics of security. Students will be introduced to key concepts in international/political theory and debates by focusing on case studies.
courses.description: This course is designed as an advanced level introduction to the politics of security. Students will be introduced to key concepts in international/political theory and debates by focusing on case studies.
learning_outcomes: Communicate advanced level of knowledge on the politics of security.
Apply security theories to empirical cases.
Obtain basic knowledge about theoretical discussions on the politics of security.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: What is/not a security issue?
Hafta 2: Security, the concept
Hafta 3: Human mobility: Who counts as ‘us’?
Hafta 4: Identity: Contesting ‘us’ from within
Hafta 5: Political economy: Whose material interests
Hafta 6: Popular culture: Manufacturing consent for security
Hafta 7: Securitization: Speech acts and framing
Hafta 8: Beyond securitization: The politics of security
Hafta 9: Democracy: The costs of security?
Hafta 10: Nuclear politics: Constructing the enemy
Hafta 11: Gendered security: Expertise is gendered?
Hafta 12: Inside/outside: Security through culture
Hafta 13: International politics: Whose ‘international’?
Hafta 14: workshop
resources: FULL syllabus on Moodle

courses.name: Regional Security in the Middle East
courses.purpose: An advanced level introduction to regional security in the Middle East.
courses.description: Key concepts and debates on regional security in the Middle East.
learning_outcomes: Familiarity with the key literature on regional security in the Middle East from multiple perspectives.
Competency in discussing the strengths and weaknesses of the key literature on regional security in the Middle East.

courses.name: Democratization Process in Turkey
courses.purpose: This course examines the constitutional and legal amendments made in Turkey over the last 20 years that were designed to improve democratic standards.
courses.description: This course deals with the constitutional and legal amendments in the last 20 years designed to improve the standards of democracy in Turkey.

courses.name: Life, Nature and Politics
courses.purpose: This course aims to survey past and current approaches in the social sciences that explain social and political institutions by emphasizing human beings as living entities interacting with nature.
courses.description: The course focuses on contemporary sociobiological research and its use of models from the life sciences to analyze language and communication, social hierarchies, gender relations, moral and religious values, and the formation of habits and customs.

courses.name: European Politics
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the main political, economic, and social developments in Europe, with attention to both Eastern and Western Europe. It also seeks to provide a wider pan-European perspective for understanding contemporary political issues.
courses.description: The course covers the main aspects of politics in both Eastern and Western Europe, including the political, economic, and social developments of the continent. It also examines the post-war histories of the two rival blocs, the eastern and the western, and the principal political issues in contemporary European politics.
learning_outcomes: Analyse theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflections in practice in European Politics.
Have command of fundamental concepts in European politics and of relationships between concepts in European politics.
Have awareness to fulfill undertaken duties and responsibilities by preparing for class requirements and active participation.
Act according to social, cultural, scientific, and ethical values during the process associated with the accumulation of European politics related knowledge and expertise.
Communicate field related knowledge and skills in writing and speaking through participation in requirements covering themes in European politics.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction-The Origins of the European Union
Hafta 2: European States and Societies: Models of Democracy
Hafta 3: (European) Models of Governance
Hafta 4: Changing Forms of Policy-making
Hafta 5: Welfare States in Europe
Hafta 6: Euro and the Financial Crisis
Hafta 7: European Integration
Hafta 8: Liberty, Security and Immigration
Hafta 9: European Citizen and Democracy
Hafta 10: Enlargement of the EU: Why and How?
Hafta 11: Economic and Social Governance in the EU
Hafta 12: Towards EU Foreign Policy
Hafta 13: Debating Europe's Future
Hafta 14: Conclusions and Review

courses.name: Film and Politics
courses.purpose: This course examines film as an extension of politics and a medium of political engagement.
courses.description: Concentrating on the second half of the twentieth century, the course discusses the fictions and realities of various historical events, such as Hiroshima, the Cold War, the Red Scare, decolonization, mass emigration, the fall of communism, and terrorism, as they were represented in mainstream, avant-garde, and documentary film. It also considers the extent to which these representations served as propaganda, a form of protest and resistance, or an attempt to formulate political alternatives, and what political influence they were able to generate.

courses.name: Democracy, Development and Human Rights
courses.purpose: This course is designed to introduce students to the issues of democracy, development, and human rights from the perspective of comparative politics.
courses.description: We will discuss different conceptualizations, theories, and measurements of democracy, development, and human rights, analyze the extent to which existing theories contradict or complement each other, and examine whether their policy prescriptions have been successful in the real world. The course also addresses the effects of domestic and international institutions, natural resources, political culture, and globalization, with some attention to regional differences among Latin America, East Central Europe, Asia, and the Middle East.

courses.name: Issues in Comparative Politics
courses.purpose: This module introduces students to advanced research in comparative politics and helps them develop a deeper understanding of how political systems, institutions, and processes are analyzed comparatively.
courses.description: This module introduces students to advanced research in comparative politics. It centers around topics such as politics and government, the state, democracy, authoritarian rule, the comparative approach, political culture, political communication, political economy, political participation, elections and voters, interest groups, political parties, constitutions and the legal framework, multilevel governance, legislatures, the political executive, public management and administration, and public policy.
learning_outcomes: Analyse theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflections in practice.
Evaluate the knowledge acquired in the field with a critical approach by investigating causal relationships.
Have the capacity to interpret events and improve views about social and behavioural facts by using the body of methodologies and information provided within the field.
Direct training to a more advanced degree in the same field or to a profession at the same level.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Meeting and Course Introduction
Hafta 2: Karl Polanyi. 1944. The Great Transformation: The Political and Economic Origins of our Time. Beacon Press.
Hafta 3: Karl Polanyi. 1944. The Great Transformation: The Political and Economic Origins of our Time. Beacon Press.
Hafta 4: Sheri Berman. 2006. The Primacy of Politics: Social Democracy and the Making of Europe’s Twentieth Century. Cambridge University Press.
Hafta 5: Sheri Berman. 2006. The Primacy of Politics: Social Democracy and the Making of Europe’s Twentieth Century. Cambridge University Press.
Hafta 6: Herbert Kitschelt. 1994. The Transformation of European Social Democracy. Cambridge University Press.
Hafta 7: Herbert Kitschelt. 1994. The Transformation of European Social Democracy. Cambridge University Press.
Hafta 8: Nadav G. Shelef. 2010. Evolving Nationalism: Homeland, Identity, and Religion in Israel, 1925–2005. Cornell University Press.
Hafta 9: Midterm Exam
Hafta 10: Nadav G. Shelef. 2010. Evolving Nationalism: Homeland, Identity, and Religion in Israel, 1925–2005. Cornell University Press.
Hafta 11: David Art. 2005. The Politics of the Nazi Past in Germany and Austria. Cambridge University Press.
Hafta 12: Milan Svolik. 2012. The Politics of Authoritarian Rule. Cambridge University Press.
Hafta 13: Asef Bayat. 2013(2010). Life as Politics: How Ordinary People, Change the Middle East. Stanford University Press.
Hafta 14: Presentations
resources: Assigned readings are available through Moodle. No additional textbook required.

courses.name: Liberalism and Socialism: Past and Present
courses.purpose: This course examines the rise of liberalism and socialism from the nineteenth century to the present. Its objective is to distinguish between political ideologies and political theories and to provide students with a firm historical and theoretical background on two of the most influential ideologies that have shaped world politics in the last two centuries.
courses.description: The course focuses on the radically different conceptions of freedom, the good life, and collectivism in liberal and socialist theories, tracing their development from the nineteenth century to the present.

courses.name: Issues of Urbanization
courses.purpose: This course examines the social and political consequences of rapid urbanization, especially in relation to rural-to-urban migration and squatter settlements. It also aims to develop a critical perspective on how migrants are integrated into new urban environments, with particular attention to the Turkish case.
courses.description: Rural-to-urban migration, accompanied by squatter settlements in the physical realm, has been transforming many Asian, African and Latin American societies since the 1950s. The course covers theories that explain the social and economic transformation of modernizing societies and their urban development. It focuses on squatter settlements and their residents, and addresses gender, ethnicity, religion, and regional identity.

courses.name: International Political Economy
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to international political economy by examining how political and economic forces interact in shaping national and global systems.
courses.description: Focusing on international political economy as inspired by both politics and economics, this course centers around the neoclassical conception of an economy, new economic and political theories, national systems of political economy and the international trading, monetary and financial systems, multinationals corporations, economic and political development, and regional political and economic integration.

courses.name: Aesthetics and Politics
courses.purpose: This course examines how changing conceptions of aesthetics and selected art movements intersect with major political ideas such as conservatism, liberalism, Marxism, and fascism. It also questions what counts as political through the lens of aesthetic objects, tastes, and experiences of reality.
courses.description: Course exploring how changing conceptions of aesthetics and certain art movements intersect with such political ideas as conservatism, liberalism, Marxism, fascism, and the disappearance of the political from the eighteenth century to the present digital age.

courses.name: Individual, Society and Violence
courses.purpose: To comprehend the power relations between institutions and between individuals by examining the implicit violence that exists in everyday life and considering how far it stems from the individual and how far it is forced upon the individual.
courses.description: Investigation of a number of philosophical, sociological and psychological studies which are now considered classics. Examination of how the findings of these studies are reflected in everyday life in the society we live.

courses.name: Contemporary Political Theory
courses.purpose: This course examines key concepts, questions, and recurring debates in political theory from the seventeenth century to the present, with attention to how these debates shape contemporary political thought.
courses.description: The course is an inquiry into some of the key concepts, questions and recurring debates in political theory from the seventeenth century to our present. The first part examines different implications of the use of the notion of contemporary in four contexts: modern, temporal, ecological, and as calls for inclusion amidst loss and spectacle. The second part focusses on how representation and rights have been problematized in the historically specific debates on sovereignty, democracy, legitimacy, citizenship and freedom. The third part scrutinizes the role of law and constitutions, globalized market economy, and biopolitics in addressing specific problems concerning the so-called state of exception.

courses.name: Contemporary Political Theory
courses.purpose: This course examines key concepts, questions, and recurring debates in political theory from the seventeenth century to the present, with attention to how these debates shape contemporary political thought.
courses.description: The course is an inquiry into some of the key concepts, questions and recurring debates in political theory from the seventeenth century to our present. The first part examines different implications of the use of the notion of contemporary in four contexts: modern, temporal, ecological, and as calls for inclusion amidst loss and spectacle. The second part focusses on how representation and rights have been problematized in the historically specific debates on sovereignty, democracy, legitimacy, citizenship and freedom. The third part scrutinizes the role of law and constitutions, globalized market economy, and biopolitics in addressing specific problems concerning the so-called state of exception.

courses.name: Introduction to Psychology/Cognitive and Biological
courses.purpose: This course provides an in-depth introduction to the theories, methods, and findings of psychological science. It aims to build a foundation for understanding psychology as a scientific discipline, with emphasis on biological and cognitive aspects of behavior.
courses.description: The course focuses on the biological and cognitive aspects of behavior. Topics covered include the brain and nervous system, perception, learning, thinking, memory, motivation, and consciousness.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of major theoretical perspectives in psychology
Demonstrate knowledge of important topics and concepts in the major sub-fields of psychology: Cognitive and Biological areas
Demonstrate knowledge of important research findings in the major sub-fields of psychology: Cognitive and Biological areas
Demonstrate knowledge of how psychologists design experiments and collect data
Demonstrate knowledge of basic statistical methods and scientific tools that psychologists use to conduct research and analyze data

Hafta 2: Neuroscience and Behavior
Hafta 3: Neuroscience and Behavior
Hafta 4: Sensation
Hafta 5: Perception
Hafta 6: Spring break
Hafta 7: Consciousness
Hafta 8: Learning
Hafta 9: Midterm

Hafta 11: Language
Hafta 12: Thinking
Hafta 13: Psychopathology
Hafta 14: Review
resources: Understanding Psychology, Feldman, R. S., 15th Ed, McGraw-Hill

courses.name: Local and Global in Cities
courses.purpose: This course explores the relationship between local dynamics and global developments in the context of cities. It aims to reveal how today's cities are transformed through the interplay between local and global forces.
courses.description: The course brings together research on cities in both the global South and the North, focusing on how urban life and urban change are shaped by local and global processes.

courses.name: Introduction to Psychology/Social and Developmental
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to major areas and perspectives in psychology, with emphasis on social and developmental psychology. It aims to build a foundation for understanding psychological science and its applications.
courses.description: This course is a continuation of Introduction to Psychological Science 1, PSYC 101. Topics covered in this course include testing and intelligence, human development, personality, social psychology, abnormal psychology, treatment of psychological disorders, and industrial/organizational psychology.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of major theoretical perspectives in psychology
Demonstrate knowledge of important topics and concepts in the major sub-fields of psychology: Developmental and Social areas
Demonstrate knowledge of important research findings in the major sub-fields of psychology: Developmental and Social areas
Demonstrate knowledge of how psychologists design experiments and collect data
Demonstrate knowledge of basic statistical methods and scientific tools that psychologists use to conduct research and analyze data

Hafta 2: Human Development (Chp.12)
Hafta 3: Human Development (Chp.12)

courses.name: Cognitive Psychology
courses.purpose: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the area of cognitive psychology. It explains the emergence and importance of cognitive psychology as a field of scientific research.
courses.description: Issues and findings are presented in sensation and perception, learning, memory, problem solving, thinking and reasoning, and language.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the important concepts of cognition such as sensation, perception, attention, memory, language, reasoning and decision-making (exam and in class activities)
Demonstrate knowledge of important theories and theoretical perspectives in cognitive topics such as sensation, perception, attention, memory, language, reasoning and decision-making
Demonstrate knowledge of important research findings in the major sub-fields of cognitive psychology
Demonstrate knowledge of how psychologists design experiments, collect data, and analyze and interpret results in major sub-fields of cognition

Hafta 11: Language
Hafta 12: Problem-solving
Hafta 13: Judgement & Decision & Reasoning I
Hafta 14: Judgement & Decision & Reasoning II

courses.name: Statistical Methods in Psychological Science I
courses.purpose: This course introduces the main concepts and methods of statistical analysis used in psychological science. It aims to help students understand how statistical techniques are used to analyze data and test research hypotheses.
courses.description: The course covers probability theory, graphic representations, distributions, measures of central tendency, non-parametric statistics including Chi Square, and t-test. Students also practice applying these statistical methods.
learning_outcomes: Quantitatively describe data and their distributions.
Identify the structure of data and the appropriate statistical tests to conduct on these data.
Conduct distinct inferential statistical tests using a professional statistical software package.
Interpret and articulate these results in the context of research hypothesis testing.

courses.name: Statistical Methods in Psychological Science II
courses.description: This is a continuation of Psychological Statistics 1, PSYC 203. Topics to be covered include analysis of variance(one-way and multivariate), correlation, regression and multiple regression.
learning_outcomes: Identify the structure (e.g., scale of measurement) of data and the appropriate statistical tests to conduct on these data.
Conduct distinct inferential statistical tests using a professional statistical software package.
Articulate these results and interpret them within the context of research hypothesis testing.

courses.name: Research Methods in Psychological Science II
courses.purpose: This course builds on Research Methods I and aims to deepen students' understanding of psychological research design and proposal development. It prepares students to critically evaluate research and to design, present, and report their own studies.
courses.description: This course is a continuation of Research Methods 1 (PSYC 204). Students will be exposed to more complex designs such as factorial designs as well as correlational designs and quasi-experimental design. A number of studies will be conducted.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate ability to critically evaluate psychological research
Demonstrate in-depth understanding and hands on experience in designing and conducting research
Demonstrate learning of how to report research in reporting research results in the form of an APA-style manuscript
Demonstrate learning of how to prepare and present a poster presentation
Demonstrate learning of how to prepare and present an oral presentation

Hafta 2: Why are research proposals important?
Hafta 3: Literature Review
Hafta 4: Literature Review

Hafta 6: How to write an introduction

Hafta 10: Presenting Proposal Ideas: Oral Presentations
Hafta 11: Ethics
Hafta 12: Peer Review Process
Hafta 13: How to prepare a scientific poster?
Hafta 14: Poster Presentations & Overall Feedback

Publication manual of the American psychological association (7th ed.), American Psychological Association, A. P., 2020, Washington, DC American Psychological Association

courses.name: Social Psychology
courses.purpose: This course introduces the field of social psychology and its empirical approach to understanding how individuals think, feel, and behave in social contexts.
courses.description: This course is a comprehensive introduction to the field of empirical research which examines the individual in a social setting. It covers areas such as social cognition, social perception, attitudes, conformity, and aggression.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of major theoretical perspectives, concepts, and research in social psychology
Demonstrate knowledge of recent developments in social psychological research
Demonstrate knowledge of how social psychological research is conducted
Students can evaluate strengths and weaknesses of social psychological research

Hafta 2: Introducing Social Psychology

courses.name: Brain and Behavior
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the brain and nervous system as the biological basis of mental activity and behavior. It aims to explain how brain structure and function relate to mind, behavior, and psychological processes.
courses.description: This course covers the structure and workings of the brain, the main characteristics of the nervous system, and their relationship to mind and behavior. Topics include cells of the nervous system, psychopharmacology, vision, sleep and biological rhythms, emotion, learning and memory, human communication, neurological disorders, schizophrenia and affective disorders, stress, anxiety, and neurodevelopmental disorders.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the biological mechanisms that underlie behavior
Demonstrate knowledge of the role of genetics, evolution, hormones, brain structure, and function and how they influence human behavior
Students will learn and demonstrate knowledge of the gross and fine structure of the nervous system
Students will learn and demonstrate knowledge of the basic functioning of the nervous system and the interaction between genes and environment.
Students will learn and demonstrate knowledge of the research methods used in biological psychology

courses.name: Research Methods in Psychological Science I
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the ways psychologists study behavior and to the logic of psychological research. It aims to build skills in research design, hypothesis development, and scientific writing using appropriate bibliographic sources and APA style.
courses.description: This course covers research design in psychology, hypothesis development, scientific paper writing, and the use of bibliographic sources. Students carry out a series of studies and write them up in American Psychological Association style.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate ability to critically evaluate psychological research
Demonstrate in-depth understanding of different research designs design components
Demonstrate learning of how to identify reliable resources and draft a research review outline
Demonstrate learning of how to read an article
