courses.name: Industrial Design Project I
courses.purpose: Capstone design project. Technical and innovative group project emphasizing engineering design principles on a specific topic in any field of computer science and engineering in collaboration with industry partners.
courses.description: Documentation on the specifications, analysis, and requirements of the project. Presentation and demo.

courses.name: Industrial Design Project II
courses.purpose: Continuation of the capstone design project started in the CS 493 course, with the same team. The course aims to develop students' ability to carry out a technical and innovative group project by applying engineering design principles to a specific topic in computer science and engineering, in collaboration with industry partners.
courses.description: Technical and innovative group project emphasizing engineering design principles on a specific topic in any field of computer science and engineering in collaboration with industry partners. Documentation on the detailed design and implementation of the project. Presentation and demo.

courses.name: Innovative Design and Entrepreneurship I
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamentals of design and entrepreneurship through the development of an idea into a marketable end product within the framework of a simulated start-up company.
courses.description: The course covers the inception of a start-up company, business plan preparation, fundamentals of project management, product design stages, and the incorporation of standards, quality directives, social factors, and environmental factors. Seminars by experts in the field and concept demonstration of the end product are also included.

courses.name: Principles of Microeconomics
courses.purpose: This course introduces the core concepts of microeconomics and shows how they can be applied to current events and real-world decision making.
courses.description: Introduction of core microeconomic concepts, focusing on application of these principles in current events. Main topics include demand and supply analysis, firm behavior and the studying of market structures and their welfare analysis.
learning_outcomes: Can interpret real life events using microeconomic principles (K1)
Can differentiate between economic concepts that govern social decision making (K2)
Can explain the deviations of some theories from observed social phenomena and recommend changes (L3)
Can carry out counterfactual scenarios by using lectured material (S4)
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Economics: Foundations and Models [Ch 1]
Hafta 2: Trade-offs, Comparative Advantage, and the Market System [Ch 2]
Hafta 3: Where Prices Come from: The Interaction of Demand and Supply [Ch 3]
Hafta 4: Elasticity: The Responsiveness of Demand and Supply [Ch 6]
Hafta 5: Economic Efficiency, Government Price Setting, and Taxes [Ch 4]
Hafta 6: Externalities, Environmental Policy, and Public Goods [Ch 5]
Hafta 7: Externalities Continues
Hafta 8: Technology, Production and Costs [Ch 11]
Hafta 9: Firms in Perfectly Competitive Markets [Ch 12]
Hafta 10: Firms in Perfectly Competitive Markets [Ch 12 con’t]
Hafta 11: Monopolistic Competition Monopoly and Antitrust Policy [Ch 13&15]
Hafta 12: Monopoly Continues
Hafta 13: Oligopoly [14]
Hafta 14: Oligopoly Continues
resources: Economics, Hubbard&O'Brien, Pearson Prantice Hall

courses.name: Principles of Macroeconomics
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to core macroeconomic concepts and shows how they are applied to current economic events. It aims to build a foundation for understanding aggregate economic performance and policy analysis.
courses.description: Introduction to core macroeconomic concepts, focusing on their application to current economic events. Main topics include determination of output, inflation, interest rates, employment and unemployment.
learning_outcomes: Can evaluate the economic performance of a nation at the aggregate level: To measure GDP (gross domestic product), to calculate inflation and unemployment rate, to calculate short and long term growth rates and analyzing policies for long term growth.

Can analyze theoretical knowledge (i.e. Aggregate expenditure and AD-AS analysis) regarding short term economic fluctuations (or business cycles): To measure equilibrium GDP, expansionary and recessionary gaps.

Can decide on application of monetary and fiscal economic policies as well as their directions to fight with recession, expansion, stagflation and unemployment problems: To calculate the proper amount of taxes or amount of money supply in dealing with business cycles; distinguishing of short and long term Phillips curves regarding policies related with unemployment-inflation trade off.

Can analyze and decide on various macroeconomic policy decisions in an open economy: to evaluate balance of payments, to decide on monetary and fiscal policies in an open economy and compare these policies with a closed economy. To analyze the transactions in foreign exchange markets and to decide on the values of the exchange rates accordingly.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Chapter 19: GDP-Measuring Total Production and Income
Hafta 2: Chapter 20: Unemployment&Inflation
Hafta 3: Chapters 21,22: Economic Growth, the Financial System and Business Cycles;Long Run Economic Growth-Sources and Policies
Hafta 4: Chapters 23,24:Output and Expenditure in the Short-run;Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply Analysis
Hafta 5: Chapter 24:Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply analysis
Hafta 6: Chapter 25:Money,Banks,and the Central Banking System
Hafta 7: Chapter26:Monetary Policy
Hafta 8: Chapter27:Fiscal Policy
Hafta 9: Chapter28:Inflation,Unemployment,and the Central Bank
Hafta 10: Chptr28:Inflation,Unemloyment,and the Central Bank
Hafta 11: Chptr29:Macroeconomics in an Open Economy
Hafta 12: Chptrs29&30:Macroeconomics in Open Economy;The International Financial System
Hafta 13: Chptr30-31:The International Financial System
Hafta 14: Review
resources: ECONOMICS, R.Glann Hubbard&A.Patric O`Brien, Any Edition, Pearson-Prentice Hall

courses.name: History of Civilization I
courses.purpose: This course provides background to the origins of early Western civilizations and examines the emergence and spread of the earliest civilizations in the Ancient Near East. It also introduces the development of civilization in classical and medieval Europe and its broader globalization process culminating in Western Europe.
courses.description: The course deals with the nature and spread of the earliest civilizations in the Ancient Near East and the development of civilization in classical and medieval Europe, with attention to their political, social, economic, and religious life.
learning_outcomes: The students can demonstrate a familiarity with the main political, social and ideological developments in world history during the ancient and medieval periods.
The students can demonstrate an awareness of cultural differences in the past as well as today.
The students can read and analyze critically a variety of historical texts, in original English or English translation.
The students can research and write a coherent and structured essay by using a variety of resources.
The students can prepare and deliver a clear and structured presentation about a specific historical theme.
The students can collaborate with other students when working on more than one assignment.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: Topic 1: The Ancient Middle East (1)
Hafta 3: Topic 1: The Ancient Middle East (2)
Hafta 4: Topic 1: The Ancient Middle East (3)
Hafta 5: Topic 2: The Classical World: Greeks and Romans (1)
Hafta 6: Topic 2: The Classical World: Greeks and Romans (2)
Hafta 7: Topic 2: The Classical World: Greeks and Romans (3)
Hafta 8: Mid-Term
Hafta 9: Topic 3: Monotheistic Religions (1)
Hafta 10: Topic 3: Monotheistic Religions (2)
Hafta 11: Topic 3: Monotheistic Religions (3)
Hafta 12: Topic 4: The Middle Ages and Renaissance (1)
Hafta 13: Topic 4: The Middle Ages and Renaissance (2)
Hafta 14: Topic 4: The Middle Ages and Renaissance (3)
resources: Civilization in the West, Vol. 1 to 1715, Mark Kishlansky, Patrick Geary and Patricia O’Brien, 7 (2008), Pearson
Documents supplied by Instructor

courses.name: History of Civilization II
courses.purpose: This course provides background to the origins of modern Western civilization and helps students understand the development of European society from around AD 1500 to the present.
courses.description: The course deals with the political, social, economic, and religious life of the West from around AD 1500 until the present, focusing on the development of modern Western civilization.
learning_outcomes: The students can demonstrate a familiarity with the main political, social and ideological developments in world history during the modern period (after AD 1500).
The students can demonstrate an awareness of cultural differences in the past as well as today.
The students can read and analyze critically a variety of historical texts, in original English or English translation.
The students can research and write a coherent and structured essay by using a variety of resources.
The students can prepare and deliver a clear and structured presentation about a specific historical theme.
The students can collaborate with other students when working on more than one assignment.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: Topic 1: 15th and 16th Centuries (1)
Hafta 3: Topic 1: 15th and 16th Centuries (2)
Hafta 4: Topic 1: 15th and 16th Centuries (3)
Hafta 5: Topic 2: 17th and 18th centuries (1)
Hafta 6: Topic 2: 17th and 18th centuries (2)
Hafta 7: Topic 2: 17th and 18th centuries (3)
Hafta 8: Mid-Term
Hafta 9: Topic 3: 19th Century (1)
Hafta 10: Topic 3: 19th Century (2)
Hafta 11: Topic 3: 19th Century (3)
Hafta 12: Topic 4: 20th Century (1)
Hafta 13: Topic 4: 20th Century (2)
Hafta 14: Topic 4: 20th Century (3)
resources: Civilization in the West, vol II, Kishlansky et al.
Documents supplied by Instructor

courses.name: Innovative Design and Entrepreneurship II
courses.purpose: This course is the second part of a two-course sequence (GE 401 and GE 402) designed to help students develop and present startup projects in a realistic entrepreneurial setting. It aims to strengthen students' ability to refine business plans, communicate with investors, and bring a product concept to a working prototype and presentation.
courses.description: The course covers presentation of simulated companies to potential investors in a virtual stock market, marketing of products, investor relations and company publicity, modification and finalization of initial business plans, quality certification processes, completion and presentation of working prototypes, planning and design of the associated production plant, and seminars from experts on startup company management issues.
learning_outcomes: Apply financial management principles to develop reports and business plans.
Develop projects using appropriate project management methods.
Design and develop high-quality web sites.
Apply venture capital fundamentals to fund project development.
Apply knowledge of the stock market in stock market exercise.
Develop a marketing plan and strategy.
Design and develop product using product development tools and techniques.
Develop and present high-standard presentations.
Develop high quality reports that are well-organized and free from errors.
Collaborate with peers from various disciplines to complete project.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to GE402. Discussion of the problems and schedule by the whole group.
Hafta 2: Web page design and typography
Hafta 3: Experiences of an entrepreneur 1: Entrepreneural spirit
Hafta 4: Fundamentals of Project Management
Hafta 5: Company Valuation, Mergers Acquisitions and Initial Public Offerings
Hafta 6: Experiences of an entreprenuer 2: Do's and Dont's in entrepreneurship
Hafta 7: Traders' conference: Trade Market, Marketing
Hafta 8: Experiences of an entreprenuer 3: Traps and ways of attracting capital investment to your startup company
Hafta 9: Competition law
Hafta 10: Experiences of an entreprenuer 4 How to start and run a small business
Hafta 11: Venture capital: How to apply for venture capital
Hafta 12: Experiences of an entreprenuer 5: Technical challenges and creating the focus in a startup company
Hafta 13: Quality certification process
Hafta 14: Demonstrations of products and peer grade assignment process: How to get along in a multi-disciplinary environment.

courses.name: Introduction to World Politics
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to international relations by presenting the basic concepts, approaches and major contemporary currents in world politics. The purpose of the course is to provide students with a framework for analysis whereby they can understand and evaluate international phenomena.
courses.description: It covers a wide range of topics including security issues such as war, terrorism, diplomacy and arms control; ecological issues such as climate change and resource depletion, and economic issues such as development, world trade and globalization.
learning_outcomes: Identify and relate to diverse perspectives and views regarding international phenomena
Distinguish between central concepts and employ key methods of social inquiry
Apply the appropriate level of analysis to past and present world events
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: Histories
Hafta 3: Theories I (traditional)
Hafta 4: Theories II (critical)
Hafta 5: International Organisations and Global Governance
Hafta 6: International Security
Hafta 7: Actors Beyond States
Hafta 8: International Law
Hafta 9: Poverty and Wealth
Hafta 10: Global Health
Hafta 11: Environment and Climate
Hafta 12: Connectivity
Hafta 13: Mobility
Hafta 14: Transnational Terrorism
resources: Foundations of International Relations, Stephen McGlinchey, 2022, Bloomsbury Academic
FZ policy: A student must have taken the mid-term exam to qualify for the final exam, please see your syllabus
FULL syllabus, go to Moodle

courses.name: An Introduction to the History of the Ottoman Empire
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with an understanding of what history is, why Ottoman history is studied, and how the legacies of the past continue to shape the present. It also situates the Ottoman Empire within Turkic, Islamic, and world history.
courses.description: Ottoman Empire, from its emergence in the early 14th century as a frontier principality in the northwest Anatolia until its dissolution after WWI, was one of the longest-lasting empires in the history of the world. Topics include pre-Ottoman Anatolia, emergence of the Ottomans, institutionalization of power, land regime, slave system, Ottoman law, Ottoman economic mind and trade, and intercommunal relations.
learning_outcomes: Acquire a general understanding of the course and context of the history of the Ottoman Empire
Discuss particular topics within the history of the Ottoman Empire
Present arguments in written form on particular topics in the history of the Ottoman Empire
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: What is History? Why We Study Ottoman History? Assessing Ottoman Empire as a Part of Turkic, Islamic and World History; Legacies of the Past in the Present
Hafta 2: Pre-Ottoman Anatolia and Rumelia, Byzantine Empire, the Mongol-Steppe Empires, Migrations into Anatolia, Anatolian Seljukids / Political, Cultural and Intellectual Life in Medieval Anatolia
Hafta 3: Ottomans as a Frontier Principality / Emergence of the Ottomans / Daily Life and Culture at the Borders
Hafta 4: From a Frontier Principality to an Empire
Hafta 5: Ottomans as a World Power / Ottoman-Habsburg Relations; Conquest of the East and Its Consequences / the Safavid Empire
Hafta 6: Ottoman Understanding of Sovereignty and Its Institutions; Ottoman Land Regime, Slave System, Ottoman Law in the classical period
Hafta 7: 17th Century - Period of Crisis, Adaptation and Transformation; the Question of Decline
Hafta 8: 18th Century - The Age of Ayans and Decentralization; Ottoman society and social life / Intercommunal Relations
Hafta 9: Ottoman Economic Mind / Trade and Commerce
Hafta 10: The Social Fabric of the Empire, Gender, Family and Everyday Life
Hafta 11: Ottoman Empire and Modernization; 19th Century - The Age of Reforms
Hafta 12: Towards Nation States - Disintegration and the End of an Empire
Hafta 13: Globalization and the Ottoman Empire
Hafta 14: Recent Trends in Ottoman History and General Discussion / Evaluation
resources: The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age, 1300-1600, Halil İnalcık, 1973
An Economic and Social History of the Ottoman Empire V.2 1600-1914, Halil İnalcık and Donald Quataert, 1994, Cambridge Unv. Press
Between Two Worlds: The Construction of the Ottoman State, Cemal Kafadar, 1995, University of California Press
History of the Ottoman Empire and Modern Turkey, vol. I, Stanford Shaw, 1976, Cambridge University Press
History of the Ottoman Empire and Modern Turkey, vol. II, Stanford Shaw, 1976, Cambridge University Press
Turkey, Islam, Nationalism, and Modernity, A History: 1789-2007, Carter Findley, 2010, Yale Unv. Press

courses.name: Global Environmental Issues
courses.purpose: This course aims to help students understand how global environmental problems have emerged and why they have become a major concern for the international community. It also introduces different ways of protecting the environment and the challenges involved in doing so.
courses.description: The course examines the environmental crisis in the context of world population growth and technological development. It covers major causes of global environmental problems and evaluates a range of responses, including diplomacy, governance, finance, individual responsibility, resistance, and localization.
learning_outcomes: Knowledge of global environmental issues/problems
Knowledge of contending explanations of and different responses to global environmental problems
Ability to reflect critically on the causes and resolvability of global environmental problems
Ability to apply acquired course knowledge in written and oral discussions on global environmental issues/problems
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course introduction
Hafta 2: Cause: Tragedy of the commons
Hafta 3: Cause: Population growth and poverty
Hafta 4: Cause: Capitalism
Hafta 5: Response: Conflict and securitization (case: water scarcity)
Hafta 6: Response: Multilateral diplomacy (case: sustainable development)
Hafta 7: Midterm exam
Hafta 8: Response: Transnational governance (case: climate change)
Hafta 9: Response: Aid and finance (case: deforestation)
Hafta 10: Response: Individualising responsibility (case: unsustainable consumption)
Hafta 11: Response: Problem displacement (case: hazardous substances)
Hafta 12: Response: Resistance and localization (case: unsustainable agriculture)
Hafta 13: Appraising global environmental governance
Hafta 14: Concluding the course
resources: Global Environmental Politics: Problems, Policy, and Practice, Hayley Stevenson, 2018, Cambridge University Press

courses.name: Politics I: What is it?
courses.purpose: This course introduces foundational concepts and themes in political science. It aims to help students understand the nature of politics and the major ideas and structures that shape political life.
courses.description: Foundational concepts and themes in political science, focusing on the nature of politics, political ideologies, political regimes, state-society relations, nationalism, and globalization.
learning_outcomes: Have command of fundamental concepts in political science.
Identify deficiencies in knowledge and practice related to the field.
Have awareness for lifelong learning and questioning.
Communicate field related knowledge and skills in writing.
Analyze theoretical knowledge and evaluate its reflection in practice.
Learn how to receive feedback and make progress accordingly.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Class Meeting and Introduction; Begin Discussion on ‘What is Politics?’ (Chapter 1)
Hafta 2: What is Politics? Basic Concepts and Approaches (Chapter 1 continued) Political Ideologies: Liberalism, Conservatism (Chapter 2)
Hafta 3: Political Ideologies: Liberalism, Conservatism (Chapter 2 continued)
Hafta 4: Political Ideologies: Socialism and other ideologies (Chapter 2 continued)
Hafta 5: Politics and the State (Chapter 3)
Hafta 6: Politics and the State (Chapter 3 continued)
Hafta 7: Democracy and Legitimacy (Chapter 4)
Hafta 8: Regimes of the Modern World (Chapter 5)
Hafta 9: Nations and Nationalism (Chapter 6)
Hafta 10: Nations and Nationalism (Chapter 6-continued)
Hafta 11: Political Economy and Globalization (Chapter 7)
Hafta 12: Politics, Society, and Identity (Chapter 8)
Hafta 13: Politics, Society, and Identity (Chapter 8 continued)
Hafta 14: Review
resources: POLITICS, Andrew Heywood, 5th Edition, Macmillan Red Globe Press, 2019

courses.name: Introduction to Psychology
courses.purpose: This course provides a general introduction to the main issues and findings in psychological science. It introduces the kinds of questions psychologists ask about mind and behavior and the major methods used to answer them.
courses.description: The course covers the major research fields of psychology and presents key findings from biological, cognitive, social, developmental, and personality psychology. It also includes a brief introduction to mental disorders and clinical psychology.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of major theoretical perspectives in psychology.
Demonstrate knowledge of how psychological research is conducted.
Demonstrate knowledge of important topics, concepts and research findings in the major sub-fields of psychology: Biological, cognitive, clinical, and social psychology.
Students will learn and demonstrate knowledge of how psychologists conduct research.
Students will learn and demonstrate knowledge of the major findings from biological, cognitive, clinical, and social psychology.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Greetings, Introducing the Course and the Syllabus
Hafta 2: Introduction to Psychology (textbook Chapter 1)
Hafta 3: Psychological Research (textbook Chapter 2)
Hafta 4: Neuroscience and Behavior (textbook Chapter 3)
Hafta 5: Learning (textbook Chapter 6)
Hafta 6: Learning cont'd (textbook Chapter 6)
Hafta 7: MIDTERM EXAM
Hafta 8: Memory
Hafta 9: Personality (textbook Chapter 13)
Hafta 10: Psychological Disorders (textbook Chapter 15)
Hafta 11: Treatments of Psychological Disorders (textbook Chapter 16)
Hafta 12: Developmental Psychology (textbook Chapter 13)
Hafta 13: Social Psychology (textbook Chapter 17)
Hafta 14: Review
resources: Understanding Psychology, Feldman, R. S., 2019 (14th Ed.), McGraw-Hill
Students need to purchase Connect code from the University bookstore

courses.name: Introduction to Social Psychology
courses.purpose: This course is designed to acquaint the student with the nature and causes of individual behavior in social situations and to identify the factors that shape feelings, behavior, and thought.
courses.description: The course covers the research methods of social psychology, social perception, social cognition, attitudes, prejudice/discrimination, interpersonal attraction, social influence, aggression, individual behavior in groups, and applications of social psychology in health, the legal system, and the work setting (organizations).
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of major theoretical perspectives, concepts, and research in social psychology.
Demonstrate knowledge of how social psychologists design research studies and collect data.
Students will learn and demonstrate knowledge of the nature and causes of individual behaviors in social situations.
Students will learn and demonstrate knowledge of how social psychologists conduct research.
Students will learn and demonstrate knowledge of social influence, the research methods in social psychology, social cognition, attitudes, group processes, and interpersonal attraction.

Hafta 2: Introduction to Social Psychology
Hafta 3: Methodology
Hafta 4: Social Cognition
Hafta 5: Social Perception

Hafta 9: Group Processes

courses.name: Introduction to Sociology
courses.purpose: Introduces students to the subject matter, major concepts, and theoretical approaches of sociology. It aims to build a foundation for understanding how sociologists analyze social life, institutions, and change.
courses.description: Includes readings in the works of both classical and modern theorists. Emphasis is on social organization and stratification, community, power, and social change.
learning_outcomes: Discuss a range of social theories and concepts
Interpret various types of media and literary work from a critical sociological lens
Select and justify basic research methods with regard to various research topics
Critically assess contemporary debates and discourses through the application of core sociological concepts
Criticise the influence of different, intersecting social structures existing in local and global society
Contextualise social issues in relation to local and wider global structures, such as nation states and globalisation
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the course
Hafta 2: Sociological Imagination. Required Reading: C. Wright Mills, The Sociological Imagination, chapter 1 “Promise" (sections 1 and 2)
Hafta 3: Classical Sociology: Durkheim. Required Reading: Émile Durkheim, Suicide, chapter 2 “Egoistic Suicide”
Hafta 4: Classical Sociology: Marx. Required Reading: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, The Communist Manifesto, chapter 1 “Bourgeois and Proletarians”
Hafta 5: Homework, Quiz, and Discussion 1
Hafta 6: Classical Sociology: Weber. Required Reading: Max Weber, The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism, chapter 5 “Asceticism and the Spirit of Capitalism”
Hafta 7: Sociological Perspectives. Required Reading: John Macionis, Sociology, chapter 1.5 & 1.6 “Sociological Perspectives”
Hafta 8: Midterm Exam

Hafta 9: Globalization. Required Reading: Immanuel Wallerstein, World-System Analysis. An Introduction, chapter 2 “The Modern World-System as a Capitalist World-Economy: Production, Surplus-Value, and Polarization”
Hafta 10: Work. Required Reading: André Gorz, Reclaiming Work, chapter 2 “The Latest Forms of Work”
Hafta 11: Homework, Quiz, and Discussion 2
Hafta 12: Gender. Required Reading: Judith Butler, “Performative Acts and Gender Constitution: An Essay in Phenomenology and Feminist Theory” https://doi.org/10.2307/3207893.
Hafta 13: Race, Ethnicity, Nation. Required Reading: Stuart Hall, Selected Writings on Race and Difference (edited by Paul Gilroy and Ruth Wilson Gilmore), chapter 19 “Race, the Floating Signifier"
Hafta 14: Homework, Quiz, and Discussion 3
resources: Pdf files of individual texts for each week (available on the Moodle)

courses.name: Basic Concepts of Law
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the basic concepts and structure of law. It aims to help students distinguish legal rules from social rules and understand the role of law in social life.
courses.description: Sources of law. Social life and the law. Definitions and concepts. Division of public and private law. Branches of law.
learning_outcomes: A student who completes this course successfully acknowledges the rule of law, by differentiating legal rules and social rules
A student who completed this course successfully analyzes general rules of law under the legal context by categorizing Private and Public Law
A student who fulfills all requirements of this course critically conceptualizes the relationship between law and legal rules in terms of jurisprudence by considering statutes and provisions
A student who completes this course successfully categorizes legal principles and statutes which have effect and bear consequences legally
A student who completes this course successfully embeds legal systems into Turkish legal context by identifying rules and principles in the context of history of law and practices
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction
Hafta 2: The Nature of Law I - The Concept of Legal Validity
Hafta 3: The Nature of Law II - Legal Positivism and Sovereignty
Hafta 4: The Nature of Law III - Legal Rules and Social Rules
Hafta 5: The Nature of Law IV - Natural Law Theories and Adjudicaiton
Hafta 6: The Nature of Law V - The Problem of Legal Interpretation
Hafta 7: Tutorials
Hafta 8: Contemporary Legal Systems I - Constitutional Law
Hafta 9: Contemporary Legal Systems II - Rights
Hafta 10: Contemporary Legal Systems III - International Law
Hafta 11: Contemporary Legal Systems IV - Persons and Property
Hafta 12: Contemporary Legal Systems V - Punishment
Hafta 13: Contemporary Legal Systems VI - Contracts
Hafta 14: Revision
resources: Course Reader, will include each week's mandatory reading

courses.name: Signals and Systems
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the mathematical foundations needed to analyze and understand signals and systems. It prepares students for further study in signal processing, communications, and related engineering topics.
courses.description: Basic discrete and continuous signals and systems, linear time-invariant systems, Fourier analysis for continuous and discrete signals and systems, filtering, sampling of continuous time signals, FIR and IIR filters, z-transform, elementary modulation techniques.
learning_outcomes: Master the mathematical foundations of linear signal theory including concepts such as discrete and continuous time systems, shift-invariant systems, the Fourier transform and frequency-domain analysis, filtering, sampling, modulation, and so forth.
Exhibit mastery of mathematical foundations by applying it to the formulation and solution of signal processing problems
Acquire working knowledge of numerical tools such as Matlab
Use numerical tools to formulate and solve signal processing problems with computers
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Signals and Systems
Hafta 2: Linear Time-Invariant Systems I
Hafta 3: Linear Time-Invariant Systems II
Hafta 4: Fourier Series Representation of Periodic Signals
Hafta 5: The Continuous-Time Fourier Transform I
Hafta 6: The Continuous-Time Fourier Transform II
Hafta 7: The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform I
Hafta 8: The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform II
Hafta 9: Time and Frequency Characterization of Signals and Systems
Hafta 10: Sampling I
Hafta 11: Sampling II
Hafta 12: The Z-Transform I
Hafta 13: The Z-Transform II
Hafta 14: Communication Systems
resources: Signals and Systems, Alan V. Oppenheim, Alan S. Willsky, with S. Hamid Nawab, second (new international) edition, 2014, Pearson

courses.name: Software Engineering Project Management
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the management aspects of software engineering projects, including planning, estimation, scheduling, and control. It also develops awareness of teamwork, leadership, communication, and organizational issues in software projects.
courses.description: Software development process models. Project planning techniques, developing an architecture decomposition view, Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), creating project schedule, resource profiles and Gantt charts. Software project effort and cost estimation techniques and software product size measures. Software metrics, measuring and controlling software products and processes. Risk management, teamwork, leadership, communication and organizational issues.

courses.name: Basics of Signals and Systems
courses.description: Basics of discrete- and continuous-time signals and systems, sinusoids and complex exponentials, phasor representation, spectrum representation, sampling and aliasing, Shannon/Nyquist sampling theorem, finite impulse response (FIR) filters, frequency response of FIR filters, z-transforms, infinite impulse response (IIR) filters, continuous-time signals and systems, linearity, time-invariance, linear time-invariant (LTI) systems, convolution, causality, stability, frequency response of systems, continuous-time Fourier transform.
learning_outcomes: mastering the mathematical foundations of linear signal theory including concepts such as discrete- and continuous-time systems, shift-invariant systems, the Fourier transform and frequency-domain analysis, sampling, and filtering.
representing signals in different domains and mastering the related transformations (z and Fourier transforms).
understanding digital computation within the context of digital filters; modeling systems using first- and second-order digital filters.
understanding and testing for basic system properties such as linearity, time invariance, causality, and stability.
exhibiting mastery of mathematical foundations by applying it to the formulation and solution of signal processing problems.
acquiring a working knowledge of MATLAB.
using numerical tools to formulate and solve signal processing problems with computers.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: overview of the course, sinusoidal signals and their parameters, complex exponential signals and phasor representation
Hafta 2: time domain vs frequency domain, frequency spectrum representation of signals
Hafta 3: Fourier series representation of periodic signals
Hafta 4: quantization, discrete-time sequences, sampling, oversampling, undersampling, Shannon/Nyquist sampling theorem
Hafta 5: Nyquist rate, folding frequencies, reconstruction, aliasing
Hafta 6: discrete-time sequences, digital computation, causality, finite impulse response (FIR) filters
Hafta 7: linearity, time-invariance, linear time-invariant (LTI) systems, discrete-time convolution sum (1D, 2D, 3D)
Hafta 8: frequency response of finite impulse response (FIR) filters
Hafta 9: z-transformation
Hafta 10: infinite impulse response (IIR) filters, 1st- and 2nd-order systems, pole-zero diagrams
Hafta 11: continuous-time systems, linearity, time invariance, and causality revisited, convolution integral for continuous-time systems, stability of systems
Hafta 12: frequency response of continuous-time systems
Hafta 13: continuous-time Fourier transform
Hafta 14: continuous-time Fourier transform pairs and properties
resources: Signal Processing First, J. H. McClellan, R. W. Schafer, and M. A. Yoder, 2003, Pearson, Prentice-Hall
Signals & Systems, A. V. Oppenheim, A. S. Willsky with S. H. Nawab, 1997, Prentice Hall Signal Processing Series

courses.name: Programming Languages
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the fundamental concepts and design principles behind programming languages. It helps students understand how language features are evaluated and how different language paradigms support problem solving.
courses.description: Language evaluation criteria. Describing syntax and semantics. Tools for constructing lexical and syntactical analyzers. Names, bindings, type checking, and scopes. Data types. Expressions and the assignment statement. Statement-level control structures. Subprograms. Abstract data types. Concurrency. Exception handling. Functional programming languages. Logic programming languages.
learning_outcomes: Apply engineering design process to produce solutions that meet specified needs
Contribute and function effectively on a team
Help creating a collaborative and inclusive team-work environment
Work with team mates to establish goals and plan tasks
Work with team mates to meet objectives set
Acquire new knowledge using appropriate learning strategies
Apply new knowledge as needed
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Preliminaries, syntax, semantics
Hafta 2: Describing Syntax (Regular Expressions & Context Free Grammars)
Hafta 3: Lexical Analysis and Parsing
Hafta 4: Parser generator tools (Lex & Yacc)
Hafta 5: Names, Bindings, and Scopes
Hafta 6: Names, Bindings, and Scopes
Hafta 7: Data Types
Hafta 8: Data Types
Hafta 9: Expressions and Assignment Statement, Statement-Level Control Structures
Hafta 10: Subprograms
Hafta 11: Implementation of Subprograms
Hafta 12: Concurrency, Exception Handling, and Event Handling; Functional programming
Hafta 13: Functional programming
Hafta 14: Logic programming
resources: Concepts of Programming Languages, Robert W. Sebesta, 12th Edition, Pearson

courses.name: Software Product Line Engineering
courses.description: Software reuse, SPLE methods, domain engineering, application engineering, commonality and variability analysis, variability modeling, reference architecture, application architecture, software product portfolio management, software product line scoping, testing in SPLE, organization structures for SPLE, risks of SPLE, adoption strategies for SPLE.

courses.name: Computer Architecture
courses.description: Basic hardware structure of modern computing platforms: memory systems, storage systems, interconnects, multiprocessors, accelerators, hardware/software cooperation. Key issues in performance, efficiency, scalability, reliability, security, safety, predictability, and quality of service.

courses.name: Computer Networks
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and principles of computer networks and the Internet. It aims to build a foundation for understanding how networked systems communicate and how common networking protocols operate.
courses.description: Introduction to computer networks and the Internet. Application layer protocols: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS. Socket programming; client/server model; peer-to-peer networking. Transport layer protocols: TCP, UDP. Congestion control in TCP. Network layer protocols: IP. Internet routing. Link layer: error control, multiple access. Data link layer protocols: Ethernet. Local area networks.
learning_outcomes: Apply knowledge of mathematics
Design and implement a software system to meet desired needs
Participate in a team work
Identify requirements of systems and applications
Formulate problems related to computer systems and applications
Solve engineering problems related to computer systems and applications
Use modern software systems and tools
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Computer Networks and the Internet
Hafta 2: Application Layer
Hafta 3: Application Layer (cntd)
Hafta 4: Application Layer (cntd)
Hafta 5: Transport Layer
Hafta 6: Transport Layer (cntd)
Hafta 7: Transport Layer (cntd)
Hafta 8: Network Layer
Hafta 9: Network Layer (cntd)
Hafta 10: Network Layer (cntd)
Hafta 11: Network Layer (cntd)
Hafta 12: Link Layer and Local Area Networks
Hafta 13: Link Layer and Local Area Networks (cntd)
Hafta 14: Link Layer and Local Area Networks (cntd)
resources: Recommended - Textbook: Computer Networks, A.S. Tanenbaum, 2003, Prentice-Hall
Required - Textbook: Computer Networking, J.F. Kurose and K.W. Ross, 2010, Addison Wesley
Recommended - Textbook: Computer Networks, L.L. Peterson and B.S.Davie, 2007, Morgan Kaufman

courses.name: Software Architecture Design
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the fundamental concepts, methods, and techniques used in designing software architectures. It also helps students understand why architectural design matters and how to analyze and evaluate architecture design approaches.
courses.description: Basic concepts, methods and techniques for designing software architectures; rationale for software architecture design, modeling software architecture design, architectural styles/patterns, architectural requirements analysis, comparison and evaluation of architecture design methods, synthesis-based software architecture design, software product-line architectures, domain modeling, domain engineering and application engineering, software architecture implementation, evaluating software architecture designs.

courses.name: Algorithms for Web-Scale Data
courses.purpose: This course introduces algorithmic techniques and models used to process and analyze very large-scale web data efficiently.
courses.description: PageRank algorithm, locality sensitive hashing, map-reduce model, online algorithms for web advertising, recommender systems, frequent itemset counting, and social network analysis.

courses.name: Dynamic and Social Network Analysis
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts, tools, and methods used in social and dynamic network analysis. It aims to help students understand how networks are represented, measured, and analyzed in real-world settings.
courses.description: Overview of network analysis; introduction to social network analysis tools. Social network data: structure and composition; graph and algebraic representation. Classical metrics. Centrality and prestige. Network topology structural balance and transitivity. Detecting communities. Fuzzy overlapping groups. Small world phenomenon. Random networks, dynamic and complex networks. Network formation and evolution over time. Authorship, science, citation networks; geospatial networks; social media networks, malicious networks. Health and disease spread. Term Project.

courses.name: Machine-to-Machine (M2M) Systems
courses.description: Introduction to Machine-to-Machine (M2M), Internet of Things (IoT), M2M node manufacturing, M2M node programming, sensors and sensor programming, Global System for Mobile (GSM) modules, GSM programming, Global Positioning System (GPS) module and GPS programming, Smart Cities, Intelligent Operations, End-to-End (E2E) testing.

courses.name: Parallel Computing
courses.description: Parallel programming platforms: distributed memory, shared address space, accelerators. Principles of parallel algorithm design: decomposition techniques, tasks and interactions, mapping for load balancing, interaction overheads, parallel algorithm models (data-parallel, task-graph, work-pool, master-slave, pipeline). Basic communication operations. Analytical modeling of parallel programs: sources of parallel programming overhead, performance metrics for parallel systems, scalability of parallel systems (speedup, efficiency, cost, overhead function, isoefficiency, cost optimality, degree of concurrency, granularity), parallel programming paradigms: programming using MPI, programming shared address space platforms (threads, OpenMP, Intel Thread Building Blocks), programming GPU's (CUDA, OpenCL). Parallel computing kernels: matrix transposition, matrix-vector multiplication, matrix-matrix multiplication, matrix partitioning schemes for load-balancing and communication minimization.
learning_outcomes: Use performance analysis tools and software pacakages to estimate parallel performance improvement and optimization opportunities
Implement parallel algorithms using different parallel paradigms including OpenMP, MPI, pThreads, and GPU programming
Design and implement parallel programs using parallel algorithm design methods and analytical modeling of parallel programs
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Motivating Parallelism Scope of Parallel Computing Organization and Contents of the Text
Hafta 2: Implicit Parallelism: Trends in Microprocessor Architectures Limitations of Memory System Performance

Hafta 3: Parallel Programming Platforms Dichotomy of Parallel Computing Platforms Physical Organization of Parallel Platforms Communication Costs in Parallel Machines Shared Address Space Parallelization: OpenMP

Hafta 4: Parallel Programming Platforms Routing Mechanisms for Interconnection Networks Programming Using the Message Passing Paradigm Principles of Message-Passing Programming The Building Blocks: Send and Receive Operations MPI: The Message Passing Interface

Hafta 5: Basic Communication Operations One-to-All Broadcast and One-to-All Reduction All-to-All Broadcast and Reduction All-Reduce and Prefix-Sum Operations Scatter and Gather All-to-All Personalized Communication Circular Shift Improving the Speed of Some Communication Operations
Hafta 6: Principles of Parallel Algorithm Design Decomposition Techniques Characteristics of Tasks and Interactions Mapping Techniques for Load Balancing
Hafta 7: Principles of Parallel Algorithm Design Methods for Containing Interaction Overheads Parallel Algorithm Models

Hafta 8: Analytical Modeling of Parallel Programs Sources of Overhead in Parallel Programs Performance Metrics for Parallel Systems Effect of Granularity and Data Mapping on Performance Scalability of Parallel Systems Minimum Execution Time and Minimum Cost-Optimal Execution Time

Hafta 9: Programming Using the Message Passing Paradigm Topologies and Embedding Overlapping Communication with Computation Collective Communication and Computation Operations Groups and Communicators
Hafta 10: Parallel Programming Concepts Coverage Granularity Locality Advanced MPI
Hafta 11: GPU Programming: CUDA
Hafta 12: GPU Programming: CUDA
Hafta 13: Parallelization of Kernel operations: GEMM SpGEMM SpMV SpMM
Hafta 14: Parallelization of ML applications: Tensor Decomposition SGD GNN
resources: Introduction to Parallel Computing, Grama, Gupta, Karypis, Kumar, 2nd Edition, Addison-Wesley

courses.name: Information Retrieval Systems
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and methods used to store, organize, search, and retrieve information efficiently. It aims to help students understand how information retrieval systems work and how they are evaluated in practice.
courses.description: Introduction to information storage and retrieval (IR). IR vs. DBMS. User perspective, search models, evaluation of IR systems. Formal IR models. Data structures and techniques including inverted files, signature files, information filtering, clustering and cluster-based retrieval, hypertext and multimedia systems. IR and the Internet, browsing strategies, search engines, web robots and intelligent agents.

courses.name: Software Design Patterns
courses.purpose: This course introduces software design patterns and the principles behind them. It aims to help students design and develop scalable, maintainable, and flexible software systems using object-oriented design principles, refactoring, and proven pattern-based solutions.
courses.description: Introduction to software design patterns and principles. Fundamental principles and techniques for designing and developing scalable, maintainable, and flexible software systems. Object-oriented design principles and their application in software development. Refactoring techniques for improving software quality, reliability, and performance. Creational patterns for managing object creation, structural patterns for organizing system components, and behavioral patterns for modeling complex interactions. Case studies and research in design patterns. Mining design patterns from open-source software repositories. Design patterns in real-world scenarios.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Software Design Patterns
Hafta 2: Object Oriented Design Principles
Hafta 3: Object Oriented Design Principles
Hafta 4: Refactoring
Hafta 5: Refactoring
Hafta 6: Creational Design Patterns
Hafta 7: Structural Design Patterns
Hafta 8: Structural Design Patterns
Hafta 9: Behavioral Design Patterns
Hafta 10: Behavioral Design Patterns
Hafta 11: Mining Design Patterns
Hafta 12: Midterm
Hafta 13: Case Studies and Research in Design Patterns
Hafta 14: Final Project Presentations
resources: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software, Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, John Vlissides, Grady Booch, 1994
Head First Design Patterns, Eric Freeman, Elisabeth Robson, Bert Bates, Kathy Sierra, 2004
Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code, Martin Fowler
Clean Code: A Handbook of Agile Software Craftsmanship, Robert C. Martin

courses.name: Automated Program Repair
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental ideas and techniques used to find and fix software defects automatically. It aims to build students' understanding of debugging, fault localization, program synthesis, and patch validation methods used in automated program repair.
courses.description: Fundamental ideas and techniques for finding and fixing software defects. Software defects and fault localization: types of software defects, debugging and fault localization techniques, data collection and analysis for defect identification. Program synthesis and transformation: heuristic-based, template-based, constraint-based and learning-based approaches. Patch assessment and validation. Evaluation metrics and benchmarking.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to automated program repair
Hafta 2: Software defects and fault localization overview
Hafta 3: Types of software defects
Hafta 4: Debugging and fault localization techniques
Hafta 5: Data collection and analysis for defect identification
Hafta 6: Program synthesis and transformation overview
Hafta 7: Heuristic-based approaches
Hafta 8: Template-based approaches
Hafta 9: Constraint-based approaches
Hafta 10: Learning-based approaches
Hafta 11: Patch assessment and validation
Hafta 12: Midterm
Hafta 13: Evaluation metrics and benchmarking
Hafta 14: Project final presentations
resources: The Debugging — Book Tools and Techniques for Automated Software Debugging, Andreas Zeller, 2023

courses.name: Embedded Systems
courses.description: A First Look at Embedded Systems. Characteristics and Quality of Embedded Systems. Hardware Fundamentals. Embedded Software Development Tools. PIC, AVR and ARM Overview. Standard Single-purpose Processor Peripherals. Interrupts and Survey of Software Architectures. Interacting with the Real World: Sensors. Interacting with the Real World: Actuators, Serial Interfaces. Introduction to Real-time Operating Systems. Operating System Services. Basic Design Using a Real-time Operating System. Linear Feedback Systems. Wireless Sensor Networks, Internet-of-Things and Cyberphysical Systems.

courses.name: Distributed Systems and Algorithms
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and algorithms of distributed systems. It aims to develop students' understanding of both the theoretical foundations and practical mechanisms used to design, analyze, and build distributed applications.
courses.description: Fundamentals of distributed systems and algorithms. Theoretical aspects include proving program properties, time synchronization, logical clocks, distributed global snapshots, mutual exclusion, consensus, and fault-tolerance. Practical aspects include remote procedure calls (RPC), distributed objects, message passing interface (MPI), distributed shared memory (DSM), tuple spaces, distributed file systems such as NFS, web-based systems (DNS, web caching, and content distribution networks (CDNs)), map-reduce systems, key/value stores, BSP (bulk-synchronous processing) style computation, and peer-to-peer systems.

courses.name: Cloud Computing
courses.purpose: Hands-on introduction to cloud computing and its applications.
courses.description: Cloud computing services and infrastructures, including virtualization, data center networking, wide-area storage/replication, distributed file systems, development tools, fundamental tradeoffs and algorithms such as the CAP theorem and NoSQL systems, and applications such as big-data analysis, real-time data systems, and large-scale web services. The course also includes hands-on exploration of cloud services via Google Cloud Computing and a term project involving programming applications with backend components running on the cloud.

courses.name: Learning for Robotics
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the fundamental ideas and techniques used to construct intelligent robotic systems that act in the world. It emphasizes how learning-based methods can be combined with robotics to enable perception, planning, dynamics, and control.
courses.description: Fundamental ideas and techniques for constructing intelligent (robotic) systems acting in the world. Perception (including approaches based on deep learning and approaches based on 3D geometry), planning (robot kinematics and trajectory generation, collision-free motion planning, task-and-motion planning, and planning under uncertainty), as well as dynamics and control (both model-based and learning-based). Deep reinforcement learning approaches (policy gradients, actor-critic algorithms, value function and q-function methods, inverse reinforcement learning, meta-learning). Highlights of state-of-the-art methods and application domains.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Intelligent interaction: Passive vs. Active/Embodied AI
Hafta 2: Robotics Fundamentals: Kinematics (FK, IK, Diff IK, Optimization)
Hafta 3: Kinematics + Motion Planning
Hafta 4: Motion Planning (Trajectory Optimization, Sampling-based Methods)
Hafta 5: Perception: geometric vision, point clouds + integration with robotic tasks
Hafta 6: Deep perception: representation learning for robotic manipulation
Hafta 7: Vision-Language-Action (VLAs) Models
Hafta 8: Vision-Language-Action (VLAs) Models
Hafta 9: Vision-Language-Action (VLAs) Models
Hafta 10: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)
Hafta 11: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)
Hafta 12: Sequential Robotic Manipulation, Task and Motion Planning (TAMP)
Hafta 13: Learning for Task and Motion Planning (TAMP)
Hafta 14: Final project presentations
resources: Robotic Manipulation: Perception, Planning, and Control, Russ Tedrake, 2023
Deep Learning, Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville, 2016
Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction, Richard S. Sutton, Andrew G. Barto, 2018
Reinforcement Learning and Optimal Control, Dimitri P. Bertsekas, 2023

courses.name: Artificial Intelligence
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental ideas and techniques underlying the design of intelligent systems.
courses.description: Topics to be covered include search methods, optimality and heuristics for problem solving, probability theory, decision-making in fully informed, partially observable and adversarial settings, introductory reinforcement learning, learning from data (machine/deep learning), graphical models and causality, and application domains of state-of-the-art methods.
learning_outcomes: Design algorithms for engineering problems under given memory and space constraints
Demonstrate understanding of the impact of technology on society
Demonstrate knowledge of contemporary issues related with computer engineering
Write programs in new programming languages
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to (Artificial) Intelligence, and Search Problems
Hafta 2: Problem Solving by (Uninformed) Search
Hafta 3: Problem Solving by Search, Optimality, Heuristics, and Adversarial Search
Hafta 4: Probabilistic Reasoning (Bayes Nets: Representation, (Conditional) Independence)
Hafta 5: Probabilistic Reasoning (Bayes Nets: Inference, Sampling)
Hafta 6: Probabilistic Reasoning (Advanced Topics: HMMs, Particle/Kalman Filters, etc.)
Hafta 7: Sequential Decision Making: Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), Reinforcement Learning (RL)
Hafta 8: Sequential Decision Making: MDPs, RL
Hafta 9: Function Approximation: Why and How?
Hafta 10: Function Approximation: Why and How?
Hafta 11: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)
Hafta 12: DRL
Hafta 13: Evolutionary Algorithms, Genetic Algorithms, Evolutionary Strategies
Hafta 14: Learning/AI for Robotics
resources: Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach, Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig, 2020/4th Ed., Pearson Education

courses.name: Software Verification and Validation
courses.purpose: This course introduces the motivation, concepts, and techniques used to verify and validate software systems. It aims to help students understand how software quality is assessed and how verification and validation methods are applied throughout the software development lifecycle.
courses.description: Introduction and motivation for verification and validation; software testing overview, fundamentals of test process, general principles of testing, definitions and concepts, testing in software development life cycle, types of testing, levels of testing, test metrics; software inspection and code reviews, technical reviews, pair programming; specification-based testing, input-based partitioning, equivalence class partitioning, boundary value analysis, state transition test, decision table technique, used case testing; structural testing, graph coverage, logic coverage, syntax-based testing, statement coverage, branch coverage, condition coverage, path coverage, instrumentation and tool support; system, acceptance, and regression testing; model-based testing; run-time verification; model-checking, temporal logic in finite-state verification, computational tree logic; safety analysis and software reliability engineering.
learning_outcomes: Understand the concepts and theory related to software verification, validation, general concepts and theory related to software testing
Understand the concepts and theory related to model-based testing, model-checking and know how to apply these
Understand the concepts and theory related to run-time verification and know how to apply these
Select and apply appropriate software verification and validation techniques in development projects
Understand the possibilities and limitations of software verification and validation
Learn to use automated verification and validation tools
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Course Organization
Hafta 2: Motivation for Software Verification and Validation; Chapter 1,2,3,4
Hafta 3: Software Quality Models; Software Reliability; Chapter 1,2,3,4
Hafta 4: Finite models, dependency and data flow models; Chapter 6,7; Quiz 1
Hafta 5: Software Testing Levels
Hafta 6: Test Automation Framework (JUnit); Test Case Selection and Adequacy; Chapter 9
Hafta 7: Functional Testing; Chapter 10
Hafta 8: Combinatorial Testing; Chapter 11
Hafta 9: Finite Models; Chapter 5, 6
Hafta 10: No Lectures; Mid-term exam, Nov 12
Hafta 11: Dependence and dataflow models; Structural Testing - Coverage Metrics; Chapter 12, 13, 14
Hafta 12: Structural Testing - Dataflow Testing; Quiz 2
Hafta 13: Process; Test-Driven Development; Chapter 20,21
Hafta 14: Model Checking; Summary; Chapter 8,14
resources: Software Testing and Analysis: Process, Principles and Techniques, Wiley, ISBN 0471455938., Mauro Pezzè, Michal Young, 2008, Wiley
Foundations of Software Testing (2nd Edition), Aditya P. Mathur, 2013, Pearson
Software Engineering And Testing: An Introduction (Computer Science), B.B. Agarwal, M. Gupta, S.P. Tayal, Jones & Bartlett
Software Engineering, “A practitioner’s Approach” 8th Edition, Roger S. Pressman, Bruce R. Maxim, 2015, McGraw Hill International Edition
Software Engineering, Ian Sommerville, 2011, Addison-Wesley

courses.name: Introduction to Machine Learning
courses.description: Probability and statistics review, estimation (maximum likelihood, maximum a posterior), loss functions, model selection, feature representation, feature selection, naive Bayes, linear discriminant analysis, logistic regression, k-nearest neighbor, support vector machines, deep learning, linear regression, decision trees, ensemble methods (bagging, random forest, boosting) and clustering.
learning_outcomes: Apply knowledge of mathematics
Design and conduct experiments on algorithm time complexity, analyze and interpret the results
Utilize and extend known algorithms, design paradigms and data structures for the solution of engineering problems
Use modern software systems and tools
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Machine Learning
Hafta 2: Probability/Statistics Review and Estimation
Hafta 3: Naive Bayes Classifier
Hafta 4: Feature Selection
Hafta 5: Feature Extraction and Performance Metrics
Hafta 6: Google Colab Tutorial
Hafta 7: Linear Regression
Hafta 8: Logistic Regression
Hafta 9: SVM
Hafta 10: Neural Networks
Hafta 11: Progress Presentations and PyTorch Tutorial
Hafta 12: Decision Trees
Hafta 13: Ensemble Learning
Hafta 14: Clustering and Project Presentations

courses.name: Computer Graphics I
courses.description: Graphics systems and models. Graphics programming. Interaction and animation. Geometric objects and transformations. Viewing. Lighting and shading. Rasterization: from geometry to pixels. Modeling and hierarchy. Curves and surfaces. Advanced rendering.

courses.name: Application Lifecycle Management
courses.purpose: To introduce the application lifecycle management process and its role in large-scale software development organizations. The course aims to build understanding of how software projects are planned, controlled, and improved across the full lifecycle.
courses.description: Application lifecycle management process, software development in large-scale IT organizations, software development productivity, agile software development, project management, requirements management, architecture and design, software development, software test management, software configuration management, change management, and build management.

courses.name: Introduction to Applied Cryptography
courses.description: Cryptography, Symmetric-key cryptography: Block ciphers, Stream Ciphers, Message Authentication Codes. Public-Key Cryptography: RSA Encryption, Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange, ElGamal Encryption. Elliptic Curve Cryptography: ECDSA, ECDH. Authenticated Key Exchange: TLS 1.3. Power and timing side channel attacks. Lattice-based post-quantum cryptography. Applications of digital signatures, secure multi party computation and zero-knowledge proofs in blockchains.

courses.name: Numerical Methods
courses.description: Power tools of the trade, polynomial interpolation, piecewise polynomial interpolation, numerical integration, matrix computations, linear systems, the QR and Cholesky factorizations, nonlinear equations and optimization, the initial value problem.

courses.name: Computer Systems Performance Analysis
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the fundamental principles and methods used to model and evaluate computer systems performance. It provides the analytical background needed to understand system behavior and performance trade-offs.
courses.description: Introduction to modeling and evaluation of computer systems. Introduction to queueing theory. Little's law and other operational laws. Discrete time Markov Chains. Ergodicity theory. Real-world applications of discrete time Markov Chains. Poisson process. Continuous time Markov Chains. M/M/1 queue. Server farms. Networks of queues. M/G/1 queue. Smart scheduling.

courses.name: Data Privacy
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts of privacy and privacy properties, and examines why privacy matters in modern computing systems and data-driven applications.
courses.description: Introduction to privacy and privacy properties, privacy-enhancing technologies for data anonymization, crypto-based solutions, machine learning security and privacy, location privacy, privacy of healthcare and genomic data, privacy in e-cash systems and blockchain, privacy in e-voting systems.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Privacy I
Hafta 2: Introduction to Privacy II
Hafta 3: Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs) for data anonymization
Hafta 4: Crypto-Based Solutions
Hafta 5: Machine Learning Security and Privacy - I
Hafta 6: Machine Learning Security and Privacy - II
Hafta 7: Midterm Presentations
Hafta 8: Privacy of Healthcare and Genomic Data
Hafta 9: Privacy in E-cash, Blockchains
Hafta 10: Privacy in E-cash, Blockchains
Hafta 11: Privacy in E-voting
Hafta 12: Location Privacy
Hafta 13: Final Presentations
Hafta 14: Final Presentations
resources: Recommended - Lecture Notes: No text book is required. There will be slides about the modules and weekly reading material.
Slides

courses.name: Biometrics
courses.purpose: Introduction to biometrics and its applications, with emphasis on understanding biometric systems and their role in recognition and security.
courses.description: Introduction to biometrics. Applications of biometrics systems. Biometrics signal processing. Biometrics system design. Physiological biometrics: Face, Fingerprint and Iris recognition. Behavioral biometrics: Speaker (voice) and Gait recognition. Other Biometric modalities. Multi-modal biometrics systems. Performance metrics and Standards for Biometric recognition system. Biometric System Security (Presentation Attack Detection, a.k.a. PAD). Physiological biometrics PAD. Behavioral biometrics. Performance metrics and standards for biometric PAD.

courses.name: Introduction to Cyber Security
courses.description: Basic definitions of cyber security, overview of cyber threats. Cryptology, symmetric/asymmetric cryptography, PKI, CA trust model, SSL/TLS (HTTPS, SFTP etc.). Network basics, overview of TCP/IP, network attacks, security of basic network services, cyber threat prevention mechanisms, network forensics. Overview of penetration testing methodologies and tools. Application security (including web application security). Overview of computer forensics. Malware Analysis. Mobile Security, introduction to mobile operating systems, mobile security concepts. Reverse engineering of android applications. Overview of information security management concept. Cyber security posture: The World and Turkey.

courses.name: Computational Geometry
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental algorithmic techniques used to solve geometric problems in computer science and engineering. It aims to develop students' ability to analyze, design, and apply efficient algorithms for geometric data and spatial computation.
courses.description: Introduction: algorithmic background, data structures, geometric preliminaries, models of computation. Geometric searching: point-location problems, range-searching problems. Convex hulls: problem statement and lower bounds, convex hull algorithms in the plane, Graham's scan, Jarvis's march, Quickhull techniques, divide-and-conquer algorithms, dynamic convex hull, convex hull in 3D. Proximity: a collection of problems, a computational prototype: element uniqueness, lower bounds, the closest-pair problem: a divide-and-conquer approach, the Voronoi diagram, proximity problems solved by the Voronoi diagram. Triangulation: planar triangulations, Delaunay triangulation. Intersections: application areas, planar applications: intersection of convex polygons, intersection of star-shaped polygons, intersection of line segments, 3D applications: intersection of 3D convex polyhedra, intersection of half-spaces.
learning_outcomes: Apply knowledge of mathematics
Utilize and extend known algorithms, design paradigms and data structures for the solution of engineering problems
Use modern software systems and tools
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction: algorithmic background, data structures
Hafta 2: Introduction: geometric preliminaries, models of computation
Hafta 3: Geometric searching: point-location problems
Hafta 4: Geometrc searching: range-searching problems
Hafta 5: Convex hulls: problem statement and lower bounds, convex hull algorithms in the plane, Graham's scan, Jarvis's march
Hafta 6: Convex Hull: Quickhull techniques, divide-and-conquer algorithms, dynamic convex hull, convex hull in 3D
Hafta 7: Proximity: a collection of problems, a computational prototype: element uniqueness, lower bounds, the closest-pair problem: a divide-and-conquer approach
Hafta 8: Proximity problems: the Voronoi diagram, proximity problems solved by the Voronoi diagram
Hafta 9: Triangulation: planar triangulations
Hafta 10: Triangulation: Delaunay triangulation
Hafta 11: Intersections: application areas, planar applications: the intersection of convex polygons, the intersection of star-shaped polygons
Hafta 12: Intersection problems: the intersection of line segments, 3D applications: the intersection of 3D convex polyhedra
Hafta 13: Project presentations
Hafta 14: Project presentations
resources: Computational Geometry: An Introduction, F. P. Preparata and M.I. Shamos, 1985, Springer-Verlag
Computational Geometry: Algorithms and Applications, M. de Berg, M. van Kreveld, M. Overmars, O. Schwarzkopf, 2000/2, Springer-Verlag
Computational Geometry in C, Joseph O'Rourke, 2/1998, Cambridge University Press
Computational Geometry Pages, Jeff Erickson, 1999

courses.name: Algorithms II
courses.purpose: This course aims to build advanced algorithmic problem-solving skills by introducing fundamental data structures and graph algorithms used in efficient computation. It prepares students to analyze, design, and justify the correctness and efficiency of classic algorithms.
courses.description: Augmenting Data Structures: augmenting red-black trees, dynamic order statistics, interval trees. Advanced priority queue implementations: binomial heaps, Fibonacci heaps. Data structures for disjoint sets: linked list representation, analysis of weighted union heuristic; disjoint set forests, analysis of union-by-rank with path compression. Elementary graph algorithms: breadth-first search, depth-first search (DFS), use of DFS for topological sort and strongly connected components, correctness proofs. Minimum Spanning Trees (MST): generic MST algorithm, proof of correctness, the algorithms of Kruskal and Prim. Single source shortest paths: Bellman-Ford algorithm, Dijkstra's algorithm, difference constraints and shortest paths, proofs of shortest-paths properties. All-pairs shortest paths: two DP formulations; matrix multiplication and Floyd-Warshall algorithm, transitive closure of a directed graph, Johnson's algorithm for sparse graphs. Maximum Flows: flow networks, Ford-Fulkerson method, maximum bipartite matching, push-relabel algorithms.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Augmenting Data Structures: augmenting red-black trees, dynamic order statistics, interval trees.
Hafta 2: Advanced priority queue implementations: mergeable-heap operations, binomial heaps, fibonacci heaps, runtime analysis using potential method of analysis
Hafta 3: Data structures for disjoint sets: linked list representation, analysis of weighted union heuristic
Hafta 4: Data structures for disjoint sets: disjoint set forests, analysis of union-by-rank with path compression.
Hafta 5: Graphs and trees: definition, representation and notation. Breadth-First search: correctness of BFS, Breadth first trees
Hafta 6: Depth First search: timestamping vertices, parenthesis theorem, white-path theorem, edge classification.
Hafta 7: Topological sort of dags: DFS-based algorithm, proof of correctness, Kahn's algorithm
Hafta 8: Strongly connected components, use of DFS, proof of correctness, component graph
Hafta 9: Minimum Spanning Tree (MST): generic MST, proof of correctness, two famous greedy algorithms: Kruskal’s and Prim. Fast implementations of Kruskal's and Prim algorithms.

Hafta 10: Single-Source Shortest Paths (SSSP): shortest paths and relaxation, SSSP on dags, Dijkstra’s algorithm for positive edge weigths, Bellman Ford algorithm for general case. How to use Belmann Ford algorithm for a system of difference constraints for solving the feasibility problem on a system of difference constraints.
Hafta 11: All-pairs shortest paths (APSP) - two DP formulations leading to matrix multiplication and Floyd-Warshall algorithms.
Hafta 12: APSPs: transitive closure of a directed graph, Johnson’s algorithm for sparse graphs.
Hafta 13: Maximum Flows: flow networks, Ford-Fulkerson method, residual networks, augmenting paths, max-flow mincut theorem, Edmonds-Karp algorithm
Hafta 14: Maximum Flows: bipartite graph matching, integrality theorem, push-relabel algorithms
resources: Introduction to Algorithms, T. H. Cormen, C. E. Leiserson, R. L. Rivest, and C. Stein, 2009/3rd Ed, Mit Press and McGraw-Hill
Applied and Algorithmic Graph Theory, G. Chartrand and O. R. Oellermann, 1993, McGraw-Hill

courses.name: Bioinformatics Algorithms
courses.description: Algorithms for sequence analysis, Needleman/Wunsch and Smith/Waterman alignment algorithms. Multiple sequence alignment. Pattern matching algorithms and sequence similarity search. Sequence database search. K-mer indexes, probabilistic data structures such as Bloom filters. Alignment-free k-mer composition analysis, applications in metagenomics. Graphs in genome analytics. Practical issues in genomics.
learning_outcomes: Implement and extend known algorithms to solve questions that arise in the field of bioinformatics
Trace existing algorithms on problem sets
Design new algorithms and modify classical methods for biological data analysis
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: A brief introduction to computational complexity and algorithm design techniques
Hafta 2: DNA mapping & motif search. Introduction to exact string search algorithms.
Hafta 3: Exact string search algorithms.
Hafta 4: Exact string search (cont’d) and indexing.
Hafta 5: Elements of dynamic programming, Manhattan tourist problem, introduction to sequence alignment. Global alignment.
Hafta 6: Local alignment, linear space alignment. Bit-vector alignment algorithm.
Hafta 7: Four-Russians trick. Multiple sequence alignment. Partial order alignments.
Hafta 8: Heuristic sequence search. Short introduction to BLAST. Hash table indexes, minimizers and chaining.

Hafta 9: Maximal exact matches (MEMs), maximal unique matches (MUMs) to speed up search. Mapping tools such as BWA-MEM and minimap2. K-mer index structures (hash tables, minimizers, CQF). K-mer “containers” (Bloom filters, SBTs, BSTs).
Hafta 10: Alignment-free k-mer composition analysis. Minimum perfect hashing, MinHash, Jaccard Index.
Hafta 11: Phylogenic tree construction.
Hafta 12: Graphs in genome analysis. OLC, de Bruijn, string graphs. Aligning reads to graphs.
Hafta 13: Applications: short introduction to genome sequencing. Current platforms and data types. Standard file formats.
Hafta 14: Applications: programming libraries, application-specific programming languages.
resources: An Introduction to Bioinformatics Algorithms, Neil Jones and Pavel Pevzner, 2004, MIT Press
Algorithms on Strings, Trees, and Sequences: Computer Science and Computational Biology, Dan Gusfield, 1997, Cambridge University Press
Genome-Scale Algorithm Design, Veli Mäkinen, Djamal Belazzougui, Fabio Cunial, Alexandru I. Tomescu, 2015, Cambridge University Press

courses.name: Natural Language Processing
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental ideas and methods used to analyze and process human language computationally. It aims to provide students with the background needed to understand how modern NLP systems support prediction, extraction, semantic reasoning, and practical applications such as translation and personal assistants.
courses.description: History of natural language processing (NLP). The provenance of analysis and transformation of language by computational techniques. General linguistic preliminaries. Representations of text and speech that can aid prediction, extraction, and semantic reasoning over language. Automatic mining of knowledge from the web. The discipline of machine learning and its significance for NLP. Deep learning as a fundamental method for NLP. Recent technological developments in NLP, including automatic language translators such as Google Translate and personal assistants such as Siri.

courses.name: Introduction to Computer Vision
courses.description: Image acquisition, sampling and quantization. Spatial domain processing. Image enhancement. Texture analysis. Edge detection. Frequency domain processing. Color image processing. Mathematical morphology. Image segmentation and region representations. Statistical and structural scene descriptions. Applications.

courses.name: Teaching Project in Computer Engineering
courses.purpose: This course is designed to help students develop learning modules using contemporary teaching techniques. It aims to improve students' understanding of key issues in the Computer Engineering curriculum.
courses.description: Developing learning modules encompassing contemporary teaching techniques that enhance students' understanding of some key issues in the Computer Engineering curriculum.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate a better understanding of the curriculum
Develop an understanding of contemporary teaching techniques that promote learning
Develop assessment methods that measure critical knowledge and skills required by the curriculum
Improve technical writing and presentation skills.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction and meeting with the target course instructor(s)
Hafta 2: Teaching project
Hafta 3: Teaching project
Hafta 4: Teaching project
Hafta 5: Teaching project
Hafta 6: Teaching project
Hafta 7: Teaching project
Hafta 8: Teaching project
Hafta 9: Teaching project
Hafta 10: Teaching project
Hafta 11: Teaching project
Hafta 12: Teaching project
Hafta 13: Teaching project
Hafta 14: Final report and presentation to instructors/committee

courses.name: Senior Research Project
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to research techniques in computer engineering and science and provides an opportunity to conduct an independent research project under faculty supervision.
courses.description: Students work on a research topic as an independent study under the supervision of a faculty member. The course includes literature survey, research activities, interim report preparation, presentation, and final report preparation. Students also prepare academic papers to present the results of the study.
learning_outcomes: Apply knowledge of mathematics
Use mathematical tools such as asymptotic notation, probabilistic analysis, and amortized analysis for computing the complexity of algorithms
Design and conduct experiments on algorithm time complexity, analyze and interpret the results
Demonstrates creativity in designing novel solutions
Design algorithms for engineering problems under given memory and space constraints.
Prepare reports with high standards in terms of content, organization, style and language
Find relevant sources (e.g., library, Internet, experts) and gathers information and follows technical publications, including web sites
Able to make presentations with high standards in terms of grammar, vocabulary, body language, voice quality, understandability, organization, focus and use of visual tools.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Literature survey
Hafta 2: Research
Hafta 3: Research
Hafta 4: Research
Hafta 5: Research
Hafta 6: Research
Hafta 7: Research
Hafta 8: Research
Hafta 9: Interim Report Preperation
Hafta 10: Research
Hafta 11: Research
Hafta 12: Research
Hafta 13: Research
Hafta 14: Final Report Prepreation

courses.name: Advanced Topics in Computational Biology
courses.purpose: This course introduces advanced computational methods used in modern biological data analysis, with emphasis on genomic and transcriptomic data. It aims to equip students with the conceptual background needed to understand and apply bioinformatics techniques in research and data-driven biology.
courses.description: Methods for genomile analyses. Capillary sequencing and next generation sequencing. Read mapping, SNP, indel and structural variation discovery and genotyping. Genome assembly and scaffolding. RNA-RNA interactions.

courses.name: Linear Algebra in Data Analysis and Machine Learning
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the linear algebra background needed to understand and apply modern data analysis and machine learning methods. It connects core matrix concepts with practical computational techniques used in data science.
courses.description: Linear algebra with applications to data science and computations. Applications of singular value decomposition, weighted least squares approximations, signal and image processing, principal component analysis, covariance and correlation matrices, directed and undirected graphs, matrix factorizations, neural nets and machine learning.
learning_outcomes: an ability to identify, formulate, and solve complex engineering problems by applying principles of engineering, science, and mathematics
